I don't even know what the point is of this game anymore. It's not pushing any boundaries graphically, it's actually fairly low resolution in a lot of places, last gen assets (PlayStation 3 port, content from PlayStation 4 release completely missing).  Combat is good, I feel at home straight away having played every major release since Dead or Alive 3 on the original Xbox. Online functionality is extremely limited to just 'look for opponent' with nothing to do in between (no online lobby of any kind). And now it's Free to 'Demo'.. So what's on offer?  4 fighters. Versus, Survival, Practice and Online modes. That's it.  Team NINJA and KOEI had a great opportunity on their hands here to make this series absolutely endeared by all if they just bit the bullet and made the entire thing Free to Play. They have *so much DLC* on the store, and while I am against them prostituting their female cast with all these skimpy costumes, they could've set a very high standard for fighters on PC and lived off microtransactions for half a decade... But no.  In my opinion this is the best fighting game franchise ever. I love the countermoves and movesets in general and most characters are fairly varied but not 'supernatural'. It feels fairly down to earth for a fighter. But I cannot recommend the PC version - Stay far, far away from it, it's absolute dognuggets. If you have a PlayStation 4 consider buying it for that, if not... well, don't buy anything here. Download the free demo and try it out, but know that there's a way better version for other platforms and that content from that version is never being brought here. Ever. Because Team NINJA and KOEI are absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and don't know how to handle PC releases, or they're just stupidly stubborn Japanese developers who can't admit fault because it would be shameful. Or both.  1031€ in DLC... Now, I know the entire point is for you to pick a 'favorite character' and buy outfits for them, like with League of Legends skins or heck even Counter Strike weapon skins but that's just not how it works in a fighting game. Pricing is ridiculous and if I can't have all the costumes I'll just not have any at all.   Come back to me when you don't have a fragmented piece of garbage on offer - I like my fighters to be complete, out of the box, with all features included. Until then I'll probably continue playing the free version because this is not worth anyone's money.

This game is very addictive. Seems a little simplistic and the controls take a bit of getting used to. I crashed quite a bit until I got the hang of it down and I still am not very good at shooting down ships without a lot of misses but I have been really addicted to this game since I started playing it.   Your a space ship captain and you fly around different sectors completing jobs, mining materials, and fighting bad guys to make money and upgrade your ship. There is a story line but to be honest I really haven't been paying much attention to it as I've been more focused on getting new ships and the best equipment for them. I've almost gotten the heavy cruisers, which is the heaviest ship in the game unlocked and I'm excited for it.   I've found that mining is the easiest and safest way to make money and is almost always necessary as fuel to fly costs money so you can't just go from station to station without either fighting or mining to keep at least enough money to fuel your ship. Fuel is expensive so I recommend upgrading your mining equipment quickly so you can get the best materials so you an offset costs quickly so you have more money for upgrades.   I definitely want to note that the developer is very active with this game. I happen to have started playing the day they released a patch that broke the game. I was stuck in the starting system and couldn't complete the tutorial to leave. They had the issue resolved in less than a day so while it wasn't the best experience, at least they are active and pay attention when issues arise.   I haven't played multiplayer and probably won't as I'm more into the single player experience so I have no recommendations on how that works out but I bet it would be a pretty good time.   I definitely recommend this game if you are interested in a game where you are given a ship and almost free reign to make your fortune and get the most powerful equipment you can.   Only negative I can think of that I ran into other than the update issue is that sometimes you have to end up with issues because the game is so intense in regards to fuel. I ran into a few times where I got stranded because a few systems in a row didn't have any place to refuel. Recommend you keep some power cells handy just in case. Some people may also find some issues with the slow pace of mining as well. Each instance takes about 5 seconds and you are just sitting there. Doesn't seem like much but when you only get 60 pieces every time you mine, it can get tedius to fill a ship with a big cargo hold.... this is especially prevailent when you are going for a specific ore for a mission and are only getting 2 to 4 each time and you need 350 to complete the mission.   Definitely a great game though and I recommend it for anyone that likes space ship games. Definitely not X2 as far as economy goes but its still a great game.

Third-Person shooter/Stealth game with heavy RPG elements. The game has a strong narrative focus and enjoyable stealth and gunplay. The complex dialogue trees and smoothly integrated sidequests are the main replay value, as the rookie and veteran builds have their own dialogue options. Character advancement is fluid, and the high-level skills become overkill in many cases (eg: Chain Shot + Brilliance), while others become virtually essential (eg: Stealth and Infiltrator paths). Perks are interesting and well-implemented, and feel rewarding for trying different approaches to missions.  The game is not without flaws, though. Movement and platforming feels restrictive, specially when compared to pure action 3d platformers. Cover also feels rigid, as some walls and objects don't allow you to hide behind or require you to approach at the right angle or distance to activate the option. Gunsights are not pinpoint acccurate, which leads to a certain frustration during fights, as bullets go stray all the time. Stealth is consistent enough, but tends to shift infrequently, making enemies spot you while on their blind sides some times and not reacting to you in front of them at others. The assault riffle only shoots 3-round bursts. It would've been awesome if you could use one-shot and auto-fire modes. But these flaws are not that significative, since the game is not meant to be an action shooter, full-on stealth or 3d platformer, but a narrative RPG where you can take multiple approaches to challenges.  Gameworld design is more linear than comparable games like Deus Ex: Human Revolution, but is still open enough to allow multiple approaches to the same objectives most of the time. Dialogues are smooth and flow seamlessly, with nice voice acting. The in-mission chat with the controller adds to the experience, and the limited time to choose replies during conversation adds a degree of challenge to 'cutscenes'. Intrigue storyline is interesting and engaging, and changes according to what you did (or didn't) during previous missions, where you've been and who you've met before. Graphics feel a little dated for 2015, but that's a natural byproduct of technological advancement.

UPDATED REVIEW (Andjusted Review for BLACK ICE UPDATE!)  I've put many more hours and was finally able to play ranked matches since my last uninformed review. So here's my stance on the game right now: ♥♥♥♥ing Fantastic, for the most part anyway.  This game has everything it needs to grow a reliable and consistent playerbase. Rainbow Six Siege's asymmetric style of gameplay is unique, and it seems very fresh. This arena shooter pits two teams of 5 operators against each other in intense extremely close quarters combat. The environment could be either your biggest ally or your worst enemy, depending on which team you're on or how your team's been handling a situation. While I think this game is great I cannot argue that it has some issues. Here are simple pros and cons to wet your appetite for destruction.  PROS:  - Fantastic destruction based gameplay forces you to think and adapt to any situation.  - Has serious competitive potential. Ranked mode is good, but obviously the people playing for keeps aren't so nice. Ever.  - SO MANY OPERATORS!  CONS:  - Microphone communication is a must. Text chat is limited to less than 6 words, making communication difficult for those who are either afraid to talk to people, or don't have a microphone. Sometimes, people don't even talk. At all. This makes the game unwinnable a lot. (EDIT: UBISOFT HAS EXPANDED THE AMOUNT OF LETTERS IN TEXT CHAT SIGNIFICANTLY, DISREGARD)  - The community is very hit or miss at best. One can play 10-12 games and only 2 out of those 10-12 would be good, fair, non ♥♥♥♥♥y teammates who take responsibility for their shortcomings instead of blaming the rest of the team. Blaming the rest of the team is only okay if the rest of the team is actually to blame, which is often not the case.  - Matchmaking seems to enjoy hooking people up globally. Which means that damn language barrier strikes again.  - Terrorist hunt. My darling Terrorist hunt what have they done to you... Classic Terrorist Hunt mode is a rarity at best when Ubisoft decided it would be a good idea to just shoehorn in other game modes. And they're terrible.  Overall, Rainbow Six Siege is a great team based game if you have a mic, patience, and good sportsmanship. So grab a corner, make yourself comfortable, and pray to god the enemy team doesn't have Thermite.  7/10  P.S.: The reviews angelizing the community are incredibly situational and subjective. Those reviewers got lucky and got matched up with great players. I never do. Don't buy the game based on the community because you will be thoroughly disappointed.  BLACK ICE UPDATE: Oh Ubisoft. You make me so happy but also so, so mad. Black Ice is the first official content update of R6 Siege and it looks promising. It adds a new map and two new operators. It was released on Feb 2nd, but I can't access the content until the 8th... what the hell Ubisoft. They are pushing this season pass HARD.  I considered lowering the score and giving the game a thumbs down but hell. Every AAA company pushes their season passes so who cares. I can wait a tad longer. I am glad that I get access to the balance update at least. And boy what a hefty balance update it is. Also I found it hilarious to see that Ubisoft still has a sense of humor.   Thatcher finally got his shotgun. Thanks, Ubi.

I never played the board game but I have been a bit curious about it for some time. I'm really glad that I picked up the computer version. The game seems to be one of those elegant games that are easy to learn, but tough to master.  The idea is that you collect gems that you can use to purchase cards. Cards give you bonuses towards purchasing other cards, but also can give you victory points. To be fair to the person who goes last, the game ends at the end of the round whenever a person accumulates 15 victory points.  You also can earn 3 victory points by gaining a noble -- whenever your total bonus matches the numbers on a displayed noble, you earn that noble (assuming no one else has beaten you to him). Earning a noble also gives you a gold gem, which is like a wild card.  The game is certainly casual and light, as strategy games go. But it's fun. If you want a strategy game that you can spend 15 minutes or so playing, to kill some time now and again, but will still satisfy your brain, then I highly recommend it.

Can you, a small child who has just fallen down into a large cavern, survive in a world full of monsters whom want your soul? An interesting question, that's for sure. Do you have the determination to make it through? Undertale is quite the experience. While the primary arc of the game is quite serious, it does a great job of weaving decent amounts of humor to help those rough times melt away... if not slightly.  In playing it, I felt like I was playing a game in the vein of EarthBound, one of my favorite games of all time.  The Good : The Music, the plot twists, the various smashing of walls, the unique battle system, and the fictional world. The Bad : I'm not sure how well younger players would be able to appreciate this game. It can get quite serious, and in order to truely be a hero, you're going to be playing the game on a budget, and constantly solving puzzles. I also kinda wish the ending went a little differently, and I truely mean a little differently. In any case, not a showstopper.  The Ugly : That stupid troll. (You'll know what I mean once you start playing.)  Overall : I think this is a game any EarthBound fan would enjoy. I also think the game could be liked by many others.  There's lots of care baked into the pie that is this game. The dev team should feel proud.  Great work guys!

Not at all a legitimate part of the 'Gothic' series. Terrible story, awful narrative and devoid of any profound similarity or entertainment value had with the games developed by Piranha Bytes.   Takes all the charm and rewarding qualities that made 'Gothic' unique and turns it into a casual hack-and-slash.  The best example of how they screwed this game up is... In the other games you get skill points which you use with skill trainers to teach you how to be and do what you want. With magic, especially, it's difficult to acquire because you have to get accepted into the mage's guild/monastery before you learn how to use magic. So you're taught to save your skill points until you can become a mage, if that's the way you want to go. Making becoming a mage an actual ACHIEVEMENT and something difficult to pull off. But it's the most powerful class in the game(s) because the fireball ends up being able to one-hit-kill. It's an incredibly rewarding feeling.  In this game? At level 4 you can use magic. No skill trainers. Just a character screen and you click the little plus sign.  And magic is completely meaningless.  Without putting any points into any skills, you can be waving a massive sword around wiping out mobs like there's no tomorrow. Again, you originally needed to train to be able to use large weapons. Again, it was an achievement to get that.  It goes to show how some publishers (and new developers working with an old IP) don't comprehend what gave these games their dedicated fan-base

This excellent platformer will start to frustrate you with its high difficulty quite early on. Don't let that put you off though as a lot of practice on each level should do the trick, and you will end up repeating the levels until you finally succeed. The learning curve is thankfully almost perfect as each level is slightly more challenging than the previous level. Beware though that the levels in the latter stages of the game are extremely difficult and your level death count will probably be in the hundreds before you complete them.  There is a ton of content in the game, with a vast amount of built-in and user created levels, and many levels and characters that are unlockable and hidden away. The characters control very differently to each other as well. Finally, as well as attempting to complete all of the levels, you might spend as much time trying to beat your and others best times on the leaderboards.

app_name: Serious Sam HD: The First Encounter
review_text: Serious Sam, huh? More like Serious ♥♥♥♥. That's all this game is. One big ♥♥♥♥. A steamy pile of doo doo.  Serious Sam at its core is a mindless run and gun with no heart or soul. The main character tries so hard to be Duke Nukem for no apparent raisen. Sam himself is an idiot amongst idiots. Every enemy you fight is just a faceless thing throwing itself at you in waves. The developers must have been really confused with character design for the enemies. They just toss in whatever random crap they can think of. From what little story there is, most of the enemies don't really fit the genre of the type of enemy you're supposed to be up against. Which is aliens, I guess... but most of the time you fight weirdo ♥♥♥♥ like feral screaming idiots without heads trying to suicide bomb you. There's a scorpion man with a machine gun attached to his arm... some sort of flying harpy... a skeletal demon thing.. the stupid bull creature from Star Wars Episode II... Oh, and the final boss is Satan.   Anyway, the story makes little to no sense at the best of times. The egypt setting is kind of cool, but little is known as to why it takes place in egypt... I know it's an older game and I shouldn't be so hard on it. But ♥♥♥♥ this game, I've played games way older then this and most of them haven't been nearly as ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ as this thing.  I'll give the HD version of this credit for actually looking decent with a nifty engine. But what good is any of that junk when the game itself is poo poo labanza?   Final Verdict (1.1 out of 5) - and that's me being generous.

Dragon quest on steam what an epic time to be alive. I never got the chance to play this on the PS4 due to not buying one but man I was close to missing out on one of Koei tecmos best games to date. Its your standard hack and slash Koei style at first glance but the fresh monster summoning mechanic and the crazy dragon quest monsters you have to fight make it 100times better.   Pros -  - The story is square enix all over but if you love there stuff your going to get sucked in. - Combat is crazy good some might say repetitive but they must not be swapping it up enough.  - Monster towers is a great I dear can change the whole flow of the game where you use what you have to summon. There are 2 types - sentry to guard pathways and fend off everything that comes their way and then the saviour which will either attack the enemy's and vanish or add elements to benefit yourselves. (this feature alone makes me think a pokemon crossover would be biblical) - Character selection - there is a lot of choice you will start the game and have to pick one main character out of 2 who will be your leader but once in battle you can chop and change between your 4 and they all have their own unique move sets. - The soundtrack and general effects are some of the best, I personal eat this stuff up even the menu screen has the ultimate dragon quest jolly tune. - Graphics have the nice DQ coat and the art style is what you would expect from a series of this calibre but don't be fooled the trailers do have a refined polish compared to the ingame look. - RPG elements are just right it has a nice town hub to take side quests in, buy new weapons and armour you can even go to the tavern to pick new characters to take to battle and then you have the monster suppression list and they have even integrated the achievements in game which is a nice touch that most games usually don't bother with.  - Cut scenes are wonderful they always push the story forward and every time one comes about you know your getting close to finding a new character or fighting a epic boss monster and that refined polish make them look glorious. - Controls are fine had no issues I used a 360 remote so not sure how the mouse and keyboard players will find it but from a 360 point of view tight and responsive.  - There are more pros with this game but ill be here all day listing them all so last pro would he helix what a legend.  Cons- - Win7 compatibility mode, I can only play this while set to this mode it did only mean me having to go to the DQ config launcher but I feel this shouldn't be happening in 2015 I understand its a port but I doubt it will ever be sorted. - Multiplayer has been left out on all levels, split screen or online would make this game gold standard its a real shame. - Pricepoint does seem a tad to much would have been nice to see it at 40euro but I get that all Koei ports cost a bomb. - Sink now im not sure if it because im in WIN7compat but the odd cutscene has gone out of sink and it can be a big gap if anyone else is having this problem you know it can be a good 4 to 6 second gap.   Overall Im really happy I picked this up, its cons don't overshine the pros and we need more collaborations like this, It really is a fresh take on both sides. Its a game thats good for all ages if you love dragon quest you will very much enjoy this and if you love DW,SW Toukiden this is another must have to the collection. If you've never tried either game go watch a review you'll know if its your sort of thing.   9/10 - Serious slash.

Apart from the story, gameplay is nice but there are too much driving and it's getting kinda boring after a while. The city is great but it feels like a ghost city. I mean no one's talking unless you push them around. I really liked the snowy Empire Bay, tho. Wish we had more chapters with snow all around the city. Another thing I liked was polices' reactions. Quick and simple overview: 5000+ hrs logged. I gave this a thunbs down so people reading the negetive comments can get a more accruate description of issues and read the positives.  Active players 10/10 Community 8/10 Game breaking issues(none), bugs 9/10 Enjoyment (MOBA) 10/10  Overall- 9/10  PROS:  1) *Rewards the player for the effort placed into the game.* Very challenging game to play/learn. How willing you are to do reserch(online) and sit in created games to practice combos/lh ect.. will dictate how well you will progress.  2) *Easy to jump right into with a group of friends.* If you have a group of friends that play it can be very fun to LEARN tegether.  3) **It is a competative enviroment** if you are a competative person this is the game for you.  4) **A lot of classes of heros to choose from** over 100. Although some are better then others every hero in the right/wrong situation can be very useful or bad.  5) **NOT A PAY TO WIN GAME**... Doesn't even nickle and dime you to death. You pay for HUDs, skins, loading screens ect; But, nothing that can affect how well players play.  6) **Dev's are quick to respond to issues on offical forums** They also fix them in a timly manner.  CONS:  1) **A lot of reserch and guided practice is NEEDED to be decent at this game.** This might be a bummer for those who like to d/l and game and insta win.... If this is you stay away...far away  2) **The 'guantlet' most mmr players need to go through'.** This 'guantlet' consist of other people players will be teamed up with that will troll, not speak your selceted language, or just not give a damn about your game. These people tend to ruin games and will hinder advancement in the MMR (ranked) or hidden mmr (un-ranked) system. The 'guantlet' consist of the lower tiers usually low hidden mmr and 1-2.8k MMR area. It is possible to pass the guantlet but a lot of practice is needed or you can party with friends.  3) **Server Issues** Everynow and then they will have laggy servers or down networks randomly but a restart of steam or a bathroom break easily solves these uncommon issues.

Ok, I am not exactly a fan of the BioShock series and honestly did not really expect much from this game. I bought it knowing I could potentially get a refund on it if it did not meet expectations ....  Note the play time I have registered on it thus far - a good chunk of that was in a single day. A. Single. Day.  The story and many of the game play mechanics surprised and impressed me - I have not played many FPS games from the more recent decade that manage to actually have a decent enough story or game play mechanics. Most these days seem to be aimed at the 'Shoot first, shoot again, shoot some more and then MAYBE ask questions' crowd. You know the types I am talking about (I would hope). This game? Action/Adventure styled RPG elements mixed into an FPS system and done fairly well (another game in my library does a slightly better job) with gameplay mechanics that show the designers actually tried to balance things out and a story that actually tickles the intellectual part of my mind.  My only complaints thus far deal with a particular part at the end of the main game. Anyone that has played this knows exactly which part I mean ... That is NOT how you design such a thing folks!  All in all though ... a game I have no trouble recommending to friends.

I played the game for the first time roughly 4-5 years ago and... it is no longer the same game. It has expanded at least 3-4 times to become the most content rich game on Steam PERIOD! There is not a single game that has as much content as this game on Steam; there isn't one unless you count creative games where you basically get to write the game's code but that award goes to Minecraft alone.  But this game does so much more than what Minecraft does, it has for an example: + A real combat experience with A LOT of different weapons and styles of combat + Insane variety of enemies to fight + Fair boss fights that can prove challenging at times but never losing their 'fair' component (altho' they will brutally murder any new player who starts up having never played Terraria before) + A VAST world to explore... 'EVERY' single time you generate a new world + Outstanding community tools to learning every nuance of the game + A LOT of variety to create amazing pixel art and structures that are just stunning to look at (no 3D models as everything is 2D but this game proves that you just need to be able to go Left, Right, Up and Down and still create amazing art) + You can set up dedicated servers so you can make it really, really easy to play with friends online but... + ... playing on your own is also exceptionally rewarding albeit with a certain requirement to set up your own goals or else it might be a bit overwhelming + HUGE custimization options both for the world generation, the difficulty and character creation Downsides: - I guess it takes a few minutes to install the game?  Do I recommend this game? I will say this much: If you like survival games where you get to loot, mine, fight, craft or any of these things... you are making yourself a disservice by not owning Terraria already! Stop reading! Go buy it! If it is on a sale... WHY ARE YOU STILL READING THIS?! BUY IT!

Alan Wake was quite the journey. The atmosphere and music in the game is enough to suck you in. This along with the story thread, ambiguity between reality and fiction, and character development that comes with the scavenging for flares, coffee thermoses, and ammo was enough to have me thinking I was Alan Wake in the quest to conquer the thriving darkness. I love some of the nuances of the game like interchanges between characters that most tend to skip or walk away from, the radio broadcasts, and television show ads among others. I played the game within a few days and couldn't put it down. I wanted to uncover the answers to the many questions the game poses. Although some of the actions in the game can be repetitive, especially when dealing with the taken, there are numerous refreshing elements, twists, and some moments where I jumped at unexpected foes. I have not played the DLC yet, but I do look forward to it. Alan Wake is a game I would recommend to fiction lovers, those who thrive on uncertainty and suspense, and anyone looking for a game that'll take you into a new state of experience.

app_name: Sins of a Solar Empire: Rebellion
review_text: tl;dr: This is good game. Cheap for what you get, lots of mods. Good optimization, much pretty. Music nice, gameplay nice, must play for space RTS fans.    Sins of a Solar Empire is a space RTS with no campaign, but endless replayability. Every game, the universe that the game is played in is randomized, or selected from a premade map (which offer specific challenges to make the game more interesting). There can be many stars in one universe, and each star can have very few or very many planets, asteroids, wormholes, etc.   The game offers a very nice blend of combat, research, and dimplomacy. But research really trumps everything, which leads into one of Sins' flaws...  the difficulty curve . This game will kick your ♥♥♥ if you are a newcomer to the RTS genre, and even if you are a veteran, you will find a good challenge. This game is not for the easily frustrated, because going against AI, or other players, can really pump up the blood pressure sometimes.   The game itself runs very well for my system. I'm running it on an AMD FX 6300 Processor, NvidiaGTX 660, with 8 GB of RAM, and it runs perfectly fine. It is very well optimized and looks beautiful. The backgrounds of space look phenomenal and the ships look pretty good too. Watching the space battles can be so nice to look at that you forget to make sure they are actually doing alright.   Anyway, This game is worth the $40, but I would also cash in for the DLC, because they are very good for the price. The modding potential for this game is amazing as well. If you ever get bored of the basic game, you can just browse the giant selection of quality mods on Moddb. If there is a popular movie/TV/game/book series taking place in space, there is a good possibility that there is a well made mod already created, or being crafted of it.   Thanks for reading, if you got this far, and I hope this review helps, although the fact that there are already 1,000's of positive reviews made for this game should be a clear indication of the quality of Sins of a Solar Empire: Rebellion.

Any of you grow up playing SubSpace? That old online top-down space shooter, that could have been the first MMO? (lots of players per server, 'till the very end, and that game was OLD - the days of dial-up. Beat that.) Well, this game feels a bit similar to that and Asteroids - fly around in a spaceship, blow up asteroids, and like in SubSpace, collect stuff to make your ship cooler. But in this, you collect minerals from the asteroids and enemy capital ships with a tractor beam, and you can only hold so much before you have to drop it off in the hangar. To actually make stuff out of it, you have to do some R&D - research better stuff, then actually build it. Both actions take minerals, and there's only so many minerals per area. And they don't come back. Thankfully we have a dangerous zone with infinite resources. *gulp* Oh, and this will play on pretty much anything. Woo!

Just played the main game and have yet to finish the DLC. The game is well done, but it has minor flaws. I will avoid spoilers. The combat was fine; it was mostly a click time based system where having knowledge was more valued then skill. When fighting x enemy it is wise to us x sword and x oil is the most common example in the game. This however leads to many encounters where you may need to just run away and come back prepared.   Maybe I’m getting old in terms of Video games? I thought the graphics where fine. The environments felt nice and had different feels, many subtle, but still there.   The music fits, until you get a heavy metal sound track from time to time, which is sweet, but if that's not your thing I won’t judge.   The single greatest complaint I have though was the game at chapter 2 was almost teetering on making me quit. The first chapters suffer from dragging on and have large amounts of back tracking and walking around the same places, you won’t get lost but you will walk the same routes a dozen times. If you get to chapter 2 and feel like giving up, do not. It’s the slowest point in the game and once you get it over things get better.   There are mods to fix many of the problems I could list like having a fast travel system or increasing run speeds, but I made it through on vanilla settings, and overall I would say get the mods if the vanilla game.

The replayability is a large function of the game, meaning that no one character can experience all of the possibilities and events the game has to offer. If you don't like that, you can bugger off. Warframe is a cooperative Third Person Shooter which involves a strange blend of melee combat, shooting, and parkour. There, now that we've gotten the standards out of the way, Warframe is a good game, good meaning that it has its pros and it's cons. When it comes to pros, the graphics quality is amazing for a Free-To-Play game, everything stays moderately difficult (and even harder if you play like you're supposed to, you know, 2 or 3 friends all starting at the same time), and the parkour system is very smooth and easy to use, even for new TPS players. Since the last time I reviewed this game, it has changed greatly. Whereas before I was too scared to make a new weapon due to credit and resource restrictions, I've found that resources are much more common in most cases, so I've been free to experiment with guns and find the ones I like. (Latron I love ya baby.) Now I have a set of cleavers, two hammers, a machine gun, rifle, shotgun, several swords, and the list goes on. The best part about this, though, is that, since credits are more easily achievable now, I've been able to experiment without fear of losing much. Also, the way you earn warframes has also changed much. Whereas before, there was a chance that it may or may not spawn once every 5th moon after you've sacrificed your firstborn, now you're guaranteed to get a frame part after fighting the boss that drops them, so I've actually coughed up some plat and now have six warframes, all of which provide an incredible spin on gameplay, forcing you to change your playstyle for each frame. Of course, I've got a favorite, (Nyx I love ya baby.) but each frame is a refreshing take on the game that renews it completely. To continue, the levels are now actually changed. The levels change style depending on who's in control of the planet. Of course, if you play the same planet alot, then you may begin to notice that the levels are the same, but that can be expected of any game. However, it can still be a tad grindy at times, and several of my friends found the beginning of the game too hard a hurdle for them and quit early on due to the upper levels carrying them through the low levels and making the game boring for them, so if you're going to enjoy this game, it's a NECESSITY that you don't play public matchmaking. Players tend to rush through the levels and not allow the lowers to really enjoy the game or even get a kill, for that matter. If you're going to play, you either need to play by yourself or with friends.  I Recommend this game, (Provided you've got some friends to play this with.) Zombie Chicken

It's kinda like how the human body has replaced all of its cells by the age of 7-10, while growing about 5-10 times as big. Metacritic reviewed the newborn baby that is Warframe, leading many people to think it's still crawling on all fours to this day. They really should update that score...  So yes, I recommend Warframe, being my most played game ever, and constantly keeping me interested with weekly content. ༼ つ ◕_◕ ༽つ Praise Booben ༼ つ ◕_◕ ༽つ

They could be good, I can see where it could be fun, but it's just not for everyone. They send you in with no real idea what the controls are unless you go to the settings, and even then they leave you to figure out how to lower your hand and how to turn it on your own. After countless tries and two seizure inducing syringe fails (as I was trying to save the patient), I gave up on the first operation. Honestly, you play with a doctor with the motor skills of drunk two year old, which is kind of the point, but it just doesn't work out. Maybe I'm just biased, or maybe I just suck, I am likely to play it again some time but until I see anything to stand out about it and make it worth it (especially at full price), I do not recommend it. If you get it, play it with friends or on YouTube, it's only worth it if you intend to have someone laughing as you mess up. If you are alone and not a YouTuber, 10$ for failure and rage is not recommendable.

It is a good game, fun to play alone. it is also a bad game, terrible to play with friends. If you play alone, you may find the occassional bug or get annoyed at timers that are insanely short, or perhaps you will notice that dissapearing blocks stay solid after they show their dissapearing animation. but when you get onto the multiplayer, you will find terrible, fun destroying problems that will make you dislike the game. frogs climb on top of each other infinitly, puzzles that are possible for one person, but for the others, they will constantly do the puzzles wrong and end up dying without doing anything different from the person who did it successfuly, and one problem that made me way more annoyed than it should: standing on your friends head. Im playing in the arena, trying to play basketball with my friend, when they stand on my head, making me unable to move, and becasue I cannot attack when I have the ball, I can do nothing but get angry and try to tell them to get off of me. Another problem I have with the basketball game is that if they get infront of you, you are going to get scored on, there is nothing you can do about that. One more problem I have is with another multiplayer game, horse, in horse you go to try to steal your opponents 'horse' and get that back to your base. The horses are overpowered, and if somebody gets in front of you with the horse, you loose, nothing you can do, the horse is just as fast as you making you unable to get your 'horse' back. The game is also quite generic and brings nothing new to the platforming genre. It has okay comedy if you want to entertain a kid with a lot of poop jokes and almost swears. Overall, I would not recommend this game due to the hit or miss comedy, the bland single player, and the broken and agrovating multiplayer. My friend and I also were forced to quit a level after it set both of our spawnpoints into an inescapable wall.

Pros: - The physics feel fun and realistic - Tracks are tricky and narrow, not perfect set pieces - Audio is excellent and extremely detailed - Customizable and tweakable (e.g. HUD, driver assists, controller responsiveness/sensitivity etc) - Steam Cloud  Cons: - Grass has a semi-noticeable, weird grid-like effect which is apparent on some tracks (only on AMD cards I think) - Rare graphical glitch where the stage is engulfed in black - Online mode lacking - No ghosts or easy way to look up all your laptimes that I've found - When doing time trials, you have to wait like 10 seconds to restart again which adds up  People often say there are not enough tracks, which is true for the online mode, but only partially true for the rally portion. While there aren't many rally tracks (I think like 10-12), they are very lengthy and even at 150 hours I don't know all the ins and outs of all the tracks (though I do remember most turns). Going back to Dirt 3 and noticing the absurdly short rally tracks is kinda laughable. In fact, everything about the older Dirt games feels way too simple - from the audio to the physics. The old dirt games feel like you're driving a toy on some sort of flat and perfect course.  In summary this is one of the few racing games worth playing right now. The pros heavily outweigh the cons. It's especially great if you appreciate realistic feeling, yet still fun racing games. I haven't played a racing game this much since the older Need for Speed games (which I stopped playing when Criterion Games removed manual transmission and casualized the Hot Pursuit series).

I had been waiting to get this since I heard about it in the late beta, but never got round to getting my hands on it until now, and frankly, I'm not at all dissapointed.  This game gets tense even though a lot of the situations can be as simple as running low on fuel. It looks brilliant, and often goes from being quite relaxing - with what I think is an excellent soundtrack - to moments of sudden tension.   The sheer scale of the map (the entire galaxy) is ridiculous, and holds so many opportunities for players for things to do, and if we can get planetary landings in the future, this game could be one of the best space games ever made, in my opinion.  Definitely worth the money if you enjoy a game that switches between the laidback approach to suddenley very tense moments, and if you love space simulators, I can't recommend this enough.  When the VIVE and Oculus rift are fully released, this game will likely be one of the best for them, I think.  The learning curve is fairly steep but shouldn't take long to get past.  Love it, can't wait for even more features.  10/10

While my hour count on Steam for using this is low, know that I have actually used this for years. I started on version 2.49b, which, I think, was five years ago? I forget exactly, but Blender was my first foray into 3D work and, thankfully, back in version 2.4x it was a much, much easier program to learn, which made 2.5x and up, which is where its power really began to manifest, much easier for me to learn. But I digress, the fact that Blender does everything it does for free is simply mind numbing. Later on I tried Maya and 3DS Max and simply could not be bothered to keep using them, because Blender was not only more familiar but also more powerful in my eyes. Modeling, animation, rigging, skinning, UV mapping, compositing, motion capture, game logic, it's all here, though honestly the game logic features are rather limiting when there are things like Unreal Engine out there for free, and I've yet to have the means to test the motion capture. However, the modeling and sculpting, while not as intensely detailed as ZBrush, is immensely powerful, though I have noticed that at polycounts of 1 mil and up that it starts to get sluggish (not that I need to go that high for my purposes as an amateur game developer). But where Blender really shines is its animation features. I have yet to see a program with a more capable animation suite than Blender that does not solely dedicate itself to 3D animation, Blender is easily the most capable all-in-one program I've seen as far as animation is concerned.  Materials are also relatively easy to handle, though still a big pain especially when you get into UV mapping. Again, this is coming from a veteran who's already overcome the steep learning curve the program offers. Compositing is a section that I have little experience with, as I've still not gotten a full grip on compositing in general. I hope to update this review as I get to where it's needed for my fan project, so that I may better convey the strengths of Blender's compositor.   As far as I can tell, the biggest downfall of Blender is most definitely its learning curve. It was a struggle for me to learn it despite it being a simpler program when I started, and it's also the main gripe I've heard from other people. If they would only include a built-in tutorial, this issue would be greatly alleviated. Adding the search function was a step in the right direction, though, that much is certain.  VERDICT: An immensely powerful, and free, all in one 3D software that is a bit too steep to learn, but given time and perseverence it becomes very easy to navigate. I greatly recommend Blender to any hoping to get into 3D work and are willing to tough out a steep learning curve.

Jackbox Party Pack is a great game for groups of people. Anybody with a phone or tablet can enjoy it. It doesn't matter if they are a gamer or not or if they are old or young. If they can send a text message or type in a URL on their device, they can enjoy this game.  Fibbage and Quiplash are my favorites. The first involves making up lies to fill in a blank about some obscure fact. You try to fool others into guessing your answer while you pick the true answer. The second one is a bit like Apples to Apples or Cards Against Humanity except that you can answer anything you like, not just choose from premade answers on cards.  Bidiots and Earwax both have their moments and are pretty nice as a change of pace every once in a while. One involves drawing things and placing bids on drawings working with limited information about what each one might be worth. The other involves choosing sound effects to go along with some wacky prompts.   The game seems to go on sale quite a bit. If you have a lot of friends or company over often, then this is a great thing to do with them. If you're often on your own at home, then you'll get less mileage out of this of course. I believe you can play it over Twitch, which could be pretty amusing, but I haven't tried that.  Bottom line: If you're looking for a fun and intuitive party game to play with groups of people (including non-gamer people), then this is a great option. Recommended.

For 12 bucks, this ride was pretty cool. Yes it's light in the pants... most of these things are as developers are chucking together whatever they can with whatever SDK templates they use in order to capitalize from sales.  However, this very succinct title is the first time I felt in awe of war while playing a game about it. Sure it's not the most detailed of games or the most deep, but when you are in a broken building, looking down over a German occupied street, and you see the automaton severly basic UI, you think meh... but when it adds in the other touches such as the planes flying over top and bombing the area you are in, it gives you the opportunity to at least experience a glimpse of the 3D experience war might give.  It's worth it to experience the war aspect....I just wish it was a full fledged game, rather than 4 little shooting gallery experiences.  Either way... I want a WW2 game now. I don't care if you can't run around. If you were able to have interactive paths to go to the sniper portions, I'm fine with that. Tailgunner, naval gunner, helicopter gunner, and whatever else you can shoe horn in there, would make it like a tour or all the terrible ♥♥♥♥ our relatives had to go through.  Firing a machine gun down at the beaches as the Americans storm, only to get to your encampment and enter your pillbox... you fumble to shoot them with your hand gun...it's a frightening experience. I wish there was WAY more of it.  If you aren't interested in being in those environments, then it's just a mediocre to crappy shooting gallery.

6/10 In short, Guacamelee is a Metroidvania beat-em-up with luchadores. It has good fighting mechanics, and a fun aesthetic, with alternate dimensions and chickens, to boot. The mechanics can get choppy, though, especially with the overly hard platforming. When you have to bash 5 buttons at once, but then switch to 5 new ones in less than second if you don't want to redo 3 minutes of platforming, it goes far beyond rage-quit material. When you do get to fight, it's a great time on of the best fighting systems in 2D game I've ever experienced. But the platforming is so TIME-CONSUMING, BORING, and DIFFICULT, that it draws focus from the well-designed parts of the game. All in all, a fine game, but the paltforming drags it down.
I highly recommend this for platformer fans. Great controller response, fun visuals, and entertaining but minimal storyline.

TL:DR AT THE BOTTOM!!!  In-Depth Version:  Although at first I did rate this game up, it is now a definite downsider to me. Not only are specific factions overpowered (Terran Republic's STARTING rifle takes 3 shots to kill someone, NC's starting AND SMG that I bought from their horribly grindish store were all 5-9 shot kills) but this game has every CoD-like aspect in it to make you want to throw the game away and move on to something more intense and punishing/rewarding like Red Orchestra 2.   Yes, this game IS an MMO shooter. A real MMO shooter. There are thousands of REAL players on your screen at once fighting, and though it does completely destroy your CPU (even with my up-to-date computer with more than decent specs) its still playable and fun in some ways. But having to shoot someone 6 times with a rifle to kill them while 20 people are firing at you (not to mention the guy you're shooting at is running away at lightning speed so you'll NEVER hit him) is not fun. Also getting randomly 1 hit killed by a Tank or AT Gun from hell knows where is not very fun. On top of that though, shooting someone with a massive bolt-action Sniper rifle that shoots a round the size of your foot and not killing him after hitting him 4 ♥♥♥♥ING TIMES is not fun. This game, though fun in its rare moments, is generally speaking not fun. It's frusturating and feels more like a chore than anything else.  It has a lot of potentional but is recieving criticism on people who shouldn't be giving it. People who play games such as CoD or Battlefield, but want some stupid overpowered game mechanics (like the MAX robots who have triple health) or ♥♥♥♥♥♥, borderline Pay-To-Win guns to make them feel like the couple extra bucks they made at work today was worth it. A game like this is good in theory but only should stick to certain formulas, such as CoD crack hype style where you can kill someone with at max 5 shots, or Red Orchestra realism style where if you're hit you're out cold. This in between ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t is and will ruin this game from now until its inevitable closing.   So to conclude, should you try Planetside 2? Sure. Will you have fun for most of the time you play it? Probably not. Is it cool to just kinda see? Yes. And is it fixable? That's if Daybreak stops their ♥♥♥♥ and stops listening to a bunch of idiots who know nothing of game designing.  TL:DR version: 4/10, not a complete dumpster but still trash in most areas.

I've been a long time strategy gamer, and have mixed feelings about it. There are things that I love, and things that I hate. I will also repeatedly compare this game to Supreme Commander, as it's very similar (it is a spiritual successor).  What I Love: Runs beautifully Good community Great graphics, UI, mod support. Hits a sweet-spot of occupation - there's always something to do, but I never feel so overloaded that I can't manage it. There's excellent sets of tools for management, such as the secondary map (which is a beautiful idea). Few bugs Well done economies. I personally find it a little resource-rich, but it's pretty good. Excellent general gameplay - on-planet gameplay is very well done, and battles are always fun and exciting.  Cons: Game-breaking units. This is the big one. In a good RTS, every strategy should be counterable - there should never be a strategy that has no way of being defeated. This game has too many. -> SXX laser platform - there is no defense to them coming in above your commander and getting off a single shot each. I have had countless fun and even games end with an enemy simply bringing them directly above the commander, and there is no good way of preventing it. It's no fun. -> Halleys - it's too difficult to counter a planet in motion (nothing can get to it other than teleports, and if they have halleys you probably don't have a teleporter). -> Halleys and catalysts are, in my opinion, too cheap. There cost would be better if doubled. Storing resources is also a bit too easy. Dividing storage capacity by 4 would help.  Overall, this is a great game, especially for the beginning RTS player who wishes to get into serious RTS. Its economy is padded, meaning production of units is usually easy, but can still offer challenges. It runs like a dream, and is a truly beautiful game from a graphics perspective. Battles are fun and entertaining.  However, once you become reasonably good at RTS games I would have to advise Supreme Commander over this game. The economy is a little too padded for competitive gameplay, and there are enough units that can be exploited, especially in late-game, to destroy advanced and many-planet games.  If you've never played RTS games before, pick it up. You'll enjoy it, and it will teach well many of the skills necessary to play RTSs well. However, if you consider yourself at least moderately experienced an RTS player, I would recommend other games over it, and to be aware of the problems associated with it before a purchase.

This game wasn't quite what I expected. It's one of those titles where the enemies are pretty tough & do a lot of damage, but are slow and telegraph their attacks. Your character, on the other hand, is fragile and doesn't hit very hard, but can attack with great rapidity. So, your only chance of winning (esp. on the higher difficulties) is to keep your foes stunlocked with an all-out assault, chaining hits and special moves together to never give them an opening. As I've stated before, I don't really like fighting games, where you have to pull off specific key combinations with millisecond timing. This is pushing things a little, but I was still able to use the combos they had programmed in, which is fortunately not that many. This does limit the depth of the system and mean the combats can get very same-y after a while, though.  So, it feels kind of incongruous. Your protagonist is basically a walking abattoir, chewing through monsters with gusto. Except he's also supposed to be a rabbit, I guess? And all the other characters are cartoon animals too? The story similarly veers back and forth between srs bzns and light-hearted banter. It doesn't help matters that the game is one of the worst offenders in the Cutesy Sidekick That Never Shuts Up category.  The art and animation are top-notch, considering this is a small-budget title. It's not quite up there with the Odin Spheres of the world, but damn close. The whole game is a bit on the short side, and probably much shorter still if you don't bother hunting down all the sidequests and collecting everything. That said, I had a lot of fun with it, and would recommend it to anyone who enjoys action-RPGs.

Manning a checkpoint and stamping visas may not sound like gripping gameplay but Papers, Please somehow manages it. With your family on the brink of starvation, political trouble brewing and a cast of characters whose lives you briefly glimpse (and possibly permanently alter) the impetus to be alert and make the right choices is very strong. It's a very hard game to put down.  The graphics may seem basic but they serve the dual purposes of paring back extraneous detail to help you concentrate while simultaneously reinforcing the dreary Cold-War era atmosphere. The moral quandaries you frequently find yourself in (you can barely go a day without being offered multiple bribes for a start) don't usually have clear right or wrong answers. Indeed, whenever I lost (often) I didn't get the impression it was because of any moral failings on my part, just making choices (or mistakes) with harsh consequences.  On the subject of losing, this game has one of the best save systems I've seen. It saves at the end of each day so that you can go back and restart from any day of any playthrough you like. You can go back and fix a mistake you realised from two days ago, or try something different or whatever you like and it displays it all in a way that is easy to navigate.  I'll finish by saying that this game might not be for everyone but I have personally loved it. If the idea of a game that's a bit different, that will make you think, that can actually draw you into the life of a poor worker stuck between a rock and a hard place, this is definitely worth a try. It won't break the bank and it will definitely leave you with an impression.

Amazingly fun game! It is a fresh take on city-builders. Instead of clear, laid out formulas that if 'discovered' it becomes a simple matter of just placing enough buildings, Banished adds monkey wrenches into that mix (and not in a bad way). Production isn't always the same year to year, season to season. You have 14 workers now but they are all over the age of 70, well they are gonna die soon. . .better have some children coming along to fill the gap.  Banished removes the simplicity of most city builders of place it and forget it.  Population management and 'controlled expansion' is key to the entire game.  Added 4 more houses. . .hopefully you have enough food production for the children that are bound to follow! Haven't added a house in awhile so you won't upset the balance you found? Sure hope your paying attention to the ages of your workers cause they die when they get old, and old folks don't make more children!    Go into the game expecting to fail the first couple of times.  But instead of giving up, ask yourself what you did wrong! Make saves and go back in time and try it a different way. Pay attention to things mentioned in the tutorial and read the in-game documentation on things. I won't give hints cause its learning it that's half the fun not to mention the challenge :P Experiment!   The trick to the game?  Play, Learn, Play again!    Banished has hours of fun and challenges that are different every map. I would recommend it to all!

One of the most beautiful and magical games I’ve played. On its surface the game can be thought of as borrowing from Ecco the Dolphin and Metroid. Fantastic artwork seems to flow and move naturally, creating one of the most visually rich and dynamic environments in gaming. This creates a game environment which feels amazingly emotive, and ultimately feeds in to the feelings of loss and loneliness which are woven into the plot. Exploration forms a central part of the gameplay and is truly rewarding in itself. The plot is simple on the surface, but has a subtlety to it which rewards closer inspection. It is a rare game where you can actually identify themes, and a still rarer one where those themes are interwoven in different parts of the game design.

Plenty to explore and discover. Planets and moons look great. The creatures are mostly funky but can sometimes scare you with their procedurally generated features. Most of the game is a self paced, player-driven trek to the center of the universe. Occasionally, space pirates will try to shoot you down for your loot. Space combat is simple, but still engaging since it is unscripted. The plants can try to kill you too. The environments can be hostile to life and force you to survive the harsh conditions. Survival can be hectic and gives the exploration a fresh take.  Don't buy this game if you are expecing Star Citizen or an open world Destiny. Instead, DO buy it if you like a casual space exploration game where you drive the action.

I'm no star at writing reviews, so I'll keep it fairly short.  This game is fun.  Too short? Okay. This game is lots of fun.  But seriously. I really enjoy(ed) this game. The aim is to become the Most Renowned Explorer...well duh. You do this by collecting treasures, gaining Fame, Fortune, or Scientific discoveries, and kicking bottom. You travel across a map that is slowly uncovered as you go along, and fight in turnbased battles, or beat the odds by spinning a wheel, until you reach the final destination. Once you have conquered whatever it is that waits at the end of every expedition you get to spend what you found, improve your character, add helpers to your team, and get ready for the next expedition. Slowly you'll become stronger, and you might just become...duh.  I love the style of the game, it's nice and crisp and colorful. The battles don't take too long, and the map will have you thinking where to go next instead of just blazing through. There's plenty of gear and stuff to fiddle with if you want to micro-manage everything, or just click, click, click and go!  So is there anything bad I have to say about the game? Sure! My friends all have higher scores than me...but they had the game long before me, I think the scoring changed so I'll have a hard time beating them...or I just suck...  Highly recommended game, you don't need me to tell you that, but hey, you read all my 'nonsense' anyway. Cheers!

Titan Souls has a lot to offer. A beatiful soundtrack, amazing retro style graphics, challenging gameplay, and most low/mid spec pcs (to my knowledge) can handle it. But, it is simply not fun to play.  Every time you die to a boss (which will happen about 90% of the time), you respond at a checkpoint in the overworld, and you have to travel a long way to get back to the boss. After losing, I just want to fight the boss right away. I don't want to have to roll across the good looking but, empty overworld to return. It may seem like a minor gripe at the game but, it made me lose intrest over time. I do however recommend this game if you want to really test your patience and/or it's on sale.

Art and Music - Absolutely beautifully done, and prefectly fits the setting and tone.  Story - To call it 'non-existent' would be false, but it's functionally non-existent.  Gameplay - This is where the meat of the game is, whether it passes or fails for you. In a sparce (but very pretty) setting there are a number of rooms each with a different boss in them. Each boss is at once a test of timing, aiming, and deduction as you try to figure out exactly how to kill it. You will die a lot, but each death brings you one stop closer to beating the boss, either through figuring out how you're meant to kill it, or getting the timing/aim just right.  This game is certainly not for everyone, it is only for people who get a sense of extreme satisfaction for overcoming very difficult challenges. Would recommend watching a video that goes through some of the early gameplay before you decide to pick this one up.

Geometry Dash is a remake of the old classic The Impossible Game, on steroids.  The difficulty curve, this time, exists, but spikes incredibly higher. The hitboxes are smaller than neccesary and therefore extremely fair, and 99.999% of deaths are absolutely your fault.  The attention to detail by the developer (yes, THE, RobTop is one person) is immaculate and, behind the curtains, is tuned perfectly and logically, for the sake of making a game just for a level for others to build on the concept.  There is a full-fledged LBP-style level creator and sharer, which is a little difficult at first to use (the tutorial has never been updated, unfortunately) but once you understand is a work of brilliance and simplicity.  For those unfamiliar, the game uses one button to play, which makes the character 'jump' in most situations. While sounding simple, it turns into a complete hell rather quickly, as each death makes you go back to the beginning. There is a practice mode where you can set respawn points wherever you go.  Robert Topala borrows a few concepts from other games to create other game modes, which are forcefully set on you with rings (or 'portals') in a level, forcing you not to just master the old 'timing the cube jumps right' game, but flying a ship (trying to fly it straight takes a LOT of practice), swapping the gravity of a spiky ball (think 'VVVVVV'), controlling a very bouncy UFO (a mobile game, 'Flappy Bird'), controlling a 'wave' line (taken from the mobile game, 'Wave Wave', which looks very similar in style to 'Super Hexagon'), and a robot that behaves the same as the cube except holding makes you jump higher (mobile game 'Jetpack Joyride', a certian powerup). All the mechanics have been tweaked a little and no textures have been stolen.  A few complaints are the developer's recent lack of communication with the community, and 'Update 2.1', an update for the game that was supposed to release in February but could release any day now, constantly keeping the community on edge.  A good amount of the community was, at one point, swarmed by little kids (and I'm not going to lie, I was one of them) and turned into a hellhole, but about a year later the community is MOSTLY friendly and supportive. In the sense of twitch chat, literally.  Oh, and you can use ANY song from Newgrounds for your levels! I'm pushing for Soundcloud support but it's probably not gonna happen.  Buy this game if you like difficult and musical platformers. Buy this game if you want to hang yourself as you see the words 'NEW BEST: 98%' on the screen. Buy this game if you don't like to give up too easily, because you will either way a lot of the time. Buy this game if you need an excuse to waste 400 hours of your time. (it's good though) Buy this game if you praise our lord and savior, Lenny. ( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°) Don't buy this game for the modern-day graphics, YET... while you can make some amazing things yourself or other people already have, the default graphics the game has isn't very detailed... but some content in 2.1 says otherwise. Don't buy this game if you're easily offended and HAVE to talk to everyone who plays the same game as you. Don't buy this game if you get a headache from listening to music over and over repetedly.

HOW TO WIN THE INTERNET.  You've led a humdrum life up until now. You went virtually unnoticed in school—average grades, no girlfriend and a short-lived JV basketball stint (you mostly sat on the bench, but whatever). In college, because your parents did not want you sleeping on their couch after graduation, you majored in business, even though you secretly wanted to be an artist. Now, post-undergrad, you're working in a 9-to-5 you hate and your only escape is the Internet. Well, you're in luck, because things are about to change. Our 10-step guide on How to Win the Internet is sure to turn you into an #influencer in no time. And remember: Winning is now your only concern.   Create a Tumblr (preferably one that covers an obscure subculture).  It all begins with making a name for yourself. Have an original voice is the most important thing on the Internet. It's become a truism on social media that 85 percent of the people are basically saying the same thing. As Benjamin Hart recently pointed out on The Awl: 'a dulling sameness of phraseology, a glib shorthand that has bloomed everywhere, but especially online, and especially on social media. If you hang around Twitter long enough, you’ll become alternately inured to and irritated by the grinding repetitiveness of a few stock phrases, which come at you like a Greek chorus of unoriginality.' So, yeah, if you're going to stand out from the pack, don't be afraid to be you and create the blog you've always wanted. Find untainted content on which you can post first.  On the Internet, there is no greater feeling than being the first person to post an exclusive photo or publish a previously unwritten story. You'll quickly make a name for yourself this way, too--especially if you're the guy people can depend on to post news they won't find anywhere else. After all, Internet posers love to be in the know (mostly so they can rub it in their friends' faces that they knew so and so did so and so first). Trust us, clicking 'publish' never felt so good. Be the CEO of SEO.  Claim it now: I AM THE CEO OF SEO. It's one thing to have a cool blog with a cult following, but if you plan on bringing your awesomeness to the masses, you'll need to perfect your SEO. A few easy tips to bump your blog to the top of the Internet search pile: cross link pages, update content frequently, use key words in the URL, etc etc. Now go forth and be great.

Think back to the older Final Fantasy games. 7, 8 or 10 (at least these are the ones I played) did not look pretty either, but nethertheless everybody loved them. app_name: Minecraft: Story Mode - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: On the surface this game appears to be a cheap take off Minecraft with a basic and shallow storyline. Well I can tell you that none of that is true:   Firstly:  The game has a surprisingly good storyline which kept me fully engaged throughout all eight episodes.  Secondly:  The dialogue is amazing. There's nothing better than hearing dialogue that's well written and well said.   Thirdly:  The characters work really well when combined together. If characters don't interact well with each other then they just become a bunch of boring individuals. Thankfully, MC Story Mode provided us not only with good characters, but their personalities simply worked when combined together.   Fourthly:  It's actually nothing like Minecraft. Don't get me wrong, it is technically set in 'Minecraft' but it provides a completely different experience to that game. Minecraft doesn't exactly have a set storyline or characters apart from achievements and you/your friends. In short, the experience is yours to make. In MC Story Mode, the storyline is given to you, but there's multiple ways you can choose to go through it. Whether this is a good or a bad thing is up to you to decide. That's the whole point. It's the decisions that matter.

I normally don't buy $1 games but, seeing that this is an atmospheric-puzzle-exploration game with    Lovecraftian    horror, it caught my eye. Then, I saw the sale of it being 51% off. This was strange for an already curious game. So, I gave the game a chance.  Reveal The Deep is short and sweet. It manages to capture the idea of    Lovecraftian    horror when other games don't understand the source material. This game did cause me to look over my shoulder in game and in real life when there was an unusual noise or passing by an open door. This horror game utilizes other horror motiffs to achieve their atmosphere instead of jumpscares. There are some genuine startling moments in each chapter.  There were times the paranoia depleted when I became lost in the maze-like ship. The pacing can suffer if the player becomes lost. My advice to any new player is this:   Keep moving forward. Avoid backtracking as much as possible.    If this game sounds interesting, then you may enjoy it. The game is worth the price of admission.

The graphics and the narrator had so much influence on my motivation to finish the story, Even the ruins of Caelondia look so beautiful, and Rucks memories of the place really made me want to undo the Calamity. Sidequest don't feel like a burden at all, even though they're optional I really enjoyed playing through Weapon Challenges and 'Who knows where's because of the challenge and for the background informations on side characters and the Kid.   The weapons feel great, the control mechanics really match the different type of weapons. The upgrades allow enough weapon customization to match your playstyle, and playing through the game with any combination of weapons is not only possible, the game won't set you back because you're using the starting weapons in the last level.  The narrator allone is worth it for you to pick this game up. Even in New Game + his comments on my every move didn't get old, I love his calm deep voice and prefer this way of storytelling to big budget cutscenes. The story itself is not too complex but very enjoyable.  This game is best compared to the first matrix movie: The whole package! Story, graphics and gameplay work peferctly together, but every part of it is great for itself.

To the Moon is a 16-bit point-and-click adventure game heavy on story. You play as Eva and Neil who work for a nameless organization that reconstruct memories for those who wish they could change something in their past. For one man it's going to the moon, but through his highly complicated love life, he doesn't realize it until he's too old. Eva and Neil retrace his life to see what they can alter to make it different, and in the end, it's one emotional rollercoaster. This is a prime example of masterful storytelling, and to tell a story of this caliber with this medium is phenomenal. This game is an instant classic in my book; a game you'll want to play over and over again. Eva and Neil are quite dynamic together as well which warms you up to the unique progression. The biggest drawback seems to only be some unfunny dialogue and some needless pop culture references that sully its timelessness, but those are petty. I can only hope they make more games like this in the future. 9 / 10

Ys Origin plays like a top-down hack-and-slash but can at times be so frantic it feels like a bullet hell. Think Zelda on steroids. An interesting story, awesome gameplay, and very challenging. Well worth the money and made me purchase the other Ys games - which I am also enjoying.  You are given the choice of playing as a girl with a big axe or a guy with magic. After you beat the game you can go ahead and replay it with the other character or with a new, 3rd character. Playing it with the girl or guy you're first given feels generally the same, although you obviously have an entirely different set of skills. All three characters pretty much trek through the exact same dungeon layout but the 3rd character you unlock fights a set of new bosses and has an entirely different story. Playing on higher difficulties actually changes things besides HP and damage - although the story stays the same.  One downside is that I feel that the leveling was pretty tedius. You will find yourself in positions where you need to grind out 5 levels because it is literally impossible to beat a boss if you don't have the damage output. This only takes maybe 10-15 minutes before each boss but later in the game I remember having to spend an entire hour just beating up 1,000 grunts.  Defintely one of the most enjoyable games I've played in a while.

Assassin's Creed III is the fifth game in the franchise and takes a completely new twist to the series by going to colonial America. You play as Connor Kenway; a native American who trains to be an assassin in order to get revenge for his mother's death. You also play as his father, Haytham Kenway, at the start. Along the way you, Connor, ignite the fires of the American revolution and decide to help them purely because the Templars are helping the British. This entire idea feels pointless though because the patriots and Washington decide to burn your village so at the end you turn on them, whilst you see Templars killing royalists. Yeah Connor messed up...  The game introduces a new mechanic to its free running in the frontier sections, with the ability to climb trees and use these instead of rooftops. This is great and well executed but feels limiting, very streamlined, and is pointless seeing as most of the time nothing happens in the frontier. The rooftop free running is still there however guards detect you almost instantly and it kin of discourages going on the roof, meaning you spend most time on the ground. This feels wrong for an assassin game, even more so with the fact you stand out wearing the assassins robes; at least Altair could hide among priests. The combat is great however after a while it feels repetitive as guards do the same moves, whether patriot or royalist, whether highlander or officer. Also you are so overpowered in this episode of the assassin's creed franchise.  The story is quite interesting and very in depth however Connor has no emotion or charm like the other assassins. He is quite plain, particularly when compared to his father who plays the relaxed aristocrat. In addition, every time you get somewhere good in the story Desmond shows his face and you have to endure half an hour of modern day pointless Desmond. The ending is boring and the credits last 20 mins, you can't even skip them. The beginning isn't great either, with a long tutorial, it takes about 5 hours before you even get the assassin robes. The lockpicking system is horrible (just copy Skyrim for goodness sake) and overall the game feels clunky and rushed; particularly the anti-climatic battles.  However, as a history buff and a fan of the colonial era I felt at home with the game. I did get annoyed with the historical inaccuracies (there are a lot) however there were a few nods to people such as myself within the story. I felt as though the story itself was good, it was fun to go on killstreaks, the naval combat wasn't too bad and the graphics lived up to their standard. The seasonal and time changes were a joy and it was nice to see so much content; the hunting is an example of this, it had a lot of content but really was just pointless.  Overall I enjoyed AC 3 however I feel as though the game is only enjoyable to people who appreciate the era and who don't over hype it.  6/10.

The Comminuty is is actually very helpful, there are not many games where voice chat is really either necessary or desired due to hostile players, but this game practically forces players to communicate with eachother in order to succeed. as for the game itself, there are 3 classes; engineer, pilot, and gunner. they each have special items each related to the class and one item of choice from the other classes in order to be able to swap in case of emergencies aboard the ship. this allows players to be able to make up for others if they are busy doing something else (pilot is normally glued to the wheel) as for the air ships themselves, you have several to chose from depending on your personal style and prefered offensive and defensive layout, and with each ship you can change and choose what types of guns you want to have at each gun station aboard the station (guns can be classified as light and heavy artillery)  Provides great team implementation with 4 person teams (or AI) classes are well used and made, air ships provide an amazing and immersive feeling of battle, customization of ships guns allows pilots to have a ship custom to their and their teams play style  (Very fun when playing with a personal friend, I recommend the 4-pack)
