FTL just got better with the free  Advanced Edition  update and you really should be playing now that it's out.  Remember playing as with the Kestrel and throwing all those missiles but you just end up sitting on a huge pile of unused drone parts? Hacking is a worthwhile and powerful way to use those things! Why target their shields when you can just hack them?  Tired of losing your crew to seemingly luck of the draw scenarios like astreroid spiders? Install a clone bay and throw caution to the wind! Send them to dilapidated mining structures! Plague ridden colonies! Mantis interceptors! Send them to repair oxygenless hull breaches!  And mind control. I mean, do I really need to explain mind control? And these are just the new things added to FTL. Every hopeless adventure to the ends of space is a unique story just waiting for a spectacular failure that is oozing with emergent storytelling.  Like the story of how a two man crew overcame a four mantis boarding crew by venting the whole ship of oxygen and hoping that their cloning bay would keep working past their normal mortal coil.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Do not by this game.  I'll start with some of the good points.  - The graphics i think are very good, both in battles and on the campaign map but mainly in the battles with the close ups on individal soldiers, effects and looking on the battle as a whole.  -Diffrent factions- unlike shogun 2 for example the factions all have different soldiers and there are loads of them giving the game much more re-playability.  -there is loads of strategy involved and you can control your faction in loads of different aspects (if you like that kind of thing) .  Now why you shouldn't buy it. unlike many people my problem with it is not the early software issues or how the soldiers fight or anything so detailed in the gamplay. My problem with it is much worse, it is incredibly boring.  -The politics and all that is fantasticly dull and is way too complicated, especially if your playing as Rome.  -The game is really restrictive in how you can play. In old TW games you could have as many armies as you like. The games were much less complex. In Rome 2 your number of armies is limited based on the size of your empire and those armies have to have generals. This leads on to the fact that the generals are too important and complicated and i couldn't care less about them.  -It takes ages to do anything. You have to spend about 2 hours playing each battle endlessly pausing and playing and, God, the clicking, so much clicking. Then you finish the battle and re-enter the misery of the campaign where you cannot, due to the army limit and the fact that settlements take so long to become happy after being captured, capture any more settlements in less than about 79 million turns.  -There's just too much to think about when your playing. Food supplies, people's happiness, technology and buildings, politics and diplomacy, generals, armies, settlements, recruitment, navies, it goes on forever.  Basicly the game is too complicated and boring for most human mortals.

Christ, and I thought Dear Esther was bad. The game features no objectives but instead wants the player to immerse themself in an incredibly boring walking simulator. While 'walking simulator' could be used as an insult towards titles such as Dear Esther, Gone Home, and Doctor Robotnik's Mean Bean Machine, it literally is what the game wants to be. I can't blame it for that, honestly. It is relaxing to venture forth this tiny, poorly designed island every time you start up the game and begin your walk on water like Jesus ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ed Christ. However, this shouldn't be seen as a game as much a tech demo for some old computer a decade ago. What really strikes me as unbelievable is the lofty price tag. It's ten dollars to walk on an island created by somebody who pointed at Minecraft and an Atari 2600 and decided they wanted to play god and create some unholy combination of the two. To summarize my point, never, ever buy this filth, but if you scored it from a humble bundle, go nuts and stumble aimlessly for all of 5 minutes before you realize you'd get the same experience taking a walk around your neighborhood, with the objective being exercise.

Far Cry 3 is an enjoyable sandbox shooter that I will easily recommend. The vast open world is beautiful to look at and explore. There are almost always multiple ways to approach a given task, guns blazing, stealth, some mix of both. The core shooter elements consistently deliver. While I felt the story was a mixed bag, there are a bunch of memorable characters you'll work with and/or fight against over the course of the game. While I've never been a '100% completion' type of player, I did end up enjoying a lot of the side mission work in the game. This one will give you good value as far as hours played.   Apart from enemy AI that can be a bit lacking at times, the negatives are primarily stuff not related to gameplay. I won't give away any story spoilers, but overall I didn't really care about the main character or the competing factions of the island.... Speaking of this island... there are vending machines where you can literally purchase a modern assault rifle... a machine gun vending machine!! Of course, if you want to carry any ammunition you have to wander out into the jungle and kill some animals for leather to make a pouch. Seriously? Who thought that made a lick of sense? Also the extra layer of UPlay kind of stinks, but it didn't give me any real headaches.   Still, it's easy to overlook such goofball contrivances when the core game is as fun as it is here. I didn't purchase this when brand new, got it later on sale, but this would have been worth full retail as well. While it's not the best or most memorable shooter I've ever played, it's a consistently fun experience.

TL;DR: This tool has been amazing to work with and has really helped organize my content but is laggy on lower spec'd machines.  I had a system to managing my content, flows, and notes with a hierarchy of folders and files and that system was working to a degree. But it was still more effort than I wanted and keeping track of all the different files and documents was becoming a daunting task.  When I saw this tool I wasn't sure what it would do different for me, but even having a single organized source of all the data was a selling point. Then I looked more into the customizable templates, data validation options, visual maps, character maps, and most importantly the journey flows that you can create and was thrilled at the features.  That said, I use this on two machines; my dev machine while developing and a laptop when I am out and about and want to log thoughts/ideas. On the dev/gaming machine it runs incredibly smoothly, no issue whatsoever, but on my laptop it is laggy. I won't argue the minimum requirements, but it has not been a solid experience on my lower spec'd machine.  The sharing and collaboration options are also very exciting, though I have not had an opportunity to test those out yet.

Mars: War Logs is a bit of an enigma. Some have deemed it the 'poor man's Mass Effect' but this is incredibly misleading as the similarities between the two games stop at the sci-fi setting.  When I finished the game I found myself wondering how I made it from start to finish without utterly giving up on it. If it wasn't because the game is only 10 - 15 hours long then the only other reason I could come up with is that the game really makes you ponder what it could've been if it had come from a more experienced developer with a bigger budget.  All the usual trappings of an RPG are present but none of them feel particularly fleshed out or provide any kind of depth. The combat harks back to the 'old school' style of pause-and-play (ala Knights of the Old Republic, Dragon Age: Origins, etc) but beyond the fact that you can pause and then choose an ability none of the other aspects of the style are there. On top of this, while the game allows you to have a companion character you can't choose their attacks, outfit them with weapons/armor, or even take control of them in any way other than choosing their 'stance'.  A dialogue system is also in place but a lot gets lost in translation - literally. Since the developer is French the game is localized for anything else and it's terribly done. You'll find countless examples of this when your character says something that doesn't even kind of sound like your selection and/or when another character reacts to you in a seemingly opposite or confusing manner. Additionally it doesn't help that the voice actors bring disgustingly wooden performances to the table as well.  The inventory system is barebones (perhaps 4 different sets of 'armor' and maybe 6 weapons), the crafting/upgrading system is totally out of whack, despite having skill trees to that branch into RPG archetypes (rogue, warrior, magic user) the game almost seems to want you to max out everything and use everything in one playthrough, a romance system is there but is so poorly done it's easy to find yourself not knowing it's even there at all or glitching it/breaking it yourself, and finally the story (while pretty unique) is at times confusing, ill-paced, and withholding.  How/why did I finish this game? In the end perhaps it was it was a strange desire to see a good idea shoot itself in the foot with poor development and virtually no QA. Mars: War Logs is a yet another example of something that could've been great, that wanted to be great, but just couldn't.

Kiss your life goodbye.   PoE is an ARPG akin to Diablo, with extremely rich character customization options. You acquire skills via gems that function identically to the Materia system in Final Fantasy 7. You equip gems to enable skills, and can equip support gems to modify those skills to your liking. The combinations are endless.  Character development is done through a gigantic passive skill tree that can be a bit overwhelming at first. Your first couple tries might not be very good, but once you understand the game and what you need it will be much easier to use. It greatly resembles the Sphere Grid of Final Fantasy 10.   The game can be played in Single Player virtually the whole time, but multi-player is preferable at later levels. Trading is a necessary part of the game later on, however it is very clunky and sometimes quite painful.   By far the best aspect of the game is that it is NOT pay to win, you can purchase convenient upgrades for your inventory or aesthetic effects, but by no means will putting money in help you advance through the world. The game increases in diffculty greatly in the 3rd difficulty level and will challenge even the msot experienced players of ARPGs.   There is a story but it's not really required to understand the game, I've never bothered to look into it. Quests are abundant, although rewards could be a bit better at times.   It's free, almost to a fault. This is a game you SHOULD pay $40 for. 200+ hours and 4 different builds later and I'm still enjoying the experience.   8/10: The trade interface (or lack thereof) and other minor quality of life features that are missing keep this score from being higher, but the core experience is absolutely delightful.

This game is on a level above SimCity in terms of satisfaction gained when you see what you've built. Its closer to real. The logical next step is to add city politicians and citizens vetoing your plan to build a park in a poor area of town because why waste the money in such a low property area? Or a city council demanding you start building roundabouts in all residential areas to make the city 'more European.'. Or voting against you buying more land to expand the city.  At this point, we know how to build a perfect city, so now we need to throw problems at it for difficulty. You can crack the code so to speak on how to build good cities pretty easily, but it's amazingly fun to watch develop and adjust according to your plans and policies. Ultimately you do gain public approval, but you weild the power of a dictator. This is your strength and your flaw. While you never face re-election, so who gives a ♥♥♥♥ about happiness, everything that goes wrong is totally your fault.  I had done some big development in my game, and expanded my borders. People moved in and suddenly trash became an issue. I had to build another dump to take it in. Those things are expensive, which is stupid, because I'm pretty sure you can just say anywhere is a dump if there's a sign. So I'm broke, waiting for money to come in. In the meantime, people are getting sick from all the garbage everywhere.  Now I need more medicare. So I figure, get the dump. Then the garbage clears out, the people recover, I don't need another hospital just yet.  Finally I have enough cash, buy a dump, and start cleaning up the city. But at this point I have a problem. People have now died. There are dozens of apartments in my town abandoned due to death, with the tenants still in them, surrounded by trash.  Now people wanted a cemetary. Unfortunately, like the dump, you can't just say die a @#%!ing hole any #$##mn place you please because 'feelings'. That thing costs way more than the dump. I'm now plunging into debt because my population is crashing. I now have whole buildings abandoned with dead tenants. Basically, there are three types of people; the ones who are on their way outta town, the near dead, and the dead. The garbage is really catching up though so thats nice.  I grab my last remaining shot in a high interest loan from the bank in the hopes that I can build the graveyard quick enough that someone will still take a job there. I get the loan and place the graveyard. I press play, and suddenly it's revealed that the graveyard needs power. For whatever reason, I have no idea. I'm too broke to build a connection. I have to zone housing and enough people are insane enough to build a house between an empty graveyard and a literal city of corpses. Turns out there was, and after successfully connecting to the power grid, the bodies started to clear out. I quit the game at that point. I felt it was only right I step down.   If any of that sounded cool to you, buy this game. It's city builder porn.

Lichdom battlemage, first of all, is about playing as a combat mage. Not a wizard. That means it's more like Borderlands than Skyrim.  First of all, there are 3 parts to every spell, 3 kinds of magic (necromancy, fire, ice, etc) per slot, and 3 types of spells: sheild/teleport (think similar to telefragging, damaging enemies by teleporting, called 'blinking', ranged, and charged area of effect attacks.  This game is quite like borderlands. There's a spell grinder. There's spell merging. there's tons of havoc-inducing combat. There's tons of enemies. There's bullet-spongy enemies, etc. Just like borderlands.  Here's the thing: this thing isn't an rpg. it's the mage character from Hexen/heretic meets borderlands, minus the open world aspect.   BUT  In borderlands, you couldn't forge your own guns with parts. In this game, you can.  The result is a fair-feeling loot-based first person shooter with tons of havoc and mobbing and spongy super enemies.  If any of this interests you, buy it. If none of it does, don't. This is a good mechanical personification of the game. However, this is a far cry from the actual game. The card library is very slim compared to what you would encounter in actual Magic: The Gathering. That being the case, a weenie overwhelming creature deck is usually the right track to victory. That's not to say there aren't other ways to play online here, but most of the complexities of the game are lost based on the fact that the vast majority of your opponents won't have access to a large enough card library to play anything other than a weenie deck of any color. Also, the fact that boosters contain six cards is total ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t. That is just a built in compelling factor of the pay to play aspect. You can play this game for free, and you can even succeed for free. However, you acquire cards VERY slowly because the boosters are less than half the size that they are in real life. Overall, the mechanics of the game are pretty spot on. I'm giving the mechanics 9/10. The gameplay is really that of Magic: The Gathering. However, the communal friendship / card acquisition aspect couldn't be farther from the real world. That I give a 5/10. If you average the two, the game is looking at a 7/10 which I think is a very fair overall evaluation of the game. It's super fun. If you like Magic: The Gathering it is great but if you don't like grinding for advancement then it is NOT for you. If you want to pay to play then you can totally enjoy without the grinding. If you don't want to put any money in then you're in for a lot of grinding and a lot of losing online before you can really expand and play the game as Magic is meant to be played.  Again, I give it 7/10 overally because it's well made and well executed but it doesn't even touch the real feeling of the actual card game.

Traverser is a complete package that does everything well. It takes very familiar game elements such as physics puzzles and platforming and uses them quite effectively. Physics puzzles may be not super original as a game feature, but its the way their applied that is so well done here. I never knew what to expect and Traverser introduces these puzzles in subtle ways and never spells it out for you. I enjoyed this very much. Matter of fact the game does little hand holding and lets you figure out most of what needs to be done. Aside from the puzzles, the entire universe of Traverser just draws you into its unique narrative and charm. From the buildings architecture to the awesome flyers on the walls. This game has alot of personality and it shows. The voice acting is top notch and the story flows at a nice pace. The only gripe I could come up with for Traverser was the way game treats death. While there is no punishment there is a bit of a 5-10 second wait to reload and depending on how far a checkpoint was, retread the same areas. Also instant kill guards can be slightly annoying. This is a minor complaint though as death is very rare unless you mess up. In all I had a great time with Traverser and personally can't wait to see what is around the corner and what clever puzzles are there for me to solve.  I made a video review to show off some of the features and gameplay of this game!  https://youtu.be/GU8MODXKyuA  I hope you enjoy! Martyr

If you're new or an old fan of The TellTales games, and a fan of Borderlands, defiantly pick this up, it has all the humor and wackyness from the other Borderlands. PROS: -Gameplay is very smooth in general. -'Evolution' is easy to control and very intuitive. -Art style carrying over from previous game is this series signature element. -The idea of replacing having to eat all of one colour with having to complete quests is a good idea in itself as it makes the game less repetetive.  CONS: -Can't tell if it's about creationism, I don't appreciate having someone else faith thrown at me while playing a game. I mean sure if it's meant to be a philosophical game, but this is a game about a non descript aquatic animal eating other more descript aquatic animals. I mean maybe I'm just being paranoid and it's actually about a mad scientist turned ecologist, which is a pretty entertaining idea honestly.  -Obvious lack of knowledge of and/or lack of willingness to depect evolution and animal diets realistically. 'Blue = omnivore' .... yah.... I mean I get it's all simplified for gameplay mechanics, but it's not good to perpetuate myths and falsities about evolution (evolution is a multi generational process, it does not involve animals changing shape before your eyes). And combined with my preivous note about annoying religious overtones, this only serves to make me a tad uncomfortable and disappointed in the developers actions. Even if it's all a big coincidence it feels like it's spreading myths and that doesn't sit well.  -General art style is very nice, but I'm not a fan of the new player animals designs. In Sparkle 2 they weren't based on a real animal either, but these look like something from the Rayman universe with their segments magically held together without connecting.  -Quests are a good idea in general, but they remove much of the freeform element which is largely what made Sparkle 2 unique. Have you ever had that moment (looking at you Lego games) where you can't die but a level is just that tiny bit too difficult to deal with at the moment? So you're just endlessy trying again and again? Some of the quests really begin to feel like that and it spoils the otherwise relaxing atmosphere of the game.  Overall I give Sparkle 3 a 6/10, a decent game and worth trying out especially for the price.

Transformers War for Cybertron takes place on the planet cybertron and tells the story of the two sides, autobots and decepticons as they fight for control of the planet. Right off the bat you can choose between the autobot campaign or decepticon campaign. If you want to go in order of events then play the decepticon campaign first with the autobot campaign taking place after the events of the decepticons. The stories of both campaigns are very well done with you tearing apart the autobots as the decepticons then trying to fix things as the autobots. Each campaign offers a variety of characters to play as all being veterans of the transformers series. The missions in the campaign are each about an hour long or more offering plenty of playtime throughout the entire game. there are two types of levels, the normal ground missions where you play as the vehicle transformers like optimus or megatron. Then there are the air levels where you can play as characters like starscream and jetfire. Both are very fun and challenging in their own right. One of my favorite things to do in this game is transform which is something you can do just about any time you want and really gives it a transformers vibe to it. Another aspect I really enjoyed was the designs of the characters they still held their trademark appearances, but at the same time had a cybertronian style as well. One thing that is slightly annoying is the framerates are a little slow around a 30 benchmark. This could be due to not changing settings on my end, or it could be that it was locked at 30 either way it isnt too noticeable. There is a multiplayer and coop section, coop being for the campaign or escalation gamemode however I did not try either as there are not many people who still play the multiplayer of this game. Overall this game is a great transformers game that can appeal to hardcore fans but also be something for new people to enjoy that is well worth the 20$.

A mix of a visual novel and Groundhog Day, this game is about trying new options - and when I say options, I mean more than your dialogue choices. Something new about this particular VN is the ability to customize the protagonist's stats and inventory at the beginning, giving you different options as the story progresses, and affecting the success rate of certain choices.   There are a few bugs, but they aren't game breaking. For example, when equipping inventory, the first item may be visually unresponsive until a second action is done. The game only features about two songs in it's soundtrack, but this is natural for the game's length, and size, as well as price (Free!). It would benefit from a 'Skip Read Text' section, as your starting stats and inventory choices may affect the text sublety, which you may end up skipping entirely, or reading the same text over and over again to find a simple - but possibly vital change in dialogue.

Gay game and overrated  I sort of feel my time was wasted playing this geek game  Dota amazes in complexity of details - it appears to be a very interesting game not because of the story line, or the characters, or anything in particular, but only because of the complexity of its rules (items, abilities, mechanics, etc)  Which sorts of reminds me of that episode from Friends when Chandler was trying to give Joey some money and came up with a cards game to lose on purpose. Queen of Spades beats 2 of Clubs, but hey wait, the 2 of Clubs was the 5th card in the deck, so you need to take 3 cards, but if you have an Ace among the 3 cards, you lose 20 points. Add a million rules like this.   Yes, the game is complex in its rules and the multitude of ways to tackle a single Dota match at a time, but go deeper than that and you will realize NOTHING in that game is real - the characters are all fake, the creeps walk in a straight line and always clash at the same spot, the neutrals always sit in the same spots, all creeps spawn at :00 or :30, etc etc  Unlike popular games like Counter-Strike, Company of Heroes, Age of Empires, ETC ETC ETC that are based on real elements (SWAT operation, a medieval war, a world war 2 fight) Dota is based on the wild imagination of a few people who just sat their a*sses on the couch and came up with this gibberish (fantasy and magic) only geeks love to read/play.   Conclusion - yes, I know Dota can be fun, but bottom line DOta is by far THE geekiest game out there, created by geeks, played by geeks, with little if anything to learn useful in real life, or stimulate your intellect.  Therefore, I highly recommend this game to geeks, but urge normal people to stay away from it, as you might turn into a geek (like I did, but then thanks god a light stroke me and I saw through it)  Also don't play LoL or HoN or any of these games from this branch of gay magic :D  GG

As someone who has logged hundreds of hours on TF2 and Garry's Mod, I am no stranger to the first person perspective but I am newer to the puzzler aspect of Portal games. I played through the first installment and absolutely loved it. It was short, sweet, and reasonably challenging while having a great core mechanic and awesome sense of humor. Now, Portal 2 has built on it and has added more of the fun from Portal. The sense of humor is not as sardonic and dark as the first one but still pretty funny. The game itself is a bit dull at points but the physics engine is beyond amazing and the scenery is really crisp and engaging especially on my HD monitor. The game doesn't add too much new to the core mechanics which is a good thing because they have no need for a change. The story drags a bit but overall the few criticisms I have for it are far outweighed by the fact that it is still one of the few games I will play over and over again, once I beat it. Above all, play the first one before you even touch the second, the story will make much more sense and you will find better humor and tighter gameplay there. But again, I highly recommend Portal 2, it just has the unfortunate position of being the sequel to what has to be my favorite game of all time so, I still score it 94/100.

BioShock Infinite is a decent addition to decent series. Ambitious in its plot and beautiful in its graphics, Infinite is a game that is simoultaneously fun to play and wonderful to look at. Gameplay and mechanics are very similar to the previous Bioshock games, with the addition of the Skyhook, which opens up a new oppurtunity for diverse tactics. The game is heavily linear, and features a (optional) quest direction indicator line, which holds your hand as you play through the story and keeps on track. Those who are fans of the linear style of Metro Last Light would enjoy this game (however, the aesthetic is the opposite of MLL). The plot combines political and personal intrigue with science-fiction and quantum mechanics in an ambitious and entertaining story that false just short of making sense. At times, the concepts left me wondering what the hell was going on, and distracted from the otherwise enjoyable experience. Ultimately, Bioshock Infinite is a solid game with an interesting, if confusing, plot and gorgeous graphics. If you are looking for a linear experience with familiar FPS gameplay, Bioshock Infinite will not dissapoint.

So, where do I begin? I'm a really big fan of Mad Max movies, especially the classic first and second movie. Although, the game and the new movie gives us a little bit of a different view of things, this is a welcomed change. The game itself has an astonishing atmosphere. The looks of the wasteland, the background music and the kinda sorta big world makes even roaming interesting.  The tasks on the other hand getting a bit repetitive after an amount of time. Clearing out all camps, tearing down all the scarecrows and such can get a bit boring. The story and side missions (called wasteland missions) are fun. The only downside is there not much of them.  Customizing the car to your style of playing is a nice addition. In early game I thought getting more armor and bigger fenders are the key. Then I realized speed and firepower is everything. You can also choose from various car bodies and paintjobs. So the car can and will look anyway you want. Well, in it's boundaries at least.  The game can be completed on a basic level in 35 hours and to max it out with Max (pun intended) you'll have it to work with it around 50 to 60 hours I think. It's worth it. You can fool around with the photography mode, which is a really cool screenshot maker and editor, that gives at least +50% fun factor to the game.    After beating the game you will get the Falcon XB, alias the Last V8 Interceptor, along with the iconic leather jacket and the sawed-off double-barrel shotgun. This and the fenomenal photo mode is an undepletable source of fun if you are into things like this.    Now I wait for the Bullet Farm DLC and hopefully some kind of multiplayer combat arena, at least a destruction derby. Too bad it's not gonna happen.  PROS: - Large, fully discoverable world - Widescale customization of your ride (and Max himself) - Interesting missions and tasks - Awesome looking wasteland with all of it's detail  CONS: - After a time some tasks can get a bit repetitive - Minor bugs, nothing game breaking - Storyline is short, way too short

I will be writing a full review covering everything , if you're interested in ever buying this game read : )  To start i really wanted to give this game a bad review and (Not recommend) because i was disappointed for it showing so much promise in 'beautiful story-line' at the beginning but then ends up hours and hours of puzzle after puzzle with no point to feel that it connects to the story somehow .. i can say the same for several indie games...  So as you can see in the screenshots its a puzzle 2D Platformer, with a very well-done animation and the artwork is completely outstanding very very beautiful indeed.  You die from one hit, so the game is set for you to be able to avoid, by dodging and jumping your way through obstacles and enemies but you can also fight so no worries.  if you like puzzlers this game is for you it is well-thought on that matter so it stays interesting, you change between Red/blue (opposites attract) and just figure your way out of the puzzle and use it to fight enemies, and the boss battles , don't even get me started on them they are truly both amazing and challenging.  So summing it up  Whats Great : 1 - Artwork, the game is definitely worth its price just for that i mean wow, the screenshots don't show half of it, there are so many beautiful things not shown there 10/10 2 - Music + sound effects , both work out perfectly for the game 10/10 3 - Puzzles, challenging , keeps getting interesting without repeating themselves but are a lot 8/10 4 - The story and (Theater plays) are great but they didn't stick to it for 80% of the game 7/10  Whats not so great: 1 - Achievements ... they are not really achievements you just get one every-time you collect one of those lightbulbs or whatever they are. 2 - Probably the only thing that made me feel bad about the game is how they did not stick to the story and just try to refresh it every now and then, yes the theater plays are good and i like them but it was just pure puzzle after puzzle without the sense of the story carrying on , thats it there is litterly nothing else i hate about this beautiful game and i am being 100% true and fair with my review  So if you're into this type of game, just look in some videos of it ( not steam videos) And you will simply fall in love with it Definitely a game worth buying at full price to support those awesome devs , yes you might get a bit disappointed if you notice or simply wants the story to carry on but it will only at the end of the game and it still will give you the satisfaction you need to feel the closure  overall rating of the game 8/10  1- for achievements ( probably should have done an achievement for finishing a puzzle in a certain way or something) 1- for not keeping up with the story until the end

This is my first review because I feel like I need to say somerthing about my experiece in the multiplayer aspect of this game. I was not going to buy this game due to some of the reviews that said that this game is pretty much like BO3 which I really did not like at all because of how the weapons and the movement felt. I bought this game on sale becaue I was only interested in the MW4R and decided not to wait for stand alone because it might be months before they release it by itself. I decided to take a chance on this and if I did not like it, I would return it. After playing MW4R for quite a bit and loving it, I decided to install IW and I was getting myself ready to be disappointed. But I was pleasantly suprised that the multiplayer is a lot more fun and feels a lot better to me in every way than BO3 did. The movement is a bit slower and more methodical and has weight to it and when you pick up guns they feel different from each other and they feel right when you shoot them. There is power and weight to weapons that I did not feel with BO3. Overall I am glad I bought this game as I will be playing MP in IW a lot more than I did BO3. So if you are like me and did not care for B03, I would suggest you try it and you might be very suprised on how much better it is. But if you liked B03 than this might go either way.

This game could be fun. Unfortunately it comes with  no documentation.  There is a small palette onscreen when you start a game that briefly outlines how to do the first two stops of a line. That is all.  When you combine that with the picky way the game wants you build road and track systems, you will find yourself struggling to connect lines and stations, because the game can't manage its own topography. It simply has no tolerence for any variation in terrain, curvature or elevation. Straight lines are a breeze- but connecting all those lines is a chore.  Don't even try complex interchanges on railoads until you've experimented for a while- for example, as I understand it you cannot at time of writing build a road across existing railroad tracks, at all. You can only build tracks over roads- otherwise you will have to bulldoze the track, connect your road and rebuild the railroad again. And every time you tear down a section of road or track, you'll also have to use the leveling tool as well, because you won't be able to build anything other than what was already there until you flatten the terrain again. Because of this, I don't even bother with anything other than 'flat' terrain maps, as anything else is just asking for extra fiddling around.  To add insult to injury, as you pixel-hunt for the few places you can actually build on or join sections, the game likes to makes sound effects. So as you patiently wiggle the cursor around, hoping for that little green check mark, you've got a little jackhammer noise rattling away everytime you move your cursor. rattle rattle rattle.  You can get the hang of it after a while, but even then you'll be fiddling around. I just quit the game after I built a whole industrial complex of road-train cargo lines supporting the local indsutry - the wagons simply will not find a path. I have three stops and a depot, and not a single stop works with the pathfinding. The road itself is fine from what I can see. Gaaah.. I just couldn't be bothered any more. Every project involves massive setbacks as you discover you can't connect something with something else, becase the game gives you no guidance and no hint as to how much space the segments need. It's pure guesswork.  Once you manage to get everything working, it's fun to watch the trains. But there isn't a lot of depth here. You'll build train stations and depots and stops and plan lines until you're bored, and then one day you'll do it again. or not. You can't control scheduling, you can't set fares. One thing I find particularly annoying is that if you have a new line and buy new vehicles for it, you can't just buy a fleet and set them on their route. If you do that the game will send every vehicle on the new line all at once. It doesn't make sense.  There are a few other issues as well. Performance could be much better (zooming causes heavy stuttering), the interface is unfinished (palette locations aren't saved, so every time you open a game, you will have to re-open and reposition every palette again. THis happens with new games and saves), I've had several issues with road vehicle pathfinding at cargo stations- they can't tolerate more than two vehicles at a time it seems). Other minor quibbles but those are the main issues.  It's disappointing. Train Fever could be much better- it might even already be - but I can't tell, because as I said,  the game has no documentation.  No links in the forum, no wiki, not even basic controls are explained. The game feels like a promising early access title, not a full release. It's not finished. Don't buy it until it is.

tl;dr; It's a high cost but quality visual novel.  Long version: I won't say all that much about the contents of the visual novel itself. You either like them or you don't. CLANNAD is massive with tons of choices which matter... but it's also very expensive.  I'll rather say things about the technical aspects of the 'game': - There's a checkbox in settings, 'Make this program run more slowly in order to make other programs run faster', by default it is off. The only difference I can see is that when off, the game will hog 100% of one processor at all times. When turned on... the game takes virtually no processing power. LIKE IT SHOULD AS AN IMAGE AND TEXT DISPLAYER. So, make sure to check that. - The game seems to have massive issues with Steam Overlay at the moment. There are many resolutions and scales (the game has scaling for 50%, 75%, 100%, 150%, 200%) where the Overlay simply won't show up. If you come across this and want to use the Overlay (useful for screenshots etc.) try changing resolution and/or scaling and it might pop up... while lagging terribly. Still seems to work, at least for me. - Otherwise, the game is rather barebones as far as settings go. It has the basics (like turning off voices per character, text window background color changing, etc.), but it lacks some stuff like being able to turn off the custom mouse cursor. Which is, to be honest, slightly infuriating, as the 'click point' of the cursor is on the lower left, rather than upper left like it is just about everywhere else. - The save system is pretty cool as it allows you to write memos on each save file, which is really useful considering how the game has - probably without exaggeration - hundreds of choices which actually matter (not just 'pick this option or you get a bad end' options, but options which determine what girls you'll meet. Some girls will even interact if you've met them both, whileas some will prevent you from going to see another girl (like the class rep stopping you from skipping class, where you'd meet another girl)). However, it completely lacks an auto-save function, which would be somewhat useful... even though it'd need to record 10-20 saves to be useful. Oh, and the system also allows you to move or delete saves with ease, which always nice to see. - The load system, unfortunately, doesn't show you any message backlog after loading a save. If you're coming back to a save after a while, you might be completely lost at what point of the game you're at because of this. - Dangopedia is pretty cool, though if you're a complete weeaboo you probably don't need it to explain what a kotatsu or bento are. Really useful for people new to the genre. Though, chances are such people would pick a cheaper title to start with...  PS. Kyou best girl, but has the worst route. I had already played this years ago in the fantranslation, and ragequit after completing that route. Did the same here. Not sure was I even expecting anything else, really!

The customization is great and one of the better one's that I have seen in a long time, again. Don't take my word for it when it comes to multiplayer because I've only played it very little, I'll update this once I've played a thorough amount.   To sum it up the singeplayer is great and I would recommend it to every person out there who has been looking for or waiting for a great FPS singleplayer environment. The multiplayer being the core part of any FPS game is fast paced and has a very steep learning curve but if you put in the hours I'm sure you will do fine, there is no bs like in other modern shooters it's just well balanced.

Pros: -Good customization, your planes don't have to be made out of voxels. -Challenges to keep you occupied so it isn't just a sandbox game. -Includes weapons. -Decent physics, I don't know if they're realistic but the physics are alright. -Smooth framerate -Good mobile port, compared to other mobile ports.  Nitpicks: - Difficult to fly with a keyboard, much like all piloting games. Joystick is a must if you're planning on doing some of the challenges. - Difficult build mode controls, but you can get used to them after about 30 minutes of building. - Not enough warbird parts/weapons. - Not enough air intake parts for Jets. - Pulsating glowing rings in race matches are annoying, they mess with your depth perception. -Weapons are too powerful, 1 bullet will make your aircraft explode no matter where it hits. -Some bugs when it comes to part collisions, specifically the landing gear. -Lack of texture for planes, though this can be atributed to the art style or the fact that it's a mobile port.  Cons: -Some challenges are too difficult for the average player. -The Lack of flares / missile countermeasures make fights with missiles repetitive.

Despite what Steam says about my play time for this game, I've played Dead Space 2 twice, and if I had the time, I'd play through it again.  The good: gloriously haunting atmosphere, action packed, starkly deep characters, and a beautiful continuation of the Dead Space story. While the horror aspect is knocked down a half-notch this time around, everything related to combat is dramatically increased; this gave me the feeling that this game was more of an action-horror rather than just a horror that its predecessor was. Isaac is fleshed out beyond being the boyfriend-in-mourning, and the new cast of characters are given their fair share of the spotlight such that you will come to pity, fear, love, or hate them. As well, the story continues seamlessly from the first game, and for fans, it's a real treat.  The bad: for fans of immersion, this game is a big step back. In the first game, Isaac had no voice actor and did not communicate other than through body language. You WERE Isaac. You played as him. You dismembered necromorphs as him. You died as him. Now, Isaac has a voice actor, and that does take quite a bit away from the immersion aspect as Isaac now has his own voice.  The ugly: honestly, nothing comes to mind.  For fans of the original, Dead Space 2 is a worthy successor. For gamers who want a lot more thrill and tension in their action games, this comes highly recommended.

Easily a contender for my favorite game of all time. The story is a fantastic example of handling serious issues without being preachy, never forgetting to be fun and amusing. The blend of 2D stealth, puzzling and platforming gameplay is just about perfect, and challenging without being excessively punishing. The controls are similar to Tomb Raider, in that they are very precise; they follow strict rules, and your every move is calculable. The visuals are also pushed to the absolute limits of the PlayStation; you won't find any polygons in this one, and the cutscene-to-gameplay transitions are so seamless it's uncanny.  The only reasonable gripe I can see people having with it is the difficulty. The game is not without its trial and error, for sure, and this game lacks the quicksave option of Exoddus. I've heard some describe its challenge as ranging from 'unreasonable' to 'impossible.' On the front of the old PlayStation jewel case, you'll even see it blantantly say it has 'inexplicably challenging gameplay,' and all you have to deal with that is a functional brain and infinite lives (you'll need them.) Oh, and magical powers. The game might have 'forgotten' to tell you that until the third level... whoops. Yes, all this game teaches you are the basics. There isn't much hand-holding, and you will likely get the bad ending on your first playthrough. However, you will get a chance to fix that, starting from the beginning of the final level. This was certainly a deliberate design decision. If you have reservations about the difficulty of this game, play Exoddus first, for the quicksave, and an even larger game.

This is the perfect game for anyone who likes turn-based combat, but finds most other games in the genre to be slow paced, limited in tactical options, overly concerned with micromanagement, or any of the dozens of other faults you can fairly accuse turn based games of, especially clones of the original X-Com. The UI is clean and simple, giving you the information you need in easy to understand ways; if you want more detailed information, it's usually a button click away. The game plays just as well on a controller or KB+M, but if you use a non-Xbox controller, you'll probably get weird mapping issues like the left stick's Y axis being inverted and button swapping between the shoulder buttons and the triggers.  In general, both the stock Enemy Unknown game and the Enemy Within expansion have a difficulty curve that eventually flattens out 2/3 of the way through the game as you run out of new enemies to encounter and new technology to research. In Enemy Within, the early difficulty is raised by having a ton of missions trigger in the in-game month of May based on your research level, which can be a problem if you are playing on Classic difficulty. In general, Normal difficulty in EW has been changed (at the time of this review) to be horrifically easy, while Classic has been made even more difficult than it was when the game was first released. If you are a new player or getting back into the game after a hiatus, stick to Normal before jumping into Classic. That said, you can look into some of the mods on the Nexus forums if you want to rebalance the difficulty or alter the experience to suite your tastes.  The main flaws of the game are the Interception mini-game, which is basically a Quick Time Event with consumables instead of commands, the panic system that makes you focus all your early game efforts on satellite coverage, and the ending mission, which is kind of anti-climactic with the right squad. These don't ruin the game, but they take some of the luster off of repeated playthroughs.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: (TL;DR at bottom) - There are a lot of games out there that create artifical difficulty, and I don't just mean raising stats without any other improvements. I mean things that are just outright cheap, anything that requires an element of luck in high volumes.  Dark Souls provides legitimate challenge, nothing is particularly easy, somethings are very difficult. But nothing requires luck (excluding ONE specific boss, literally can not think of any other examples).  As for the story, it requires you to be observant and read item descriptions, talk to NPCs and shopkeepers. You have to think a little to understand what is going on because much like the combat, nothing is spoon fed to you. Some people will find this bothersome, I found it quite enjoyable. It feels more realistic because of that need to ask people questions and do a little connecting the dots!   TL;DR - If you are looking for a legitimate challenge with a story you have to connect the dots and think to understand then this is a game for you! Very fun!

Crysis + Just Cause 2 = Far Cry 3... but better  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  Take the FPS gun-play of Crysis, but with more relaxed enemy AI (depending on difficulty). Now mix in the sandbox play of Just Cause 2, but with a smaller, more manageable island size that makes hiking across it much more reasonable. You pretty much get Far Cry 3. It still has some annoyances of 'open world / 50+ hours of gameplay!' games, but it's far more entertaining. It's a 'summer popcorn movie' video game. Turn your brain off, and you'll have a blast.  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  PROS:  + Smaller world size + less dense off-road areas makes getting about (even on foot) much more manageable and enjoyable. (ie: they don't need a cheaty-feeling gimmick like Just Cause 2's grappling hook and parachute to get about, because simply hiking around gets you where you're going pretty fast.) This is one of the few open-world games where I feel they got the world size just right.  + Unlike Far Cry 2's or Just Cause 2's 'everything looks the same' worlds, FC3's areas are different-looking enough to be distinct and interesting to get around. Yes, it's a lot of green, but they mix up areas of vegetation (bamboo forests, trees, fields) and elevation (mountains, hills, plains, pits, etc), then mix in cave networks (!) to add enough variety to keep it interesting hiking and exploring. Because the world is small enough to cross on foot reasonably, I find myself driving along and seeing something interesting. So, I get out of my vehicle to go explore and site-see. I don't really do that in other open-world games, because usually everything looks the same and there's nothing interesting. FC3 does it right.  + Yes, like almost all open-world games, there's item 'scavenger hunts' that act as gameplay filler. But, the landscape is so varied, and they hid some stuff in cave systems, so it makes exploring and finding stuff actually pretty entertaining! Plus, they give you the option to buy maps that show all loot & special item locations. This way you can choose to either explore randomly, or highlight all locations and purposefully go get them. Even when you get to the location of some loot, you may be surprised to find it's 20M below you. So, you then have to go find the cave opening that takes you down to it. Some loot is located in ship-wrecks in shark-infested waters. So, you head out on a jet ski, look around... looks like the coast is clear... dive in... SHARK! I usually find scavenger hunt BS to be the bane of existence in open-world games, but I've been loving the heck out of it in FC3.  + The concept of hunting animals to upgrade gear seems a bit gimicky, but I found it enjoyable. I spent most of my early hours in the game just tracking and hunting animals... with an AK-47 and sniper rifle!  + When games started delving into better graphics, they went overboard with Bloom, HDR, post, etc. I turn all that off, because the game ends up looking like a smudgy, blurry mess and I can't tell what's going on. In Far Cry 3, they seem to have hit some kind of perfect balance. I have the graphics cranked up to max, even post, and I can see things vividly, bloom isn't over-done, everything looks great. I think my one small beef with the graphics is that the sky in the game engine seems unnaturally dark, even mid-day. The clouds and blue in the sky just seems like it's not bright enough to seem realistic. It's like there's a perpetual smog haze in the sky.   NEUTRALS:  * they padded the game with the usual open-world tropes ... racing events, mini-games, oddball missions (some about hunting, some about killing a random pirate, etc). You don't have to do them, but the hunting ones tend to be a must-do, since they hand out the best animal skins needed to craft the best gear.  * you have to install and create a UPlay account. I know folks find that annoying, but from a business perspective it's clever. They can cut the price of games like FC3 way down to use as loss-leaders to get folks to install UPlay. Once UPlay is installed, chances are good that folks will browse what it is and what's in the UPlay store. It's a way to get folks to check out your online store. But, I do find it annoying. There's just too many online game 'services/stores' these days to keep up with it all, and UPlay only has a small library of games. It's annoying when companies make you install an entire game service/store when there's hardly much in it. Everyone wants to be the next Steam.   CONS:  - Respawn rates are still annoying, seemingly ripped from FC 2. If you're in hostile territory, enemy vehicles magically show up to a fight every 30 seconds. Some areas only clear out after story missions, and they will keep spawning enemies while you're standing there. Seems a bit awkward.  - Ammo is dropped by enemies based on the guns they use (makes sense). Since most early-game enemies are only using assault rifles or shotguns, you get tons of that ammo and hardly any other. So, you find yourself hunting animals with an assault rifle. You buy a sniper rifle, then hardly use it since you don't find enough ammo and have to buy more. It's annoying.  - There's gun shops. The way they work is that you buy a gun to unlock it, then you can always come back and swap to it if you want to change your load-out. I like that, but I think they could have done away with the gun shop and just made guns part of the scavenger hunt. You can find some of the guns on enemies, so if you grab it you can bring it back to the shop to unlock it for free. They should have kept that method and just had you find all the guns, and simply have a storage locker at bases. The gun shop gets abusive pretty fast, because, if you're a completionist, you'll have cash coming out your wazoo and have all the best guns unlocked early on without much to look forward to.  - There's an rpg-lite xp and skill system. It seems contrived and annoying at times. Instead of just letting you naturally get better as you do things, you get xp to 'build your character as you like'. But, most skills are locked away until you do more missions. So, if you're a completionist that loves to explore and finish areas before moving on, you'll be sitting on lots of unspent skill points waiting for skills to unlock. It's just super annoying. Unlock them all from the start. Stop punishming me for taking my time to play the game.  - Your character is super-whiney. If you muted his voice, you'd think you were a bad-a** in the game with everything you do. But, he makes a whiney little remark when you slaughter an animal for the umpteenth time. Cut scenes have him whining about stuff, or making really dumb decisions that you wouldn't make in the open-world game play. He's a bro-ster d-bag supposedly coming of age, but he never seems to grow up.  - The open-world gameplay lets you tailor how you engage enemies and bases. Then the story missions throw all of that out the window by forcing contrived handicaps on you (eg: must sneak, must stealth kill, must run-n-gun, must ride in a vehicle and man the mounted gun like a rail shooter). And your idiot character decides to walk right into traps over and over. Don't let me be a bad-*** in open-world game play just to make me feel like an idiot in cut scenes. It's insulting.  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  Overall, FC3 gets the open-world part right, but drops the ball on the story line. However, FC3 gets so much right, and has been a royal blast to play, it's easy to overlook the cons. I'm getting older, and a game has to really impress me to keep my attention. I've been playing FC3 pretty much nonstop since I installed it, and that's saying something. Problem is, the open-world part has kept me coming back, but the trudging through the story has kept pushing me away. I finally finished it, and once the sotry is done, the open-world part and collecting stuff seems pointless. But, it's an interesting sandbox to explore.

review_text: This is by far the buggiest, most poorly put together, least thought out, most unsatisfying piece of ♥♥♥♥ I've ever played. I could write an encyclopedia's worth of reasons why I don't like this game, but the main reason is that it is incredibly unstable. It crashes every 10 minutes, if it starts at all. I am convinced that Bethesda released this without any kind of quality control at all; it needs about a year's worth of extra work just devoted to fixing bugs before it begins to live up to its hype. Don't buy this game. However, if for some reason you do buy this game, don't try to run it without any mods. Just mod the ♥♥♥♥ out of it before you even bother trying to play. That's the only way this thing is worth the CPU cycles needed to start it up.  If you want something like this game that's actually good and worth playing, look into the S.T.A.L.K.E.R. series. It's much, much better.  I can't understand why so many people like this game so much, and I want my damn money back.  7/10 it's pretty good.

This game CAN be fun, at least for a bit. However, once you realize that you can get all your basic items to 'highest level' in only a few hours you start to wonder. Then you realize that your weapons/frame are pretty much useless next to a Prime-Frame or weapon, or really any other weapon than what you start with.   So what do you do?! Why, you figure out how to get those other weapons!   How do you do it? ....Grind.... Grind the same levels over and over until the piece of it drops (which is only like 1/4th of the actual weapon), then you find the next level to have a piece and do the same.  What happens next? You have to build it, which can take 12-24 hours depending on what it is (That's Real-Time), but you can have it instantly if you only pay a couple buck!  Speaking of that: You can also have anything in the game you want instantly and without grinding for the low price of like... $20.00 per item. (probably slightly exaggerated, look into it yourself). Though that's not REALLY a problem, it just depends on if you want better stuff or not.  Ok, I have the weapons/frame I want, now what? I can have a pet?! Yup... Go grind that for 2 hours, just to get an egg. THEN you can wait on him to grow up, and slowly die unless you get items that 'stabilize his DNA'  Done, now what? Well... You grind, or you go to PvP and grind there, or you grind the items you need to farm to grind the items you need to farm.  My point is, this game is one big-♥♥♥ grind. If you're okay with grinding, it CAN be fun.

So once again I was led to think a old game is great. Never played before. I love space RTS. Sins was by far the best ever made. Because of all the hype and people claiming HW is the best I just had to give it a try and was happy to see they were remastering it and selling here on Steam. Big mistake to listen to people once again.   For all of you out there that are looking for a great RTS/4x space game, and expect the UI to be good then do not buy this game. Here's why. If you come from playing Sins of a Solar Empire you will know that the user interface is simply the best and easiest. Why any game out there does not do this to this day is beyoned me. SInce the Devs claimed they 'remastered' this game I expected that they would truly put it up to par with what a great game should be today. They did not. I suspect the UI was no diferent when the game was first made to now with the remastered. So really all that was done was graphics update. Thats it. Made the game look pretty. Well this was great but why the hell could you not update the UI? I'm sorry but I just think once again I wasted my money on another space game that could have been great with very simple UI updates. How hard is it to do a 'stratigic Zoom' such as in Sin's???? Why is this so hard to accomplish by game Devs? Why do I have to go though steps and press multiple keys in order to zoom in on a ship and pan around to get a good view of a battle? Why?   Hopfully there will be a new game soon that can accomplish this very apparently hard task.   For old players returning I'm asumimg you dont care and love the game just the way it is. Fine. If thats whom the Devs want to sell to okay then. To anyone new looking to buy the game and expect it to be easy and intuitive UI dont expect much.   Pros:  Im sorry but its not fun to have to deal with the UI and therefore has ruined the game completely. No pros. Poorly remade. Devs wanted a quick buck by remaking the graphics and changed nothing else to make it a fun and easy to use game.  Cons:  1. Crashes on a pretty regular bases. Did not harp on this to much because I'm asuming is a simple bug that will get adressed soon?  2. Horrible old UI that is just completly a turn off. Waste of a potentially good game.  3. No multiplayer. Or at least did not see how to do it.  4. Horrible tutorial. Very buggy. Very basic. Crashes. 5. In a good game when you hover the mouse over a subject in the game you get a good description in a paragraph or so describing what it is. This was very poorly done if at all on some items and areas of the game. Basically figure it all out your self guys.   Verry didsapointed. Please someone make a good space game like Sin's again with a seamless and sratigic zoom. There is no reason I should have to push multiple keys to acomplish camerea and UI features. Should all be simple and done with the mouse.

I don't know where to begin. Right now I don't know if I can recommend Advanced Warfare because of how buggy it can be. Sometimes it will connect to a match and either take longer than usual and the match has already started (even though I join before the lobby has connected) or it'll freeze whilst 'synchronising settings' and then quit due to connection failure or something. When playing online, I cannot tell you how ANNOYING it is to be mauled by some ♥♥♥♥♥♥ weilding a laser. Oh, and it's not just the odd one, I mean I faced an ENTIRE TEAM full of them at one point lol. They take no skill because you can literally use it like a bloody lightsaber!   The singleplayer is... typical COD. No innovation. I mean, Black Ops II had a branching storyline for God's sake. I skipped Ghosts altogether due to the rather rubbish reviews for the PC version. But, seems like Sledgehammer Games are afraid of being risky and adventurous with a story. My brother, who hardly plays games full stop, decided to give this new COD a crack, and casually breezed through it in a few nights.  On top of all of this, the game likes to quit completely and recommend 'Safe Mode' due to DirectX errors. I have like every version of DirectX that I need (and the game will go ahead and install the ones it needs anyway...). My PC is good enoguh to run this, but even on 'Optimal' it still has errors. I'm talking 8 GB of RAM, Core i5 4690K, GTX 660. However, this isn't the first game as good ol' Need for Speed: Rivals does it too. I'm not the only one. But, what can I do?  Also, if I crank up the settings up to maximum and play the story, the textures are aweful! Flat and no visual substance whatsoever. Plus, it runs like poop. Oh, but do 'Optimal Settings' and things are fine. I don't get it.  I've had fun with this title, when the connection is good and stable. But I can't quite recommend this in the condition it is in. Activision need to promote the idea of 'bug checking' and 'ensuring EVERY verison of our game is up to scratch' and not, yet again, leave us PC players feeling left behind.  11/10 would construct again

This game does an exceptional job at forcing the average person to make uncomfortable, unethical decisions they probably would never confront in real life. Even making the best of the worst choices, I still somehow managed to get multiple children murdered.  Yes, we are always sometimes monsters.  (But then, so is this game. More on that in a moment...)  Unrelated, and disconcerting to me personally, I also lived solely off of bacon burgers (despite hundreds of attempts to live a good freegan lifestyle with dumpster diving), because somehow I was ALWAAAAAAAAYS starving. I think I may have purchased upwards of 1,000 bacon burgers in 12 hours of game play. There should have been an option to check her cholesterol levels on Day 1 and Day 30. Now THAT's every day, real world issues right there. Much more than the pursuit of winning back/stalking/'if you truly love something, let it go'ing the ex that this entire game is centered around (because, let's be honest here, most people just use social media to do that in this day and age).  Which is why I wouldn't necessarily recommend this game. Yes, I enjoyed most of it, but: all the decisions you make don't seem to REALLY end up mattering, because while this game talks about nature vs nuture, destiny vs free will, consquence vs punishment... it's all just talk in the end. The game wants you to confront your ex. And no matter which way you choose to do it (as others have mentioned, the ending really is dictated by the last two or three days of choices - not the first twenty-seven - so work at a cannabis farm, rig an election, break into a doctor's home to blackmail him ... IT DOESN'T MATTER [by the way, why the heck couldn't I work at the stripclub? I really would have liked that option! I'm sure there's a market for chubby, bacon-burger bellied girls])... and that makes it frustrating enough to just call it quits and go back to your real life.  Where, while I might be starving all the time, I don't have to eat my proverbial weight in bacon burgers.

Metro 2033 Redux is a pretty solid fps. It's story driven and in that regard has a feel that reminded me of The Chronicles of Riddick: Butcher Bay (which is high praise). In the 'hub' areas people are just milling about, talking, adding an atmosphere of boredom and destitution. The gameplay is mostly well done, the weapons all feel good and (booya!) you've got 4 shotguns to choose from. Thankfully, the late game weapons all felt appropriately powerful. While this is touted as a horror fps, unlike other horror games, you have strong capability in dealing with said horrors. The guns are all pretty weighty and customizable, while looking the part of post-apoctalyptic doodads. Your main enemies are mutant-type creatures or evil humans bent on dominating the remnants of mankind. All deserving of shotgun spray. My only real gripes are the level called 'Defense' and the voice acting on children. Defense is utterly unfun. You have small, fast-moving enemies that crawl out of the walls and often attack you from behind (think headcrabs, but faster). On top of that, the game nerfs a specific aspect of your control so that now you are fighting these cheap enemies at a further disadvantage from lack of complete control. On top of which (!), you are forced to listen to some atrocious voice-acting. My advice is to just run through this area as fast as you can, without dallying. And those children are obviously just voiced by some low-rent adult actors. Another annoyance is the preponderance of ripping control away from the player, especially late-game 'mind-altering' experiences (think Max Payne dream sequence). Aside from those issues, this game was really enjoyable to me. I'm a big fan of first-person games where your hands aren't only for implementing death, but also for opening doors and picking up objects, and just generally making me feel like I'm actually interacting with this world.

I've been a fan of Egosoft since X-Beyond-The-Frontier, and I own all of their games... I was playing their games before steam was even a conceptual thought, and have logged god only knows how many countless hours into the entire trilogy, which includes XBTF, X-Gold, X2: The Threat, X3 Reunion, X3 Terran Conflict, X3 Albion Prelude, and all those games were awesome, the best in my opinion is a fair tie between Terran Conflict and Albion Prelude. I liked Prelude because of the stock market, that didn't exist in previous releases.  ...then came X-Rebirth... an entirely different story... we waited a long time for it's release thinking they were putting a lot of heart and effort into the game, to make it as good if not better than the previous sequels, then when it was finally released as a FULL release mind you, and not the Beta release now displayed along with it's title, it was a complete mess, with nothing working. We all paid full release prices for a game that wasn't even worth calling an 'Alpha', and to this day, the patches are released far from what we would have otherwise wished for on the scale of progress in giving gamers what they paid for.  I suppose this is a game that Egosoft was hoping the modding community would bail them out, and for some just may have, but for the hard core gamers, and the gamers-4-life, such as myself, being 43 years old, and a gamer all my life on PC platforms, I can say I won't touch this game anymore, until Bernd stops stuffing coke up his nose, and gives us paying customers what we paid for.  My verdict: YOu don't always get what you paid for, especially now days, sometimes you get ripped off. Don't buy this game, and if you do plan on buying it, at least watch others play the game, and keep an eye out for the problems that people are finding with the game... Don't just look at what works, because it's the game breaking things they aren't telling you, thats gong to affect your overall enjoyment of this game!   So now I'm playing Space Engineers, an early release game, that receives an update every thursday, and a fixer patch for that update on fridays, it's a promising title by Keen Software, creator of Miner Wars, a game I haven't played, but I'm willing to give them a chance, because I really want Space Engineers to make it to Game Of The Year once they've completed it, but that part depends on them, and how much they listen to their customers, and the amount of effort they put into fixing the existing game crashes. I'm going to give them the same chance I gave Egosoft, and hopefully we won't get ripped off as Egosoft has done.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: The story is really good in my opinion. it has many new aspects in the game but still very much so stays to what made metal gear, metal gear. i do miss the long cut sense from MGS4 though to be honest but it still has rather long ones. Also, yes the open world maps are well made and full of bases with tons of guards but other than that there isn't really anything else out there. i wish there were a descent amount of civilians that can take away heroism and mission ranks if they get hurt or kill by you and different cars scatted around to make it seem more realistic. The movement system could use a little work as well. A fast crouch would be nice for when you need to go fast but stay low, and trying to turn around and crawl a facing a different detection can be confusing at times. but other than those things the game is flawless and i haven't seen anything even close to game braking. The new vice for big boss is very good as well, didn't even notice it was a different person till i watched this video. another very cool thing is that there are tons of secrets, unlockables and customization with weapons, vehicles, your base, a your lode out. just like other MGS game there is tons of content with over 50 main missions(some take up to a hour+) and around 150 side missions. i have 49 hours of game play with minimal messing around and I'm only 39% complete, and haven't really ran in to any bugs or unfair/poorly designed parts. everything just works. like i mentioned the game still plays like metal gear, if you wanna play it that way. the stealth mechanics from the prier games have been build up upon and are better than ever but if you want to run and gun through the next mission you can do that as well and it doesn't feel like you aren't suppose to. that being sad if you try to do only one or the other, its going to be quite challenging. stealthing everything takes practice, patience, and timing. while ramboing takes lucking and good aim. for me i like a mix of both which feels amazing and just works. Over all with the innovative game play, plot, setting, and customization of everything, makes this new Metal Gear Solid title feel right at home with the series and look like Game of the Year material. i would give MGS5 a 'Solid' 9/10 and say, 'yes it is defiantly worth buying for just the single player alone, maybe even more so when the online comes out.'

If you already own Dungeon Defenders 1, the price point for this game is exceedingly reasonable. Basically consider it a full reset, with new maps and several new features. Do you have to start over? Yeah, because of the new server-central architecture. But hey, the early levels were the fun levels anyway.  I got it when it was on sale (and the pre-owner discount stacked) so I paid $6 and am more than happy.  If you don't own Dungeon Defenders already, then this is the game you want instead. Dungeon Defenders is an epic combination of Tower Defense and Hack-and-Slash RPG and there are no other games in this genre that even come close. Sure, there's Sanctum, but its mechanics and defense building are so much less interesting, its weapons have less kick, there's fewer options, and oh yeah. The enemies have 100% immunity armor. Good luck sniping the weakspot!

Great idea\\atmosphere but not much fun to be had. Reasons for this are : 1. your ship is SO SLOW!. even with the best engine it will take you 15min to cross the map. 2. you cant travel far because fuel/supplies tether you to london (the outer ports either dont sell them or have jacked up unreasonable prices). So i hope you really like going back to the spawn point because you basically have to constantly for the sole reason of fuel/supplies which is just frustrating. 3. the quests are all esoteric fetch quests, hope you like pixel hunting every island on the map to find your random objectives. 4. Quests are mercilessly padded by making you wait between sections even if you have everything needed to complete the quest right now. 5. the ingame events are all auto resolved depending on stats (so you can outright fail a quest because it needs one of the stats you are not pumping, and if you try and pump them all you will just fail every test). 6. no overarching story or narrative at all. Literally all little side quests that quickly grow stale. 7. combat is pointless. all monsters drop the same garbage at low or high levels. At low levels its worth it to kill things at high levels just run past because not worth the trouble.  So yeah great idea but they just dropped the ball on this one.

Pros: - Great narrative, every single dialogue is voiced. - Good graphics for it's time. - Quests are interesting and rarely 'kill 10 boars and talk to this NPC' - Open world - AMAZING soundtracks - Not Pay2Win, there are EXP-boost scrolls in the store but the rest are cosmetics and mounts. - You can almost do any content in the world as soon as you start playing the game with the release of 'One Tamriel' which makes the world scale up/down to your level so that content is never too easy, too hard and you can always choose where you want to go. - Clean UI - Alot of key-bind customization - Great crafting system - You get to choose between 4 classes in the character creation screen, but you can play with any weapon you want to and build your character in whatever way you want much like the previous Elder Scrolls games. - Player housing coming in 2017.  Cons: - Combat is quite dull and sometimes feels very tedious. - Only 4 classes in the game. - Character customization, there are too few hairstyles and markings and such. - Extremely large download size (about 60GB)

review_text: My Grandfather smoked his whole life. I was about 10 years old when my mother said to him, 'If you ever want to see your grandchildren graduate, you have to stop immediately.'. Tears welled up in his eyes when he realized what exactly was at stake. He gave it up immediately. Three years later he died of lung cancer. It was really sad and destroyed me. My mother said to me- 'Don't ever smoke. Please don't put your family through what your Grandfather put us through.' I agreed. At 28, I have never touched a cigarette. I must say, I feel a very slight sense of regret for never having done it, because your game gave me cancer anyway.

The Moon Silver  is a walking simulator game with a story. It's not even a game at all, since you're just technically reading a story itself. The storyline can get quite confusing since it's not linear. In other words, you can freely proceed to the 'wrong' area and get the story messed up. Moreover, people have different interpretations on their own. The story is not narrative nor clear.   Graphics  seemed okay in proportion to its file size. Simple enough to be understood and seen properly. The scenarios will definitely set the tone. Barren lands and post-apocalyptic elements give you the feeling of desolation, desperation, and loneliness. Just what the mood of the game is all about.  The  Audio  and the music sounds a little bit off for me. Yes, it sets the tone and the ambiance of the story but it's a source of distraction. They also have scenes with dead air which is not totally needed. I'm actually expecting something big to happen during the dead air. Unfortunately, you just have to read again. Overall, I think the music needs a rework.  The flashlight mechanic is somewhat annoying. You must head to an outlet everytime you ran out of batteries. The element is not really needed since it's just a waste of time and doesn't even connect to the story.  If you're asking, 'Is it worth the $2.99?'  I say NO. It's better to read an online novel rather than to purchase this game; or support your local authors by buying their tangible books. To be honest, I got this game for FREE on Holiday 2014 Christmas Auction and it didn't cost me anything at all, just a thousand Steam gems to grab a copy of the game.  I got overwhelmed when I got this game. This game was already in my wishlist on September 2014. My expectations were so high. But none met my expectations, so I ended up getting disappointed badly.  Rating:  2/10   Disclaimer: Everything is purely based on my opinion. Thanks for reading!~
