Bought this for the cooperative mode. Turns out even the coop mode couldn't bring back any replay value. Is this is a broken game? Well, it's not entirely broken but it's not a good game either. I had 1 playthrough and I felt like deleting it off from my HDD. Unfortunately, I wanted to get Upoints from for completing all of the character's campaign. It was suffering since all of their campaign were pretty much playing the same levels again.  The good thing is that each character has their own perspective view. For instance, when your character is on the call, you can hear the conversation while others can't. This applies to coop mode where your friends can't hear what you're listening from your phone call. Let's get straight to the point.  The graphics is awful. It's very blurry. When you aim, anything apart from your center of your aiming scope gets blurred. Sometimes when the enemies are running around and your aim focused on the tree, everything gets blurry. It's hard to aim at the enemies. Using the directX 11 doesn't seems to have any difference. This game graphics is bad.  I gave the sound average is because of the voice acting. Anything else felt like low quality sound effects. Your gun sound effects doesn't feel powerful. The soundtrack isn't memorable. At least they tried to use back the cowboy soundtrack. Very weak in this department.  Gameplay is just a typical shooter game but feels very boring. You have multiple car chase, multiple gunfights and multiple enemy chasing on foot. Yes, they are repeating those same events through the 3 campaigns. It gets buggy like enemy pop out of no where. Enemies disappearing. The game gets broken where the event doesn't start. As for gunfight, you fight 4-5 enemies, then done, next area, 3-4 enemies, then move on, 3-4 enemies again. Very repetitive and very boring gunfight. Even in coop, it's nothing interesting about this except the challenge like who gets the 1st 5 headshots within the time limit. There are some other challenges but it's just competing with your friends That's it, nothing else. For public game, good luck finding one.  Story is about the corruptions. The only reason you want to replay is because you wanted to know everyone's perspective. There are multiple ending as well. I spent so many hours on this game is because of that and the U points. Nothing much to say about this.  I can't recommend this game as it is very weak. I got this game from promotion sale so I don't get much loss.  My Rating: Excellent Great Average Bad  Graphics: Bad Sound & music: Average Gameplay: Bad Story: Average

Slowly belly-crawling through the underbrush, I scan the distance for signs of enemy troops. I come to a sudden stop; inexplicably, an invisible wall blocks my forward progress. I crawl to the edge of the brush, frantically run across an exposed trail, and into the foliage to the left of the path, figuring that a different route might be in order, but I'm stymied again as I find myself stuck on a rock that raises only six inches off the ground. Finally, I find a way past these obstacles in the brush and silently arrive at my GPS designated destination marker, but my objective won't clear. I give up. Defeated, I walk back up the hill, walk down the path, which triggers a voiceover, which enables the GPS marker, which spawns enemies on the path ahead of me that kill me.  This is my experience with Sniper: Ghost Warrior in a nutshell.   6/10 Presentation Sniper is sandwiched between static menus and boring, grammatically challenged loading screens.  7.5/10 Graphics The vibrant, busy environments are striking with some great lighting, but awful character models and a shoddy framerate hurts the experience  6.5/10 Sound Boilerplate music and bad Latin American accents can't hide Nolan North slumming it in the jungle.  5.5/10 Gameplay The sniping mechanic is sound, but its undercut by terrible level design and unwieldy run and gun sequences.  7/10 Lasting Appeal The single player campaign will last about 12 hours, and multiplayer offers a decent variety of gametypes.

First of all: This Game Is Amazing.  Oh and besides, I'm sorry for any mistakes, I'm not a native speaker. And now a bit more detalied:  Rise of the Tomb Raider (~22 Hours of playtime) is a 3rd person adventure-action-roleplaygame, which is set in the actual time and world, but if you want to play in giant modern sitys I might recomment to look for another game. You're playing as Lara Croft, a young archaeologist, and seeking out to find that tresure, your father allways wanted to find. But you are not alone. As you arrived at youre destination, the problems are starting very soon. So far with the Story, wich is slightly less gripping as the one from 'Tomb Raider' but still quite good (my oppinion). There also are sidequests with it's own storyline and varied objectives.   Technically: It's up to you if you want do be like Rambo or prefer being stelth. The latter allows to get more xp. The way of accomplishing missiones has no impact on your story. Perspective of view is 3rd person, the camera driving is good. Graphically it's one of the best games I've played. Sharp textures, good moddeldesign and a phantastic randerer provides for a spectacular sceenery. Is it open-world? Well, technically you have 'acctionbubbles', areas in witch you can move and decide freely, but you can't switch these bubbles by walking only by a quick travel system. I'd say 'Yes it is!' because you can use that system when ever you want and not only for quests. There also is a crafting system, thich manly consists of collecting scrab (whitch is like coins) and upgrade your weapons. Sometimes you will be able to find some ancient coints, wich are used to by stuff in the black marked (weapons, atachments etc.) By unlocking objectives you can also unlock new armor for smal bonuses like better huntingskills or something. Leveling gives you skillpoints, which are your main source of progress. You use them to upgrade nearly everything (halth, killskills, perception). The game runs smoothly (I was the only one of my group of friends who had some framedrops while changing between cutscene and game, havn't figured out why yet). In the whole game I havn't experienced a single bug. A multiplayer exists but I've rarely played, so i do not want to tell my half-knowledge. With ~22h playtime - by looking arround carefully but not going for achivements ore something - it has a lot more content than its predecessor 'Tomb Raider'  Conclusion: A must buy! 9 out of 10.

(SPOILERS AHEAD IF YOU HAVEN'T PLAYED AT LEAST THE DEMO)  Now that Undertale is making the rounds on various end-of-year Top 10 lists, I figured it was time to share my experience with this game, and why, when I first played it, I would have given it a thumbs-down.  I picked up Undertale in October, after hearing Dodger gush about it on a recent episode of the Co-Optional Podcast. TotalBiscuit and his crew had previously turned me on to Final Fantasy Record Keeper and Life Is Strange, both of which have defined my year in gaming. So, somewhere about the middle of the month, I grab this game- as well as Davey Wreden's The Beginner's Guide- and sat down, ready to break me some fourth walls.  If you've played the Undertale demo, you've gone just as far- actually, a little further- than I did in my first attempt at a playthrough. I came in knowing its status as a Game What Judges You, and was further warned by its Steam summary- 'the RPG game where you don't have to destroy anyone.' So, I went in ready to cut a swath of love and peace that would make Vash the Stampede shed a tear.  Aside from one unfortunate dummy (thank you, brave soldier, you taught me that the 'FIGHT' button is very literal and not a way to access one's diplomacy skills), I behaved like a perfect gentleman through Toriel's guided tour of the Ruins. My stick remained but a rustic fashion accessory as I learned the strange, endearing ways of the monsters that lived amongst the puzzles and leaf-piles. I made an eyeball-monster's day by not picking on him. I complimented a ghost on its fashion sense. I gently respected the personal space of a large fly-like creature. It was a good day.   I eventually proceeded to the point where Toriel was standing guard at the doorway out of the Ruins, heartbroken yet steadfast in her refusal to let me escape to Snowdin and the realms beyond.  The problem was, I didn't want to leave.  I already knew about Flowey's save-reading tricks, and the game, up to this point, had only rewarded me for staying my hand in a conflict. Furthermore, the game had given me no specific reason why my young-child avatar had gotten lost in the mountains in the first place. Why so far from home? Is the realm of humans really a place that they, or I, even wanted to go back to? Especially with Goatmom here offering free room and board for as long as I liked? Sure, she was doting, to the point of being clingy. But, Undertale had challenged me to use the full force of my personal judgment, and that judgment was struggling to find any reason at all to turn down Toriel's generous offer.  Thus, while Toriel stood guard at the gate, waiting to answer a challenge I really didn't want to make, I kept sleeping in my new bed over and over again, thinking I would trigger a cutscene, or a Lavos-style non-standard ending. However, it seems that for all the player reactions I later found Toby Fox had prepared for, there was no acknowledgment of my attempt to actually take Toriel at her word, aside from a few anonymous exhortations of how I was the future of monsters. (Dude, I'm ten years old and hungry, go find another chosen one.)  At this point, I felt I was being railroaded- more so when I found no immediately obvious way to talk Toriel down without a fight. I knew that, even if I killed Toriel and then reloaded the save, that freaking flower- which I'd then mistaken to be the voice of the game creator- would be there to mock my cruelty and my cowardice, even though I was trying desperately to display neither.  So, to spite what I thought was my tormentor, I stopped playing. My story ended with my young-child self chatting with frogs, dancing with slime molds, and developing a taste for snails, with my new guardian hopefully taking the hint after a few days. (With all of the Undertale AUs floating around Tumblr, I sometimes wonder how ConscientiousObjectorTale would play out.)  I could have been a prick at this point and petitioned for a refund- but besides not seeing 'ideological disagreement' as worthy grounds to recover ten bucks, I still wanted Toby Fox to have my money. Even with my decision, I liked what he was attempting to do enough that I wanted to see what he would make next- perhaps something a little more elaborate, expanding on the theme of subverting the tropes and trappings of traditional RPGs.  Turns out, he did. And it's called 'Undertale'.  I stood by my choice, having felt the concept of a game sternly judging the player's actions was becoming old hat three years after Spec Ops: The Line. But I was curious enough to read ahead about the rest of the story, with no concern about spoiling myself on a game I felt I had finished playing. I didn't know who most of the characters were, so the plotline was a pretty massive knot of lore to disentangle, but I soon stumbled across a single fact that started a massive shift in my opinion of this game.  I had presumed Flowey to be the mocking voice of the game creator, shaming the player for having the audacity to fight and kill in a genre chiefly based around fighting and killing. On this, I was massively incorrect.   Flowey the flower's fourth-wall flouting is very, very much canon, and both the reasons why and the implications thereof are  horrifying .   This dandelion Deadpool was up to something, and in my search to find out exactly what it was, I began to truly understand the things that have endeared this game to so many people these past few months. I had turned tail and ran to dodge the shots of a satirical game developer, only to later find that his gun was never loaded and I had locked myself right out of his world of wit and wonder.  I'm still working on my own playthrough now, even though I now know everything that happens- in recent weeks, I've sought out as many different Let's Plays and Twitch streams as possible, just to see different people react to the mindfreak that is Undertale's endgame. It's late December now, and this has yet to get old. In addition, I've also read plenty of Undertale AU comics and fan theories, and bought both the original soundtrack and RichaadEB's cover album. Just over two months after I initially dismissed this game as a morality play at the expense of the player, I'm as fully absorbed in the world of Undertale as nearly all of its fans. I'm a bit saddened that I experienced Undertale's best moments second-hand, but I'm overjoyed that my initial cynicism about this game has been proven astonishingly wrong.  Congratulations, Toby Fox. Even on a meta-textual level, you found a way to subvert another player's expectations. Now, if you'll excuse me, I've got a bone to pick with a certain royal guardsman.

This game is a real classic. There are a wide variety of physics-based puzzles which become increasingly difficult as the player continues through the test chambers. Has a very interesting (and progressively darker) storyline that is loosely woven into the Half Life universe. Also features sarcastic AI narration that guides you through the test chambers and makes comments on your progress.  This is a very short game. (There are only nineteen test chambers, and the first twelve are really just a tutorial and training for the final more difficult ones.) Despite the game being so short, there are plenty of challenges. After completing the game all the way through twice, I was feeling pretty good about my performance. Then I tried the Advanced Test Chambers. At first, I performed depressing badly. After playing through nearly all of the the Advanced Test Chambers, I was once again feeling pretty good about my performance. Then I tried the Challenges. I performed depressing badly, unable to obtain even bronze medals for most of them. So, there is lots of replay value here if you are ready to dive in and really practice.  Thoroughly excellent game!

If you are a fan of visual novels, but have found yourself becoming sceptical of western forays into the genre, you will probably enjoy this game. Sepia Tears follows the classic 'Crying game' story progression that Key is famous for. The story was enjoyable with an unexpected twist, though I would have liked to see multiple endings.  The audio was good - except for occasionally obvious looping, and the lack of voice that is characteristic of independent VNs. Overall, the writing was witty and had a pleasant, conversational quality; though the pacing could have been better, as it occasionally lingered too long on the protaganist's train of thought, or rushed over scenes that could have used more exposition. The art was nice; however, it was clear that the artist was either still inexperienced, or was working with cheaper equipment - which is to be expected for a free game. My suggestion for the artists and writers would be merely to continue to hone your abilities to a more professional level.  I should also note that much of the humor in the game derives from references to anime and VN pop culture, some of which are quite esoteric; if you do not have extensive experience with other VNs, many of these references will likely go over your head.  All in all, Sepia Tears is a nice little visual novel, with a few hours of gameplay, and a touching romance. I will be hoping for more releases from this group, as I have certainly enjoyed this one.

I grew up playing Mahjong and I've always really loved it. This game is pretty fun so far.  UPDATE: The short review I made was for a 'mission' at the beginning of the game. Now that I've gotten all the achievements on the game I feel like it is time for a new review.   I quickly became addicted to this game and it became a part of my daily routine. Every morning I would get on this game to use the energy I had accumulated over night. I would also get on multiple times throughout the day, even if I only had enough energy to do one puzzle.   There were two things that I really hated about this game but, considering it is a free game, they are to be expected. The first is the way the energy works. Basically like a Facebook or mobile game where you have to wait for it to recharge. At first it was awful having to wait. Overtime, once I got used to it I really didn't mind too much and quickly found ways to make it less awful. The second thing I hated was that if you were doing a puzzle and ran out of moves, that was it. You just wasted energy on that puzzle for no reason. The only way to shuffle the pieces is if you have the right tool for it. Or by buying the tool with gems which you can pay for with real money or aquire a few upon leveling up. Eventually, I found the puzzles for each area that I could beat without messing up and just played those each time.  Overall, it's a decent game especially being free. I would recommend it for someone who wants something to kill a little bit of time each day. And someone who likes puzzles. There are other puzzles besides Mahjong.

Ground breaking with many new RTS concepts. It still remains best in class in comparison to other similar titles, for example Company of Heroes.   It is also very old, and even though the balance between the races isn't perfect, consider it a challenge. Mirroring the Warhammer universe quite well, the balance comes with skill. A new player could dominate with the Space Marines, but it takes true micro and macro mastery to properly handle the Eldar. I find this to be a bonus, since you can pick a weaker race when showing your friends the ropes.   Certain races are excellent counters against others. So DoW lends itself best towards massive team battles between multiple players. Thought 1v1 tends is thrilling, when playing with somone you know well, you will probably both Random. Since your opponent can't see what you have randomed until they scout you. Meaning they can't preempt and counter your most efficient strategy.  This game still remains my all time favorite in the genre, and I love this genre.

This is a fun, 2D, gravity based, open world, physics game. It reminds me a bit of Osmos, in that you can absorb smaller items to become larger. However, this game is less structured than Osmos, in that the game isn't divided into levels (not a bad thing). Different activities are available at the different size levels (asteroid, planet, star, black hole). It also has the added twist that your planet(s) can 'evolve' life once they become large enough, and it's great fun to see a fleet of friendly ships defending your solar system from an enemy fleet (though you have no control over the ships, other than that they try to stay near their planet(s).  The player may choose to play in a calm, relaxed, explore the universe manner. Or they can try and tackle all the missions and achievements for a greater challenge.  Overall, I enjoyed playing this game, and would certainly recommend it to others.

Simply put, this game it not worth 60 dollars. Once it has been out for a year, I would recommend anyone to buy it. The problem is that every new chapter I became more and more dissapointed with missed opportunities. I love that this game has lazer weapons, but the fact that you can sit behind a wall next to an outlet and just kill everyone hurts this game. I wanted to be rushing around shooting people with double assualt rifles, but why when I can just use an infinite ammo sniper? This is just one way the game messes up your power fantasy. I wanted to be on a bridge and snipe hoards of people running at me, I wanted to shoot nazis on the moon with super no-gravity jumps. Bridge to moon might seem like a far away comparison, but they are literally only one cutscene away. This game just skips so much over. The levels start to feel like a task because they aren't connected in any way. The story was pretty good, but its been a few days and I haven't thought about it, so the effect wasn't lasting or powerul. Also the texture quality is pretty bad, dual wielding weapons blocks half your screen, and you can't disable depth of field. If it goes on sale, buy it, there were some fun experiences in it, and could be considered a step in the right direction. Health and armor systems are awesome, you may start to question yourself (should have been expanded), and most of the guns are fun.

I initially gave this game my 'Pile of steaming ♥♥♥♥' rating, due to being an obvious console port, buggy/crashy, a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ 'social' connection/activation process, as well as basically being unplayable using dx9.  All of the above is still true and accurate, but if you actually manage to play the game, it's actually quite a good storytelling experience from a pacing and delivery perspective. It also helps that it's basically a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ for fans of Michael Mann movies and style.  I was a little disappointed in the departure from the film noir presentation (from the previous games), but that's not that big a deal, as in many ways, this is spiritually a modern film noir. I DO have to ♥♥♥♥♥ about the CONSTANT & obnoxious visual effects to try to convey Max's alcoholism and drug addiction, as it's bad enough that it's nearly a dealbreaker.  The only other thing that needs to be mentioned, is this game could have been called 'Cutscene 3: Rockstar Wants To Make Movies', and while it does add a certain stylistic element to the game that actually adds to the Mann-style presentation, I'm torn by how annoying it gets.  This game could have been ♥♥♥♥ing awesome, but instead, it falls squarely in the 'pretty good' column, mostly because of what I mentioned in the first paragraph. And the fact that it's 3rd person, but if you're a Max Payne fan, you knew that going into it, so I can't really hold that against it.  From the perspective of the series, this is very much a Max Payne story. Max's soul and tortured existance comes through exactly the way I would expect from a third installment, and is completely believable that this is where he ends up.   All in all, it's a good game, despite it's problems.

Do yourself a favor and don't pick up this game. ESPECIALLY if you're a fan of the first 2 games in the series. Disclaimer: I have not finished the game, despite trying repeatedly over the course of about a year. I just couldn't enjoy it.  Pros: -Nice visuals, such as skin falling off enemies. -Solid shooting mechanics -Can get pretty spoopy  Cons: -Enemies, weapons, everything has been stripped from the previous games and replaced with very generic stuff. -Piggybacking on the above comment, the setting and pacing of the game do NOT fit in the franchise. -Terrible A.I. You'll run into two basic enemies, Armacham grunts who aren't a challenge even on the hardest difficulty, and what are essentially very poorly done zombies. Sometimes they have bombs strapped to their chests. The A.I do not react to gunfire or anything. You do fight these soldiers that are electrified? And they somehow shoot electricity and spawn more soldiers? I don't ♥♥♥♥ing know. The mechs are insanely easy to fight, just take cover and spam since they pretty much only use machine guns. -Regenerating health and endless amounts of ammo, effecting the pace of the game -Absolute ♥♥♥♥-tier story. Honestly feels like it was written by a child. Fettel and Pointman are a joke, you don't feel nearly as connected with either as you did with F.E.A.R 2's protagonist, or even Pointman himself in the first game. -Dead multiplayer -RPG elements CONSTANTLY popping up on your screen. 'LEVEL UP!' 'FASTER RELOAD!' 'DOUBLE XP!' 'FIND THE BODIES FOR MORE XP!'. It's absolutely ridiculous how intrusive it is.  Overall it just feels like one of the most generic games I've ever played, and definitely one of the least fitting in its own franchise. It's a horrible disappointment. If you loved the first two games, just pretend this doesn't exist. It's what I'm desperately trying to do.  Also, I know I constantly compare it to the other two games. If you haven't played the others, play them. If you want to play this on its own, buy it for less than 5 dollars, but don't buy it full price. It isn't fun, and it's definitely not worth putting on your backlog.

While it's the very best space shooter I've ever played, it is also a huge grind-fest. The only way to make this game worth your time is if you keep up a license subscription, and take advantage of the various sales that happen about each week or so, including buying the DLCs that give permanent boosts when they are on sale. If you do this, then you’ll really enjoy the game and the grind will be tolerable, otherwise you’ll want to ship this one. So while it is indeed “Free to Play”, you won’t really get anywhere with the game or really be enjoying for long without spending some real money on it, but if you do, you’ll be rewarded with a pretty awesome game, that’s only getting better and better over time so far. The game devs are generally very responsive to the community complaints and requests. Unlike many other game companies out there, this one actually cares about the success of its game here, so it’s one worth supporting.

Set Phasers to FUN! Just kidding, that was lame. But seriously, this game is awesome. Random encounters ensure a variety of playing experiences, and your own decisions can dictate how well you perform. You do not control the ship itself, but the crew; ordering them to different sections of the ship, and really only controlling the targetting for weapons systems. A single playthrough is about 40-60 minutes, depending on your strategy. I've had playthroughs where I got the the 'boss' with only basic weaponry and a fully upgraded ship, and other times I was so overpowered that I heated up my replicator-delivered coffe with his photon torpedos and laughed maniaclly at his feeble attempts to break through my shields. Alas, no sexy half machine goddesses.

Very good game, with a strong SP campaign, and very enjoyable multiiplayer modes.  I wish the SP campaign were a bit longer, and the story was more fleshed out. In some of the settings/environments, there were deeper stories which were glossed over and finer details ignored. I would have liked to spend time with some of the characters more....but they just got one level, and then you moved onto another TF. It played kind of like a quick survey course in Transformers. Lost in the immediacy and the hectic action thriller pace of the plot were the deeper questions of where Metroplex came from, why was the planet Cybertron was dying, and what Megatron was really after?  Those things are never clear though...and thus the motivations of the plot itself are not so clear. It seems like the set pieces are there to tell a deeper tale....but the game had to be rushed to market, and lot was glossed over.   All you know from the getgo is that this is the time when the Autobots and Decepticons take their leave from Cybertron, and will (ostensibly) end up on Earth.  I'm one of those fans that has always felt that the best Transformer stories were the ones that took place on Cybertron....where you got a little bit of Cybertronian cultural history to go with your usual plot McGuffin Device. This game had a similar opportunity, and did a good job with it, but it would have been much nicer if the treatment just had a bit more depth. How about a subplot with Vector Sigma, or some information about Primus and or Unicron?   On Earth...things kind of get meh and stale. There's only so many power stations and oil refinery raids you can stop...and if you're a fan of G1's first couple seasons, you tend to see them running out of stories eventually by the end of Season 2.

This game sucks so much. I bought it new for XBOX 360 when it came out and, just like Dead Island, I got rid of it immediately.  Unlike Dead Island the PC version in the future is no better. The FOV is that of a concussed duckling, requiring constant and bizarre corrections to do things like pick up ammo, and accidentally climb a ladder while trying to pick up ammo.  If you want to interact with a breakable piece of the environment while trying to kick an enemy, RE6 is the game of your dream. The stupid camera design, and scrunched FOV, really shine when you get autocamera'd into kicking a crate while trying to melee your way out of a desperate struggle and can't cancel the glacial animation.  To cap off this hot ♥♥♥♥ mess, a patch has made it so some users have audio play independently of video during cutscenes, ruining what could have been a bright spot for this crunchy snoozer.   I'm not sure but it looks like some enemies are still immune to damage while in their 'react-to-being-shot' animation.  4/10, will wait for Capcom to Tomb Raider 2013 this soup sandwich of a franchise.

tl;dr included  For the record this was gifted to me by a friend and had it not been, I wouldn't have finished more than about an hour of it....I would've been wrong in doing so as playing through all 4 campaigns with said friend was able to shine some light on just how TRULY tedious, exhausting, nonsensical, ugly, monotonous, cheesy and haunting of a game it really is.  I don't even know where to start with this abomination of a game. This game seems to be a congregation of promising ideas with the most abysmal implementation known to the video game industry. Apparently no one could decide if they wanted an interactive movie or another true installment into the Resident Evil franchise and just said '♥♥♥♥ it' morphing whatever they could think of into a tangled mess of a game. Don't get me wrong Id love to like this game. Its nothing short of a neat concept to have 4 seperate stories take place all at once ultimately ending in one cohesive vision of action and suspense.  So I'll start with the positives in this game, it loads. Well...no not really on several occasions there were times when trying to join a co-op session cause the game to lag horribly unless the creator quits and restarts the lobby. And alt tabbing would inexplicably cause the framerate to lag without and indication of going back to normal.  Ok so whats wrong with the game? Everything. A barage of bullet sponge enemies acompanied with 30 second moments of action inbetween pointless cutscenes only to immediately lead into another part of the story making me wonder why the game included it as gameplay rather than just an entire cutscene. (seriously the chapters felt about as shattered as Lost Planet 2) The camera and controls were pretty pathetic, taking the gameplay many steps back instead of progressing into something refreshing. Not to mention something simple like rebinding sidemouse buttons won't work as the game doesn't even recognize them. You'll often be thrown to the ground or flung about being disorientated forcing you to trudge through parts of the game. Which would be fun if the gunplay didnt feel like shooting scarecrows. You can't be tactical or skillful in encounters. Headshots are a hit and miss for either killing the enemy or just triggering a transformation. Thus your best bet is to either unload as many clips as possible and hope you can tell when theyre dead (a health bar honestly wouldve done wonders for this game) or try to incapacitate with a single shot and use melee (which you are limited on and eventually get slowed down if you run out of stamina) and just hope the enemy doesnt land a cheap shot before youre able to thus flinging you about some more...becoming incapacitated yet again and....do you see the pattern yet?  Lets not forget the muddled story. Breaking it off into 4 segments wouldve been nice if it wasnt filled with the same chore missions/enemies throughout several campaigns instead of being done and over with it and if they had made SOME effort to bring any suspense to the story at all. 0 build up. Get guns, shoot things. Thats it, theres some garbage character development love story in one of the campaigns and nonsense drama (you killed my dad i dunno how to deal with that ok im better now we're friends lets save the world) (uhoh my evil clone is doing ♥♥♥♥ better not let anyone in on that so they all want to kill me because im mysterious and cant break character by using my ♥♥♥♥ING brain)   Lets not forget that the graphics have gone NOWHERE. Given that capcom games fundamentally focus on gameplay and story I was prepared for that. However this games story and gameplay were drivel so it would've been nice if the engine had actually changed or at least looked better than the previous title. Youre left romping about an ugly world (not for the sake of atmosphere, it looks more like lack of effort) with poor contrast and an overall muddy template.   Last but not least Id like to mention that playing number 2 to ada wong in the 4th campaign had me feeling more useless than watching someone else play solo. You magically teleport when she grapples, you cant pick anything up, you can't open doors or interact with anything, and you start with less weapons. Did I mention theres a good 5-10 minutes when youre in a helicopter doing absolutely nothing because the person playing ada is driving and you can only shoot straight ahead, which wouldve been fine if for some arbitrary reason my controls on my half of the weapons systems werent locked only to the right side, thus making it impossible to aim.  tl;dr this game is a travesty and massive blemish on the RE franchise. It shows you how to do everything wrong. I dunno how they got it funded but I had more fun writing this hateful review than playing the game. Anyways heres wonderwall

Oblivion, the prequal to Skyrim and the sequal to Morrowind, released 20th of March 2006 was the fourth installment of The Elder Scrolls franchise.  There are things i think that Oblivion did better than skyrim; 1. The class system was better, you chose your star sign right at the start NOT JUST THE THIEF, MAGE AND WARRIOR, all of them, your attributes and class was chosen right at the beginning of the game as well as the star signs.  2. I thought that the magica system in the game was better as you could use the restoration or any destruction spell whilst wielding your weapon instead of having to unequip your weapon and equip the spell.  3. I overall think that the main questline for Oblivion was better, as i spent a good 20 hours on it compared to when I completed Skyrim's questline at just 5 hours. The questline of Oblivion was also much more diverse and detailed compared to slaying Alduin which is nearly exactly as difficult to take down as a normal dragon. I found defeating Mankar Camoran was quite the challange  I'd just like to add that i totally prefur Skyrim over Oblivion just because there is much more to do in the way of exploring and the guild questlines were better in my opinion.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: I generally don't write any recommendations for games I buy, but I was pleasantly surprised by this game. It's not a long play, it took me just a hair over three hours, but it was a great experience the whole way through. The visuals are beautiful, the art style is fantastic, and the music alone made the purchase worth it. I did have a bug that halted my progress for a bit, but I feel I can overlook it. Brothers is the last new game I'll play for 2013, but it's one of the best I touched all year long.

Interesting game, but not worth the money or time. The entire game / content can be absorbed in about 15 minutes, as there are only a few playable characters and moves are simply combinations of other moves. If you have a group of hard-core fighting game friends you may still be bored out of your mind after a few play sessions, as there is simply not enough things to do to keep playing the game. To compare, Soul Calibur has 20+ characters each with dozens of unique moves.  To make matters worse the game is entirely multiplayer. There is absolutely no single player content or anything to do by yourself. The 'practice' mode is not even helpful. Compare to Soul Calibur, you have multiple modes ot try things out and learn against AI opponents. This game gives you one randomly spawned AI character.  Being only multiplayer means you will be subject to ping issues, trolls, server disconnect/lag, and in general you will find yourself spending a lot of time watching other people fight, finding maps, or swtiching between levels. A common session is start playing, join a server, then wait for a while to play. Right before your turn the server changes maps.

A rather good singleplayer experience in the vein of classic Diablo. Surprisingly polished for a free-to-play title, I especially like the perk web and skill gems compared to static skill trees, and the NPCs actually have sensible things to say - at least for your first go around.  No payment necessary to play and advance, as all paid content is cosmetics, which I think is rather fair.  The multiplayer is okay, but PvE gets dull fast, as there is not enough variety to pose a challenge and the skills that are fun in singleplayer quickly turn into overpowered steamrollers in multiplayer. Bosses are are a little limited for a modern game, very few different stages and either the fight is over fast or gets protracted. The lenient respawn system alleviates frustration.  I would definitely recommend the game for anyone into hack'n'slash, as it is free and of decent quality.

Love this game! Yes, it's not as jump-out-of-your-seat scary as it's predecessor, but it's certainly terrifying nontheless, mentally if not visually. This game gets a lot of flak for removing some of the gameplay elements present in the original game, but isn't this why people praised the original game? Getting rid of unneccessary gameplay mechanics commonplace in the modern horror genre?   People shouldn't have expected much else knowing the game was developed by thechineseroom, who took their own approach to the franchise by emphasizing story rather than gameplay. Yes this is a different experience and yes sometimes it's not as visually terrifying as a title such as Outlast or the original Amnesia, but you can't confuse 'Different' with 'Worse' to paraphrase rockpapershotgun's review. This game has a noticeably different emphasis on telling a damn good story (which is way better than the first's) and so you should expect the gameplay to reflect this ideology.   TL;DR It's more like Dear Esther than Amnesia: the Dark Descent, but only in the best possible ways.

While it might not be a game for everyone, the multiple routes (albeit, drasitcally simplified from the first game), charming characters, and unfolding story are worth a look for people interested in visual novels.  I personally love the level of immersion that the devolopers aspire to, forcing you to wait 12 real-time hours between days. While 'annoying' if you're just trying to get through the game, it also lets you feel like you really are taking on the role of a detective, traveling through space waiting to get to earth over the course of 3 days.

I don't normally write serious reviews about games. However, this game really took me by surprise.  When I bought Crime and Punishments I figured I was getting myself into another detective game with repeating puzzles. C&P's puzzles do sometimes look very similar, but overall they are actually quite good. From that description you would think it is, in fact, just 'another detective game'. However, it is far from the normal. What sets it apart from other games in the genre is the story. In fact, the game is so reliant on story that you are able to skip the puzzle minigames altogether.   C&P envelops you in every case. Through the clue connection system in the game a simple case turns into a complex story of twists and turns. It allows you to fully understand the suspects and make decisions based on hard evidence and morals.   Really its the best detective game I have played and I recommend it to anyone who has an interest in mystery adventure games.

Shelter is an intriguing concept the developer neglected to actually construct a game around.  The mechanics are incredibly simple and never build in any way, such that any given minute of the game is virtually indistinguishable from any other minute. Go forward. Grab food. Occassionally go fast or go slow to avoid some hazard. There is no particular goal, no score kept, and no story, so there is no sense of progression or urgency.  Your single goal is to keep the badger cubs alive, but they have no personality, no narrative surrounding them, and there is no penalty if they die, so there is no emotional connection to them: You won't really care if they die or not.  I don't know how long the game is, because I didn't finish it, but however long it is, it's too long, because the game's charm wears off after about 15 minutes, and once the charm is gone, there is no reason to keep playing.

The ground battles are fun enough, reminds me of a slimmed down and less grindy (thankfully) version of the old star wars battle front games. space combat is pretty fun, fast enough to not be dull and not to slow and grindy to be boring, having an equipment screen and more weapons and gear is nice, adds a little more flavour to fighting, same with kitting out your ship. To the creator/s nice job and good luck with future games.   in all its a good fun, decent game, thats well worth a look at and defo worth a play

I really really really like this game. It has the same 'fun' factor as Super Mario Bros. 3. It's a 2D schmup platformer with a rewind mechanic. It saves your previous iterations to fight along side of. This creates a sort of single player co-op mechanics which takes trail and error, planning ahead, and lots and lots of bullets.  Lots of replay-ability, in my opinion, because it adds an 'Ultra Force' as well as a Super Hardcore mode upon completing it the first time. There are also a TON of unlockables.  It's totally subjective, but I also adore the visual style and the music. The story is ... silly and predictable, but no one's here for a compelling plot, so who cares? Great game, worth every penny. Enjoy!

Best $2 you'll ever spend.  The game's current conclusion (Void Update) is not very satisfying as it feels incomplete. But the journey is why you should play this game. Additionally, there's heavy implication that more content will be added at a later time.  The game is a blast to play. The mechanics are top notch. The visuals are brilliant. And the best part? You will die a lot in this game.  That's right dying is the best part. Death taunts you. Laughs at you. Enrages you. Beckons you to try again. And you do, and you get further than last time. Then you die again. But you know. You know that you can conquer death. And after many deaths, you succeed. You never doubted yourself, and neither did he. This is one of the few adventure/sandbox games that I found frustrating to play. With lack of proper tutorial or instruction, this game is pretty hard to play by jumping right into it. I did end up exiting, and didn't go back. I will probably find my way back to this game in the future, kicking and screaming.  I'm not going to recommend this game, for several reasons:  1. This game is tough to understand. That's a subjective statement, but I will stand by it; it plays like a game trying to be more than it actually is. Why complicate the game with things that it really doesn't need? And why push it all on a new player at once? The menus are atrocious and simply not conducive to someone trying to play the game for the first time.  2. This game will likely never be fixed or revamped. The latest developer news included this passage: 'Piracy is buggy and the internal code is messy, it'd take a whole new code base to FIX it, and. While we can never remake it, look out for Q1 2016, but that's a different piece of news for a different moment [...]'. I'm not a developer, and don't claim to know what they're doing, but it sounds like they're dumping Pixel Piracy for a new game they're developing: Pixel Privateers.  I bought this game while it was in Early Access, hoping it would amount to something. It didn't, and I believe it's just another excuse to point and scream at the Early Access system, ignoring the fact that this game doesn't feel planned at all, and just a collection of random ideas thrown into a game by three people.  Is this game worth it, to the objective buyer? I would say no. You're better off saving your money, and scoping out their new game. Now that they've had what I will deem a flop, they have no excuses, barring acts of god, to drop the ball again. And with their new game being in the same style, with the same concept (Pirates/Sea Adventure), the expectations are even higher.

“Digital happiness has done right job what horror game should really be in recent years.”  I’m pretty sure Dreadout is compared to Project Zero/Fatal Frame, but Dreadout shines in its own style. The story begins with a group of student trying to investigate an abandoned village. What I really like about Dreadout is how the atmosphere changes from daydream to nightmare.  Dreadout presents thick and haunting atmospheres. It feels dark, mysterious, and creepy everytime you take step into each area with great ambient soundtracks. The whole story encourages player to think and find reference from dialogues and notes. There is not much connection between Linda and side characters which make storyline feels pretty decent.  The solid part of Dreadout is gameplay as Linda can use her sense ability to discover item, puzzle, and incoming ghost. The way of game design and how you adapt your camera function to apply in each puzzle and ghost fight are genius and well executed. Most ghosts are both scary and fun to fight due to well-written script and how they behave to player in each encounter.  The graphics and animation are decent and some textures are too blurry for presentation. Moreover, the game has some gameplay glitch and poor optimization for some users like dropped framerate in school area. But I’m sure it can be easily fixed in future patch.  Dreadout surprises me in a lot of horror and gameplay aspects. It has variety and strong setup even the content is quite short. And the end holds me more questions, but for me it gives such a memorable experience which I hardly taste from other horror games in past few years.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: GROUND ZEROES
review_text: I was expecting very little. I was well aware of how short that the main story mission was and that it could be finished within a few hours, tjough far less for those who are more run and gun, less of a perfectionist than I. Still, I was pleasantly surprised by how much more that there was.  Even ignoring the POW rescues and collections in the main mission (the latter of which would prevent the unlocking of a side op), completion of the core mission unlocks side ops, which are basically extra missions for those of you who are like me and care not for jargon. These missions take place in the same map, but with new objectives and variables that require different thinking or play. Well made, and I certainly was not expecting to see them, even less to enjoy them to any extent.  The visuals are very well done, and the framerate remained smooth throughout. Some very good looking rain helped to set the mood (and was missed in the daytime side ops).  The sounds were great with a good set of headphones attached. Listening for footsteps was a joy, as well as other audio cues that would give hints or identify incoming danger.  I loved the gameplay of the core campaign the mist. Multiple avenues of approach, side objectives that had to be discovered, and plenty of room for exploration. Perhaps my only disappointment here was that the subsequent side ops were somewhat too focussed at times, but this was more preference than a real problem.  Note that I purchased this on sale at $5, so my opinion may be colored by that.

app_name: STAR WARS™: Knights of the Old Republic™
review_text: This is one of the best star wars games and is an amazing rpg with dice roll combat. It also does one thing better than any other star wars product be it movie, game or book out there. It is the only time i have seen the dark side of the force explored so heavily, for once they are not just the bad guys they have their own belief and value systems as well as history and artifacts to explore. This game only adds to and deepens an allready vast universe. And to top it all off it has a very enthralling story as well as an interesting cast of original characters.  Although to be honest by far the best part is the senseless slaughter of all who oppose you. No seriously murder is very rarely not an option. Now theres a lesson fo the kids.

First off let me say I reccomend this game to anyone that loves (or even remotley enjoys) Shadow Warrior, Borderlands, Diablo, and other games of that type. This game is a very serious departure from the Shadow Warrior Reboot, it has a loot system, hub/open world gameplay, side missions, and much more. Even more so than the reboot it has fantastic RPG elements, with a very dynamic weapon customization system that is very similar to armor and weapon socketing, if you are familiar with the Diablo series. It implements all these new elements while still keeping gameplay fresh and addicting. You discover COUNTLESS weapons that are divided into categories, each of them you can socket with elemental effects, and many other bonuses. From here on out I will divide into different elements that I will mention.  Gameplay: The gameplay is great, it has a very familiar feeling to the Shadow Warrior reboot. The guns are AWESOME in this, they have absoloutley improved the gunplay and swordplay in Shadow Warrior 2. One of my favorite new additions is the GLORIOUS movement system!! The dashing and double jumping is great, the devs did a stellar job making you feel like a true ninja!  Loot system: The loot system will feel very familiar to Borderlands, and even that of Diablo in many ways. Most of the loot you will find from regular enemies includes money, gems (to use for socketing), and of course ammo. You will also find lots of loot in chests, you will defintiley want to search around to find ammo and money, certain types of ammo (medium bullets) I have found to hard to find. When you fight elite enemies, they will commonly drop weapons, whether they drop swords, chainsaws, assualt rifles, energy pistols, revolvers and everyting else, you will feel very rewarded, which to me is crucial for a good loot system. You will also, get plenty of weapons from mission rewards when you complete a main or side quest.  Levels: As you have probably heard, Shadow Warrior 2 has randomly generated levels. This sounds like a great idea, and for the most part it is, the designs of the enviroments are beautiful and very real to the franchise. HOWEVER...becasue the levels are randomly generated you can go a long time without finding many enemies. Sometimes you will be searching for a good five minutes and not find enemies. This is especially true when you travel off the beaten path sometimes.  Weapons: The weapons in this are again awesome, there are so many guns its hard to count. I love it becasue it can be so hard to choose between different weapons to have in your quickslots! No matter what the gun is, it will feel unique, and there will be nothing else like it. The melee weapons are all awesome!!! Even though they are all melee they feel very distinct from one another, which is greatly appreciated. You will absoloutley adore the weapons in ShadowWarrior 2.  Co-op: One of the flagships in marketing in this game is the long awaited co-op campaign. Co-op is a blast with some friends, however I did notice a few difficulty spikes in co-op, we would shred through all the smaller enemies if you in a matter of 10 seconds, but when an elite enemy cames it gets *very* hard sometimes. Be warned though if you progress ahead of your own single player game in a friends game it WILL NOT regognize you completed the quests you did in the other person's game, and you will be at the same place in your single player. However it will save all of the loot you got which is nice. I highly reccomend co-op mode!  Story and Dialouge: Lo Wang and his array of ♥♥♥♥♥ jokes are back and beter than ever. His *sparkling* personality has not changed at all, and he is just a funny as ever, if you don't mind vulgar humor you will love Lo Wang! The story is sort of 'meh...', it doesn't offer much, but keep in mind the game is not about the story. All in all the diaglouge, and jokes are awesome!   Pros: -Great gameplay, its over-the-top action you will love! -WEAPONS ARE GREAT 'nuff said -Movement system feels awesome and is a very worthwil addition to the game -Minimap, much appreciated becasue it was E-Z to get lost in the reboot.  -Lo Wang and his hilarious personality, and sense of humor. -Stunnning graphics of weapons and grisly gore! -Enviroments are nice -AMAZING Borderland-ish loot system that keeps on giving!  Cons: -Story could have been a lot better -Lack of enemies in almost all the levels -Sometimes there will be backtracking to certain locations -Co-op is ok. Not as good as I expected   Final Score: A solid 8.5! Great game, will keep you on the edge of your seat for hours on end!!

-Started game. -Chose play as survivor mode. -Dwight for the win. -Followed the killer. -Killer noticed me. -Chased me like the chicken I am. -Placed me on meat hook. -Proceeded to watch me bleed until death 10/10 would get hooked again  It's like a giant game of hide and seek except once the seeker finds you they require a blood sacrifice. I've played over 100 hours so far and it definitely has a learning curve in the beginning. As the survivor you want to power 5 generators if you start the game with a full lobby to ultimately provide power to the exit gates and escape the killer that is hunting you the whole time. Any mistakes you make such as failing a skill check on the generators will provide a visual notification to the killer allowing them to find your position. This creates a constant rush of adrenaline ( at least for me) because you never know where the killer is or when they are going to show up and smite your ♥♥♥.  As the killer your one and only goal is to find every last sorry soul in the game and hook them until they bleed to death and ultimately feed the one thing that drives you. The entity. Of course the one downside to playing the killer is when you get into the higher ranks and you come across the players that abuse pallet looping. That is until you become experienced enough to realise that it is better to leave them when this happens and hunt easier prey in the meantime only coming back for them when everyone else has been sacrificed.  This game will provide hours upon hours of entertainment to anyone that is interested even slightly in the genre and I highly recommend it to anyone that wants to be killed over and over again to their hearts content.

Just a casual game. If you like simple clicking profit games like adventure capitalist, you really should buy the oil blue. Definitely a good choice then. Even better actually. But if you are looking for interesting gameplay, ignore this game.   [-] You just use a couple of machines simultaneously by clicking their buttons at the right time to collect oil. There is not much of controlling something, mostly. You keep an eye on the machines wear and sell the oil on the market and repair the machines by doing a simple minigame. Thats all the gameplay has to offer -.-  Really sad  [+] because the game has (had) a huge potential for being well designed. [+] It is authentic in a postmodern world, the story is easy but good. [+] The machines relive the old and heavy characteristic and the sound of their buttons work nicely [+] good athmosphere  [-] but for clicking again you have to wait for the buttons to cooldown which can be a real pain because time is limited. I dont know what they were thinking. Thats not exciting or stressing, it is only annoying.  [-] no business or managing features at all. Just sell all the oil and receive money. Who cares how much money when you just end up gaining more and more. A mission can sometimes be about not making oil but making money in a specific time, which means you have to gain more money... whaaaatever. Just gaining by clicking. On every level.  [-] You level up in this game. I see that it is about your career ladder. Leveling up in the first mission like 15 times or whatever to improve your royalty and income. You can use money to level up machines, btw. They also level up by being used. Like 10 levelups for the machines each day. Increases their stats. Makes more money which the game makes you not even care about.  The only thing a professional oil blue player would think about is finding patterns of using the machines very efficiently and selling the oil for a good price on the market to maximize the profit. But i dont find that interesting.

I bought this game because I'm a big fan of PewDiePie, but... The gameplay is weird, especially jumping mechanics. There are times when you jump around enemy like crazy, and can't hit them. Characters tend to slide off platforms, they might also jump and reach the platform, but somehow not land on it (and fall to their death in most cases). This happened especially on holiday maps (Halloween, Valentine's Day and so on) Also, the gameplay is repetitive, you go from place to place, kill the boss after few arenas, and so on. Level designs are ok, but could be better. Artstyle and music are really nice. Voice acting is decent, I believe Markiplier did the best job. Extra note: I didn't play in Polish (I switched to English) but I can see why people are complaining about translation, looking only at achievements section. It's both bad and cringy. In the end, I give this game solid 4/10. It's not the worst, but could be (and should be) a lot better.

I'm mostly into the creative side of Gmod but that's not the only thing you can do in Gmod where with Sandbox you can make funny poses, faces or even messing around with the fingers, You spawn enemies, allies to cause a war for your own kinds of fun. But what can you do with the resources at your disposal? Don't have anything that you need? That's where the Steam Workshop part comes in! However You may need to download certain games in the Steam Library to get some things working. Most notable Counter Strike and all of the Half Life Games to avoid Texture errors. [that's how it went for me]  However there are good gametypes you can try out though some have their downsides. But here's what I know to those who seek to buy the game.  Trouble in Terrorist Town: A fun game of sorts where there are three types of teams. The harmless and yet still carry guns innocents, The detective who is after the main badguy that is known as the traitor: There are ways to tell if one is a traitor or not depending on what kind of weapon does he have that most others can not have? Very addicting game, my first look into the series, and a first look into the genre even.  +Charming characters +Nice soundtrack +Plays well, didn't take too long to figure out how the combat works, though there are some intricacies that you probably wont pick up until later +Dual Audio (Japanese/English) for people who might want to hear the original voices  +/- Grindy, not for people who want to wing it through a game without some grind +/- Not really a complaint just be sure to save often it doesn't auto save, only pops up a save option at the end of chapters  -The mouse being always active unless disabled is annoying, if your mouse hovers over an enemy it will always try to target that even if you only use keyboard for everything -Unplugging or changing your audio source will crash the game 100% of the time, at least for me it always does. -Apparently you could skip animations in some versions of this game, but not in this one; some of the animations are really quite long so I would like to see this added

Playing IMSCARED was an interesting experience. Although the game is very short (3 hours at best if you're looking to find all the secrets) it's filled with nice and never seen before ideas. The whole concept of playing with the game as an entity instead of just playing the role of a character inside of it is amusing and very well executed. Don't expect to find a real 'plot' here, because what you'll be doing will just be...playing with the game. Literally. You'll understand more only if you'll try it yourself. Here I can just say that it's truly something unique and worth to try, even if you're not the kind of person who enjoys the so-called 'walking simulators'. I am not, just to say.  The only real flaw that I can find in this title considering the fact that has been developed by a single person resides in its graphics: I understand that the pixelated style is intentional, but a 256x144 resolution is really ridicolous. 480p should be considered the minimum for decency in this kind of games, going lower than that just makes everything look worse than it should. Aside of this, I think that IMSCARED is a little gem that delivers an experience that requires thinking without being pretentious or excessively cryptic, and that keeps a substancial coherence with its premises. Which alone is, in these times, something more valuable than everything else.

8/10 ITS LIKE A KFC FAMOUS BOWL (MANY OBSCURE INGREDIENTS STUFFED INTO ONE PACKAGE BUT ONCE ONE BITE IS TAKEN, YOU'LL BE BEGGING FOR MORE)  PROS: - great story  - great gameplay - crisp graphics - short campaign (depending on your perspective)  CONS: - no replay value - short campaign (depending on your perspective) - cliffhanger ending - sequels most likely not happen  BELOW IS MY PERSONAL OPINION ON WARHAMMER 40K SPACE MARINE, READ IT IF YOU HAVE TIME! I have played many shooters in my day across many different platforms and even though space marine didnt have  anything unique to offer besides the warhammer setting, somehow i was very captivated by it until the very end. Its story is well paced and its gameplay fast paced and challenging. its gameplay is a combination of gears of war and  dynasty warriors. its like drakengard with guns. The game had me by the edge of my seat and with every chapter that  passed by i got closer and closer to the edge i was afraid i would fall out of my chair! I can hardly find anything to  complain about this masterpiece of a game but if i had to mention something i would probably say 'it has no replay  value' but that only if i was forced to find a negative about the game itself. i can easily see myself giving this a second playthrough within the year. after watching the ending of this game however i began to wonder to myself 'ok, so wheres  space marine 2?' unfortunately since the game didnt receive alot of attention at its launch and it wasnt producing 'call of duty' numbers, sequels are unlikely. hopefully the current IP holder (sega) decide one day that this work of art deserves a sequal that all fans of the space marine game will be able to enjoy.

X COM is an excellent game, and, at least on Steam at the moment, an excellent example of why the gamer-entitlement hivemind is idiotic. Whatever sale pricing outrage that occured is long past, but the now incomprehensible bile randos threw at a game deserving of praise endures. A victory for the endless cycle of net outrage, I guess.  To the game...  If you have any love for turn based strategy, you will lose hours and hours. I played Enemy Unknown and Within on PS3, beating both twice, which I almost never bother with. One of the first games I played that made a convincing case for perma-death as a mechanic. Will beat you up the moment you get lazy. Rarely does the AI lose do to inane decisions. Rarely does the player lose for any reason other than lack of patience, preparation, and tactics.  A modern classic, in my opinion. Enough so that I bought it twice, with no regrets.

I find works of Alfred Hitchcock, Stephen King and David Lynch pretty interesting. When Remedy Entertainment released info about Alan Wake, it sounded like a jackpot. Let’s be honest, not only because those people were making part action, part survival horror game greatly influenced by King’s work, but you know what else they’ve developed? Max Payne. Yup, that’s right. One of the best, if not the best single player experience of all times. I could play it over and over again. Don’t need no multiplayer, no sandbox or multi choices world. Just a bottle of painkillers and Max’s rage to the max.  Alan Wake was a good game. It had its set of flaws: it was a bit too long, a bit too hard at certain points and a bit too boring by the end, but it was an honest homage to one of the greatest living authors. Its story line, characters, ideas and moody atmosphere haunted players long after they were done with the game. Basically — it was Twin Peaks digested by Stephen King. Add some nasty black birds for Hitchcock’s own personal enjoyment.  Alan Wake’s American Nightmare (AWAN from now on) is not a sequel, but a different game with similar mechanics (use a light source to weaken your opponent, use your shotgun to blow his brains out) . It doesn’t spoil the fun of playing the first AW game, but it surely provides enough references so you should probably play this one after you're done with the original game. Don’t listen to Remedy Entertainment when they say it’s a great starting point for a new players right there on AWAN Steam page because it isn’t.  Unlike Alan Wake, AWAN is more action and adrenaline oriented game, with less storytelling, less characters and less plot twists (if any). Alan went from mentally scarred writer suffering from writer’s block to gun wielding one liner super hero. Usually, I don’t like these kind of changes, but the whole game is reskinned, settings and atmosphere are changed, and it blurs the reality and fiction in a strange way, so at the end you might ask yourself: is Alan dreaming? Is this real? Maybe, just maybe you are playing Alan’s scenario for a Twilight Zone’s inspired show The Night Springs?  It took me approximately four hours to finish the game, I’ve collected almost 3/4 of manuscript pages, so if you’re into collecting you’ll probably need some more time to find all the weapons, radio and TV shows. Game also has some pretty good cutscenes that pop up from time to time and main antagonist Mr. Scratch — Wake’s doppelganger, is sooooo ridiculous, but it fits the whole Arizona setting nicely.  After you’re done with Story Mode, you can try your luck and skills at Arcade — in Arcade mode your objective is to survive until dawn (read: sunrise). Can you make it? Do it in style, don’t let ‘em touch you, collect points, guns and skulls of your enemies so you can place your ♥♥♥ on leaderboards and see how you compare to your friends. It’s a fun mode, but believe me, only for a few rounds — unless you have fanatical friends who love playing it all over and over again. I don’t.  The best way to think about Alan Wake’s American Nightmare is this: if original Alan Wake title was a Stephen King’s book, then Alan Wake’s American Nightmare is Stephen King’s movie. You know how they usually chop down his movies, twist ‘em around, skin ‘em alive and dumbify them as much as possible? Yes, Alan Wake’s American Nightmare is King’s version of “The Shining”, “The Night Flier” or “The Thinner”, but you just LOVE those cheap shots at silver screen glory. If you’re coming from Alan Wake and you’re expecting the same kind of rich storytelling — don’t, because you don’t need to end up disappointed. Leave this short game for one boring weekend and get ready to shoot and shoot and shoot and shoot. Wait. Reload. Shoot again. OK, they’re all gone.  ***  Hey, you've liked this review? Hated it? It made you buy the game / abandon the idea of buying this game? Super awesome! It only shows 4.5 hours of playtime for me because there's no proper store page for W2:DC, but I've put well over 100 hours into this game (I'm not even done with the game yet but I'm close!) and it's been a blast. Even on my potato laptop that probably falls below system requirements, I could still play this game at a reasonable FPS (ok, it was more like ~10 FPS, but since it's not a fast-paced, real-time game I was totally fine with that).  Here's a pro-con list I just threw together.  PROS: - Dozens of hours of entertaining gameplay and humorous dialogue/interactions throughout - Huge variety of weapons, items, locations, and characters to interact with, each with their own unique feel - Additional voice casting in W2:DC makes the story more immersive - Well-written story and lore that takes hints from the original Wasteland - Soundtrack by Mark Morgan (of Fallout 1/2 fame) fits great w/the post-apocalyptic setting - Difficulty curve (at least on normal) is pretty fair and stays on pace with the weapons/items you're able to acquire at various stages of the game (for the most part, anyway)  CONS: - Soundtrack can get a bit repetitive (it became more like background noise at ~40 hours in) - Skill-based actions (such as lockpicking, safecracking, etc.) feel like RNG's and having to keep reloading quicksaves after several consecutive critical failures gets annoying after a while - Combat can feel a bit random too, and sometimes all you can do is just save beforehand and hope for the best - Lack of a designated button on the UI for the local map screen (you have to press M for it to appear) made me feel totally lost until I figured out the hotkey for it - Story and environments in California don't feel as polished and noteworthy as they are in Arizona in the 1st half of the game (a lot of locations in the 2nd half of the game are pretty forgettable) - Most NPC's that you'll encounter still aren't voice-acted, so there is an enormous amount of text to read (personally I didn't have a problem with the reading, but it is a bit odd that some major characters aren't voice-acted) - Character/creature portraits and sounds are recycled over and over again, and sometimes they don't even match who they're supposed to represent (ex: why does Pistol Pete's portrait look nothing like his in-game character model, and why does the portrait look almost the same as Lieutenant Wade Woodson's?)  Overall, this game definitely feels like a modernized version of both the original Wasteland as well as the first two Fallout games. The gameplay and interface are so much better than those games, and the story and overall feel of the environments are original takes on the archetypal post-apocalyptic RPG's it was inspired by. It does take a few hours to really get into it, as far as story progression and character leveling are concerned, but the payoff is worth the wait. I'm extremely delighted to see what inXile has in store for Wasteland 3 (once I get a PC that can run it).

This is the definitive version of any SimCity. Much better than the sequel SimCity: Societies.  TL;DR: Even though it is 13 years old it is still one of the best examples of a city builder today. I'd rate it   8/10    This game was one of the major contributors to Cities:Skylines as it is Paradox's main inspiration for the game. The  U-Drive it  Missions are unique to city builders wherein you can interact with your city on a much more indepth level. The economics aren't simplified and the advisors offer advise that can actually be used. For being a very fixed camera in an isometric setup, you get plenty of gorgeous angles and shots. The modding community for this game is still alive as far as I recall, although a lot of it is becoming harder to find certain things such as Network Addon Mod (NAM).   The main drawbacks of the game are mainly due to its age. Unlike Cities it isn't based on both a free building and grid based snapping system. This is solely grid based and it has a very big drawback, bridges. You can only build bridges certain directions and in straight lines. The other main drawback is performance issues on certain new machines and crashes. But I believe you can find a patch around for a fix for the crashes. A minor more nit-picky issue with the Steam version is that unlike a box you don't get a pamphlet that gives good advice about RCI and how to increase and meet demand, but again you can probably find a picture of the RCI pamphlet on the internet.  Overall the game is excellent. Even though my hours on the Steam edition are lackluster, playing on the two-disk box version is where most of my hours really come from. As said above,   8/10

EvilQuest is a fun Action RPG in a 16 bit SNES style, similar to games like Zelda. Story wise the big difference is this time rather than trying to stop the generic EVIL bad guy bent on world domination you play as the EVIL bad guy bent on world domination. The story is fine and the EVIL twist makes it all feel fresh even though its about as simple as a RPG story gets.   The gameplay consists of attacking enemies with swords and such, holding down the attack button for a projectile attack, and some different magic attacks that you can find while exploring, or buy if you save up enough gold. There are plenty of armor pieces to find to make you stronger, and accessories that make you more powerful in various ways, even a cape that makes you invisible.   The graphics look good for a 16 bit style, some of the enemies are merely reskined as you progress in the game, but others like dragons and ghost make for a nice visual treat as you make your way thru the game. The levels are all based on elements like fire and water and look and play nice enough with just enough exploration to give the game some meat. My only complaint is enemies respawn after you slightly move away from an area, so you can't clear a level per say, the enemies are always respawning. This dose give you plenty of opportunity to gain exp points and level up, but as the game wares on it all gets a bit repetitive, but at about 5 hrs for a diligent explorer EvilQuest isn’t long enough for this to become a problem.  Overall EvilQuest is a very fun ARPG that lets you experience the generic RPG story of those 90ies RPG's from the eyes of the bad guy and plays like a dream. For the cost of less than a gallon of gas (in the US at least) this game is an absolute steel and should be played by anyone who likes Zelda like ARPG's or wants to be EVIL.

I recommend this game if you've got some idle time, as it's short, free and it paints a beautiful, sometimes upsetting, sometimes uplifting view on one of the most common mental states in the world.   It's presented as a visual novel, it's low budget, and it shows. Regardless, if you are patient enough to immerse yourself in the fictional character through the text, I think the experience warrants it. It employs one particularly clever trick - showing you options which you want to pick, but preventing you from picking them. This gives you (the person behind the keyboard) a sense of frustration, and through that, an understanding. Those banned options go through your characters head too but, like in real life depression, they  can't  do them any more than you can click the buttons.  It has some story telling issues, the biggest of which was that time was poorly represented. I suspect the events play out over many months, yet this isn't made obvious, and it feels like weeks instead. Also, it's 'obvious' which are the better options to pick - at least when you're in a healthy mindset - but I'm not sure how it could be done better.   If you're suffering right now from depression, this might make you feel terrible, but you might still want to persevere - it's a story of hope, as much as it is a story of depression.

This game at first looks like another Japanese game with no substance or thought, but it is actually quite the experience. Yes, the dungeons are a bit samey and the combat starts off very slow, but past that is a great JRPG.  An interesting setting of a multiverse where different game companys embodied as different nations are ruled over by these beautiful Goddesses. The characters are lovable and diverse, and all interaction between them (including fanservice) is always a treat. The story is somewhat basic but gradually steps up into an interesting adventure. The soundtrack is great, full of upbeat electronic battle themes and catchy field themes. The game comes in japanese or english. I preferred Jap (being weeb and all that) as it made the characters more expressive and moe. In English, instead the characters sound like typical anime dubbed characters, but with more catchy punchlines.  The combat is unique as it is '3D Turn-Based' where characters may move around a specific area during their turns before they unleash a button mash of combos and other special JRPG combat shenanigans (transformations, finishers, etc). And the enemies certainly live up to the theme of a gaming world with many traditional RPG tropes like dragons to pop culture references (there's an enemy which is literally a screenshot of a dating sim xD). Character progression is pretty simple here with typical level ups and gear crafting. Theres also a friendship system between characters where they can perform special combos and assist eachother in battle, even if on of them aren't in the party group.  Overall, a great JRPG which is one of many in it's series. I have yet to play them at this point in time, but before those, why not try out this gem of a videogame?

★★★☆☆ - 'Worth a try, but only on a discount'   PROS: - Its obvious Ryse was a showpiece for Xbox One but frankly the game is beyond stunning - Ryse is all about cinematic shots and making feel electric. - I dont like strategy games and all Roman games are exactly that so a pro point here just to be able to play in that era. - Although only about 7 hours long it does boast a very good story. - Not as wave-like as first anticipated, the action is free-flowing and brutal.  CONS: - Repetitive, I expected it to be from the games Xbox reception and ultimately it was although not as bad as feared. - I could deal with the quick-time cause I signed up for it but it was far more linear than I expected with poor attempts of collectables which stood out like sore thumbs if you even felt like bothering. - Theres little variety in the missions, you can sometimes order your men from two options, use missile like attacks, or use stationary crossbows but whilst the locations change the missions dont and for a recent major release its simply not good enough.  VERDICT? Ryse is shallow and should only be bought at a sale price however I knew what to expect, bought into it and thoroughly enjoyed the game.

review_text: I will just go ahead and say, the campaign is your typical COD game storyline. It's the same generic story, but a little bit more complicated (in a somewhat good way).
