I can't completely say it's a crap game, but it definately is in what it seems to be an 'early stage' of it's official release.....I bought the deluxe edition because of the comments honestly. Knowing that down the road there will be DLCS most likely, if it's any sid meier's, there will be DLCS....I'll say it again.   Unfortunately I can agree that I had more fun with Sid V than I did with this...and for a few reasons. I'm not as in-depth about the problems I notice in the game, like someone mentioned how 'roads are useless and workers can't build them and the only way to build them is to waste a trade route' yea...I did notice that, and it is a little upsetting because if you're trying to match reality and give the player freedom to make his own decisions...let him build a ♥♥♥♥ing road himself. (just saying) if he builds it through the rainforest of nothingness and nothing gets accomplished with that road as far as military advancements or whatever, then that's their 'loss' in my opinion.   I am also saddened that there are very few options for 'starting a game' I find myself resetting constantly because I feel the game just doesn't 'cling on to me' or the person I picked doesn't really have much to offer. I liked the simplicity of Sid V as far as the 'benefits of a leader' and I think districts are pretty stupid (not going to give a reason, just don't like them) I also feel like the 'luxury resources and bonus resources' are pretty useless....again, not giving a reason, just my opinion.   I also noticed that workers....when you make a farm or give them something to do, I don't know what happens but they ♥♥♥♥in disappear and that just pisses me off that they poof after It took like 10 turns to produce a worker and he disappears after making 1 farm...I've had it happen to me before, I 've had it happen randomly it seems, idk if it's a bug or they go into like a 'production mode' and just...'work the tile' but in my opinion that is very ....very.....very....stupid and annoying. Makes me produce another worker and another, and another, really I find myself not having workers most the game because they ♥♥♥♥in fall off the earth or seep into a tile or whatever...idk, I definately fine the older version better, and am not entirely upset about wasting $80 at the same time...it is an improvement but god ♥♥♥♥ing DAMN they seemed like they were experimenting with TOO MUCH and have Tom, ♥♥♥♥ and Ernie's desk projects implemented in their game when maybe not a lot of people like ♥♥♥♥'s idea or Ernie is a little bit on the dumb side and just likes making things broken. Idk, anyway, this is my honest opinion about the game, I know I don't have a lot of hours but i've honestly never had an $80 game seem 'boring' in a sense....So I feel like I've been ripped in the way that I thought I was buying Civ VI, when really I'm buying something 'like Civ V' but for dummies...seems to be less to actually 'do' in the game.

TECHNICAL ISSUES It's been nearly 2 months since the game was released and for 2 months the dev team cannot solve the problem of a quite a lot of people, which makes the multiplayer game impossible. Steam doesn't show that the person is online in the game, and if this person tries to join someone's game, it says something like 'failed joining multiplayer session'. The only solution I and my friend (same issue) had is to launch another game (which would show that a person is playing something) and that would work< we could actually join each other's lobbies. But a while ago this method has stopped working and, as a result, we are not able to play multiplayer civilization at all! I already wrote in forums, one of the developers assured me they would look into this issue, but, well, 2 months - no results. I didn't buy this game for single player.

An FPS game that's all about cooperation. If you're looking for a game that's all about the 'kills' then this is not it for you. This game has three modes: Execution, Stopwatch and Objective. All of them are heavily influenced by teamwork. You can't win this game going solo (unless you hacked). Nexon successfully incorporated several tactics to enhance the game teamwork spirit. Defusing together can speed up the defusing. 'Stick together' means you get extra exp. Getting on the top scoreboard can be reached even with a 0/11 kdr. An example to that would be being a medic. As long as you heal your teammates you can get a lot of exp even with 0 kill you can still reach to the top scoreboard. Several annoying problems would be the ping and the team imbalance. Even when I get on the right server [U.S East or West] my ping would sometimes goes as high as 300-500, even though I tested my internet and the ping is only 20. Following up to that is team imbalance, it's uncommon to get to play in a game where both teams are evenly matched. The game is usually skewed because one team would completely overpowered the other. Team shuffle at time, still doesn't fix the issue.

Upon Initial Game Release  Welp, apparently some have had better experiences than me and apparently some not so i'll share my experiences. I load up the game for the first time and instantly the game is not my resolution, understandable though so I go to change it meanwhile also changine the settings to low which apparently had no effect on my games FPS and then I was unable to get onto the resoultions and was stuck on full screen and windowed so I pressed fullscreen and now it was my resolution even though it didn't have it listed on their. It's clunky as ♥♥♥♥ but I can deal with it, I press host game as find game doesn't work and it asks me to input my name so I do and press enter...Nothing, I try spamming enter...Nothing and then randomly it works and asks me the same question again so I do, now keep in mind this is a 'PC' game not a console to PC game so you will understand my confusion when all the controls are Xbox controls leaving me clueless as to what to do, the controls are self explanatory for a gamer however so I got into the game. The game looks fantastic but for some reason when moving the FPS dies completely for me however if you have a very good computer this won't be a problem I wouldn't think. I try to quit so I press escape...Nothing, so I spam it and accidentaly hit windows help which takes me out of the game and upon returning to the game I now am running the game at 640 x 400 for whatever reason so it was at this point that I decided to alt f4 and leave the game.  The game seems to have a great idea but honestly should be an early access game due to clunky menus, graphics options which appear to do nothing, resoultion that does whatever it wants and obviously the servers are broken. Although if you want to look at some gorgeous graphics just go into host game and admire the ♥♥♥♥ out of it.

A more compressed spiritual successor to Pillars of Eternity. Playing the villanous side of a conflict is a nice change to the usual settings and tweaking your character through the Conquest decisions feels more impactful than it probably is. Dicking over poor peasants never felt that good.  The game has a nice length with my first playthrough taking me about 20 hours. That said, the ending on the path i chose felt a little bit rushed, which could be attributed to the dib in difficulty I experienced throughout the game. That could have just been me min-maxing pretty well, but some of the later bosses felt pretty undewhelming to the point where I did not even have to use consumables. then again, there are harder difficulties to choose from.  All in all a very enjoyable experience with a bunch of good to superb writing to sift through. It is a lot shorter than one would expect however that has been advertised beforhand and from what I have seen, different playthroughs vary enough to warrant two or more runs.

Not my thing...  I never had the chance to play the ps3 version of this game, so when I saw that it was coming out on steam and for only $29, I thought I would give it a try. This game was very well regarded and recommended to me, but it just wasn't my cup of tea.   Pros:   The gameplay. The large team battles, and epic monsters make the game entertaining. I enjoyed climbing up the monsters and exposing their weak points. The battles felt satisfying.  yeah thats it for me for pros  Cons:  the Graphics... I know this is a port of a ps3 game, so I shouldn't expect too much, but still the textures are trash. The Music.... nothing I can even remember Character development - Not a single character that is worth a damn Quests - With the exception to a handful of missions most of the quests are fetch quests and 'kill so many of Type X monster' which got old real quick No replay value - You can just change your class at any time without penalty so there is no reason to replay the game, just try all classes on your first playthrough.  At the end of the day I just can't recommend this game to anyone other than die hard action RPG fans that are looking to kill time until DS3 comes out. 6/10

Short, simple game. Horrible AI in the heist levels. Repetitive dialogue. They're funny the first time, hearing it afterwards is just irritating. The game is basically glorified snake with an attempt to parody GTA V, which, seeing as the game takes place in the UK, doesn't make very much sense. It probably makes more sense if you view the name NotGTAV as a marketing tactic that piggybacks on another popular game. The difficulty levels are just the game sped up, there's nothing else unique about them to warrant a replay other than the sake of completion. On the plus side, the soundtrack is decent and complements the hand-drawn style well enough. It's great that all the proceeds from sales are going to charity, but that's one of only a few redeeming qualities this game has. It's almost like an excuse for making a half-♥♥♥♥♥ game. That should not be the sole reason for you to buy it, though. You can always donate to charity on your own volition.  Is it worth the 74 cents I spent during the sale? Well, I enjoyed it more than a $1 can of soda, so yes. If you want a few laughs, buy it when it's less than a dollar, then sell the trading cards for most of your money back. Otherwise, wouldn't recommend.

A free to play game with solid foundations if a bit barebones.  As I only have a couple of hours in the game, there is a lot of thing I haven't tried yet. The good: It's free. You can try it and decide if you like it, without losing anything. You start off with a loan and the choice of three different ships, after that you are free to do whatever you want. The game offers you a string of tutorial missons which show you the ropes of the basic playstyle of your ship, while gives you some small pointers as well.  You can have an entire fleet of ships which can escort you and you can issue them simple commands. You can add stuff. While the game is in it's infancy I can imagine there will be a boatload of user generated content to it. And the whole thing should be easy to do as well.  The bad: Using passenger or trading ships can become boring at first, as you only jump and land, and hope that pirates don't kill you. At least until you get enough money for a lot of ships (and crew) to guard you. The controls are a bit hard to figure out without checking the preferences menu, and the game only shows them for a short time on the bottom-left corner of the screen. The ships have inertia, so combat needs a lot of getting used to.  The background during flight is rather bland (what else would you expect apart from stars), and the closeness of planets, while useful, make the entire area feel just bland and empty. The game also devoid of music, only having a few sounds for thrusters and stuff, making the game feel slower, so it can quickly become boring for those used to faster-paced games. The game itself is not really hard if you know what you are doing, but there can be instances of getting swarmed with pirates after take-off and thus dying quite fast, especially when you have only a single ship. The graphics are dull. Planets only show a single picture above their menu, while ships look like low poly 3d models. This however can be easily remedied with editing, so not that big of a problem.  The game actually have a story and while it doesn't immediately point you to it, it can be interesting. All in all while the game can feel like an empty sandbox with spaceships, made by amateurs, it is solid enough to be enjoyable. And also it's free, with the option to add stuff to it.  TL:DR Its free, it's small, try it out.

When you look at Road to Ballhalla it looks like you basic marble rolling game. It should since that it was it is. Road to Ballhalla is a marble rolling game. Road to Ballhalla is one of if not the best marble rolling game I have ever played though.  Road to Ballhalla is a great marble rolling game for three reasons. First the controls are simple and tight. When you die in the game it is your fault and not because of the controls. Second the game has great level design. Care and thought were put into the levels. The game does a great job coming up with unique ways to use the different mechanics and obstacles to make each level feel unique. Finally the game is surprisingly funny. Who would have thought a marble rolling game would actually be funny. Road to Ballhalla is genuinely funny as the 'narrator' tries to trick you and taunts you when you fail.  Road to Ballhalla is not a perfect game though. The biggest problem is the game is pretty short. If you aren't into time trials or 100% levels, the game will only last around three to four hours. The game is adding Steam Workshop support though so hopefully the community can add a lot of interesting levels to the game. The other slight issue I had with the game is that it is kind of on the easy side.  If you don't like marble rolling games, Road to Ballhalla is not going to be for you. If you like marble rolling games though I think you will really enjoy Road to Ballhalla.  For my full review check out   Geeky Hobbies   [www.geekyhobbies.com] .

Addictive piece of work this one. A nature simulator which let you create your own ecosystem in grassland, desert or rainforest. The game starts with one small zone with no content. Then you use your 'energy', which accumulates over time, to place flora and fauna. More zones are available, up to five, to buy with 'Tyto coins', which are also used to unlock more plant- and wildlife. These coins come from unlocking achievements and on a weekly basis. I enjoyed the peaceful pace the game has, but you can also increase the speed a little (energy accumulates very slow). Plantlife also expands on their own on-game and off-game, as well as the inhabitants in the animal territories decrease or increase (you have to carefully plan not to have too many carnivores in a zone, or too few insects for insectivores etc). Also it is important to have certain number of decomposing elements and pollinators. I almost learned something here, geez.  Something to tweak (imo): No birds, and fish are just generic fish pre-placed on water. More biomes, tundra and regular forest would be nice Y U NO BEARS?! Bobcats are op, 2 of them massacred like 7 herbivore communities and several other carnivores sharing same territory. The whole gameplay area gets crowded even with full zones, bigger mammals really need their lebensraum.  Oh, and rats can eat a tree. Like whole.

Owned the game for a long time, decided to buy it on Steam some time ago, a very good decision!  Beautiful graphics, the shield rendering, explosions, and bullet rains all over the place!  The stock AI is kinda dumb tough, take the Sorian AI mod, and the game becomes much better, very challenging!  Just a shame it's badly programmed... even on top-notch I7's (3610QM & 4700MQ, no extreme overclocked sh**) it causes a pretty heavy load (Full load on 2 cores often), and when playing with a lot of AI's the game can even freeze up. I have no idea how anyone would play this with a 1.8 Ghz processor, which the requirements state.... Probably only possible with a resolution of 320x240.  Also the requirements state like '1 GB RAM', well even with 8 GB RAM you still can run into low memory warnings, which is a bug, because there is always 2GB+ RAM still free... it seems this is much more likely to happen when having a resolution of 1920x1080, because I suppose the game has to allocate more memory for the bigger textures. Whenever you get these low memory warnings, at any given moment your game could just crash, it can mess up your desktop. Meaning you have to reboot your computer by force. (Or if you are not the average person, open up the CMD blindly, and kill the process)  But with some tools like Core Maximizer and Large Address Aware the game runs a lot better.  Multiplayer is very nice in this game, no annoying lag, and there are enough configuration options for all the maps.  Maybe i've said some bad things about this game in the review, but it's definitely worth the money! No doubt!!! You will love this game, Skirmish mode rocks.  When talking about ratings, I would say this I guess...  Gameplay 9/10 No real glitchy behavior, factions all have good units, each with their own unique strenghts Graphics 8/10 Very good graphics, especially for it's time, turn on Bloom, and the game looks much better, or download shaders. Of course we're not comparing this to games from 2014. Audio 9/10 Nice sound effects, like the artillary cannons, or from experimentals Performance 6/10 Like I said, you need a pretty fancy computer to run this game, with ATLEAST medium graphics, and a resolution bigger than 800x600, a 1.8 Ghz CPU and GPU with 128 MB VRAM ain't going to do the job. Use the tools provided by the community to make the game run much smoother!

I enjoyed It.  7/10  Story is so-so, DLC's are better in my opinion. Most of the side quests are boring fech quests with a cuple of better ones, whitch are hard to find under dozens of go-there-go-back quest and i mean dozens, there is easly more than a hundred quests in the game. Faction quest are expample of better ones, you get some choices, story behind said facion and decent loot, so if you want to skip most of the side quests dont skip facion ones. You might consider skipping because you get heavily over leveled and open world enemies doesn't scale with your level, quest enemies do and most of the dungeon are scaled too but it's inconsistent.  World is pretty, colorful and with nice desing with some great looking landscapes and some stunning towns.  Although dungeon assets are heavely reused(it seem's like dungeons were desinged by at least 2 teams, with some having shortcut to exit, while in others you have to backtrack), same can be said about enemies, you will be fighting variations of the same mobs you meet within first 5 hours(DLC's have few new ones).  Combat. Yes this is where the game shines, meaty swings, hit sounds. Combat is great and thats what kept me playing, slaughtering hundreds of enemies was very enjoyable.  Crafting is very overpowered, whitch i took advantage of and bacame juggernaut of destruction, choosing 'nah i just wanna kill you' dialog option when some evil mastermind wanted to tell me why he does what he does and because story isn't very compeling that was the most fun option.

This game is barley playable. Hinges mostly on mic, won't detect my mouse that's otherwise working. All control schemes tried by me and a friend were wonkey, be it keyboard and mouse, xbox 360 or xbox one controler. The xbox controlers in fact didn't even let you turn. The weapons mapping on 1-4 were unintuitive ties with the keyboard (one was at the 9:00 instead of 12:00 position). This game feals like a console game, that was quickly ported to PC. But not that little attention to detail was put on the keyboard/mouse, but it surprises me to see xbox controlers bad enough to not be functional. Additionally, xbox controlers use triggers to look up and down instead of right thumbstick... WHAT?! My fingers are FAR from precise enough to look up and down without looking WAY too far up and down. The invasion mode was too simplistic (not too easy, challenge level was OK, but just low variety of enemy, and super small map). Outside of these things, the game showed a lot of potential. 'Fun' was within grasp, but just barley slipped away. I wanted to like this, it sounded really good. Unfortunately that wasn't the case.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: The natural progresion of the total war series in terms of gameplay. Lots of improvments and well implemented ideas. All that said it didnt feel historically accurate without mods, which you can find on the steam workshop. Mods to make the Major Empires, like Rome, actually powerfull. I was disapointed to find out that multiplayer was broken because the constant updates and DLC incompatabilities. As well as that no more then 2 players can be in a multiplayer campaign. I found my self reinstalling medieval total war 2 and playing that to completion 3 times before returning to rome 2.  Graphics 9/10 with mods 10/10 Gameplay 8/10 with mods 10/10 Conclusion: If you like the total war series, get this game. If you where disapointed with Shogun 2s gameplay changes, dont get it. the Total War series is a legacy series, if you liked the older ones you may like the new ones. The inclusion of the workshop allows problems to be fixed and gameplay perfected.

I can't remember when I bought Hotline Miami or how much I gave for it, but I honestly regret not playing it sooner. I also forget how long it took to finish and to get the secret ending, but I clocked in 10 hours to do all that and get a few additional cheevos.   Hotline Miami is basically Ultraviolence: The Game. Each chapter sees you as the brutally-violent 'Jacket' who dons an animal mask at the start of each level, who then proceeds to slay a bunch of guys in nice white suits while pulsing music plays through the brightly-colored rooms. You're going to leave one hell of a mess, but you have to do it without getting hit. Using the weapons lying around and in the enemy's hands (or your own bare hands), slaughter everyone. Die, and you have to restart the current floor no matter how many mooks' brains you left ground into the carpet before.   Hotline Miami plays like a twin-stick shooter with one stick or WASD to move, and the other stick or the mouse to aim. You don't have many actions but you honestly don't need much. You move at a set rate, you can move the camera slightly away from you to peek around the map, you can punch enemies down, you can finish off downed enemies, you can lock onto individual targets, you can pick up, use, and throw weapons, and you can interact with a few contextual things during your missions. You press R or the A button when you inevitably die to restart the floor. The game doesn't demand perfection but it does ask for accuracy, and being a few degrees off on your swing can be the difference between killing or being killed.   You might look at the screenshots and think the game a rather stylized stealth game. You can sneak around and off enemies with silent melee weapons, but the game encourages you to be reckless and ballsy with the scoring system. Playing it safe and going slowly will net you few points. Killing chains of enemies in a short time gives you bonus combo points, so be quick! Score high and you unlock new weapons and new masks which give minor bonuses like having lethal fists, moving faster, or making all guns silent. The game is very actiony and very fast-paced, and I'd honestly say it has some puzzle elements too. Enemies tend to stick to their rooms and some will patrol in set patterns, so it's a matter of how to dispatch them all quickly but safely. The game also slowly introduces new elements to keep the gameplay fresh, like doors to slam into enemies, or enemies that certain weapons won't work on, or windows that you (and the enemies) can shoot through. Trying to plan your next move while keeping all of these things in mind while your adrenaline's up is only part of the fun.   The story is...minimal, but the experience is very trippy and weird. You're some blonde dude wearing an animal mask and a letterman jacket (hence the nickname). You don't talk, you don't have a name, you just wake up, answer the answering machine, go to some place, and kill a bunch of people. You get a score tally, you unlock things, you go visit your friend at one of the stores he works at, and then you go home to repeat the process. It's a very arcadey experience since the story is so out of focus. You'll be left with plenty of questions if you stop to think...but who has time for that? You might be able to piece together the puzzle if you have an eye for detail.   While each chapter doesn't have its own unique song, the music really helps to get you in the killing mood. High-tempo music just goes well with the frantic pace of you moving through the rooms, mowing down scores of mooks. You might find yourself bobbing your head to the beat or tapping your foot or whatever. Then you get Chapter Clear and the music... stops.  Party's over, time to go home.   The game is pretty short. Most of your time with this game will be spent getting that 'perfect' run through each stage and chapter, so you'll be repeating content quite a bit until you stop dying. The game's hard, but I didn't think it crossed over from 'challenging but fun' to 'frustrating and disheartening.' The individual stages within the chapters are thankfully pretty short, so you rarely lose any big chunk of time to failure. If you're the type of person who likes to speedrun levels or try to beat your previous score or beat your friends' scores or are just an old-fashioned cheevo-♥♥♥♥♥, there's something here for you. And there's always some catharsis in cutting a bunch of mooks in half with a katana.   I'd like to think that Hotline Miami is well-deserving of its high praise, but it might not be a game for everyone. Some people might be turned off by the almost cartoonish pixelated gore they'd be creating, some people might not care for the whatever-genre-it-is music, and some people might want to snap their keyboards and controllers in half after dying for the 30th time in a row. For everyone else, it'll be a difficult but rewarding ride filled with lots of death. Maybe you  do  like hurting people.

This is simply the finest RTS I have ever played. The number of units (800+!!!) and attention to detail in modeling the look and capabilities of those units is second to none. The focus on realistic warfare (w/in reason) makes it one of the few RTS's that feels every bit as tactical as the best turn based strategy games. In fact, I would say it is the most tactical wargame I have ever played.   The game is very easy to control in terms of moving units around the map, but is incredibly deep when it comes to truly learning how to play the game well. I have over 150 hours in the game and am still learning the nuances.  The game looks like a dream, with excellently represented units from NATO and the Warsaw Pact. Great unit detail and great special effects with the smoke and fire and explosions. Only the infantry modeling leaves a little to be desired. The scaling is also incredible. A spin of the mouse wheel will zoom you in so that a single unit fills your entire screen in wonderful detail. A spin of the wheel in the opposite direction will zoom out to the point that the entire map is visible on your screen.   My only negative is that the SP campaign is absolutely byzantine and unsatisfying. Good luck figuring out how to play it. But the real value of this game is in the SP skirmish mode and MP. MP is brutal, but extremely rewarding after you learn the ropes. You will lose often, but it will never feel cheap. When you lose it is because you screwed up, when you win it is because you were the better player.  Deep, user friendly, and pretty to look at, this is the best RTS on the market.

So about Blade Symphony. Let me start off by saying if it hadn't been for this free weekend I'd never have picked it up.. or rather never would have tried it, as I didn't pick it up.  I absolutely loathe the asian theme, the futuristic art-style, the over-the-top animations, the character design...basically the entire presentation of this game doesn't so much speak to me as it does shout 'Stay away. Far away'.  However that is a personal preference and even though I'm essentially right in this I am also aware of the fact that there are people who have the audacity to be wrong and like this sort of stuff. Not much you can do about that.  But the game isn't just looks, so let's ignore those for now.  The fighting, the heart of this game.. well it isn't bad. It is actually rather enjoyable to begin with. It is however rather repetitive after a while. This is not - as some other people commenting here seem to think - due to a lack of content but rather due to a lack of input.  I actually like the fact that weapons are uniform across characters and I could do without the different character-traits as well. Purely cosmetic characters - making the outcome of a fight only about input-skill&speed rather than character attributes feels cleaner to me.  Input however is the problem. You have far too little actual control to actually do something creative in your attacks, which means it all comes down to timing. Once you have ascertained that your timing is better than the opponents the fight is more or less over and will play out accordingly.  I'm not going to go into balancing because I haven't played enough of it to actually notice - or care - and I've also limited myself to the Longsword, as it is the only of the weapons in this game I actually give a toss about. So while there might be balancing issues - there almost certainly are, would be the first multiplayergame not to have those - I can't comment on those.  I can however comment on performance.  Some of the maps are rather mess as far as performance is concerned and - as with all melee games - if your ping rises or isn't stable it will have significant impact on your experience. The performance-drops I noticed aren't significant enough to be blamed for getting defeated in combat (outside of really close matches on a very high skill-level which didn't really affect me all that much) but they are really irritating and a drag on the amount of joy the flow of combat delivers.  This flow sadly isn't all that great either. If your opponent is fast enough you can get locked-in by combo-attacks which basically drain most of your life before you are able to defend yourself (as you can't block mid-combo, you can block it entirely or not at all in my experience). Similarly you can get yourself locked into the combo-animation and killed because you didn't hit the opponent but also were unable to respond to his attacks. Cancelling animations to perform new actions would obviously break the animation-style the game aims for, so unfortunately there is little to be done about this.  This highlights the input problem, combo-animations may look nice (to other people) but fighting with individual attacks&strikes gives you much more control over your character and combat in general.  Right now the game is very heavily discounted and I can't bring myself to buying it, even though - despite the named issues - I had a couple of really enjoyable fights in this game. Not even worth the 4€ they are asking for it right now, at least not to me.

I have never seen a game fail at what it sets out to do quite like Rush Bros. It's honestly quite a mess from start to finish. I mean, zombies are shoe-horned in for no reason whatsoever, so that should give some indication.   The levels are about as 'affected by your music' as Turba is. But at least in Turba when a song gets fast, the game does too. Supposedly the traps change speed, but it honestly just make them look like they were programmed by a drunk guy. Speaking of drunken programming, the music selection system is the worse thing I have ever seen in my life. There is no way to choose just 1 song. You create 'playlists' out of entire folders. So if you're a normal human being and have just 1 folder for music, it selects your entire library recursively, with no way to easily remove or add in tracks. You also can't reorder or select tracks for play easily at all. For whatever reason, you can also change your music track in the middle of a game, if you can get the music controller to show up. The biggest kicker by far is that turning on shuffle affects the 'previous track' button, making it painfully obvious this was just a mediocre platformer and they just tossed in the music as an after thought. The default music is generic techno rubbish. It's so generic I couldn't even tell if the game was stuck on a track or not while playing.   The platforming aspect is bad as well. They make you backtrack (in a fast-paced platformer...) as soon as level 2. The level design is confusing and you actually end up getting lost your first time through even though there are signs telling you where to go. They just put doors and such in locations that you easily overlook. Level design also repeats fairly often, as if it were made with a random level generator. Some parts of the design are just horrid. There's one section where there are a bunch of springs you have to get past, except it's next to impossible to get past them without being flung back. The levers you have to pull force you to spam a button instead of pushing it once like any other sane platformer. You can get double jump and speed up powerups, but they are useless 99% of the time.   Overall, this is just terrible. In the only highly rated positive review, (by the developer no less) it touts one of the key features is 'you choose the levels'. Perhaps this is one of the few features that actually work (incidentally I had to spend 5 minutes trying to figure out how to start a level because there is no 'Play' button, so maybe not). The EULA is also so insane it would make Square-Enix overjoyed. It is honestly just a platformer with a music player shoved in. Platform Racing 2 is far better in every way (including music selection) and that was a free flash game from 2008.

Bionic dues is a roguelike, and plays pretty similar to standard rogue games. You take an action, then everything else does, repeat untill enemies are dead or an objective is complete. You get four party members that you can switch between at any point (costs an action) but only one of them can be on the field at a time, so you're really always controlling a single hero, not a squad.  First it should be said that, like most arcen games, Bionic Dues has amazingly good music. Although they are merely adequate as a game developer, they have the amazing Pablo Vega on staff doing all their music. This is especially noticeable in the hauntingly beautiful title track, The Home That We Once Knew. A song so brilliant i'd recommend buying this game JUST to hear it, despite its other flaws. It really sets the scene and is a truly amazing first impression, sadly it's mostly downhill from there.  This game has an unfortunate problem shared by other Arcen titles, in that the game bombards you with information right from the beginning, telling you in pointlessly explicit detail about the final battle you'll eventually have and how to win it. Aside from being overwhelming and working very poorly as a tutorial, this also instantly takes away any sense of novelty or mystery from the game, and you're never really in doubt about what will eventually happen. The final battle is hyped and foreshadowed repeatedly, and warnings about it are shoved in your face constantly.  At the beginning you choose four of the six classes, and one of the six available leaders. this is probably intended to encourage replayability, but i didn't really feel any desire to play it again.  After every mission your'e rewarded with a tide of loot with witty descriptions that modify lots of stats, and you can equip your party between missions using these parts. Each squad member has 20-30 equipment slots, so you'll spend a lot of time in there tweaking with equipment loadouts, which is kind of fun at first. However after a while of playing, the system starts to feel shallow, as there's a pretty small range of values that can actually be adjusted, and thusly not much room for 'builds' or any real modding strategy. You basicalyl want everyone to be reasonably tanky, and all their weapons to be strong enough to 1-shot enemies, with reasonable amounts of range and ammo. this isn't hard to accomplish.  Eventually you'll stop caring about most of the loot you get, and just briefly scan the inventory for unusually high values, there's too much of it, and once you've seen one +50% damage mod, you've seen them all. There's no unusual combinations or interesting unique equipment, just ever-increasing generic values, the novelty fades quickly.  Each squad member has a small (preset, non-changeable) selection of weapons, and with certain missions they can get a permanant upgrade which gives them additional weapons and equipment slots (again, preset). These upgrades are nice and add an interesting power spike to things, but there's exactly one for each squad member, and they're no-brainers you'll want to grab asap.   ***** SPOILERS AHEAD ***** ***** SPOILERS AHEAD ***** ***** SPOILERS AHEAD *****   Despite the constant warnings and foreshadowing, and especially the warning that i'd have to hold off a massive assault from hundreds of enemies, the final mission was a colossal letdown. It threw a grand total of 80-ish enemies at me (there's a counter) in a single, boring mazelike interior environment, much like any other mission. There was no colossal assault, it was literally a hunt-and-destroy mission like hundreds of others. I had to go and find THEM hiding in tiny pockets of resistance, and just nuke them with aoe weapons.   I spent a long time building up a perfectly engineered sniper and engineer-scientist combo, and created a huge fort of sentry guns expecting to have to fight off thousands of bots, and maybe some colossal monster. What i got was a pathetic skirmish with only slightly more enemies than an average mission. it was a dismal and hollow end to the game. There's no real reward or sense of closure either, after the battle you jsut get a 'congratulations you won, now go and relax' textbox, and the game just stops on the main map screen with nothing being clickable. No ending cutscene, no conclusion to the story, just nothing.   I spent £1.74 on this game, and my initial thoughts after buying it were that i'd gotten an amazing deal. But as the novelty wore off, that seemed like a pretty reasonable price. It's great fun for an hour or two, and mildly entertaining for a few more after that, but there's no real reason to replay it.  You should probably quit before you finish the game, and just imagine that it had a good ending.

I liked Democracy on one level due to the management and such, the ability to implement policies and alter funding and check population satisfaction rates etc etc.  But the reason I didn't like it, is because it's nothing like real Democracy, its like the sort of Democracy that exists in a vacuum, the kind of idealized Democracy that a Politics Scientist would talk about. Sort of like when people talk about Communism as an idealized form of Government.  The Human equation isn't present at all is what I'm trying to get at. There are no lobby groups, there are no Corporations leaning on you for contracts, the press is completely unbiased and exists in its own bubble. Your Cabinet members themselves consider themselves public servants serving the public, and are honest and straightforward with their policy opinions. As if they are all robots.  Thats the real problem I have, its that it's some sort of perfect utopia of what people would like the political system to be, but, due to human nature, it never even approaches what is in the game.

This game was, in all, pretty nice.  I'm not a real connoisseur of horror games, but this was a good kind of pseudo-horror that doesn't cram so many jumpscares down your throat that you have multiple heart attacks in one sitting. I did, however, find myself craving something more in the 'scare factor' of this game, I find it more of a puzzle game at heart, with some big monsters that you hardly ever see. There's a lot of ambience, and a few forced encounters, but I didn't fail once in the entire game(Okay, so I fell of a cliff, but that doesn't count).  I thought the map design and the mechanics were exquisite, crawling up stuff and using chairs to reach doorknobs added a new factor that made the game more intimate(keys and pressure plates are so old), but in all, it was far too short.  With all of the crowd-sourced funding and open map design, I think this should have been a $9.99-$14.99 game because there is no replay value, once you solve the puzzle, there's no other way to spend time. Hopefully, Krillbite will have FREE DLC for the game(Like adding chapter 5 maybe), or some more achievements, or an alternate path through the game, or anything...  That all aside, I suggest people get this game; but wait for a sale or a mark down in price.

A follow-up on XCOM that preserves the spirit of the franchise. It is more of XCOM, but with enough novelty to make it worthwhile.   I have completed one playthrough in more than 100 hours, and found only a few glitches. Worst was that, in two circumstances, my squad member and the enemy saw each other through a solid roof, which of course should not have happened.  Missions where the squad starts concealed add a twist, and having a counter (it is not a timer!) that limits the number of available turns to complete the mission rebalances things nicely. There are also plenty of missions without a counter anyway.  Like the previous XCOM, it has a reverse difficulty curve, where survival is more difficult early in the game, before obtaining various upgrades, trainings, etc.. However, the enemy keeps getting more dangerous, and improving the ship and the crew is fundamental.   I can only heartily recommend it, if you enjoyed XCOM EU/EW. If you have not played those, then I would recommend starting with them... why miss-out? And meanwhile, the price of XCOM 2 can go down.  I am getting a steady 60 fps at 1920x1200 resolution, with an Intel i7-2700K @ 3.5 GHz, 8 GB RAM, NVIDIA GTX 970 (Asus Strix OC), Windows 10 Pro, with V-Sync on, everything maxed out but with AA set to FXAA. With the exception of cut-scenses, that are limited to 30 fps. No mods.

Solid, enjoyable, well put-together game.  The game's difficulty on Normal is a bit too easy, although Hard mode presents a decent challenge, especially earlier on in the game (before you get access to the really OP debuffs).  Despite the RPG Maker feel that a lot of people seem to detest, Skyborn's a very polished package. I noticed a couple small bugs and a couple typos, but that was it on the negatives.  I was able to play this with a controller, which was very appreciated. I used a 3rd party knockoff Xbox360 controller, which didn't require any additional setup upon booting the game. However, I was able to hit F1 in-game and remap the buttons to something more sensible, the defaults didn't make sense on my particular controller.  Dialogue was well-written, enjoyable, and fairly humorous. Plot, while a bit short, was interesting and flowed well. Combat system had a nice brisk pace, with good yet swift animations for attacks.  The leveling and itemization was a stand-out success, in my mind: armor is equippable by all characters, but if you weigh a character down beyond their carrying capacity, their speed drops off. There are a few ways of increasing that capacity later in the game. But that broad eligibility allows a much wider amount of equipment options for each character, as you can trade off weight between different slots to equip items you REALLY want. The augment system is simple yet robust, offering another layer of customizibility to your characters. Furthermore, there are two 'class up's during the game, where you can upgrade each character to a new job class. The game does a great job of explaining the options prior to selection, so you don't need to consult a third party resource for information.  It took me about 15 hours to complete the game, playing in hard mode and completing all optional content&bosses. I'm a bit of a slow player, though, so most folks will probably take about 7-12 depending on difficulty and completeness.  I don't think I'd pay the full $15 for this one due to its length, but at sale prices it's a great purchase.  Enjoy!

This is the DBZ game I have been waiting for. In my opinion no previous DBZ game can compare with the masterpiece that is xenoverse.  10/10: Will continue to get owned repeatedly by the wonderfully challenging AI

EDIT: Since everyone voted down my review because they can't comprehend how an unplayable game could possibly be a bad thing, let's pretend for a second that the servers aren't terrible and that the game works as intended. Here is what you need to know:  PROS: - Decent imitation of the art style - Functional port with few bugs (UPDATE!!!: APPARENTLY IT APPEARS THAT SERVER CONNECTIVITY IS LINKED TO FPS, ALONG WITH THE EFFICACY OF IN-GAME ITEMS AND PERHAPS EVEN DROP RATES, HA HA IT'S LIKE DARK SOULS 2 AGAIN SO DISREGARD THIS PRO) - Option to turn off the embarrassing English dialogue - Probably the first (only?) Dragon Ball game to feature a custom character without being a Street Fighter ripoff  CONS: - Hub world replaces your menu, forcing you to spend up to 40 seconds (I counted) before you can even begin loading a story mission... After you've already loaded the hub. If this were a menu this would take less than one second. - Even when servers work, the matchmaking takes a great deal of time to start a game up and, generally speaking, it's not even worth it to play with other players unless you go in for a versus match. - There are actually very few customization options for your custom character; additionally, the best race is the 'Saiyan' because it can transform and then repeatedly throw out super ki blasts everywhere constantly, effectively giving it about 100 times the reach of other races. As far as equipment goes, you cannot customize it whatsoever, and you must only wear the items that fit your build. In other words everyone of the same build will look the same - a (better be female) Saiyan with a certain set of equipment and barely varying physical characteristics. - The combat system is simplistic and button-mashy. Combos are all the same between races except for animations, and given that your only real defensive option in the long run is a retaliatory 'evasive skill' that staggers your opponents (which you will only be successfully using to break a combo once in a blue moon) the bottom line is to attack ASAP with no regard to your health. This means the race that is best at attacking - the Saiyan - is the best race. In some of the single player games if you are under attack by more than one enemy, as happens almost every stage, there is the possibility of being endlessly comboed forever and this will definitely happen to you at some point. Locking on to an appropriate enemy when two or more are in range can be a nightmare. - In addition, combat depends heavily on meta-Dragon-Ball-mechanics like health potions, equipment, trinity-style ganking, and killing enemies with the charge up of a skill rather than the skill itself. What this translates to is a gimmicky and rather janky corruption of what you might have read in the comic or watched in the show. - Grinding is pretty much necessary to beat the story, which probably shouldn't be surprising since the game is based off of Dragon Ball Z and the subsequent embarrassments like GT and Minus, but it doesn't even take the appearance of real training. In order to get the best equipment you have to play some missions as many as 50 times, and when they each take about 5 minutes to complete, not including the time it takes to jog over to the mission terminal, you can see how this can neatly pad out the game by about 4 hours just to have a slightly greater ki capacity, for example. Meanwhile enemies in the story inexplicably gain the ability to resist any stunlock, even in the middle of melee super attacks, and gain health and attack strength at a rate disproportional to what one could attain naturally through leveling, so you must also grind for experience like a traditional JRPG. - You will want to beat the story, because this is the only way to unlock additional character slots. In other words you must grind in order to have more than one custom character: perhaps for at least 10 hours or more. You must also beat the story to unlock the full functionality of the hub world (aka slow menu). - The story is GT-level, adding more business with demons and kais and what not. Somehow Dimps managed to write a story that makes it plausible for a custom character to interact in some classic battles, but in perhaps the laziest way possible. If the characters are capable of time travel then why not go back far enough to erase the possibility of Mira gaining power in the first place? If Trunks wished for a warrior strong enough to correct the errors in the timeline then why is grinding necessary? Why didn't Trunks just wish for the strength to do it himself, or immortality, and then do it himself? Basically just a Star Trek: First Contact (read: excuse for an action movie with cheap sets) storyline. - The music is totally forgettable except for the stuff they drill into your head at the login screen and hub. The former literally sounds like a track from an EA sports title and the latter actually sounds like something you might plausibly hear in the Dragon Ball/Z/GT soundtrack, while the rest is bad 80s porno music, not unlike Dynasty Warriors, but worse. Too much guitar, not enough synth, brass, woodwinds, or synth drums. Sounds more like the Saliva/Breaking Benjamin-loving soundtrack of the Pioneer and early FUNimation releases than Dragon Ball. - Without so much padding, the story would be less than an hour long. This is not a joke. In this regard it is like a fighting game (but not MK9 or 10 or Injustice because, you know, fighting games with extensive single player stories have never existed). - If you make a mistake with your build, you also have to grind for the ability to respec (dragon balls). - To illustrate just how much of an RPG this is, I once went into a fight against 3 of the Ginyu Tokusentai with my character who had beaten Freeza single-handedly and 2 perfect Cells alongside me. I lost. There are no difficulty options, only character levels. - 'Hercule' - If you care, there are various typos regarding some adult baby who still doesn't understand the difference between 'your' and 'you're' despite developing a game that simulates three-dimensional classical dynamics. - The control scheme is utterly unintuitive and cumbersome. Block is LB, which only activates after a second or so of character idling (fitting in with the idea that defense is useless), even if you are locked on, you still can't always fly a direct path to your target when there's nothing between you (usually when they are directly beneath you), switching targets is practically random which is pretty much death when there are more than 2 enemies within a few feet of you (in the story mode there's a stage where you must fight 5 Buus, kill yourself), and the DBZ-zanzoken flicker ability which is said to be directional is barely functional, merely making you reappear at the same spot after disappearing. Do the devs realize that this move represents the characters moving at a radial velocity that can't be tracked by the eye, not just disappearing? - You will experience a wide variety of fake difficulty mechanics. Many enemies vs. you, numerically hard enemies without precedent (take the fights against 'Hercule' for instance), etc.  Character limit. You get the point.

Albino Lullaby. A game with a well deserved horror tag, and a semi well placed pricetag all things considering. Now the game itself isn't particularly 'Scary', It goes down the route of making you think. With that said looking at the game it has a genuinely good asthetic to it with the dreak and almost cel-shaded graphics paired with the low light in a large portion of the game. The games graphics also really cater to the design of the monsters/enemies trying to chase you down throughtout the game, as it lets them fit in with the rest of the world and gives a feel as though you could actual be in the characters position. Now even saying that the scenery and the monsters blend together to form a fine cream of a game with alot to think about after playing it means nothing with the overall scheme of things. The plot is generally slowly unravelled throughout the game with notes that pattern the scenery around you which can occasionally be easy to miss while running from the giant thumb monsters. And what story you do get is rather vague which adds to you having to think about what is actually going on but leaves major holes in the whole swing of things. But that's neither here nor there. The gameplay is what we want to know about. The gameplay is rather dull and leaves quite a bit to be desired to be honest but truly I can't say much since it's right along the lines of amnesia minus the one item that lets you throw these horrific faces for a spin stunning them temporarily. The gameplay focuses around stealthing past these things which honestly isn't too hard overall and then proceeding to hit buttons in no specific order and proceeding to hit another button in another location which opens something in order to progress this doesnt bring alot of variety into the game to be honest and it's required you do it 3-4 times alone in just these chapter. This game could honestly benefit from some decent puzzles. Moving on from that the combat is rather wonky and solely exists with one weapons the 'Buck Clicker' which completely obstructs your view from anything withing a 180 degree radius in front of yourself leaving it to help the thumbs more than yourself. And it's really just annoying to deal with as soon as you fire it you must rush to another charger station to charge it since it's only a one use thing. And to aim you sort of just wiggle your mouse while the obstruction of view green bubble is in your way. So overall the combat is rather lame and wonky and really not needed or atleast it wouldn't be if the game didn't go the route of DS2 and equate numbers with difficulty. Now I will put that last sentence into perspective towards the end of this 1 chapter that is out currently you bump into Buck who was locked up for who knows what reason anyway the entire fight is based around a mechanic of luring him in and out of doors to lock him up so you can progress in the game. This is easier said than done with roughly 20-30 monstrous heads hot on your tail at just about any given time. Now really it only takes 3 in a clump to end your game damn near instantaniously so 20-30 is really just there to ♥♥♥♥ you off like it did with me. So you lead Buck through these doors and if you can somehow manage to avoid not only that mummified turd as well as his lackeys you are rewarded with the way out. Anyway I'm done speaking about the game. Should you buy it? Yes. To this games credit yes indeed it is wonky beyond recognition and does deserve a fair amount of harsh critic but it does not deserve to be burned at the stake as it was a complete breath of fresh air deviating from the norm of crappy jumpscares and slender clones that litter the market today. So yes absolutely you should buy it and check it out even if the ending for this chapter is rather lackluster and goes towards the absolute bull-♥♥♥♥ end of things it's worth checking out and getting something other than lackluster 'horror' getting something you can ponder on is nice in todays overflowed crap market. So go ahead and buy the game as I said for 10 bucks you can't beat it, it's a one of a kind experience. And what else are you gonna use that money on a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ twin stick shooter that's still in beta? Amnesia is my #1 favorite Otoge aside from Uta no Prince Sama. The plot of each route is very different and interesting. Even the bad endings are really eye opening. You play as a female protagonist who has Amnesia, so her character is basically bland. (I don't blame her though. If you had Amnesia, you wouldn't have much to say either. Especially if you don't remember anyone in your previous state)  While she does have her moments of sassyness, you, the player makes up her character for whether you are going to the good, normal, or bad ends.  Every character in the game has a unique personality along with wonderful art and backgrounds. You'll also encounter numerous obstacles to get through, so choose your answers wisely!  Orion will talk a lot, but that's because you have lost your memories...and he is also worried for you so don't mind him :) He's a real sweet heart.  I suppose the only con to this game would be that it lacks the amount of save slots. So be careful to save your files because it doesn't give you as many XD This game definitely has a LOT of mystery, and it'll be confusing and a hassle to get through the first few days, but hey..it's a mystery game that you, yourself...as the heroine should solve. :P

Honestly, it seems that the developer(s) was too lazy to make a simple tutorial about the basics of the game, like how to return to the ship and what the harvestable resources do. As a result, this game is very difficult to get into. I rate 2/5 until the developer(s) decides to explain the game to newcomers with some sort of tutorial. Also, the game is buggy as hell.  In short, make a tutorial and keep developing the game; it's not done yet.

Let's get the highlights out of the way: it is awesome to have American football back on the PC. The play-calling is pretty good, there are a ton of formations to choose from on offense and defense, and there is an announcer.  Unfortunately everything else is bad.     Bad animations    Awful blocking AI    Passing the ball on offense is essentially just luck    Defending the pass is essentially just luck    Only 5-minute quarters, with a '1-minute warning' at the end of each half   And that's not even getting into the truly ridiculous, such as spelling nickel defense wrong ('nickle'?) or allowing the kicking team the option of an onside kick, but not allowing the receiving team the option to defend against an onside kick.  Great idea to bring American football back to the PC...but if you're interested in this because you want to play American football and the game is relatively cheap now, don't do it. You'd be better off finding a copy of Madden 08.

Score: 8/10  Psychonauts is a beautiful cartoonish game of jumping into the twisted mines of various people. The powers are quite enjoyable and the levels are designed quite well for the most part.   The only bad portion in my opinion is the later in the game jumping segments. You may jump to a ledge and not grab it several times. The controls could be much better. You may not jump after pushing jump over and over again. Other then that this game does force you to do collectables which may annoy some people.   The missions to balance the minds of individuals are quite unique. Each mind you enter is a new experience and a new story unique to that character. The over arching story is enough to keep you interesting and wanting to keep going.

A lot of the negative reviews here are because of poor performance. I was one of the lucky people who actually had perfect performance from day 1, so just bear that in mind. I do believe, however, that at the time of writing this now there has been a PC patch which has solved the issues for most people.  This game was a very worthy successor to the first, and I played through it twice almost non stop (aside from eating, sleeping and going to work obviously). There were a lot of mechanics that were pulled across from the first game, which gave it that familiar feel and I was able to get going pretty quickly. I played as Corvo the first time through, which was more similar to the first game than Emily's powerset. The powers and mechanics were smooth and effective, providing a large range of ways that each level could be approached.  The world felt alive, and was very well designed. There are nooks and crannies and secrets everywhere, and a whole multitude of different routes you can take to reach your destination, if you are willing to look for them. Both low and high chaos (ie stealth vs brawl-ish) play-throughs were well catered for, which is not something you find verry often.  My first playthrough, as Corvo, took me about 20 hours. I went for full stealth with no kills. I subsequently played through a Emily with high chaos, which took me about 4.5 hours as I already knew the levels inside out thanks to playing stealth first.  I can wholeheartedly recommend this game, and I'd be tempted to say it was my favourite game of 2016!

While clearing the infected cells you will fight with various enemies and dangerous bosses.  The general gameplay consists in controlling more smaller bots in fighting with parasites.  During the game you will be able to reveal unique abilities which you can combine with each other. - Recommended!

Hell, I had A LOT of fun with this game. Only few games are as addictive as Sonic Generations. Why I still don't recommend it? That's simple: The controls are some of the worst I've ever experienced. Especially if you want to earn the S rank in all 9 stages and in all 90 challenges (like I did), it's frustrating as hell. The relatively high amount of bugs and freezes I experienced really didn't make me feel better at all. I don't know how often I was about to smash my gamepad in these 15 hours and I'm really surprised that I didn't do it. Also, after 8 really great and really fast stages, SEGA suddenly pulled the break. After being able to complete each stage in 1-3 minutes, you get to play a really slow final stage which have you play for at least 10 boring minutes on your first try. And you know what's funny? The final boss is even worse. I didn't even beat him because it was too boring. Even in comparison with the final stage this boss is extremely tedious and frustrating. Flying around as Super Sonic for almost 10 minutes without having anything to do despite of holding the boost button and repeating this over and over again just because you didn't collect some rings (which are being burned to keep Super Sonic alive) really isn't my thing. Seriously, I've never been so bored by a game in my life so far.  Yes, I had a lot of fun. But I also had lots of frustration and the extremely disappointing ending finally made me get fed up with this game.

Alan wake is an survival horror game. It isn't really a game it's more of an interactive movie. The gameplay parts are just tedious parts added on too call it a game, instead of the movie it wants to be. The gameplay is completely build around the mechanic that you need to shine light on enemies so you can shoot them after it. While it's a nice concept, but it get repetitive after a while. The gameplay parts are just disrupting the story. Non of your actions has any influences on the game world.  The story of the game is fine. They have set a great creepy atmosphere. Too bad they made the gameplay not scary. Your always armed to the teeth. If enemies spawn behind you they show them with a cinematic. So you are never surprised by enemies. You also find pages of a manuscript that foretells events. It's like they wanted to make a scary game, but not to scary so it would scare some players away. The scariest part of the game is probably Alan's wife. Her face looks like she got some tumours growing in her skull. And also the lipsyncing looks horrible most of the time.  Alan Wake does what most modern games do. It focuses too much on the story instead of the gameplay that it becomes an interactive movie. That may work for most of the new mainstream gamers. For me it should have been a movie or a book instead of a game.

Best tactical shooter game ever made, yet. It's arcade-ish yet realistic to an extend and keeps you entertained most of the time. Story mode is fun and can be played with a friend, Terrorist Hunt mode is very enjoyable and you can be played with 4 people (16 if modded). Can't say I enjoy the Versus mode much because it's simply full of the usual 'online shooter game ♥♥♥♥♥♥s' and 5 year olds, but to be honest, not as much as the more popular games of this genre. The customisation in this game is amazing and there's an option for choosing gender, weapon mods, clothings and armour.  All that said, the game has an awful sound bug that rarely occurs randomly, constant gunfire, aside from that and the bots with perfect but fallible aim everything is pretty much perfect in this game. They don't make games like this anymore. Ubisoft and Punkbuster aren't supporting the game anymore, but It's cheap and very fun, and still has a great friendly multiplayer community, well worth the money.

This game is a lot of fun and has plenty of potential if it is updated further. The basic gameplay mechanics are fun and is very entertaining if you play militarily.   Many of the problems in this game lie in diplomacy. I found the game nearly impossible to understand playing it from a diplomatic angle. For example there are diplomacy points that you supposedly gain from researching certain techs and building embassies. I still have yet to see any effect of these diplomacy points in my interaction with other races, and there is no concrete change to my standing with them. When I trade with other factions, I often can't propose any treaties without any explanation why. There are also many minor annoying bugs like the income slider changing rates slightly without using it, and certain technologies not doing what they are supposed to (research treaty is one example I can think of).   A very major (and ridiculous) problem I've encountered with the game is United Planets voting. The AI is incompetent in this regard. In one game I played, all of my allies voted for an enemy (who we were ALL at war with) giving the enemy a stat boost.   I like this game and have enjoyed playing it, however, I would recommend holding off on buying it until it is further polished.

Murdered is an interesting yet flawed game. It has some good ideas but fails to execute them in a coherent fashion. Like all games created by numerous development houses spread across the globe it lacks a singular vision. Triple A games need painstaking pre production in order to succeed. Apparently the Ghost theme was pitched at Airtight by one of Square's creative directors.  The game isn’t unholy bad. It runs well on my basic PC. The voice work and plentiful cutscenes flesh out the story. Which is an standard detective yawn with Salem trial overtones. There’s a whole host of collectables which unlock side story narratives. These are a strong point of the game and pad out what would be a rather limited experience.  There is one striking feature / bug that the game has. Once you finish the main story arc, you cannot go back into the town and complete / solve any of the side missions you may have missed without starting a brand new game. This soured my overall opinion of the title. A shocking oversight that could have been fixed by a small patch. Unfortunately this will never happen as Square shuttered Airtight soon after the release of the game.  Its a pity Airtight aren't around to make a follow up. I enjoy a good adventure story and for the most part Murdered delivers.

During dramatic quick time events, like trying to hold onto a giant boss you are climbing, Gabriel doesnt respond to your button press. You either flail around and fall off or flail around and dont fall off. Stuff like this creates a disconnect between you and what is happening and can cause you to fail because you question your own timing. It wants to be an epic experience but glosses over all the little things. Get it cheap

This is without a doubt one of the greatest titles I have ever played. Being a big fan of the original Bioshock, I felt the need to go back and play it's 'spiritual' predecessor. I knew coming in that the game was probably going to have some hinderances based on the fact that it is an old-school game but I have to say, I can't really find anything here that I would consider a major hinderance. It's fairly paced, heavy sci-fi, and immersive. I want to focus on that last one for a little bit. What makes this game immersive is the level of interaction you have within the game: actuallly being involved with the research process by looking for chemicals, having to find the right OS board and installing it, manually putting in power cells; it's these little things that really put you in the game.   Although the game overall is great, there are a few issues here and there. The combat is ok, althought the melee is a little BS with how slow your swing is, that problem goes away once you find the laser rapier aka System Shock's lightsaber :). Gun degradation is way too fast, but works for the survival horror angle that the game takes. There isn't much reason for you to even focus on your psy abilities, although this might just be because of my own personal playing style. Most of the voice acting is laughable: cheesy accents and lines, one scientist sounds more like a stoned surfer dude than a scientist. Dr. Polito is the only one who seems to give a good performance.  The twist is classic but if you know anything about the game, you probably know what it is already.

Hrm, I really liked the concept and ideas behind it. Art and characters were cute too. Unsure if there's just hidden content, but it starts at Act 2 and ends on Act 5. Act 2,3 were quite nice and interesting. Act 4,5 I found impossible to get through without a walkthrough for the most part.  Even if you're into coding, it just gets so very busy (like for 1 object, there could be 6 code groups, which also have another sub-code within each of them), and there's barely any clues as to which variable(s) out of all that code you should mess with. One wrong step, you can 'crash' your game, and have to restart the room. And later on, there's this huge place with dozens of code books, which like above, you'll have no idea what's important and what to edit.  Sometimes I would do something correctly, but still get an in-game crash, it just happens randomly (making me think I did something wrong and waste time trying other things), but other times, and unsure if just my computer (which is a nicely specced desktop), the game would freeze/crash, and I would have to end the process and restart the game entirely. Luckily it autosaves each time you go into a room. -------  Overall, it's a very unique game, with great ideas, just can get kind of confusing, but I'm sure some would enjoy it. Personally the frequency of real crashes and strange increase in coding difficulty were a bit off-putting, so I'm giving it a 6/10, but would still recommend it.

Dungeon of the Endless is close to a tower defence game. I mean close as in theres a center point (your core) you have to defend and you can set up towers to defend your core and thats about all it has incommon with tower defence games. Instead of sitting back and just defending wave after wave you have to use your heroes to open doors which has a pseudo RNG chance of spawning waves of enemies. To advance further you need heroes, in other terms if you lose all your heroes you lose, if your core is killed you lose. I really enjoy this game but there are some infuriating mechanics and general hero balance just isn't on point. You will find out who the strong characters are very quickly when playing on the harder difficulty. But why are some heroes so bad? tankyness is about all it comes down to but ontop of that the people who are squishy typically do less than the tanky people unless they're some utlitity focus character or an operator in which case those are very strong in their own different ways, but squishy combat focused heroes? Trash and dead weight. The light mechanic is interesting but for the most part it will screw over your runs when you get unlucky and don't get dust(the currency that gives you light) from the RNG. Dust (light currency) is used to power up rooms, when rooms are powered you can set up turrets and modules that yields you more income on resources. But if you don't have the amount of dust to power rooms its dark and enemies can spawn inside these rooms unless you place a hero you own inside of them. Of all the balance designs they came up with they decided to make dust (the most game making or breaking resource) nearly 100% RNG with the only way to reliably light up rooms requires a massive amount of the resource 'economy'. Despite my dislike over some mechanics and the general balance of the game I still recommend this game but if you are looking for a tower defence like the ones you would find off of armor games this is next to nothing like those.

This game is basically a turn based 'choose your own adventure' game executed as a visual novel, with some roleplaying elements.  You start as a 14 year old princess, soon-to-be adult ruler of your kingdom. You have to make it to your 15th birthday in order to be crowned as Queen. That's in 40 weeks. Basically 40 turns of learning skills, managing your mood - which alters your efficiency to learn certain skills - and reacting to situations. 40 weeks to survive to be the Queen!  Some elements are always present - your mother deceased recently, and you start out as depressed (thus you're more tuned to learn artistic things than conversations initially), plus your dad asks you about state affairs sometimes. Some come random to bite your cute royal bottom - like a box of suspicious but very delicious-looking chocolates, or an invasion. Certain things - such as magic - should be earned first and then learned by hard work.  Adorable protagonist, nice atmosphere, some UI quirks, jokes and references, little unlockables, gameplay mainly driven by trial and error. It's a rather lovely game if you have the eye for it, but if you don't like CYOA or visual novels you're in for a letdown.    Here is an official video   showing much of the game's UI.

At first glance, this game seems to be your run-of-the-mill girly visual novel, however this is not the case. Long Live the Queen is, in my opinion, is a fun and almost addictive simulation/strategy game, inside a visual novel. You do not have to be female to enjoy this tittle, the plot and choices are far from girly (I doubt many little girl games have assassins, treachorous family, and murder). Just because the main character is female and has pink hair doesn't mean the game is only female oriented, it's like saying Tomb Raider is for preschool girls because there's a GIRL!!!  As for gameplay, the mechanics are easy to learn, picking classes to earn experience in certain traits (like swords and history) and then having to use these skills in scenarioes. You can play as a loved queen, or just like a character from Game of Thrones, either being beloved or feared by all. Unlike a dating sim, failing these scenarioes wont lead to a 'bad date' but instead lead to revolutions, lack of trust, and even the main character, Elodie, DYING. And you will die a lot in your quest towards coronation, each death avoidable, if you invest in the propper skills. Of course there are visual novel/dating sim features, like you can pursue a bachelor (or a couple bachelorettes), but the main focus of the game is on alloting skills to survive the year before your coronation.  Do not let the cute girl fool you, Long Live the Queen is a game that will satisfy either gender, giving you a strategy game with MASSIVE replayability and the art style and body of a visual novel.

If you love magic and tire of playing people, this is the only game there is for playing against actual ai that i know about. Finally you get to construct your own decks, the downside is to keep you from being overpowered they put in restrictions like only 3 uncommon 2 rare or 1 mythic of the same card per deck. This restriction is only for you however and not the computer, as i have played decks where he plays the same rare creature 3 turns in a row.   I was really disappointed in the garruk campaign as it forces you to use his deck against a bunch of anti you decks which I found to be annoying rather than challenging. Your deck is black and green but as an example, your opponent is all black so when you play your only 3/3 flying creature to protect yourself from his Nightmare all you can destroy is your own green creature when it comes into play and you probably have one since the low mana cost creatures in the deck are all green. Cpu takes his turn and drops cards with swampwalk and opponents creature gets -1/-1 for each swamp you control, then corrupts you. Poor game design and not fun, you cant adjust that deck at all and by the time i got to the all black opponent and played him a few times, this campaign lost all its remaining fun factor.   The second big downside to this game is the only way to get the BEST cards is to go online and play other people. EVERY other magic game in the history of magic (except the cult classic shandalar, which people have been beggin for another of for like 20 years) is about fighting other real players, I bought this SPECIFICALLY to NOT play against other people. My buddies flat out dont like playing me in magic, they have told me this and some say me and my decks are what made them quit the trading card game. This game is one of the games that I grew up playing. After several years of hoping this game would appear on Steam (My physical copy is getting old), it finally made it and I got it and the first game in a heartbeat. The game is the best space-flight combat simulator that I have ever played. Rich with its own lore and story, Freespace 2 has much to offer. Two different storylines (The main campaign and the Hammer of Light) are available right off the bat to get you started. The main campaign gives you an in-depth tutorial on how to play (I play with the keyboard because I think it is easier) and it has joystick combatability.   Another nice thing about this game is the amount of support it has from its fanbase. Freespace Open is a mod that updates the graphics to a more modern equivalent and it opens you up to different mods that include Battlestar Galactica, Wing Commander, and there's even a Star Wars mod.   In conclusion, I must recommend this game to everyone who sees it. There were some problems with the launch of this game on Steam, but if you are using Freespace Open and follow one of the guides, you will no problem getting this game correctly instaled.  9.5/10
