This is an old school tactical RPG. It is hard. It makes you think hard to beat the next fight and it makes you manage your resources (food and wounds). Every decision you make impacts your success or failure, including where to travel next and whether you will fight or flee. If you loved the unforgiving games of the 80's, this is for you. Each turn is a chess move that can mean the difference between success and failure. Save often! Like Dark Souls, this game gives you the feeling of victory when you finally beat that enemy that blocks your way. If you like easy games, look elsewhere.

ADOM  by many measures, is one of  the few  classic roguelikes that started it all.  In developement since 1994, and by reading over its history, I can say it has come a long way. This title can certainly hold its own right up there with the best of modern games. Some may have you believe a single person (Thomas Biskup) built this, but as is stated in the credits, the end product is the result of thousands of peoples hard work and ideas.  Certainly Mr. Biskup deserves the lion's share of credit, but compared to other indie titles, this is hardly something that came out of someones basement over the weekend.    Classic roguelikes are nearly infinite in replayability, and possibility.  Sure, there is a certain trade-off between user interface, graphics, and learning curve that pays out with a giant world with metric tons of gameplay and fun.  Yes, its difficult. Yes, you will need to read lots (thank you in-game manual). Yes, it is FUN.    Pros:  +Timeless game that has been shaped over 20 years, which is reflected by system complexity that goes unmatched in modern games. +The ability to seemlessly (simply push F10) switch between classic ASCII and tiles. +Tiles and music are beautifully done. +Many modes that are all well recieved. In example, Crowd mode alters the difficulty of your game by how many Steam friends you have that play  ADOM . This is fresh and certainly encourages community. +++Highscores leaderboards! +++Ghosts of your fallen Steam friends show up in your game lol. +Both keyboard and mouse support. I find the control scheme very intuitive. The updated user interface is very nice IMO. +In-depth tutorial. +Great community. +Super active developement.   Cons:  -Yeah there is a steep learning curve, but that only applies to people that are new to roguelikes. Get through it, or simply realize these types of games are not your cup of tea.   If you have any interest in PC gaming history at all, this title deserves to be in your library. What a gem.  It amazes me how this classic has evolved over time and can still outcompete modern titles.

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: I own every title in the Souls Franchise, save for Bloodborne, and Scholar of the First Sin is practically the only one I have for PC. Comparing it to the vanilla release, I really enjoy it. The new enemy layouts fit the areas they are in, item locations have changed and it feels refreshing to jump into again. I do get cocky from time to time and get overwhelmed by enemies because I am used to the vanilla layouts but I like what they did. More NPC's to summon if youre a solo player, increased and updated multiplayer capability, new phantom NPC's invade areas, the Forlorn invaders, it is truly a fun update to an existing title. I recommend this to the folks who are 'well-versed' to the franchise and give it a go-around or two. The DLC areas remain unchanged but theyre still fun to go through. If you are new to the Souls genre, give it a shot. Dark Souls 2 is by far waaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaay easier then Dark Souls 1 and Demons Souls.

app_name: Death to Spies: Moment of Truth
review_text: Ohhhoooo boy THIS GAME. After a couple of years I could finaly play it again, and it has aged well. Like a fine wine, Death to Spies: Moment of Truth is best enjoyed in short sips. Take your time with it and feel how its taste brings you into another world.  Set in WW2 and the early days of the cold war, you play as a Russian spy and infiltrate German bases and camps. There are also 3 missions in the USA, the UK and a Soviet base (with a bunch of filthy traitors! hrrrng!). The levels (except for the USA level) are AMAZING. Even tough the 'objective zone' isn't always that huge, you have the feeling that you could travel the whole world from your current location. Detailed bases and vehicles decorate a realistic, living world that really sucks you into the game. There are tons of weapons, uniforms, driveable vehicles and many different ways to solve a problem.   Tiny minus point is the German writting and speech in some of the levels, but this shouldn't bother anyone too much.

Many people seem to be complaining about Marble Age being a 'glorified Flash game'. Well, it really quite literally is, considering that is the technology it uses (and you can find an earlier position in the series on those Flash games websites like Kongregate). But this isn't really a problem. In fact, The Binding of Isaac was a Flash game, too, and so what?  No, the problem with this game lies in its mechanics. The simplicity, combined with cute graphics, pretty nice music, and semi-educational content would have actually made it a great little title, but Marble Age's convoluted and unpredictable gameplay is what ruins everything about it.  To put it short, I have never seen a game this unbalanced. The progression of numbers (e.g. resources being required, your city's population etc.) is exponential, like in so many browser-based MMO strategies and other titles. But it is also so variable. Rather than being a smooth increase, it jumps all over the place. At one point you're suffocating, your city can't grow anymore, your tech research ground to a halt and you're passing turn after turn just to regain some military power. Then, you complete a goal and suddenly you have twice as much population as you had a moment ago, tons of new resources and several free technology levels. This pattern repeats from time to time, though you never really see it coming. It means that all the strategy goes to trash since the real way to progress is to fulfill whatever goals you have (or count on a lucky random event). What you're trying to do otherwise just doesn't matter.  In fact, the whole game is built in such a way that there is an invisible path you're supposed to be following. You don't quite know where it is, so you'll always stumble a bit. Stray too far from it and you reach a place you can't get out of. Manage to stay on track and the whole game becomes really easy. This is actually quite extreme — you can essentially lose a game if you do one thing wrong in the early stages, for instance.  (Here I'll note this isn't about following historical events or simply playing in an optimal way. The game is just constructed in such a manner that you have to make arbitrary decisions at right moments to be able to win.)  To be fair, the game does try to rescue you if you stray, giving you gifts through random events, but this only exposes the gameplay's faults even more — if the only way for me to get back on track is to accept those gifts, which are just enormous, then this just shows how big the difference between the 'right' play and the 'wrong' play is, even though what is right and wrong is decided arbitrarily.  It might seem like I'm complaining a lot about this subject but that really is something that ruins Marble Age for me. The game has more flaws than that (maybe I'll list some in the comments) but this is what makes it an unplayable rather than flawed title. Stay away from it unless you like gameplay that's about trying to guess what the developers intended for you to do at any particular stage.

I want to thank the developers and early access players for their efforts. This game came out the other side absolutely wonderful.   For starters: I stole children from Orcs and gave them to a Demon so that Demon would go on a date with a Dwarf. The Dwarf gave me sweet armor.  Short, medium and long term goals are available almost instantly. Crafting inititally seems to be a 'best' materials venture until you start to see different bonuses based on your desired play style. Almost every encounter can be navigated using your party strengths or preferences. There is just so much to keep you busy, constantly upgrading, gathering, building, fighting, recruiting, researching, leveling all while engaging in a terrific underlying story line that waits for you. So much thought went into every aspect of this game and nothing feels like a toss away or unfulfilled concept. I am already looking forward to my next playthrough with new Gods, new weapons, new villagers and new structures.   For me, this game is what we all envision when we decide to back a game. The progression through early access the developer's continued responses to player feedback is what sold me on this game. Honestly I feel like I could talk about this game for ages, but basically, I couldn't be happier that I decided to buy it.

What's there to say? This is literally one of the highest rated games I've ever seen on Steam and while this may not always be the most useful information, I can say from my experience that Undertale having a score of 92/100 is underrated!  It's been months since I finished Undertale and I still am amazed that Toby Fox pulled this off considering this is the first game he's published (he's mostly done music and video game mods).  Gameplay wise the game is simple to pick up, but gets harder and harder with each boss fight. It is story rich and full of interesting characters. Undertale also is well known for its awesome soundtrack which really helps bring the game to life in many scenes and boss battles.  I won't go into much more detail since it's Undertale, you probably already know, but if you don't avoid story spoilers at all costs! Game play is pretty straight forward, it's turn based and you can either choose to kill or show mercy to your opponents. Just remember, Undertale will always let you choose and sinisterly enough, Undertale never forgets. It judges you. So choose well.

WARNING FOR WINDOWS 10 USERS AND POSSIBLE OTHERS: The game got a post-Gamespy update that will cause the game to do all kinds of crazy display stuff and crash. What you need to do is turn off anti-aliasing by default by going into the game's installation folder, preferences folder, then preferences file and make sure AA_QUALITY says AA_QUALITY:AA_ OFF. This should let you launch the game. If you wish to turn on AA from there, you can do so in NVIDIA Control Panel or any other equivalent program, as turning it back on in the menus will cause it to crash again.  IF YOU GET REALLY BAD FRAMERATES AND LAGGY INPUT NO MATTER YOUR SPECS: Right-click the game in your library, click properties, then click Set Launch Options. In the text box, type the three characters '-ne' without the quotes and press okay. This should fix that problem for most people out there.  With that done, I'd like to say that although this game is a bit primitive compared to modern TW games, it still can be a very fun game.   Pros:  -The Campaign map is very no frills compared to modern TW games. You can build any building in any city, public disorder is easy to maintain, and it just lacks feature bloat of later games. The game is all about the battles, something I'd wish would come back after games like Attila.  - The battles can look and play better than modern TW games in some ways. Losing units will actually be beaten back and enveloped by superior forces, soldiers will respond to orders at different times, and the engagements can have some real clash and weight to them.   -There's actually strategy to the strategy map. I wasn't a huge fan of M2/Empire/Napoleon/Attila's maps because they tended to have lots of wide open plains, while Rome 1+2 and Shogun 2 had lots of limiting terrain features where you could create chokepoints and use terrain like mountains and rivers to your advantage.  Cons:  -Battles can be over a little too quickly. I kind of get whiplash at how fast I go from marching, to melee, to routing an enemy army.  -The flipside to things like independent unit movement is that response can be sluggish, sometimes often so bad that I can't tell if the game knows if I ordered them to do anything.  -Pathfinding can be garbage sometimes, especially in cities.  -You can still get cascade moments where your empire is powerful enough that you're just auto-resolving your way across large swaths of the map at some points.  Overall, I was surprised by how much I enjoyed the game despite not playing it since roughly around its launch. Once you get over some usability issues, there's a game here that's aged better than it has any right to.

At this point, everyone knows the lowdown on Aliens Colonial Marines. This is not a good game. It's a terrible game with a production so troubled it's legendary. It's a game built on lies and shady behavior.   Yet I admit I don't despise this game as much as it's reputation impiles. I won't lie, I consider this game a bit of a guilty pleasure to be honest. I guess I'm such a fanboy of the Alien films that I can find a teensy bit of enjoyment. It does look and sound like an Aliens game. The sound design in general is one of the game's few good points. It does feel like an Aliens game to me, albeit an awful one.   However, while I may find some stupid enjoyment from this game, by no means can I reccomend it. Honestly it probably is one of the worst games of the 2010's. Gameplay is by the numbers at best. The Xenos are barely in it. The main campaign is pitifully short. The AI is still very dumb, though better than on launch. Graphics as is are passable but nothing great. The story is ATROCTIOUS.   Perhaps it's because I got this game with all the DLC post launch, patched and for like 5 bucks. I may find some joy here in a 'So Bad It's Good' way but that is no reccomendation. Even with the admirable and impressive mod by Templar GFX the game is still not something I would reccommend.   Just get Alien Isolation or AVP 2010. Or even the classic AVP 2000.

'Sometimes a single look says it all.' - Rucks  ______________________________________________  I just beat this game a second time, because one time just wasn't enough. I wanted to go through this beauty once more.  Starting off, I can't think of any negative aspects or anything that disappointed me at all in this game. The gameplay is amazing, the visuals are breathtaking, the narrator wins me over by himself, and the soundtrack is gorgeous (never have I dared to play any kind of music over it).  Not only is the game beautiful, but it's got plenty of action. If you're a newcomer to this type of game, you'll find the normal difficulty to be challenging enough, but if not, you can turn the gods against you to make the game harder, like I did on my second playthrough. The weapons you use are varied, and each serve a specific set of purposes. Seeing as you can only bring two to the fight at once, you have to choose those that complement each other (a melee and a ranged weapon, for example), and upgrading them offers even more customization possibilities. Not only that, but as the enemies are constantly changing, you may need to change your weapons and upgrades to deal with them.    On this second playthrough I managed to survive the Ura invasion without any of my pets dying. Few things make me as relieved.    There are other systems in the game to make it easier or harder, but what really wins me over is the atmosphere. The quest you're engaged on seems infinite, but as you come closer to the end, you realize what's really at stake. The narrator does a brilliant job here, as he efficiently contributes to the current mood of the game, be it happy, exciting, sad, panic, etc.  If you've never played it, please change that. It's a damn good indie game, that's for sure. Meanwhile, I'll go beat the Who-Knows-Where stages.

So if you can ignore the fact that the game is pixelated and without anti-alising with just a scaled up 1024 x 768 resolution then go ahead. Else, don't buy this for PC. It look's like a game from 2005. If i would recommend it on a console i don't know since i didn't play enough of it to give an in-depth explanation of how all the mechanic works, but one thing is sure. It's a piece of crap on PC.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: This game is fantastic except for the fact that they got rid of the family tree. not cool man. I like building a dynasty/legacy. the general mechanic sucks in that way. mods that go up to 12 turns a year make it a little better. the battles are great. AI is solid. the character models are gorgeous, which oddly enough is a problem. they are so gorgeous, but there are so many of them, and they are so unique and awesome looking that it makes it hard to pay attention to any of them at any given time so it just sort of becomes a huge overstimulating mess if you zoom in to watch any part of the battle. also, the battles are way too fast paced. even enormous battles sometimes last about 10 minutes, which again, makes it really hard to enjoy any of the visuals. also, every total war game before this one had better castle seige mechanics. the cities in this one, just seem WAY TOO BIG. the battles always happen on basically 1/10th of the entire city, and the AI annoyingly takes advatnage of this and sends units to the opposite side of the city to attack, making castle seiges just a total pain in the butt. Shogun 2 is better than this game in pretty much every aspect. however, i could not help but enjoy playing as the macedonians and re-taking over the success states. this game is fun, its just disappointing due to the fact that literally every game before it was better. if you love history and the total war franchise, shogun 2 is the best one. this one is good, but arguably not even as good as medieval 2. now they made a medieval 3 and did not make it as a big money grab and actually took their time with it and did not make 50 DLC's...then i would pay attention to this franchise again. i recommend rome 2, but shogun 2 is better. be Athens and conquer trade in the mediterranean. super fun.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: As underwhelming as the release of Rome 2 was compared to the promise. I must say that the game has become more enjoyable. There are still a few things that need to be corrected.   1. The AI, while aggressive is completely clueless in siege battles.  2. The AI is too aggressive, thank God they fixed the quick save issue but...it shouldn't take me 2 days to complete 1 turn. 3. Recriutment, though the Garrison only really needs Pikemen, the fact that you cannot recruit to your town or city is a departure from every Total War game before it. It does take some getting used to. 4. Why can't we recruit nobles to help with happiness? Every turn I face slave revolt imminent from at least 10 cities.   All in all though you do have battles remniscent of Medieval 2 or Shogun 2 (One thing both of those game had was that the seige battles worked). Would I buy it at release again? Probably not but I would definitely have bought it during the holiday sale.

Dying Light is Techlands attempt at making their own version of Dead Island after breaking off from Deep Silver.  On the surface the game is alright, the story is nothing too special though there are some moments where it might hit you in the feels if you grew attached to some of the characters, maybe not since you don't get much interaction with a lot of them throughout the game.  The gameplay is where it really shows its strengths though, whilst it has a lot of things reminiscent of Dead Island like craftable mods, leveling/ranking system and so on, one thing that is drastically different is the implementation of parkour into its gameplay ala Mirror's Edge style.  I will give that the parkour is a lot of fun to play around with especially with the Grappling hook which you unlock later in the game as you progress ranks. It lets you glide across the starting map and later on Sector 0 as you progress through the story, however that said its not always clear about exactly how extensive the range of what you can grab onto which can lead to a halt in flow or in some cases, death by gravity.  There are colletibles in this game but I didn't care much for them and challenges are also present do 'x' in 'x' time, you know the drill. Some can be hard to do though and for someone like me who can sometimes take a game too seriously it does not help when the challenge giver insults you sometimes after failing said challenge.  The crafting system is decent enough allowing you to craft things from medkits to throwing stars to elemental explosives later on when you unlock the ability to.   However that said, the last boss of the game is an absolute joke, its basically one big QTE and you'd think they would have not done this since the last boss from Dead Island was good though bs in it's own right but that's just my opinion.  In summary though I think it's a game worth playing for sure, the crafting system allows for some nice weapons, the parkour is fun and overall a good time. Though like most games it has its share of flaws. Brutal: Light armor and quick weapons  True, one could argue that all playstyles should be equally easy to play but if you're hit with a 200pound axe to the chest, you're dead without heavy armor in one strike. I'm giving this game a thumbs up because it IS a good game and I did enjoy it, but to be honest it was also disappointing in a lot of ways, but only because it isn't nearly as good as previous games from this developer, Frictional Games. It's also quite short for the price- took me 10 hours to complete.  There are really no puzzles (although now that I look at the game description I see there was no claim that it would include puzzles, so fair enough) and there is no choice. The character you pilot is a huge idiot.. having some dialog options for him, other than his default idiocy, would have been nice. You do get to make a few hard ethical choices in a few spots, but they are ultimately inconsequential to the story. And sometimes you are forced into doing unethical things.   It's an extremely linear game without much mental involvement on your part. You are pretty much there to press the walk button and watch beautiful, eerie, and horrifying scenery roll by while listening to another character tell you exactly what to do at every step. On the flip side, the environments and the story are both top notch.  You know how in most video games, every map looks sorta the same? Like the same assets are just rearranged in a different configuration? Well Frictional Games doesn't do that. Every area looks absolutely unique. You'll never feel like you've seen this room 10 times already. Hell, I saw pieces of clutter that I don't think appeared in more than one place. Any other game of this sort, which has no combat or puzzle solving and is basically just walking, would have become tedious quickly without this attention to detail. It really makes the world feel real. (But it should also tell you something that the most positive thing I can describe about the game is the setting.)  So all in all.. not a terrible game, but not great either. Short, not very challenging, but with a good story and atmosphere.

Ouch. That's all I SHOULD say. But I feel the need to explain. The worst voice acting I have ever encountered and I am including Gemini Wars in that comparison. It sounds as if one guy did the voices for all the characters.  The character sprites are a mess. The graphics are not TOO bad considering the game as a whole. I like RPG games and can forgive them a lot. IF you can get past the voice and the horrific translation work then it is enjoyable but considering this is the same people who brought us Space wolves.... Yeah.... The story is there but I have trouble finding it. You know the objective of the game from the start, it sits at the top of your quest log. The problem is you are never given this quest. You simply start with it. You are given no back story for your character, No idea why you are there or what you are supposed to do.  The other unfortunate thing is the dire need for a spell checker. A copy and paste into a word processor can solve any number of problems, there are many free programs that would have done the job. The music score is nice though, has a catchy tune I like. The RPG system is a little limiting, almost Spartan in its design. It is a modification of the S.P.E.C.I.A.L system used by the FALLOUT games. All they have done is change Endurance for Constitution. It works well and like Fallout you must weigh up what you will need at the games start against what you will need later as more and better weapons become available. It is a tried and tested equation that makes sense.  There are a lot of good idea's in this game and some even come to work well, but the game as a whole has just got too many weights around its ankle to stay a float.  Story: Absent presumed dead. Graphics: Passable. Voice acting: Monotone and mismatched. Translation: Lost there-in. Game play: Relatively straightforward.  Quest continuity: Following its own tail.

This kinetic novel follows the point of view of a young man who has seen more combat than he should have. He enters a mysterious facility and meets a girl who has more to her than meets the eye. No, I promise you I have not confused eden* with Planetarian. That said, if you enjoyed Planetarian, then I think you are very likely to enjoy eden*.  The visuals in eden* are good. In addition to having a variety of poses and facial expressions for each character, the characters are shown to blink and their lips sync with the voice acting. There are many CGs and each one is beautiful to see. Several locations even have multiple CGs and this gives the impression of a changing camera angle. I like it.  As eden* is a visual novel, the topic of H-scenes should be raised. I did some research and discovered that the H-scenes are apparently bonus content to unlock in the main menu after completing the kinetic novel once. This means that the H-scenes are not at all relevant to developing the canon storyline. So don’t worry about characters suddenly referring to something that happened during a deleted H-scene. That said, I also read that the H-scenes add more depth to each character despite being non-canon. Anyway, it should be well known now that if you want to see H-scenes then you should buy directly from MangaGamer.  The sound is good. The background music sets the mood well. However, it does not loop well because the end of the music fades to silence before starting again. The voice acting is good. I did not find any characters irritating to listen to. I think the emotions contained in the voice acting were appropriate for each character and the situation.  The story is told using flashbacks, and it makes some of the plot developments easy to see coming. The setting is easy to follow. The plot is decent and enjoyable, but it is not great. The translation is good. There are a few instances when I think a native English speaker would find the text unnatural but the text is still grammatically correct so it can still be understood.  Overall, I enjoyed eden* and recommend it to people who enjoy visual/kinetic novels. It is not a great kinetic novel, but it is good enough to deserve a place in the visual/kinetic novel library.

Have you ever wondered what Mechwarior would have been like as a steampunk puzzle game? Of course you haven't, that's insane. But somebody did, and yes it is as awesome as that sounds. Choose a mech, power its systems by stringing together the four different color nodes, shoot up somebody else's mech and then outfit yours from the remains of your enemies. It's a complex strategy game that's as addictive as any puzzle game.  Where it falls apart a little is in length. The campaign appears to be about sixteen short levels long (I haven't beaten the boss on 16 yet), but anytime you die, you start from the beginning. Also, dying raises your 'global level', giving you perks that make the game easier. Seeing as how my mech was destroyed in a few hits in that final battle, I'm thinking you're going to have to get your level up pretty high to stand a chance, which is going to mean some grinding. So there you have it, a short game made unnecesarily long with a cheap trick.  All in all, the game is a lot of fun, full of interesting art assets, and overflowing with variety considering its small scale. Sometimes you'll find an amazing new weapon on a simple enemy. Other times the game board won't cooperate and you'll find yourself desperate for just a little more energy for your shields. Either way, you'll never be bored in this one.

Full disclosure: I backed this game on Kickstarter, and I backed it because I loved playing Shadowrun Returns and Shadowrun: Dragonfall.   The game starts out with our hero, after you create him or her, getting off a boat to meet their childhood friend and foster brother in Hong Kong. You've been asked to go there by your foster father.  There are three battles before you even become a shadowrunner; one of them is avoidable, but yeah, there's a high probability of being killed first time out. There's even a Steam achievement for it.  The Shadowrun games involve a lot of reading. The writers seem to be more novelists than screenwriters, so they're really not concerned with anything seeming too much like a monologue. The balance between reading and playing is good, though. The reading lets you take a break from trying to shoot, fight, or magic your way out of the many encounters. Talking to your group is also encouraged, as some of them will give you missions. Some of the side characters you'll meet along the way will also help you in surprising ways.  In addition to the character quests, there are many hidden little things in the game that can be easily missed if you don't look around. There are other runners you can meet, more goodies to buy if you have the cash, and some entertaining characters.  There's also a heavier necessity for a decker, which seems to be why the decking virtual world has been given a major upgrade since the last game. It looks much better, and has a few more challenges to it. There are times in the game when a decker is absolutely necessary, and other times when a decker can help to avoid a battle, or even save everyone's lives.  Ultimately, though, the Shadowrun gameplay experience is looking very dated, especially after XCOM, and the upcoming XCOM2. Ideally, for the next Shadowrun game, I'd like to see more 3D, a rotatable map, and a lot more voice acting. Harebrained Schemes have improved their storytelling immensely since Shawrun Returns, but now it's time for them to improve the gameplay experience with some new technologies.

So far I have played this game for 6 hours after having owned it for 6 and a half. I have played a single campaign so far as the Empire and my review is based off of my experience so far. I may update this depending on how my experience goes.   TL:DR It's good but has some balance issues in my opinion. Unless you're a huge fan of the Warhammer and Total war series like me, chances are it's not worth £40/$60(?) that it costs. Wait a bit and pick it up on sale, unless you are able to spend £40 on a game with no issues.   First off, my system specs. CPU: i7 4790k clocked at a standard 4Ghz GPU:GTX 980 RAM: 16GB DDR3  Launch was standard and simple. No issues and was quick and smooth. Quickly checked options and found they had been set to Ultra so I decided to keep it at that and see how it goes. Upon choosing your faction and difficulty you can do a practice match and I did choose to do this. Game runs near perfectly and I haven't even updated my drivers for it. Good FPS, good shadows etc. Smooth and units look much nicer than in previous games and appear to fight in a more individual manner which is nice.   Battles are too easy though. That's my one complaint.Almost no skill is required as long as you have some spear in the front, ranged units behind to support and cavalry to flank with you break the enemy almost instantly. It's ridiculously easy. The campaign itself though, with politics and diplomacy is a bit more complicated in some ways. Some things aren't clearly explained so you end up having to figure it out and sometimes reloading to retry things. Irritating but not game breaking.   I haven't fought the dwarves yet and so far have only just engaged my first greenskins within the campaign, having spent my first 30-40 turns simply trying to reunite the empire. I'm not completely impressed with with the confederation option. So far I've failed to do force another faction to join me despite trying to be nice and also trying to force them with my military. Money is a lot easier to get flowing but my advice is that you need to make some serious land grabs quite quickly and to stay away from the global recruit options.   I'm going to try and play the Empire campaign a bit more and then I'll try some of the other factions though I am quite enjoying the Empire and have no real desire to play as the dwarves etc. Greatsword infantry OP af though Handgunners are underwhelming there is a mod which makes them do more damage, have a longer range and also have a longer reload. I would suggest downloading it.   Would I recommend this game? It's £40. That's a lot of money and I try to aim for the £1 per an hour of entertainment and I can see myself easily sinking a few hundred hours into this game. Yes, I would recommend, especially to Warhammer and Total war fans alike. If you aren't a Warhammer fan and are new to the total war series I would suggest either waiting for this to go on sale or to try an older Total War game first.   8/10. Has a few problems but I've already found mods that adjust for them and expect further modding will take this game to untold heights. It ticks most of the boxes for a nerd like me.

This game even though it is a DIRECT port off of the PS2 (meaning nothing has changed except the controls. You can't even use the letters on your keyboard to type your name for a save file! You must move the cursor over to each letter), is still an amazing game for as long as it's been out. I remember buying it on the PS2 thinking it was just going to be another lame racing game, and was VERY wrong! It mixes difficulty with strategy to make the game fun (if you like a challenge like me), it has soft-body physics (when you crash into something your car bends to meet the damage unlike Need For Speed or Gran Turismo), and you can play Olympic games with your car! There is a long jump, high jump, curling, bowling, skiball, and even darts! You play these games by driving your car and ejecting yourself out of your windshield causing yourself to do awesome stuff! The question I'm asking myself is, who WOULDN'T buy this for $7? I personally say it should be worth at least $10 or $15 for all the original stuff and work the creators put into this... It's seriously underpriced in my book. So what are you waiting for? There's a demo, try it!

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: I would've loved to have played Dark Souls on PC. It's considered a great game on console.  I heard it's hard, it's fun, and it's rewarding.   Unfortunately, it is the worst port I've ever had the misfortune of playing. I wish I could recommend it, I really could. But once you boil it down, it's really not worth it.  Graphical Quality  Without modding, the graphics are just plain bad. The textures aren't the worst, but that's only half the issue. Quite frankly, it performs terribly. The overall resolution is poor, and the framerate is, last I played, locked to 30 fps. This is definitely the biggest strike against the game.  Gameplay  We've all heard how hard Dark Souls is. But we've also heard how fair it is, and how it rewards the player’s skill. But the PC port gameplay is horrific. The camera control is atrocious. The mouse movement is buggy.   Difficulty It's a hard game made harder by terrible controls. That's all there is to it.   Overall If you get it for a low price, and are willing to mod the living daylights out of it, and use a controller, this game may be worth it for you. But I can't recommend such a shoddy PC port.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Total War: Rome 2 is a game with serious flaws. It was released with severe bugs; three months (not to mention seven patches) later I still experience occasional, unexplainable crashes. Gameplay-wise, the interface is poor, the AI makes mind-boggling decisions during battles and in the campaign, and the graphics leave something to be desired: even with all settings on Extreme I can't seem to get near the quality of those damnable screenshots. Oh, and the Blood & Gore setting -- a standard option in most games -- is sold as DLC. As if a $60 dollar game shouldn't have that option by default.  On the flip side, the game is eminently moddable, so if you're open to experimentation you could search for mods that make the game entertaining for you. Or create those mods yourself! And hey, despite all its rough edges, I still find Rome 2 sometimes engrossing, and you might too if you're a fan of large-scale strategy... Yet I can't help but feel that I'm forcing myself to play just so I can say I got my money's worth.

I finished this a while back so this isn't as fresh in my memory as it should. Also I have to be honest here, cyanide games are a guilty pleasure of mine. I always think there are some good ideas floating in their games but they don't manage to make the best out of all those ideas. That being said on to the main event.  Gameplay: You play as 2 characters here and you can switch between them at any time once you get the second one. The first is the orc, Arkhail, and the second is the goblin, Styx. Each of them has 3 skill trees in which you can add points to get new abilities or improve an ability in 1 of 2 different ways that adds an effect like stun or lifesteal to that ability . Arkhail has a berserker and a tank tree and the 3rd has some actions that include Styx like throwing him towards enemies and later on when you choose a specialization class(story event) you get some more skills here. Styx has an assassin tree and ranged dagger throwing tree and the 3rd again has combo actions such as throwing a heal potion to Arkhail. These skill trees/wheels also count as stances that give different bonuses. Speaking of potions there are none :P You have to keep your HP up by using skills, items with lifesteal and good planning before going into the fight. Both characters can equip gear that drops or is found in chests and is specific for each character. While there isn't a whole lot of gear each new item you find will be different and sometimes they will be sidegrades instead of upgrades so they can be useful to the build you are using for them. The fights can get rather challenging at times but all of them can be overcome with some good planning. The main thing to take into account is that Arkhail has a rage meter that can build up pretty fast and sends him into berserker mode where he is faster and stronger but you cannot control his actions and he sometimes takes pauses just to roar at the enemies in which time he gets pummeled. It's up to you if you want to go the berserker route and try to survive fights counting on his rage or go the tank way where you can control his rage and diminish with certain skills. Personally I went berserker for Arkhail and assassin for styx with a few skills from the other trees just enough to cover all my bases and it was fine though there were some fights that I had to restart quite a few times. It is by no means an easy game.  Graphics: From a distance they graphics look OK but when the camera zooms in for conversations the textures are really bad and blurry. A high res texture pack would to wonders for the game but I doubt we'll ever see anything like that. There is variety in the locations you go as far as design but the overall color palette is pretty bland with a lot of browns and greys.  Sound: I'm not really good at this part. Thesound seems ok to me but maybe someone with a better ear will tell you differently. The music is good and the volumes are well implemented (as opposed to viking battle of asgard). My only issue here is that half the voice actors are bad. Arkhail and Styx are ok though and it's their banter that you will be hearing for most of the game anyway.  Story: At the beginning the human empire is waging a massive war against all greenskins and is winning. The chief of the clan tasks Arkhail with killing the emperor as to finish the war. Arkhail needs help getting to the emperor so a guide is hired and this is where Styx comes in. From there the story has all sorts of twists and turn, some that you will expect and others that you won't. I really enjoyed the story but what I enjoyed more was Styx. He's just one of those characters that you either love or hate and if you love him you can't get enough of him.  Bottom line: While there could be improvements done to all sorts of things it was an enjoyable RPG that has replay value at least for the way you can customize the play style of the 2 characters. Also take note that a lot of F-bombs are dropped, and I mean a whole lot (just in case you wanna give this to your kids to play and you're worried about that sort of thing).

What can I say about Resident Evil: Revelations ?   I bought the game during the Steam summer sale 2014 for 9,99€ and believe me when I say that this game is worth every single Euro of it, especially if you are a fan of the older Resident Evil titles. I almost regret it that I did not bought it earlier.  I do not know how much I can actually write about the game without spoiling it at least a little bit so be aware of it. You have been warned.   Capcom tried to get back to the roots with this game after the more action based Resident Evil 5 and 6.  Therefore you will encounter a lot less of the 'Call of Duty' feeling where you have to shoot your way through the levels. In addition to that there are no real quick time events either. However, there are some little passages within the game where you have to shoot your way through the monsters. Thankfully, these passages are rather short and do not ruin the game to much.  You play the game mainly as Jill Valentine who was sent out with her partner Parker Luciani to find Chris Redfield. Even though the main character is Jill you will have to play some different characters as well. The story might be confusing at first sight, because there are some jumps here and there, but it will all make sense in the end.  The biggest part of the game plays on a ship, which is huge by the way. During your progress you will unlock new rooms and areas of the ship and some of them are really pretty. You will need to backtrack a couple of times to get new keys etc. to open new areas so a good memory or good map reading skills are recommended otherwise you will run in circles =) There are even some riddles in the game, but sadly they are not really worth mentioning. Oh I almost forgot about the 'shock moments'. Some of them really catched me off guard. I really recommend to play this game with lights out in the evening, preferably with a good headset.   I did my first playthrough on the normal difficulty and in comparison to Resident Evil 5 and 6 I actually managed to run out of ammo sometimes so do not waste you bullets. I can only imagine how brutal the hardest difficulty must be which I will definitely try to beat. By the way the last boss of the game really deserves to be called 'boss'. Personaly, I think it was one of the most hardest bossfights in my Resident Evil history. Maybe because I did not upgrade my weapons or did not use a rocket launcher (easy mode). Believe me you will be so much satisfied after you beat him words just cannot describe it. Dark Souls is a piece of cake in comparison, really it is.  I needed 12h more or less for my first playthrough, but I was really trying to look at every corner and soak up the atmosphere of the game. I guess you can finish it in 8-9h if you rush through it.  I do not know what else to say.   Buy it, play it, love it.

Download size: about 15 gigabytes Play time: about 13 hours for the single player campaign  Company of Heroes 2 (COH2) is one of those real time strategy games that is fun but sometimes infuriating even on conscript mode (easy difficulty) because of the seemingly random combat mechanics. It’s been over a year since I played the original COH, but I remember it being almost exactly the same in terms of gameplay and graphics. In COH2 however, you play as the Russians and there seems to be a lot of metagame options (DLC bonuses and things like that). I downloaded the game during the big free weekend and played as much of the campaign as I could finish, so I’m sure I missed a ton of the finer points. This is my impression of the single player campaign.  The game’s story takes place during the US/USSR Cold War and follows a Soviet officer who is facing execution for treason. The officer was a lieutenant during World War 2 and the gameplay missions are flashbacks to his time in the Red army. As the story unfolds you begin to see how the Soviet leadership was willing to sacrifice as many of their men as necessary to achieve victory, and the lieutenant did whatever he could to save them. This conflict between the lieutenant and his superiors caused him to be transferred around between different units, and consequently you get to play in various important battles of the war.   Gameplay in COH2 is pretty much exactly the same as the first game, so if you’ve recently played Company of Heroes 1, you can skip the tutorials. Technically the game is real time strategy, but the focus is tactical, so there’s almost no “base building.” There are 14 missions in total and I think I only had to build structures in 2 of them, and even then it was only 1 or 2 buildings. All of your units come from “off screen” when you build them, and the buildings are pretty much just unlocking the unit for you to summon them. Resources in the game are manpower, munitions, and fuel, all of which come from controlling command points on the map. There isn’t any “harvesting.” The command points are like capture the flag points in most first person shooters where you just need to have a unit standing next to it to capture and hold. Holding a command point sometimes creates a closer “spawn point” for your troops so if you buy a unit, it can get to the front lines faster. Troop and vehicle movement across the map is very slow, so having a closer supply line can be extremely important. Especially since the population cap is usually pretty low. You can’t just swarm the enemy with a massive army, you have to mix up your forces to counter whatever they have.  Like most modern strategy games, every unit type has a weakness and there really isn’t a unit you can mass produce and roll over everyone (no medium tank rush). There is a large variety of unit types you can build and all of them are good for something specific. You can be pushing toward the enemy base and lose your team of engineers, and suddenly you have to stop and wait for another team to replace them, otherwise you’re without repair support. I mentioned the random combat mechanics earlier and what I mean is that most of the shots fired in the game will miss. When troops engage each other, units in cover (hiding behind stuff) will be less likely to take damage. But this is not always true. Sometimes you think you have an awesome elevated position and still get mowed down. Or you can be out in the open and still kill the enemy in their entrenched position. If you’ve played the old Command & Conquer games, you probably remember a tank firing 50 shots and missing the enemy soldier every time. This seems to happen a lot in COH2. Most of your units have some sort of extra ability that can be used for tactical advantage, for example anti-personnel grenades or anti-tank grenades. This means that micro managing your troops can often turn the tide even when you think the enemy has superior numbers.   Pros: +The game is pretty much as “realistic” as you can get for small scale battles. There is troop movement time, random shots missing, and even a rudimentary morale system. If your men are under heavy fire (like from a machine gun), they can be “pinned down.” They won’t return fire, use special abilities, and can’t move effectively.  +You are forced to use various strategies to accomplish your goals. You cannot mass produce a few types of units and expect to win. You do have a “jack of all trades” type unit (basic conscript) who can pick up weapons found on the battlefield and become more specialized. For example if a mortar team dies, a conscript team can pick up the fallen mortar gun and become a mortar team. The same goes for heavy machine guns and bazooka teams.  Cons: -The game is quite difficult because of the randomness in the heat of combat. Don’t get me wrong, on easy difficulty you can certainly beat every level, it just might take a lot longer than expected because your troops keep getting picked off. You have to move slowly so all your units can keep up with the main force. Being a hard game is not a bad thing, but it can really ♥♥♥♥ you off when you lose 1 guy and have to wait for reinforcements to walk halfway across the map. -Some of the special abilities don’t seem to work properly. I was never able to summon air strikes when I wanted, and it wouldn’t tell me what was wrong. Was I too close/far? It would just say “you cannot use this ability on that target.” I’m the kind of guy who likes to bombard from long range so it was annoying when I couldn’t use some of these abilities on any of the levels.  All in all the game is fun if you’re into real time strategy, but don’t have 20 click per second micro managing skills like some Starcraft players. It’s pretty slow paced, but I imagine that in multiplayer you have to blitz in the beginning to capture command points. The game has a significant download size, so if you didn’t really like the first Company of Heroes think carefully if you want to give COH2 a try, the games are very similar. Additionally COH2 has over 400 Steam achievements, most of which are grinding (kill x many of this type of unit). Beating the campaign on normal mode would give only about 40. Watch your average game completion % drop!

As a DC fan and a long time gamer, I found this game really fun, but it definitely could use a lot more work.   Don't get me wrong, I absolutely love this game and I enjoy playing it, but I think the cons outweigh the pros.  First off, as a free to play player the game is very limited. There are certain degrees at which a F2P (free to play) player has access to content and this game is extremely limited. The game constantly expands its benefits to members and its market. At this point F2P players should get a more expansive inventory and less limitations on their bank and mailbox (sending and receiving items).   I cannot play this game for 5 minutes without running into something that is trying to encourage me to spend money. Sure, they're a business and they need to make money, BUT we have seen MANY games that do not do this and still succeed.   I love DC and most of their productions, but I've never seen them successful in video games. DCUO is one exception where I think they have done well, but I think they could do much better with more effort. Maybe all they need is an increase in their budget, but this game can be overhauled to be an even greater success.  Finally,  You should play this game, maybe even put a couple of dollars in it. If you plan to play this game long enough, pay for the membership as well. It's fun, but in my opinion it loses its appeal at level 30 because it becomes extremely repetitive and a grind for levels.   Again, however, the game could use a lot of changing in their F2P framework. F2P users have extremely limited access, moreso than most popular/successful F2P.  F2P friendly, 4/10 (Low-No|High-Yes) Difficulty, 8/10 (Low-Difficult|High-Easy) Playability (How many hours can you play the game before it gets boring), 8/10 (Low-few hours|High-several hours) How high is the paywall, 7/10 (Low-cheap payments|High-expensive payments) Voice acting, ♥♥♥♥/10 (Some characters get good voice acting others get absolute ♥♥♥♥, the dialogue can be dreadful too) Graphics, 8/10 (They're not great, but they're not bad in such a way that it isn't an eye sore)  Overall, 7/10

140 is about as minimalistic as a platformer can get. It makes other minimalistic games look complicated.  The entire game is made out of the three most basic shapes: circles, triangles, and rectangles. These shapes are all colored very simply and don't even have any texture (except for the grey static that kills you). There's a reason no artists were needed to develop this game; calling the graphics in here 'art' would be a bit of a stretch.  However, what saves this game from being as boring as its graphics is the exceptional soundtrack. After all, it's a rhythmic platformer, so the music  better  be good, since it's essential to the gameplay. Fortunately, the music is great, and it fits the gameplay perfectly.  Ironically, for a game that has such an amazing soundtrack, you won't find an official OST  anywhere . This is because the music is truly part of the gameplay, and so it's not as simple as just playing one long sound file in the background like most games do; the music is affected by what is happening in the game. The result is that the music you hear will vary slightly each time you play the game.  Unfortunately, that means we are left with no soundtracks to listen to, apart from the title screen and level select music, which are probably the only real tracks you can listen to without actively playing the game. To listen to any of the music that plays during the actual game, you'll have to settle for watching videos on YouTube. An unofficial OST put together by fans can be watched   here  . While these videos are very well done, it's such disappointment to have no 'real' tracks for each of the three levels.  And yes, you heard that right; the game only has three levels. Each one takes about seven minutes to run through, maybe 15 if it's your first time. That's means there's less than an hour of gameplay for the entire game.   But what about the mirror levels? Well okay, there are actually three more levels after you beat the first three, but I'd hardly consider them new levels. They're literally exactly the same as the first three levels, but reversed (instead of moving right, you move left). They have no new content, no new music, and no new bosses. It's just the same thing over again, except that this time, you move backwards. Oh and one more thing, the mirror levels have no checkpoints at all.  And that's the reason only about 2% of players have actually completed the mirror levels: no checkpoints means that one mistake will send you  all the way back  to the very beginning. At this point, the game turns from fun into frustrating. I guess that may appeal to some hardcore gamers, but for most people, it's not worth their time doing something that isn't even fun.  I really wished there would be more levels, since having only three is just pitiful. After you beat the third boss, the game looks like it goes into a '3D' mode for a bit. That could've been a cool way to start off the next levels. Too bad it didn't.  As much as I wanted to write a positive review for this game, because I  loved  the three levels it did have, I simply cannot recommend a game that has so little content.  Minimalistic design is ok, but minimalistic content is not.   TL;DR: Great game, but there's not even an hour of content (unless you're a masochist willing to play the mirror levels, then there's a few more hours of  frustrating  content). Now I know $5 isn't very much, but neither is <1 hour of gameplay. If the game had an OST bundled with it, then it might be worth it. But sadly it doesn't.

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: tl;dr: This game is worth your time.  Now brace yourself: Spoiler-free wall of text incoming.  I am very conflicted about the story, the story which was there, I loved, I was hooked and engaged, but in Chapter 2 I could definitely feel that the game was incomplete in the story department, which is unfortunate. The presentation in the game is so damn good, no other game comes close to it. The music, the atmosphere, the cinematography, the animation, the sound cues, the use of special effects like lens flares to draw the eye, the acting (bar a few lines) are all top-notch, best I've ever seen in a video game.  The writing itself is what I'm conflicted about. Kojima is excellent at making you think of issues that few other games do, but the way he does it heavy handed and at times does not feel natural, which could also be a localisation issue. This game can get you to think about a lot of stuff you wouldn't normally think about. The nature of language is one thing in particular which really struck a chord with me, but I won't say no any more.  The gameplay is to me a 10/10, I've never had so much fun in a game before, the mechanics are excellent, player freedom is a big theme and it is executed really well. When I unlocked weapon customisation, which was quite late in the game for me, I was incredibly surprised by the depth to which it went. All the options available to you is incredible and you can truly create a weapon that feels unique to you and your playstyle. I couldn't wait to develop more weapons just so I could unlock new barrels, ammunition and sights to use and quickly made it my mission.  The buddy system is incredibly well designed, and I genuinely cared a lot about my buddies (apart from D-Walker, it's incredibly fun to use but it being a robot I could not connect with it at all). Seeing any of them injured and having to leave the battlefield genuinely made me wince at times, because I felt like I had failed them. However, I wish you could have more interactions with them at Mother Base, and just more interaction with them overall with more cutscenes and stories involving them, the scenes which involved them were often my favourite scenes.   A real standout to me for the game was Quiet. Before launch I didn't care about her at all, it wasn't until I started playing that I started becoming interested in her. Hearing the soviet guards talk about her for the first time in the field was intriguing, and I started looking forward to meeting her, and then when you do meet her I was happy that you had very little warning, I knew something was up once I reached the area and once I heard the sniper rifle fire I was excited to have finally found her and to be able to get a chance to see what she was like. Her story arc is quite excellent (and complete) and all the cutscenes that featured her I simply adored, with the rain scene genuinely making my heart wrench. I've never cared so much about a virtual character, pixels on a screen, in my life, and the fact that I cared so much about someone who a: Isn't real and b: is a mute, greatly surprised me. A lot of good things can be said about Stefanie Joosten's performance. ______________________ These are all my opinions and they are subject to change. I don't put much stock in giving game arbitrary numbers to represent complex opinions, but here goes. To sum up:  Gameplay: I'd have to say 99/100, it is so close to perfect(but isn't) and the most fun I've had in any game ever.  Presentation: 95/10, So many excellent scenes, music choices, choice of camera angles and acting in this game. The graphics themselves are also very good.  Story/writing (obviously very subjective): I'd give it a 70/100, it brings up a lot of important subjects and provokes some thought, but it feels incredibly heavy-handed at times. Not to mention that a lot of it feels incomplete (Eli's storyline is never resolved in-game, but rather on a 20 minute video that came with the collector's edition). Chapter 2 had a lot of repeated missions from the first chapter, and I have a feeling that originally there were supposed to be a lot more missions, but time and budget constraints resulted in a rather haphazard chapter 2 and because of that I didn't get a feeling of satisfaction when I completed it. I've wondered if I would feel better about the story if I hadn't followed development so closely and just played the game with no expectations. I believe I would feel much better about it that way.  Overall: the game gets a 9/10, I enjoyed the game immensely, I've had so much fun playing it, over 240 hours. It's a truly excellent game and the best game I've had the pleasure of experiencing.

I don't recommend Civ VI because the game is too narrowly focused on starting location. In Civ V, multiplayer with experienced players generally came down to who got the best start location or how many players were around you, but in Civ IV, you NEED certain tiles to get tech bonuses, becasue without those, it will take you twice as long to research anything, dramatically increasing the effect of a good or bad start location over the last game in the series. The new amenities feature replacing happiness is opaque, and again success in amenities balance depends entirely on your starting location. War weariness makes the game un-fun to play; in the old games, when things weren't going your way, you could at least try to do some damage, maybe wrest a city or two from an adversary-- here, the war weariness will produce barb units in your territory faster than your best cities could produce similar units, and these will pillage every tile while your army is away fighting (or even winning) a war. The time scale is also off-- in quick games on small maps on the last Civ, you could usually finish in four hours if people kept the game moving. In Civ VI, it takes drastically more turns to produce, creating a slower progression; this extended production scale not only drastically increases the value of units over building a strong civ, but it also makes even the quick games take longer. The formula for unit disbanding is also much more unforgiving in this game, such that if your treasury ever dips below zero, you will lose several units a turn, instantly eliminating the effort of twenty or thirty turns it took to produce those units-- not at all fun-- especially because a single spy might steal from the treasury, destroying an army in one chain reaction of disbanding events.

Despite me only having 15 minutes logged at the time of the review, I have been playing this game on and off since late 2010. What I can say is that the game is an average game at best with okay physics but a lack of necessary features and some bugs can make the game rather grueling to endure.  The game's premise is simple: collect the cheese by using wasd or arrow keys and then return to the mouse hole to earn points. In time, you become the shaman with the ability to build platforms for the others to traverse. You also get skill points to then use in a skill tree to get more passive abilities as shaman or completely new abilities. It's a solid concept and it is enjoyable. There are also multiple room types. Regular rooms have a normal map rotation comprised of official maps and then permanent community made maps while vanilla rooms only uses the official maps made by the developer. Furthermore, the business model is sound, using in game purchases only for cosmetics rather than game changing effects.  However, there are no rebindable keys to speak of. This will turn off quite a few people. Secondly, the tutorial is clearly lacking, completely omitting any sort of shaman tutorial. Being a decent shaman is rather difficult and the community will be quick to call you names should you fail. Finally, the steam client is a little choppy. You're better off using the official site or downloading the non-steam client for the time being.  All in all, it's an okay game. The lack of key features will make the game not as appealing to most but for a free game, what are ya gonna do?  Also the community can get to CoD and Dota 2 levels of hostility.

I have several old waypoints loitering around the map which has me plough through the same dialogues each time I get too close for quests I solved long ago. I even just managed to get the same NPC in my 'family' (they call it families and not parties in this game) twice at the same time. What started as a nice game just left me wandering the icy wastelands aimlessly waiting for my party members to die of starvation...  If this game gets a serious overhaul by the developers I will most likely re-visit it since it showed great promise for the first 4-6 hours with bugs being just nagging without flat-out preventing me from progressing further. Until then I simply cannot recommend the game in its current state unless you feel fine being left 6 hours into the game with no way of getting any further (so masochists, please go ahead and purchase).

This is, no lie, the best game I've played in a while. And for the past four months, I've been steadily going through every single game I've ever had the fortune of playing, trying to find a game like this. this game, actually, is why I bothered to go back into my steam account. It got recommended to me by a friend, and he sold it as 'an rpg that does everything backwards in a way you never knew you wanted.' and he's right! Undertale is simple yet elegant, and the comedy is perfectly mixed in with the 'action' and puzzls you have to complete to move forward. Each character is developed and takes on a very distinct personality, and everyone has a reason to be the way they are. All in all, one of the best games I've ever played, and I'd definitely recommend this game to any rpg fan who likes a few jokes on the side, and a way of gaming that might not be conventional.

review_text: I was going to slam this game and Steam too at first. In my haste to play it I blew right by the set up screen and into a terriable resolution setting with seemingly no way out. Then I calmed myself down, I mean it was only 4 bucks!! Then I came back to it and low and behold an options screen. Once set up I was captivated by the amount of options in game, I mean you can adjust this, tweak that, name everything, wow good old fashion micro-management at its best! Its a great game and I recommend it to any space gamer. As for my anger with steam, they've done nothing but offer great deals, fast delivery and a choice of Games not matched anywhere!! My library holds more then my CPU memory can support!! Steam has the classics, The India games, The Bundles, You guys have it if it is called a Game. As my Game account grows and my marriage and Bank account drop...I just keep on loading, Tweaking and Smiling to myself because I know when I turn 70 I'll still be defending Castles, shooting spaceships and forever trying to Craft the best weapons and Armor in the world. I will say your not a true Gamer unless you have and use Steam. Thanks for once again pulling out a classic and adding to it polishing it up and selling it at a great price. Thanks for all of your efforts, I will die using Steam. Oh the review-buy the game and read the options. Have FUN!!

I bought and played Amnesia because it seemed to be pretty favorably reviewed. The game was praised for being atmospheric and scary. The problem is, that's not enough.  It certainly is atmospheric, and it has some startingly unique gameplay mechanics. When I first woke up in that castle I was excited to explore it.  But a couple hours in and the magic has worn off. You're in the same setting the entire time, so the environment gets old and boring, no matter how atmospheric it was to start. And once you master the mechancis the only new thing to challenge you is the puzzles, which vary from simple to obtuse, but in general are not that well designed. Moving through the levels is pretty linear and exploring doesn't benefit you nearly as much as I'd hoped it would.  The game does have a few truly scary moments, but most of it is just typical pop-out cheap-horror-movie fare.  The voice acting is really good - it's probably the game's high point in my mind. But the story they're telling isn't all that interesting. It opens some good questions in the first act, but by the time you finish this short adventure none of the intriguing points have been explored. I suspect the writers themselves didn't know the answers.  Despite a couple praise-worthy points, there just isn't a whole lot of game here.

I highly recommend Civ to anyone who likes to build their own history a little. It's a game that shines when you invest yourself in it and explore the possibilities.  As any avid civ player can tell you, you will truly form a symbiotic (or perhaps parasitic) relationship with your fellow players and AI, all the while it being virtual and fictional, and feel like history is really progressing inside your little realm. As every player schemes their way forward, that one settler takeover 200 turns earlier might be the reason why you decided to decimate someone's Civ. Or at the very least embargo them.  Do you expand rapidly, or keep it calm and small? Do you initiate combat with your neighbours, or leave them as a safezone for potential invaders at the other end. Puppets and city razing, science and faith, everything is a factor in your ultimate victory... or very untimely demise.  In short, if you don't mind sinking hours into a virtual world and rant in frustration as your neighbour finishes your favorite wonder first, a good casus beli for war if I've ever heard one, then you shouldn't really be searching for anything else, because you found exactly what you'd want just a buttonpress away. And probably a lot of cash.

This one's a really mixed bag. For the people driven by nostalgia, this will be a sour trip, or a fun revisting of a childhood game. If you're new to this game, you might want to turn back.  Sonic Adventure 2 is the PC port of one of the 'best' Sonic games. It was pretty fun back then, and now that I went back to it, I'm kinda worried. Sure it has the iconic 'Escape From The City' music, but Sonic Adventure 2 isn't very memorable. The level design is.... cool, I guess. There's a lot of different themes, and it is pretty cool. The platforming levels are pretty cool, but some levels with robot powerups and stuff are just downright annoying. This game is a slippery platformer, but it still has some charm.  The chao raising is what everyone remembers, and for good reason. You get to raise your own Chao! YEAH!  I can't tell if this game is outdated or not, because the nostalgia is getting in the way, but it can be fun, sometimes.  You're going to have to play this game is you want an honest review, because I heard about people who loved it and people who despised it. I would say it's just 'okay'.

I have played many horror games, Silent Hill 2 still being one of the best games in the genre, but Alien Isolation is the game I'm afraid to play, and I have played the likes of Penumbra, Amnesia, Dead to Fear...insert any horror game in the last 20 years. I really didn't think I would be afraid, as I have seen Alien so many times in all the movies, games etc. There was no mystery, you know the monster inside out, yet you sh^& yourself when you see him in this game, after 35 years of oversaturating the market with Alien, they somehow made it fresh.  And that is what I think makes this game so genius and an unsung masterpiece.  This is an exceptional game that is not for people who like spoon-feeding. You get a gun and you run through Sevastopol like a drunk cowboy as your motion detector blips like a torpedo, but the gun is just a decoration. When that glorious natural born killer comes from nowhere to impale you with its tail as you are in the midst of saving your game, you gotta respect him.

EDIT: Since the full release, this game has only become even more desirable. The mechanics are polished and streamlined, the itemization is almost doubled, and the engine has been optimized dramatically  You want this game.   ARPG veterans and newbies alike will find something to appreciate, and the deep skill system makes for endless possibilities. The world is not as dynamic or interactive as big AAA sandbox games obviously, but the combat mechanics are so solid they might form a black hole from their sheer density. So many items work together in so many ways and your skills can be tweaked on a very granular level with the respec system, though I might gripe that skill points are still a bit scarce as of build 29.   Seriously, just get it. It would take a long time to really describe why this game is great, but if you love action and loot, this is the place to be.

The game has great attention to detail, and the notes/readables hidden accross the game make you want to read them, because they often give you information about single faiths of people that survived or died in the cataclysm. When reading them you almost feel a personal connection and it can be very sad if you find the person's dead body, after you read six Pages about how he struggled to survive.  The multiplayer is not that great, because it is essentially a multi-people-singleplayer, meaning that there is little way to interact between the players, and it's just everybody playing for himself while beeing in the same game, this is just my feeling from a few hours multiplayer I might be wrong here.

This is a matter of taste/opinion, but for me, British accent isn't appealing in games.  The nemesis system (Sauron's army) is neither a positive nor a negative for me, overall. It's negative that someone replaces one you've killed, makes me feel I'm never going to eliminate all. It's positive to have goals, killing your way through the ranks, though. Maybe it's because I've played the Batman games a lot, which has a very similar combat system, but only just under three hours played and I'm bored of it.  If you don't mind heavy ginds, go for it. My only regret in purchasing Rocket League was the fact that I didn't purchase it sooner.  It's not the most complicated game, but it's insanely fun and has a clear competitive edge to it. Like most driving games, it's best if you have a controller (an Xbox 360 controller seems to be the best supported gamepad), so for best results...have a controller.  Additionally, for best results...when you get the game...narrow the game search results to servers closest to your region (so if you live in North America, search for only US east and US west servers). When you do first start playing, the game will attempt to search worldwide....so not narrowing down the regions could result in some nasty ping issues.    + Easy to play in short bursts  + All DLC (as of this review) is purely cosmetic  + Easy to pick up  + Competitive side (if you're into that sort of thing)  +/- The game is actually cross-platform! So you'll be playing with both PC and PS4 players.  It's also nice that at the end of every match you complete, you unlock a random cosmetic item such as a booster trail, some wheel rims, or a new paintjob. A wide variety of cosmetics is always fun to have.    Overall, Rocket League is a really fun game, and if you don't have it already, you're really missing out.

No, you cannot bring back the victims of the 2011 Japanese tsunami. What you do bring back is the humanity that perhaps you, along with the rest of us, have lost when we hear of these tragedies.  When the media broadcasts the desctruction caused by natural disasters (hurricanes, tornades, etc.), when tend to just look at the numbers then shrug our shoulders. Space Budgie wants to remind us that these are individuals just like you and me. They had friends, family, hopes and dreams; gone from the physical world, but not from our hearts and minds.  Thank you, Space Budgie. May God bless you and those you are recovering from this tragic period of time. May the victims never be forgotten.

I never thought I'd be saying this but you're better off buying this game for a console. Saying that the online experience is ruined is an understatment. It's just nigh unplayable  I just spent 4 hours trying to find a decent team for a heist or preparation and couldn't, a hacker would always come along and ruin the mission some way or the other. For instance, they killed everyone on the session, started spawning stuff, some guys 'help' making the mission really easy killing all the enemies, but that's just not fun at all. There's people on a rampage killing everyone on god mode, driving undestructible vehicles, etc.. I'd dare saying that at least 1/4 players are using some kind of hack.  Some hackers even know how to kill everyone making it seem like it was someone else, so the people on the session denounce the wrong guy and he, instead of the hacker, gets banned, and it can happen to anyone, it's just terrible, terrible control by Rockstar.  The only way to play this game frustration-free is with friends, definitely not how it's supposed to be, it's like going back to LAN parties.   Given the fact that online mode is, quite possibly, the most attractive reason to buy the game I'd recommend you stay away from the PC version, get a console version instead.  As for the good stuff:   It looks good, sounds good, SINGLE player is awesome, the city is awesome, there's so much attention to detail. Blah blah blah.

I had this game on my wishlist for a long time and it finally went on sale for 10 dollars with a free weekend at the same time. Since it was free I went ahead and played it a bit before purchasing. The game was well made and when I got into a game(it took a long time to find one) it was pretty fun. After looking through what you get with the game and what they are selling in their store as DLC content though I could not support/buy this game. From what it looks like to me they have this terrible blend of pricing schemes. They want you to pay full price for the game and then buy a bunch of additional characters seperately, like you would in a free to play game. I think either of those policies can be okay seperately but when you try to do both i find it a bit disgusting. Even at 10 dollars I don't feel it is worth it. If the game were to go free to play and then charge for characters OR let you pay 50 dollars for everything I would have probably purchased it. On the other hand if it was 5 dollars I probably would have purchased it and considered it a 'starter package'.  In addition to this there seemed to be very long wait times, even during the free weekend, to find a game. From what I can tell this has gotten even worse since the free weekend. This is another issue that may be solved by changing their business practices, but it seems unlikely.  I would not recommend this game to anyone unless you can get it for 5 bucks. Then just enjoy what you get with it and if you really like it you can determine if it is worth the money they are charging for more characters.
