Short version: -------------------- + very much fun to 'fly' through this nice looking and interresting world + I like the way the story is being told (like a bedtime story) + nice visuals  ~ sometimes hard to find the right way ~ the story itself is 'ok'. Not bad, but also not great  - playtime (about 3h - 4h)   Long version: ------------------- This is one of my favourite games. To be honest the title of this games does not sound that interresting and the graphics might be a bit outdated (but still very nice in my opinion), but it's really worth it to take a closer look at this game.   The story is being told like a bedtime story. The character you play, tells his daughter the story about his journey when he was searching for his missing uncle and discovered a different world.  You'll find a suit invented by your uncle that let you jump high and far through the scenery. Also included in this suit is some kind of grappling hook that you can (or better have to) use to swing through this new world (and to use this, is SO MUCH FUN!) So now you start your journey, discovering this world on your way to find your missing uncle.  The playtime is really short. I finished the game in about 3 ½ hours. And if you don´t fall in the ground as often as I did, you can finish it much faster. But you will fall in the ground very often ;) Like I said it was a lot of fun 'flying' through this world, but there are many places where you don´t see where to go next at the first time. So when I was wondering where to go next, what I did was to jump in a direction and try if I can grab a rock or something else nearby. Aaaand.....what a surprise.....I fell in the ground ^^ But even if you see where the right way is, it´s often not easy to follow it. That can get a bit frustrating after trying the same thing over and over again. But this is the only thing that makes this game difficult, because you don't have any enemy to fight. And that's a good thing in my opinion, because that way you can really enjoy discovering this beatiful world and the feeling to 'fly' through the air.  So would I recommend this game? YES, absolutely. BUT: Considering the playtime of only 3h - 4h I would wait for a good discount to buy it.

Normally found on an indie bundle - this little puzzler belongs in the realms of mobile gaming.... Certainly reminds me of games back in the day - fire a little ball and see if you can use the various gravitational pulls to last the required amount of time before crashing into anything... It would be a nice little puzzler 'on the go' - but it's complete one-track gameplay means that after 30 minutes you've seen all there is to see... so unless you're a fan of gravitational-based puzzles - you can safely move on...

Immediate dissappointment. I have 2 copies of this game for Xbox and I have it on PC. It's almost the same as MX vs ATV Alive but the physics are worse. A lot of the gear and music in the game is exactly the same as Alive, the graphics look just like Alive, but the bikes handle terribly. when you first start the game it takes a very long time to unlock upgrades, tires, gear etc. the bikes slide out very easily until you unlock tires that hook up better. In the air the bikes handle like tanks, it's very difficult to move the bikes in the air. The physics are what make this game so bad, the bikes dont carry hardly any forward momentum when you hit jumps, they get a lot of air and then it's like somebody turned the gravity on and they fall almost straight down. Using the clutch is a must is corners and if tap the clutch repeatedly you go faster in the straits for some reason. Also, if you put your settings on manual transmission, you can keep you're bike in 5th gear all the way around the track and clutch boost like no other, it's incredibly unrealistic. As much as I dislike this game, I did enjoy racing pit bikes! The pit bikes are the only reason I even own the game on PC, they're very agressive, and they somehow clear all the jumps that you can clear on a 250. So if you're looking for a game that you'll play for a long time and captures that real racing feeling, this game isn't the one, but if you're looking for something that might be fun for a week, it might suit you.   **Update Oct 30, 2015**  Game is a little bit better. More features and tweaks have been added to the game that've made it a little more playable. Still not that great of a game, but it does look like they're starting to put more effort into it. It needs to be optimized though, I can play GTA V on max settings, but this game doesn't even have as good of graphics and even with the textures turned down and what not, it still plays like it's in slow motion. There currently aren't any brand name bikes to choose from, and none of the bikes in the game actually resemble any brand name bikes, but my face did light up when I saw that they added a graphics kit to the game that I actually have on my bike IRL. I still don't really like this game but maybe you will.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: Well, I think this might be the beginning of the end of the COD franchise. Sad, really. I've been a COD fan since the original release over 10 years ago. However, the devs seem insanely insistent upon using the antiquated Quake engine and its inherently broken net code as the foundation for new COD releases. Anyway, I don't need to rehash all of the well known engineering shortcomings of Treyarch et al.  As far as BO3 goes, I will start with the campaign aspect. I will sum it up in two words: Not Good. I don't know what they did with the mouse interface, but, its clumsy and somewhat laggy. None of the mouse sensitivity settings, filtering or acceleration features have any impact on the problem,   The graphics are ok and appear to be better optimized than AW. However, they are still just a warmed over, repackaged offering of the previous COD releases.   As far as the actual campaign play itself is concerned, its bad. There are far too many cut scenes and despite having good actors, the acting is poor and the dialogue is worse. It seems as thought they tried too hard to make this some sort of trippy mind sch♥♥♥♥. Overall, the campaign lacked direction and was constantly interrupted by far too many cut scenes.   The campaign story line itself is the same recycled story line that follows our fearless hero through a gauntlet of issues that have the same plot twist (the ally turned villain twist) as the last few releases. Its tired and its time for something new.   The multiplayer experience is somewhat improved with the elimination of the ridiculous exo-suit jumping. Weapon usage is not as efficient as AW or the previous releases. There are still OP'd weapons and the options for perks and attachments is somewhat the same.   The inherent netcode latency is still ever present and a real downer. Of course, that is to be expected since they are still using the antiquated Quake engine. The lag compensation is horrible and once again, hobbles players with good systems and connections to the utter elimination of skill based kills.   However, judging the game as a whole, its not worth the hype and is not worth the hefty $60 price tag.   Call of Duty Black Ops III is just another stepping stone in the sad and tragic decline of a once great franchise.

I thought I'd just play this game casually but why not let it consume my soul instead?    I got this game only recently with the Holiday sale and it took a big chunk out of my life after that. I got so wrapped up into the story I'd forget the time. I honestly didn't care much for dating simulators but this one is crafted well with it's beautiful art, character designs and story. It lives up to the 'Visual Novel' selling point however this game is not perfect but is still rather engaging.   Story (Intro):  You play as Aoi, who I learned to like as a character. Who's moved back to his hometown after a cycling accident. The intro to this game is pretty hefty, only after 7 hours did I make my first choice and there was good chunks of time between each choice. The main plot point is that you become a member / pilot of the 'Soaring Club' and with Kotori, Ahega, Amane, Asa, Koru and other side characters that you strive for your goal in flying above the rare clouds of 'Morning Glory'. In doing so you must travel down one of six paths held before you and achieve this goal.  The order in which you take these paths can be of your choosing to some extent.   Kotori can be done on the first or second succesful try.   Amane and Asa can be done anytime after Kotori.   Ahega can be done at anytime.   And Yoru can only be done after Kotori and Asa.   Depending on which girl you pick, the story can differ extremely. The first 6 hours before movie I (Act 1) unfold the same no matter what. Act 2 (Between movie I and II) is where 4 of your choices are made and Act 3 (Between movie II and III) is where (at most) two choices are made. After that you're just along for the ride.   Story (Kotori):  This was the first route I completed and one I enjoyed the second most. It was also the longest to complete due to it being the first one I completed and took up 40% of the game (Including the common routes). The things I enjoyed most of this storyline was   The love confession in the new glider's test flight  ,    The way Aoi chased after Kotori on his bike even after his injury  ,   When everyone got together and pulled the glider to get it airborne and using glowsticks, gide them to the winds.   and finally   The ending, where Kotori got surgery and began to walk again    which made me shed a single tear.   Story (Amane):  This was my second completed route and easily my favourite. This was the easiest due to it having the least choices compared to the other routes. There was so much I loved about this route, like   It's the only route that let's you keep the school garage until Act 4  ,   The whole head in lap thing with Amane is ♥♥♥♥ing adorable  ,   It's the only route which made me feel somewhat sorry for Mr. Tabioka with the letter and made me understand his actions  ,   You learn everything about Isaka due to being so close with Amane and see Amane and Isaka in they're actual school years.   Not to mention   Isaka returns and is reunited with Amane and considering she's the girl on the title art and that she's half the reason the Soaring Club is still going is pretty damn awesome.   And finally,   An-Chan (My favourite side character) is in love with Isaka and because you chose Amane, you help him find his love for her again and you go on double dates and other cute ♥♥♥♥.     Story (Ahega):  Third completed, third favourite and easily the most frustrating and cheesy route. There's nothing overly wrong with this route it's just that   Aoi jumps though so many ♥♥♥♥ing hoops for this girl compared to how easy it was to get Kotori or Amane.   However the payoff made it pretty worth it with the   moments before takeoff, Ahega just straight up grabs you and kisses you infront of everyone to prove a point,      you also get a damn sports car to pull your gilder airborne on a bridge and you have the best conversation with Ahega through the Radio after take off.   Ahega is easily the worlds biggest tease and I enjoyed all of it.   Story (Asa):  Fourth down and fourth favourite, there wasn't too much (at this point in the game) that really grabbed me about this route. Asa didn't get much screen time without her sister and after    breaking Yoru's heart to go for asa  , I felt really bad and I couldn't find myself invested into the route. It's also the only route in which   the real Morning Glory comes in that second summer however it shows up two years later but you don't see Asa and Yoru fly into it.     Story (Yoru):  Last up and least favourite for multiple reasons. The big one is that   you never actually pick Yoru, you just say neither of them then she decides that you should date both of them   which annoyed me because I (personally) liked Yoru more than I liked Asa (as a character). The only thing I did enjoy was when   Asa was out of the picture and it was just you and Yoru.     Art:  From the previews above you can see the main art style of the story. Most of it is characters standing infront of a background with different poses and facial expressions but there are some beautifully drawn scenes, all of which are wallpaper worthy. There is also a chibi art style that's used to convey messages which I loved. It's also used for the acheivement 'Too bad!'.   Final Thoughts:  I played the vanilla version and didn't realise this was heavily censored, I might go back and install it to see the differences. I did still enjoy it however. The bad translating at points broke the immersion like the mixing up of pronouns (Tobioka was known as a she for a whole scene) and the mix up of names got me confused at times but all in all it was a fun 51 hours of my life.    8/10 would 100% again.

Unless you're incredibly bored & brain dead just looking for a time waster do not buy this game. If you can get it on a massive sale, or for free, and have some time to kill... here's 63 trivial achievementsl. Also just a note going in, you never get to sail the boat yourself D: It's like a loading mini-game that's just spam the mouse to shoot things in your way.  Firstly, enemies. There are barely any bosses and a severe lack of mini-bosses of any sort, leaving you to spam click your sword through low level enemies in every dungeon without any real threat to your annoyance. The Graveyard, Riptide, Old Fortress... none have a boss of any sort. Just the same ol' boring low level enemies. Not only that but pretty much every boss could just be taken down by a few bomb throws from across the arena. Very boring.  Secondly, level design. The trailers don't lie: the levels are small and cramped. But alright, you can still do great things with that limitation. Sadly Oceanhorn doesn't. The puzzles can barely be called that, and yes this is coming from a puzzle lover who's crack is a challenge that could take hours to figure out, but holy applesauce this game... There is more than a couple instances where the 'puzzle' is literally a box right next to the switch. The 'increased' difficulty for puzzles in this game just seems to be: add more boxes and switches sitting next to each other.  This game feels like it was made for young children with its 'difficulty'. The only reason I have so much time on this is because it was an easy grab for achievements and it felt like it would be an embarrassment to not have them all with how trivial it is. The amount of time is because of how slow it is to move around, fishing, & the time it takes to skulk about an area murdering all of one type of enemy.  Thirdly, the story. While the main points are fine and dandy... there's a huge problem with everything else that gets one emerged in the game. There are no interesting or fun interactions with any NPC and despite them never doing anything, learning anything about you, knowing anything about what's happening, the game forces an advancement in their generic uninteresting NPC comments as if they are all omniscient. There are promises of a fun side note/reaction to something you've done, like the “Don't break the pots” sign or the “You will never reach Master Level” sign sitting right next to the 'Master Adventurer' but just... nothing. It's all glossed over.  Another example is Neeto. No introduction convo, she doesn't acknowledge you're different than the regular townsfolk, yet when you meet her for the 2nd time suddenly she's waiting on the docks for you to arrive and help her cause you're the only one she can count on. Really..? It goes even further with the fireworks scene with her acting like she's the protagonists girl friend, asking to be taken to see the world, and then the kid gives her the super special thing he just got! Wat... Where did that all come from? Where did any of these relations come from? We never get to see, participate, or develop any of it. We only get the after notes, which leaves a player rather uninvested in everything.  I didn't have a very high bar going into this game but I had hope it could be a shadow of a Zelda game as it likes to so proudly tell everyone. But this... this was a fanfic AU of a couple Zelda mashups (majorly Windwaker if one couldn't tell from the videos) where they had to make a few minor character design changes so they wouldn't get dinged by the trademark. Featuring the Zora, the Evolved Bird-Zora, the Hylians, Octoroks, Moblins, Buzzblobs, Keese, Peahat Larva, the Master Sword & Mirror Shield, the 3 Emblems (Green, Blue, Red), Islands as remnants of an ancient civilization... I'm surprised I didn't see any Cuckoo beyond the weather vanes.

Don't expect to busy yourself searching the environments for newspapers, books, letters, or other found documents that would deepen your immersion in the game world. Instead, sit back and let the game guide you through a very curated and fascinating two hours. Some may find the ending inscrutable, but I think it wraps up nicely while still providing you plenty to think about and chew over.

DS3 employs the same stuff as before just a little faster and with more aggressive monsters.  I would say play the first before hand as this one heavily references the lore of the first, that and there is no respect for those who watch videos instead of playing it themselves. As with all Souls games, this one really begins on newgame plus. In fact they are usually designed for you to miss or mess up story arcs and 'quests' the first time around.   So yeah, adapt, 'git gud' and beat the Dark Souls games or tuck tail and retreat to the safety of your hand holding titles.

I previously made a review back when I had 500 hours for this game and chose to not recommend it. I stated that I would change my opinion when the devs fixed the game breaking bugs that were at hand and they have fixed them since so here I am changing my review. Dead by Daylight has provided me 1,061 hours of fun, laughter, fear and anger for sure, but I've met some really amazing friends along the way that really made me stick through all the bumps in the road with this game. The developers have made some mistakes but they're trying to make the game better for all of us with every update they provide. That being said The Halloween Chapter was a huge step from The Last Breath Chapter which was a big mess in my opinion. Overall, Dead by Daylight is a fun horror survival game that can be competitive at times and is worth the buy! :)

Okhlos is a roguelike from Devolver Digital, the same guys who published Enter the Gungeon. The game's main selling point is its chaotic gameplay - you rampage through 8 linear stages with your mob, looking to strike down the gods of Greece. A lot of the environment is destructible, making the game enjoyable on a weird, primal level. Your mob consists of various characters - citizens, warriors, defenders, slaves, and philosophers. The philosophers lead in this revolution against the gods - if your philosophers die, it's all over. You control a philosopher and the mob separately, but the mob can only walk within a certain range of the philosopher. You can command the mob to come together, spread out, attack, and defend. There are power-ups to collect and carry (via slaves), and traps to avoid. It's an innovative idea for a roguelike and absolutely worth picking up if you're a fan of the genre. The only major flaw is the repetition: 8 stages means you'll become very familiar with these environments very quickly. If you're not hooked on the core gameplay, the chaos and the Greek setting won't make up for it.   Okhlos is a gem, but it's a gem that might get dull if you stare at it for too long. Strongly reccomend to any roguelike fan; cautiously reccomend to anyone who isn't instantly excited by gameplay footage.    https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RaEW633eWK4

Wow. Just wow. I don't think there's anything in this game that I don't love. But that's getting ahead of myself. Let's start with the overview of the game's... story.  You play Jill (Julianne), a bartender. You get some crazy patrons in the bar. You listen to their stories, offer advice, and laugh about a drink named Bad Touch because you're an immature little girl. You do all of this in a city aptly named 'Glitch City'. I say it is aptly named because several times you'll see weird distortions show up on the screen and a girl called 'Anna' will appear. But she appears to you and only you... which made me skeptical as to whether or not Jill is a robot... The mechanical suits that the paramedics also glitch out, meaning that those inside had a difficult time removing them. Nanomachines are said to cause weird reactions in a local AI population called Lilim (apparently 'bot' or 'robot' is offensive). Glitches be everywhere.  Information is dispensed to you either through conversations with customers, or through, and I kid you not, ♥♥♥♥♥ boards. And a blog/newspaper sorta deal. All of the ♥♥♥♥♥/newspaper stuff is managed through your phone which you check every day religiously when you wake up.   The gameplay is visual novel style except instead of doing the normal thing where you pick one of several contrived responses to stuff to further the plot... you serve drinks! I don't know. Maybe it's the wannabe chef inside me, or the mathematician that loves following a special set of instructions, or maybe it's just fun to roleplay something entirely foreign to me but this bartending aspect is quite fun. And the best part? You actually have to pay attention to what the customers are saying. This isn't like visual novels where most of the text is just fluff and you can probably get through without even bothering to read. Some customers have special preferences, some customers have a usual, and it's up to you to pay attention to what's going on to keep up and serve the 'right' drinks.   I'll be straight with you, I'm not sure that there's an overarching story to really get involved with. And to be quite honest with you, I'd prefer it that way. I believe the best way to experience this game is you are a bartender just living your life. Your game route goes over the course of about a month. Not a lot of stories in life get wrapped up in a month. There's only one mini-story that gets wrapped up, but it gets wrapped up in a way that makes sense and is timely in fashion. And perhaps that's the beauty of this game. That it is just a little slice of life. It does make good sequel bait.   The aesthetic of the game is beautiful. The music makes sense and it's also quite varied. You can just tell how much effort went into making this game a work of art. It's not difficult to buy into the environment of cyberpunk city on the edge. It's amazing. It's a polished piece of work that is well worth the money that I spent on it.   So with that it seems that I've given this one a positive review! I fully recommend the purchase of Vallhalla! This is the type of game that made itself exactly what it needed to be. It's not long, it's not hard, but it's an enjoyable experience from start to finish.

Not as bad as the reviews say. I went into it expecting a pretty terrible shooter with uninspired gameplay and a forgettable story.  It's a fairly straightforward shooter with some annoying bits and poor difficulty tuning. However, the shooter elements are pretty good, and while the game tries a bit too hard on the horror-like elements, it does a good job of making the various monsters pretty intimidating.  The actual story started out semi-decent, then evolved rapidly into something really stupid, then ended pretty well. It's not something to write home about but the ending was nice.  Overall it's not bad even if it is kinda short (5-6 hours). Worth a look if it's on sale and you want to waste a few hours having fun shooting mythological creatures. I advise avoiding the hard difficulty setting, and even turning it down to easy if the challenges get annoying. The difficulty is poorly tuned and it is a bit more fun on easy than it is on other difficulty settings.

Although the 'skiing' system is original and quite interesting, a lack of better class equality and  much  better graphics (because the graphics of this game are of cube 2 level, and that's a bit of a shame for a  new  PC game), coupled with a few bugs and annoying glitches like the fact that spawn points are completely random it seems...  All in all, you can try this game if you want, well it's free after all, but in my opinion, if you want a really good free FPS game, go play Planetside 2 which is on a completely other level. (well it's not really the same budget to start with).  My final rating is 4/10, because it's not because a game is free that anything goes, even if there are some original points and ideas included in the package, I don't consider it to be enough to get more than that rating.

In my opnion, Dead Space is the masterpice of all Dead Space series. And one of the most scary, challenging, astonishing and incredible Survival Horror game I ever played since Silent Hill.  You really have to have balls to play this alone in your bedroom, at night with your headsets on, and be ready to jump of the chair and have a lot of heart-attacks through whole gameplay.  I've finished it more than once, and even at 2014 it still amazes me.   - Great Graphics (bear in mind this is a 2008 game, and can't be compared with a 2013/2014 game) - Great Story and atmosfere (the ambientation outside Ishimura's Ship are great and really makes you fell in space) - Different battle approach (here you don't kill the 'monsters' shooting just in the head, you have to dismember them to it) - Good story  The game runs well, and you don't need a very powerful rig to play it. A friend of mine, runs it in a 8800GT grafics card with everything on high (no filters on)  The only bad thing in this game is the controller port, you have to configure it and adjust the aim sensibility, because, when you change to controller the aim moves slowly. And in this game you must to be accurate and fast to kill the monsters.  But is not a hard thing to do, there is a lot of websites explaning how to deal with this simple issue.  I really recommend this title if you love Survival Horror games, really worths every penny you spend on it.

This title was off to a rocky start but with consistant updates and ehancements has gone from mediocre to most probably the best Sim out there.   Though this sim is listed as a Free 2 Play title and comes with several cars and a few track for nothing - you will need to spend a little cash in order to get the cars and tracks you want. The pricing on these addons is quite minimal and you only need to buy what you actually want and when you want it. Oh and you can test drive every car as many times as you like before deciding if you want to buy it.  Here's the rundown:  Graphics 8/10 - they are by no means bad - in fact the sim looks very natural and life like  Sound 10/10 - You can't get any more real than this without getting in the actual cars. Every detail from transmission whine and chassis squeaks to wind, transmission flex, gravel and reverb etc.. have been taken into account. The sound alone give this sim more immersion than the competition.   Physics 9/10 This score was a few points lower a couple months back, but with recent and ongoing enhancements to several group cars and more being fixed currently, R3e is more than deserving of this rating. If they get all the cars to the level they are achieving with the ones they already edited - I see a glorious 10 out of 10 coming in the near future.  FFB 10/10 - for me it was GSCE that held the title for FFB with AC and RF2 following slighly behind, but R3e has taken that title by leaps and bounds. I know this sounds exagerated, but you have to feel it to believe it - and I've never felt this good behind the wheel in my 15+ years of racecraft.  Car Content Variety 8/10 There are tons of cars available - though the list is still a bit on the short side. This is not to say that I don't have enough cars. The fact is I own more than I've had time to drive, but still wish there were a few Group C cars in the mix as those are my absolute favorites. Did I mention that you can test drive every car without buying.  Car Quality 10/10 - This title is on par with AC when it comes to detail. The exterior/interior along with suspension and tires are all done with upmost attention to even the small details.  Track Content Variety 8/10 Again the amount of tracks are quite limited compared to other titles, but not lacking. Several of my favorite tracks are included (Bathurst, Spa, Nurburgring, RedBull Ring, Suzuka, Nords, etc..) They have also included a lazer scanned Nordschleife into the mix with more lazer scanned tracks to come.  Track Quality 10/10 Right up there with AC and iRacing when it comes to track surface and detail. The sky boxes are absolutely believable and the environmental details are more than real enough to enjoy.   Multiplayer 8/10 Much better racing experience than most sims I've played, but it still has it's issues. The netcode has been updated recently and seems to be working great, but I have not had enough time with it to give it a better score.  Singleplayer 10/10 The AI have been improved greatly and their difficulty can be set to adaptive which takes some patience for them to settle in, but when they do they are a joy to race with. Also the 'Experiences' make this title a dream when it comes to playing championships.  Community 10/10 This is probably the least toxic sim community out there. Most everyone is friendly and helpful. The devs are also very active within the forums and listen to the players.  I'm not sure what all I left out, but I'll be sure to update this in the future.  Also: I know this review appears to be written by a fanboy due to the high scores given. I assure you this is not the case. I play many Sims more than I have this. Infact there was a long time which I felt this title was a waste of drive space, but as stated above - it has come a long way.  Whether you're a veteran to racecraft or are a complete newbie, give RaceRoom a try. You have nothing to lose since it can be played for free.

WARNING: This review is long  This game can be summed up in three words:  - Grind - RNG - A s**tton of updates  OK, strictly speaking that's six words, but it still sums up the game. I feel that I've recently got out of it, but that's just because I've got reasonably far and kind of lost interest. I know I'm not the kind of person who's played over 3,000 hours, but I still know the game well, having been playing it for 3 years now, totalling up to 640 hours of Warframe. There is one good reason for this: It's a lot of fun. Starting from havng nothing and finding Rare Mods, getting Prime weapons etc. is absorbing and even tactical.  HOWEVER, there is a difference between absorbing and time-consuming. You wouldn't like it if you're impatient or don't like grinds (or RNG...), and getting the good stuff in Warfame is HEAVILY dependent on both of those. But if you don't mind those, then there really is A HECK OF A LOT TO DO in Warframe. The amount of content is staggering, considering it's Free-To-Play (though most cool cosmetics are only obtainable with micro-transactions). You'll feel like a (forcing myself to use the word) noob at the start - as in REALLY feel like one - when you see the Rank 30 Grand Masters with Primes and all the Cosmetics etc,, but if you put time into it it can be VERY fun.  My main problems have sprung up in the newer updates (The War Within as ironically what finally pushed me away from Warframe, when I'd hoped for it to hook me back in), and I will list them here:  - Riven Mods (My least favourite thing ever) - Rare Mods that you really want but can never find or are actually unobtainable except through trading, forcing you to buy platinum or get rid of prime parts - Virtually unobtainable Prime Weapons (Nikana Prime...) - New content rushing in far too quickly, such as an amazing new thing coming when you're still farming for the cool new content from 2 BLOODY UPDATES AGO  Riven Mods, as a separate note, I absolutely loath. I once got one, but after trying with nearly every primary weapon I own to unlock it and nothing happening, I realised, 'This is probably for a weapon I don't even use.' It is just a schlep to unlock them and you might not even like what they offer. The only other way to obtain them is through trading, and they are traded A LOT, but that forces you to buy platinum, which a lot of people don't want to do, seeing as it's expensive.  Rare Mods can be a bit 'meh' at times, but some are just un-live-without-able, such as Jagged Edge, Vital Sense, etc. But them problem is that they're called 'Rare' for a reason. I spent 2.5 years hunting for Jagged Edge and Vital Sense before I finally got them, and had to resort to platinum to buy Crushing Ruin. Hunting for good mods is the most frustrating thing in Warframe.  I could write a lot about Warframe, but that would make the review too long. The best way to understand what I mean is to try the game out.  So you may ask, why is this review positive?  Answer: Because some people are more patient than others, and some can sink a lot of time into a very time-reliant game.  If you are patient (as in very) and can sink a lot of time into a game, then Warframe is definitely the game for you.  And when you become a Rank 30 Grand Master with Primes and all the Cosmetics etc., you will REALLY feel like one.  Give it a go, and see if you like it. Just remember that progress is slow and starting is difficult, and that there is a grind unlike anything you've ever seen.

I'm going to begin by voicing my displeasure at the fact that steam has only a two means of reviewing a game. The bright blue thumbs up or red thumbs down is the first thing people see and it is criminal that I'm forced to open my opinion with either a blatant recommendation or condemnation, as it is often never that simple. Murdered: Soul Suspect is one of those games that I feel very ambivalent towards. It gets a thumbs down though for reasons I'll gladly expound upon here but I'll start by saying that If you do want to buy this game, go ahead, but do it at a discount as there isn't enough of it to warrant its full price by a long shot.   Murdered: Soul Suspect is a supernatural intrigue game where you play as Ronan O'Connor, gangland warrior turned detective and wandering samurai warrior, who is tracking a mysterious serial killer. Ronan is about apprehend the killer in the opening cutscene when the killer decides he's had enough of the law and reenacts the opening scene from Watchmen all over Ronan's face before shooting him seven times in the chest. So Ronan is dead, but you knew that already. Ronan now has to endeavor to do in one night what the entire police force couldn't do in years worth of time and solve the case of the Bell Killer. M:SS reminds me of two very specific games I actually liked, the first of which is Alan Wake. Like Alan Wake, this game is about supernatural dealings with a distinctly traditional American feel. Also like AW, the rules for the supernatural stuff in this game are very well defined.  The supernatural lore being dealt with this time is ghosts, exploring all the traditional facets of what you might think ghosts are like, but before you go hog wild there is an exception to each of your abilities. You can pass through walls but not consecrated or ethereal walls, which mark the level boundaries. You can jumble electronics, but not to any meaningful degree. You can possess people but not control their actions. You can teleport, but not over demon holes so you still have to circumvent those on foot. Also like in Alan Wake, there isn't a whole lot to do in between plot points except hunt for collectibles. This leads me into the real issue I had with this game: its design.  Murdered: Soul Suspect is a game of extra steps. I kept a running list of things the game took more than one step to complete without needing to: Often the game will end a cutscene just to have you press the interact button to continue the same cutscene. When questioning a suspect, you have to press a button for each option you want to explore but the interrogation doesn't end until all options are explored, so it might as well be one long cutscene. You can read the minds of people you possess but each person has exactly two things to say and you have to press the button twice to hear both things when they could just say them both at once. When piecing together the clues of an investigation, if you pick a wrong option you get booted from the screen, having to reenter the screen to continue. When grabbing collectibles, you have to reveal the object first before picking it up. The collectibles are half hidden but the game tells you where they are so there's no need for hiding them. Speaking of collectibles, there are a plethora of them, all of which have very little to no bearing on the actual game. Each area has a unique object that, once all are gathered, will unlock a spooky campfire story unrelated to what you are doing. There are a series of over arching collectibles including diary pages from Ronan's dead wife (really the only means of character development), handy info blurbs copy-pasted from the Salem tourism comity website, and newspaper clippings reminding you how creepy and invincible the serial killer is.  While Alan Wake also felt the need to boldly announce the appearance of an enemy so that there is no way for you to be caught off guard, at least it had engaging combat. Every once in a while the game breaks up the monotony by presenting you with enemies to sneak up behind and quick-time-event to death. Once or twice a level, there will be demons that half-heatedly wander around in an enclosed area. You have to hide in the hiding spot and wait for the monster to turn around so you can slowly walk up behind it and kill it. This ends up being tedious and non-threatening, unit you mess up. If you manage to not properly dispatch your spectral foe, the backlash is absurd. Demons will kill you within a 3-5 second window unless you can scurry back to a hiding spot. However, once detected, the demons can see you in hiding spots; forcing you to shift between them as fast and erratically as possible until they give up. This turns what little combat there is into a game of Red Light, Green Light. These sections make no sense to the lore of the game either as there is no reason given as to why Ronan seems to be the only ghost that can dispatch the demons. No introduction is given to the power nor is it explained that Ronan is the only one capable of it, leading me to wonder why the other ghosts all flee in terror when the demons show up.  The second game I'm reminded of is L.A. Noire, because the investigation sections are reminiscent of the ones in that game. You have to walk around the crime scene and collect the clues, using them to piece together the motive of the criminal. There are six to fourteen clues per scene but only three of them at most are ever necessary to continue. It's no secret that detective work is not as flashy or intriguing as it is portrayed in popular culture, but no where is this more obvious than video games. L.A. Noire had a good mix of action and detective work, due (I think) to it's structure. All the clues were relevant because solving the case was the goal of the mission and everything unfolded naturally. Murdered, by contrast, has you investigating glaringly obvious crime scenes which, more often than not, inform you of something you already knew. For example, one crime scene later on in the game has you investigating a room full of people the Bell Killer murdered all at once. At the point of investigation, however, you have already been made aware of what the killer did afterword, so the reason for killing the room full of people is obvious. Then, once you investigate the room, Ronan stands there and tells the the reason the killer did it, even though you already knew. Really the investigating is just being a master of the obvious. It's also impossible to fail to find all of the clues, because the game tells you how many are present in the scene.  Murdered, like many games these days, would have probably made a better book or movie. The game play really just consists of different kinds of walking around and looking at things. Despite being a game made by an established developer and publisher, this is something any indie dev could have made, if not made look quite as good. In the end, games are meant to be interactive; that is what the medium excels at. Yet more and more it seems like games are being made with interactivity at the bottom of their priority list. Once during the middle of a cutscene I caught my self replying to text messages. It was then I realized how bored I was with the game; how absolutely unengaged I felt. The story was really not all that bad but being set in Salem, you can bet with reliability that it will have to do with what Salem is famous for. I would rather them have just made a movie out of it and cut out the game play entirely. At least then it would have taken me only 120 minutes to get through.

A great NES-style side-scrolling game even if you've never played an NES side-scroller (which I haven't). It also helps a lot if you're an AVGN fan, as there are a lot of comical references scattered throughout the game.  The game is ball-bustingly hard and cuss-inducingly irritating, but in a way that makes you want to keep playing it over and over again rather than making you want to stand up and throw your computer across the room (but you might do that too). Plus, there's also the comic relief you'll get from one of many of AVGN's trademark 'I'd rather' lines, which you see every time you die.  Oh, and the music's awesome, too.

... Buy this game...  Lets start with the ratings  OVERALL: 9/10  Gameplay: 10 Sound: 10 Replayability: 7 Story: 8  And now the reasoning. GAMEPLAY: 10 I came from Bastion, not realizing this was made by the same people until I started it up. In this case, that was a good thing, because I'd come to enjoy the old world charm of bastion and likely would have been turned off of the digital-world theme. However, I was rewarded almost instantly with my experience with Bastion and appreciated the enigmatic narrative taking a back seat to a consistant battle engine that was fun to play and catered to whatever my mood happened to be at the time, 'Turn' based combat, or real time. Enemy variety was interesting and posed different challenges based on my abilities and the 'terrain' in each fight. This aspect seems lost to many developers who simply script enemies with more health and hit harder without adding any depth to the combat. The ability customizability absolutely roped me in. I adore being able to customize loadouts in attempts to maximize potential of one aspect or another.  One of my favorite builds wasn't even the most powerful. Charming enemies for long periods of time and letting them kill each other while my pet ran around helping them, and collecting cells spawned more temporary friendly minions. I was having, dare I say it, FUN!  It allows people to take on whatever role is comfortable and play through the game at their own speed and play style.  I gotta give them props for the ability variety and how effective they are based on what enemy types are present.   SOUND: 10 No conflicting information. Every sound quickly becomes associated with the result happening on the screen. And the soundtrack fits the narrative. And like that cheesy review I read about pausing to listen to Red sing... Darn it if I didn't do the same thing...  My tab key got stuck several times. >_>  REPLAYABILITY: 7 I am grading this more harshly due to the fact that there ARE side challenges that you can complete to elongate the original play through, but they aren't available to you again after that. While being able to replay through the story does allow you to catch things you may have missed on the first pass, it doesn't offer too much else. However, I still catch myself playing through the story from time to time, just to remember the smart design of it all.  STORY: 8  I felt there was a few more questions to be answered, or things that could have been available to those who wanted more information. I read as much text as I could find in order to learn more about this world and the city, and was admittedly a bit disappointed in not knowing the origins of the town and other such things. BUT ONLY A BIT! The rest of the narrative was very well done, and allowed the player to fill in the gaps without insulting their intelligence. I still remember the surprise from the begining of the game resulting in immediate intrigue...   TLDR; It's a bit pricey for how long it lasts, but the entire experience is QUALITY for the time spent, in my opinion. If you catch it on sale, even at 25%... pick it up if it seems interesting to you. I seriously doubt you'll be disappointed.

A (truly) free to play hack n' slash / arpg game, set in the land of Wraeclast, it can't but not remind at least a bit of Diablo series. It has all the standard stuff you'd expect from a game this type such as waypoints, portal & identify scrolls etc. But the greatest thing this game has (other than the no pay to win policy) is the endless customization of your characters AND of your gear. You can make a melee character out of your archer/spellcaster (and vice versa), who says a witch can't have a two-handed sword and/or be a tank? There is no techtree in which you select abilities, no, you get skills as rewards and use whichever ones you want on your character. You can make your own gear, and remake it. The possibilities are endless and that is what makes this game a true gem.

The game really lives up to its expectations and appeal for Gauntlet fans without over complicating things. I'll keep my review short and objective. The pros and cons I've found below:  Pros:  - No level grinding trying to find better gear if you just want to jump in a game and not wonder if you are the right level and geared up. Those games are great but sometimes we just want a quick session of fun!  - Game sticks to its roots with all four original classes. I've found the Elf the easiest to use and stay alive. Merlin is cool too but my reflexes are not fast enough to dish out those nice spells. So whatever your playstyle is whether it be magic, ranged combat, or melee it's all here!  - Level design is neatly layed out so you don't get lost.  - Graphics are surprisingly great.  - Plenty of achievements to invest in mastery of each of the four classes.  - Nice spells with the potions. You can carry max of 5 potions so you can equip two pieces of gear before entering dungeons.  - Great local co-op fun too. My mom and I connected two conrollers and had a blast. She is new to games so she fought melee while I covered her with my Elf. It is quite a blast if you have other local players to with. Brings back memories of 4 player co-op at the arcade!  Cons:  - Tried quick match for multiplayer and was unable to join a match. I also opened my game to public but during entire session no one joined my game. This might be a glitch. I'm sure this will be fixed soon. This is really my only con so far.  Great hack'n'slash fun for everyone whether you are playing for 10-15 minutes or several hours!

Killer is Dead? More like Game is Dead. Crashes, bugs that cause your unavoidable death, terrible controls and terrible camera. Not all issues are because it's a bad port, but most of them and it doesn't look like the developer cares at all. Avoid paying more than $5 for this. The dating-sim mini game would be fun, if the game didn't just randomly crash. The combat would be nice, if you could actually see your enemy without having to turn the camera manually by 180 degrees every 5 seconds. The graphics are obviously stylized, but that doesn't explain the 30 fps cap (which can luckily be fixed, the complete lack of anti-aliasing (good luck modding that without breaking the game even more) and ridiculous loading times even on a high-end system with SSD. An AI basically doesn't exist. Monsters will just rush at you and attack from time to time. Extremely easy to predict. And way too much button-smashing. I understand that this is somehow supposed to be a main feature of the game (can destrying fingers and buttons be considered a feature?), but it's just way too much. Is there a story? Not sure. The game crashed again before I could find out. But so far it doesn't seem like there is any story that makes sense. I hope the dialogs are just poorly translated, because as they are right now, they could have been written by a 12 year old. Was told this game is like DMC. I have been told a lie.

This game CANNOT be compared to Skyrim. This game is actually a first person Diablo. Long levels, fight pack of mobs room after room, low enemy diversity, huge focus on loot, etc. Since your weapon is magic, instead of looting actual weapons you are looting materials for crafting spells, which is in my opinion much more interesting than weapons with different colors.  So, you're this badass mage who can control the elements of magic, but doesn't know how to jump. Collect loot and craft new spells, learn how spell synergies works with each other, cast crazy magic combos. The key to having fun with this game is understanding it, not just the crafting but how the spell actually works. I'm not gonna dive into the details but every time I would try a new sigil I found out crazy spell combos with other sigils. Like for example when you apply corruption to a mob then bugs spawn after death; if you are using lightning, the bugs will transfer the lightning status effect to other enemies (a shocking stun) which will result in all other enemies around you stunned by your lightning-corruption bugs.  It's like diablo, you always hunt for better loot. In Lichdom, you try to get that perfect shape with the right stats to deal big numbers damage. And then, mid-way through the game, you discover the SYNERGY shape, a special upgrade to the legendary item which enable you to mix two elements to cast some kind of ultimate spell. Now you're hunting for those special legendary items who can upgrade to the synergy shape and cast flashy spells that deals tons of damage.  Graphics are beautiful, the spell effects are amazing, the combat can be dull if you let it be, but if you're trying to combo elements and discover new ways to use your differents shapes with different elements it becomes a lot of fun for the whole game. The default BATTLEMAGE difficulty is challenging, not too easy not too hard. If you're dying often, your shield is too weak, craft a new one with higher HP.  Conclusion; it's been a while since I've enjoyed a solo game that much. I hope there's gonne be a sequel or a DLC. Thanks for reading!

Seems to take a good amount of inspiration from Valkyria Chronicles battle system, probably the only game outside the series to do so. If your not familiar it's progressive turn-based, 3D combat with real time interrupts. Airship Dragoon is more hard core (read: punishing) than Valkyria by a long shot though. Highly fun in the early to mid stages,  Has balance problems in the later phases specifically, everyone starts lugging around massive explosive weapons and the tactics combat becomes more reminiscent of 'Artillery'. There are a few other problems with the game, such as the A.I. almost infinite capacity and willingness to engage in hopeless but time consuming defensive operations, and small touches like being unable to set default equipment configurations, despite the fact that you almost always go with a standard loadout for each class (based on current available tech). That said there are ALOT of good things about this game and it's probably the most fun you will have with a game which you are unlikely to finish.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: Summary (if you dont want the honest review)  Game play 7/10 Gunplay 8/10 Perks 5/10 Specialists 9/10 Performance 2/10 Game as a whole 5/10   This would have scored higher if the performance of the game wasn't so atrocious and if there were dedicated servers for once. Sorry folks, still get kicked do to hosts rage quiting and host migrations. You would think after 10 years of these Effing games they would utilize DEDICATED SERVERS!! It appears the business protocol these days is give less while customers pay more. SO if you're a dedicated COD fan buy this game by all means! You will certainly have a lot of fun with it. If you're an average gamer and like the absolute best out of your games, wait till this gets fixed though it may be months even years and the price will probably never drop either lol. I would honestly have to say, play this on another PC before you buy it or if you have a console just buy it for that as you wont have any issues on console. Hope this helps a great deal of you.   Game play 7/10 I am no fanboy of COD and decided to Give Black Ops 3 a try since the BOps has been the better game of the COD series. The game itself is super fun with multiplayer of course being the main focus, but it's still the same Spawn/ die shooter it has always been. The mechanics of the wall running and jump boosting still feel unrefined and at times is a bit gimmicky. You think you have a wall run just right and then you just fall to your death. this COD is nothing like Black ops 2 (or as I like to call it Crack Ops) in terms of game speed, it's face paced but it's not On Crack fast paced if you know what I mean. The game feels smooth as hell (when there's no performance issue) and plays nicely, almost feels like BOps 1 again!   Gunplay 8/10 The gunplay feels more natural than most of the other games and you have a very wide array of weapons at your disposal, all with their advantages and disadvantages. With that said, there is still balancing needed for some of these weapons before this game turns into COD AW and everyone uses a META weapon so to speak and nothing else. The time to kill in this COD seems fast and also slow and weapon choice makes all the difference in this game. If you have a mildly fast shooting weapon and you go up against someone with a weapon with a higher fire rate than you, expect to lose 80% of those fire fights as the speed of the gun makes or breaks you. All the High damage class weapons shoot the slowest so its harder to get big kill counts if you're into that.   Specialists 9/10 The funnest and newest addition to this COD is the Specialists and their weapons. You have I think 10 specialists to choose from each with a offensive and defensive capability which only one at a time can be equiped and man can they be a game changer! This is a special that has a recharge rate so it can never be spammed but only charged faster by a certain perk or by doing extremely well during matches. I have not found any one specialist to be more OP than the rest as they all feel equally powerfull when using offensive capabilites. You may consider a few of them 'Cheap' to use but i reiterate, THEY ARE GAME CHANGERS when used properly.  Perks 5/10 The perks have not changed much and a lot of them do the same thing as previous games with some minor (and i mean minor) tweeks to make them play a little differently. Wild cards are a thing still so you can choose 6 perks and one weapon or mix it up for some good variety for different play styles.   Performanace 2/10 Now lets talk about performance. There has been all sorts of nasty issues with this PC version of BOps 3 and most noticable and annoying of these issues is fram rates. This game, forget about playing it if you dont have a solid GPU as anything under a 660 or R7-R6 will just not perform well with this game (it has affected some users with 970's-R9's and up, but harsher issues are more likely with lesser GPU's). I have duel 970's and let me tell you now, there is NO SLI or Crossfire support and the game will consitantly crash with it enabled. While playing Zombies or campaign I get solid frames. When you begin multiplayer PvP, the issues are so noticable it hurts. All throughout the game the frames will drop between 10-20 and even 30 frames! this causes lots of stuttering gameplay and inaccurate representations of player placements and actions. We all know the Net Code for this game and all COD's has always been garbage but this lack of optimization for PC just makes it worse to the point it is unplayable by some. My only gripe has been these cruddy dropped frames but I'm sure not much will be done since the PC community always seems to be on the back burner when major issues arise in all the unfinished games we hae been receiving over the ast few years.

Shadow Warrior is everything you loved about the first game (shadow warrior 2013) and mixed in with various elements of FPS MMOs.   Enemies will drop various items such as money, health or gems. Gems are used for weapons to alter thier stats. they have a coloured tier system as well which will be familar to anyone whos done video games in the last I dunno 15 years. However the weapons you buy/unlock/earn over time remain but the gems and combonations are up to you. You can mix up elemental damage or life drains or specialise in firing types like Charge ups to rabid fire. I havent discovered them all yet but you get a good chance to be creative with your mix. My katana has electricty and chi drain but someone else might prefer Fire, Toxic or Ice with Life Drain or extra critical damage. You get what i mean...  Enemies behave much like mobs that need to be triggered or pulled. The variety of creatures will force you to adapt quickly but depending how you shape your character overtime with your skill point collection. Some enemies will fare better than you. Which is great because the combat difficutly challenge is always present. And if you can meet up with some buddies, 4 player co-op might help take on those big nasty creatures that control a mob.   and of course, bigger mobs, bigger loot.    It's gory, wicked fun. Story quests, side quests and lots of exploring to do. I'd say this game is gonna be around for a while, I think there's strong potential for DLC and I'd recommend it to anyone who was a fan of games such as Borderlands, Diablo or Mass Effect.

Imagine trying to create a campaign for someone to play through, without the ability to create anything. That's this. Yolu have no control over what you 'make'. You click a button and it randomly spawns a dungeon/town.

Delta Force is a pretty decent first-person shooter, having several campaigns with a variety of missions you can play alone or co-operatively (though those options might be limited, considering the game is roughly 17 years old).  The story is your usual war against terrorists and such, I think they're Russian but I wasn't into the story at all.  The game offers several weapons, but there is not much purpose in using most of the weapons as all enemies die from a single bullet (on Normal difficulty), so the M4 is pretty much the only primary weapon you should bother with, while the secondary equipment barely matters.  The AI is quite primitive and have very unpredictable accuracy, which is dangerous considering you can only survive one bullet (though that is usually immediately followed by another as the majority of enemies use burst-fire rifles). The enemies work very robotically, unceasingly staring in the last direction you were seen or shot your gun at until the same happens again, regardless of how far you were or what was between you and the foe with the only exceptions being silenced weapons and the knife, but those never pay off since the entire enemy force is permanently alerted if one sees you.  There are many different mission objectives, but they largely play out the same, practically forcing you to eliminate everyone to complete each mission.  Stability is an issue that I presume comes from its age, but I only encountered about 5 crashes throughout my playthrough. These problems include occasional framerate drops out of nowhere, random screen flickering, a game-breaking freeze mid-mission or the game crashing with an error message at the start of a mission.  If you have nostalgic memories of this game that you wish to relive (like yours truly), then it might be worth buying this game. I honestly can't say whether or not I'd recommend this instead of another game, but I can definitely say you absolutely should not buy this if you have hopes of playing multiplayer, because I'm 95% certain there are some to no servers and even less players.

TL;DR: It has lost its muchness. This is a general review of the gameplay from Vanilla, through Automaton, and those really average mini-DLCs... and now back into the horrendous 1.5 patch.  First, let me straight up state that I do love this game. The RPG element is a little bit lax with there being only 4 chat options, most of which either a - 'yes, no, sarcasm, some irritating word choice'. I was looking forward to Survival mode, having sunk countless hours into the Survival mode prior to the live release of the new and improved mode.  I took a minor hiatus from the game (thanks, Overwatch); lo, when I come back, excited to sink my teeth into Far Harbor, I get a fantastic alert that achievements are disabled if you so much as utilize any aesthetic mod. I, being a PC player, enjoy using my rig to its fullest capabilities; no longer can acquire a single achievement without having to go through the arduous task of grabbing <omitted editor> and then the associated mod to counteract the 1.5 patch nerf for achievements.   Why would Bethesda do this? Greed? Anger in their mediocre DLC product for a semi-unfinished game? Jealousy that modders, fans of the community are working tirelessly to make the game as beautiful as its Elder Scrolls counterpart? I'm disappointed... Vanilla is bland, and hard to invest significant time in - when you've had lots of wonderful additions, like buildings, light fixtures, darker scenery, storms and radstorms that actually sound amazing with 7.1. I almost feel like this is a deathknell for the parts of the community that don't want to have to travel the path of script editors to get there, if you're a fan of acquiring achievements to show that yeah, you did complete x, y, and this was my favourite part because reasons.   Many of the small cost DLCs aren't worth the monetary investment, they bring very, very minor changes to the game that modders already added for free. Its only purpose is for console players that wanted a slightly better finish to a hamstrung system. Who knows, perhaps Bethesda will see what a colossal ♥♥♥♥-up the change was, and remove it - me, however? I'm dubious.

First of all, while not entirely necessary, you should first play 'Tomb Raider' (2012) before even starting this game. It will make the experience - and story much more fun.  Honestly, I really liked this game. The story isn't as addicting and well-thought out as the first one, but nonetheless a lot of fun to follow. Even so, I like the open ending, as this series should definitely keep going as it is.  Gameplay-wise they really improved a lot, it emphasises a lot on the survival and adventure aspect. However, even on survival difficulty... it's still waaaay too easy sadly. I thought I would have had to conserve my ammo and resources a lot more than actually was necessary, therefore ending the game with half my gear due to only upgrading what was needed and only using a minimum of supplies. I relied a lot on stealth and taking as little damage as possible. Those last 2 bits did make the game very fun though as I refused to continue after I took too much damage or was blazing through my enemies female-Rambo style (which is definitely a close second option xD).  Graphics are absolutely gorgeous! Lara certainly has had some serious feminists have their way with her body though *ahem* but she didn't get any less admirable because of it. An iron woman with a lot of guts and probably bigger balls than melons at the moment. But Lara aside, the environment is stunning and breathtaking. With a decent rig (I ran it on a gtx980, 32GB RAM, Intel i7 5820K) this game looks absolutely amazing. On a side note though: I was especially surprised about the setting (Purehair?) that managed the hair on Lara, in the previous one, I often played with a bald Lara due to some glitch and it seemed very... artificial, in this one, she never lost her hair AND it looked very real to boot.  So... All in all, this game hit a bulls-eye in a lot of places. And I really hope that this doesn't get to the dev's heads, having them just milk this franchise's popularity because the first ones were great and stop putting effort in the next ones like many... MANY other great games have suffered from. Keep it up guys, and I'll gladly pay full price for what you bring us!

+ Pretty decent writing, hugely inspired by Earthbound and Mother 3. Really good background and progression. + Went into this expecting a turn-based RPG, got a great twist on a bullet hell game (a genre I usually don't give two sh*ts about and find boring, but this is crazy fun) + Without spoiling anything, both the neutral and genocide endings are absolutely ridiculous, tough as hell and a great challenge. Pacifist ending... eh. + Tons of secrets to explore + Crazy good soundtrack, especially the boss themes.  - Writing has its flaws, in particular lame jokes and bad memes thrown in with the serious parts. Characters are very one-dimensional and feel like something out of a children's cartoon for the most part - No idea why people go on about 'severe emotional trauma' with this game. The writing is really good as said but not amazing, and there really aren't any soul-crushing moments at all on any of the three routes. It's well-written but not enough to really hit hard in the slightest. - Huge jumps in difficulty throughout the genocide route. Most of it is easy as balls, one boss halfway through is a challenge, and HOLY GOD DAMN THE LAST BOSS IS INSANE. Great challenges, but I wish the rest of the run was on par with these two bosses. The neutral route also leaves you unprepared for its final boss. - Definitely on the short side. I was able to complete all 3 routes except for genocide last boss in 12 hours. - Pacifist ending (probably the most hyped up of the three) is the most ridiculously boring, cliche thing in the entire game

It is hard to state something like this when you're talking about one of the most famous indie game ever released, but here we go: I hate LIMBO. What I cannot really stand is its illogical way to present the puzzles, as to the player is rarely given the opportunity to recognize a danger and work out for a solution. The whole game is just a repetitive exercise of attempt->death->retry->death->solution found->new death. The death in LIMBO is not a punishment for the player's mistake, but the only possibility to find the solution to a puzzle. Frustration above satisfaction, you'll never solve a puzzle because you're good, but because you died enough to understand the only way to pass a section of the level. Praises go to the art style and to the atmosphere, and to the decision to have merciful checkpoints. Not enough for me.

This game is absolutely amazing. You can tell that there was an enormous amount of thought put into every detail, down to the very last corner. Bioshock Infinite is my favorite game to date, no question about it.  To start, the art direction is very good. Everything is arranged to be very appealing, and the colors are very balanced. I loved just walking around the city and soaking it all up. There are 1912 style shops to go into, and they are all populated. In one area, there is even a good, three-minute quartet that you can just sit and listen to. I was very impressed with the magnitude of everything. Seldom are you placed in a small, dark room or hallway. Most of the game is set in colorful areas with large statues and airy buildings.  I liked that it did not actually put you in the dark, but gave a dark feeling when it was appropriate. As per the Bioshock games, some of the events are quite dark, and I liked the uneasy feeling without the dark corridors of Rapture.  Combat is a step up from other games in the genre. The introduction of 'skylines' allows you to jump quickly from place to place, making everything very fast paced. I didn't quite like the inventory management as much as in the original Bioshock, however. I found it much harder to deal with my Vigors, mainly because you couldn't just scroll through them like in the other game. Still, you get used to it - that's a fairly minor gripe.  I can not say much about the story. If I told you what was so great about it, I would spoil it for you. I can say that it puzzled me, and it was a few hours after finishing the game that I worked everything out. I felt like some of the points were repeated too much though. I get that they try to help everyone understand it well, but I would rather just have it all thrown at me - that way it's more fun to work out on my own.  Overall, Irrational Games has gone above and beyond what anyone expected. The only way you would find this game worse than the original is if you are somewhat nostalgic. Even if you did not like the first or second game, I highly recommend that you still give Bioshock Infinite a try. As a side note, there are a few things that you will not quite understand if you skip the first game. They are little details, but in a game like Bioshock Infinite, it is the little details that really count.  This game is worth every cent. If you can not afford it, you can wait until the next sale. The trilogy tends to go for $15~.   Some screenshots:  http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=201192796   http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=201192216

I can't even joke about this game.  There are very few games I would praise as being masterpieces, since I'm not easy with praise. This game is what I consider one of the best games of our time. Flawlessly presently, masterfully written, a great experience throughout. This is the game that other games want to be. The story, be it not flawless, is engauging and intense. The one thing I have to give boundless praise is that though the game plays very cinematically (having 'movie' moments that define the stages of the plot) It does not take away from the gameplay experience like most video games that try to be movies tend to do. No, this does it like no other. The gameplay is so fluid, engauging and filled to the brim with sheer brilliance that you don't even mind that you're playing through a linear story progression. You just live it. The characters are one thing. Elizabeth is the shining star in this game. She just acts so much like a human being, and the other thing I love is that she's anything but cliche. Most female characters go these two ways; one, they become helpless and always need to be saved, and two, they try to be 'badass' and just become a female version of male brutes in the vague act of trying to seem independent and counter the damsel sterotype. Elizabeth is a human being. She feels, talks and acts like a human being. I couldn't ask for a better character. All in all, This game is near flawless. Besides some nitpicks, this game was insanely fun and satisfying. A engauging story, flawless gameplay, perfected presentation. Buy this game, you will not be dissapointed. 9.9/10

An incredibly simplistic Diablo-esque ARPG with  Steam Workshop support  to possibly enhance replayability.  Before starting the game you're given the option to start the vanilla version and the Workshop supported version. You can move your vanilla character to the Workshop version however the character you save your progress with Workshop mods on will no longer be allowed back to the vanilla game.  The game's difficulties range from Casual, Normal, Veteran, & Elite where the differences in these are that the gold drops are lower and enemy damage & health are increased the higher the setting. In Elite mode the game doesn't just feel harder, it feels like more like an endurance because I found myself constantly having to hit, run, heal, hit, run, heal, and repeat wasting more time than I should be wasting if I was on Veteran because everything on Elite can kill you in a flash and it doesn't help that the baddies take a hell of a lot longer to kill because of the artificial difficulty of increased baddie HP. There's a an option to check whether or not you want to activate 'Hardcore' mode for the rest of a character's playthrough where all it means is that one death means you can't play that character anymore so this is really for those who like to be more pressured into being careful to stay alive.  Unlike the first game you now have a choice of 4 classes however the previous classes have been removed though they all use similar & new equipment as the first. Each class has a handful amount of unique skills but I've found that I was really only putting points in an incredibly low amount of them all the way up to the end-game & max level, 100, leaving probably 80% of the skills without even a single point placed. Though effective this leaves the combat to show how simplistic its action is which all-in-all is just hit, run, & maybe heal. No matter how fancy the skills you've put the points on it's still just run, attack, run, and possibly heal up with a potion or a spell so should heavy damage be taken. You can only 'respec' the last 3 skills you've placed points on so you can do a small bit of testing to find out what suits your preference.  The controls for the game are typical isometric ARPG style with the all too likely-to-become problematic mouse-to-move and extensively rebindable keys with the exception of the mouse buttons being unrebindable. I found that the mouse controls can often be wonky; when I try to move to the opposite side of a crowd of baddies to get away from them for some reason the character would sometimes move to them and attack instead despite the mouse being nowhere near them. During critical situations it becomes an incredible nuisance.  Though very diverse in skins, models, & sounds almost all enemy attacks are similar to one another so I found that fighting just became repetitive with the exception of having some new skills to toy with but still overall becomes the same hit & run rule in combat. Even the bosses are fought the same and the way they fight you feel hardly any different from mob attacks.  The environment as well are very limited in variety. The general idea of different types of environment aside from the scenery with some different turns and slight elevations are 'don't walk on this right now or you'll get hurt', some levers or switches you have to activate to progress the game, and putting items in environmental sockets to again further progress.  Normally when anything dies in a map they stay dead. When setting your game to an 'internet' session you can 'reset' the entire world so everything can be killed again. Even all the treasure chests will be up for another round of loot. The multiplayer's functional & pretty basic; go to the lobby, make or join someone's session, and you just play from there either helping one another or doing your own things. Each person in a multiplayer session sees & gets their own loot from drops so there won't really be any stealing of items. If you and some other people have the exact same mods and sometimes load order of mods you can play the modded game with those people.  Despite all its 'problems' and for what it is the game works and does pretty well at what it tries to be even if there's not a whole lot new or special when compared to many other ARPGs like this. It can be good fun to just isometrically dungeon crawl seemingly killing endlessly especially with someone you know but because it lacks special things it can start to be incredibly tedious & repetitive incredibly fast where your only possible source of replayability is likely trying out the different classes, workshop mods, and/or the multiplayer.

Gunpoint is a short puzzle game by Suspicious Developments; it's the story of a man trying to unravel a web of corporate espionage while wiring lightswitches to trapdoors and leaping from tall buildings in a single bound. Richard Conway is your average, run-of-the-mill corporate spy, except that he has some high-tech gadgets that assist him in his professional skulduggery. After he's caught on camera sneaking around a building at the time that his client, rather inconveniently, is occupying when she is murdered, Conway must track down the security footage to throw the police off his trail and find out what's behind the homicide.  Conway starts the game with one handy gadget, a pair of Bullfrog trousers that enable him to leap to (and fall from) great heights, and soon acquires another, the Cross-Link. This device enables him to rewire a variety of electrical equipment, including motion detectors, sound detectors, door controls, light switches, trapdoors, power outlets, elevators, and more. Because the facilities he's required to break into are generally guarded by security officers who can kill him with a single gunshot, he has to use his gadgets to sneak past, stun, or otherwise deceive these men in order to hack his objectives and escape to his next assignment.  As our intrepid hero(?) completes assignments, he earns upgrades for his gadgets of three varieties (a battery capacity, trouser charge, and trouser enhancement) and cash, which he can use to buy batteries and new gadgets. By the end of the 20-mission story, he will have the ability to rewire guards' guns, break glass silently, and kick down locked doors.  Graphically, Gunpoint won't win any awards. It's an entirely 2D game with sprites representing Richard, his employers, and his adversaries. Backgrounds are hand-drawn but unexceptional. Sound effects are likewise minimal and unsophisticated. The soundtrack, on the other hand, with its jazzy, noirish tunes, is excellent.  The gameplay isn't incredibly complicated but it's fun figuring out the best way to work through levels using Conway's toys. The game comes with a level editor but no easy way to share levels with other users and it's a bit of a tricky affair to set up levels that work flawlessly. Writer and creator Tom Francis conceived hilarious dialogue for the game's story, which remains one of its big draws.  As I stated above, the story is a brief affair, taking even the most, um, careful player (I 'achieved' the Thoughtful rating on every level) 4-5 hours to complete. Bearing that brevity in mind, Gunpoint isn't the greatest value for your dollar at its normal asking price ($9.99), but it's been on sale many times and will likely be bundled soon. I highly recommend it, even for folks who are not normally fans of puzzle games (ahem).
