I played the 2013 version first then came back to this game. It was very much worth it. It lived up to it's reputation (although it didn't really have any). I'd say the 2013 version is better because Lo Wang has a better character. + there's Hoji. This is still a great game though.  It's suprising that this game doesn't have the same amount of hype Duke 3D has though. Even though this game is Duke 3D's little brother, it's miles better than that game. Although that may be because I didn't like Duke 3D. If you ask me, that's one of the most overrated games ever made. It has nothing going for it except the character itself. Duke's level of profanity is something else (although this Shadow Warrior also has a lot of kinky jokes).  The fun thing about this game is that it gives you lots of powerful weapons, which you get to use very frequently. E.g. you get to use the rocket launcher particularly lot. It's not just something you save for bossfights. But even the simpler weapons, like the shurikens and the sword remain highly useful until the very end of the game and it's a lot of fun to use themThe one I used the least is probably the sticky bombs, because those are a bit of a hassle (enemies might get close to you by the time they explode). Then there's fun stuff, like shooting fireballs from a severed demon head, Ripper heart which creates op clones of yourself AND AN ACTUAL F*CKING NUKE!   I also played through the Twin Dragon expansion. It was worth it. That expansion had lots of interesting maps, although the final battle was a bit... anti-climatic (remember Doom 2's original final boss level? yeah kinda like that).  There are even anime references in it (Slayers, Sailor Moon). Then there' stuff from warious movies and games (like Mortal Kombat and Lara Croft)  Oh and the invisible ninjas are the best enemies ever invented. Not too tanky, but stacked with damage. They have insanely powerful fireball spells, which usually insta-kill. Sounds OP, but this creates great high-tension clutch play moments and the game gives you similarly op tools to kill them. When you encounter this ninja, I just get this adrenaline rush that a lot of games fail to give me these days. This is how monster design is done! 'If everything is op, nothing is op!'  Buy this instead of Duke 3D. Much more worth it.

As a fan of interactive adventure games such as 'The Novelist,' I found it to be tedious, and terribly slow paced. Maybe it's trying to come off as realistic, but the game offered no breaks in the monotony normally found in games such as these. The choices, though hard to make, always left you feeling unnaccomplished, because out of three characters, one of them was going to get the cold shoulder. Maybe this is supposed to reflect real life decisions, but honestly only left you feeling bad for the main character. His work, or his family? A hard choice to make both in games and in real life, but when either choice leaves someone mad at you, what's the point?   The characters are hard to relate to, or even feel sympathetic for. Random moments such as a funeral for a death in the wife's family clash with a big event that's supposed to boost your career. Yet even then your child throws a fit for not taking him to an aero show. Constant nagging from all three characters does little but leave you torn in which decision is going to benefit you the most, which usually ends in neglecting your child. The writing, while well presented, is generic and unemotional. The voice acting, though not a major part of the game, is wooden and almost robotic.   This is the type of game to be played in one long run in order to get the full experience, but the game drags on and gets very, very repetitive even when the devs try to switch things up now and then. I wouldn't recommend this game unless you were feeling in the mood to bore yourself to death.

So, with two hundred hours in the game, and over 70% of achievements, I think that I can write a helpful review. The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth is an amazing game with so much diversity included. Each run you play will be different from the one you just did, or the run you did a few days ago. The characters are awesome and make each run even more unique. For instance, one character, Azazel, is a winged demon with a short range Brimstone (red laser) instead of traditional tears for damage, like most of the characters. The original character, Isaac, is the first playable character once you download and play the game. He starts with 3 hearts and a bomb. Once you unlock it, he starts with an item called the D6. The D6 let's you change an item on a pedestal into something else. This is very useful for finding an item you really don't want, and potentially changing it into something that could help win your run. Another matter to address is the DLC to the main game. The Binding of Isaac: Afterbirth is an expansion to the original game that adds so many new items, a gamemode, and even a few new characters as well. It is very much worth the amount of money they make you pay. This game is personally one of my favorites, and my most played game. It has me always playing and trying to finish the achievements for different characters while always keeping my interest without boring me at all. I highly recommend this game to anyone and everyone, I hope you have fun with it, because I know I am.  Also, it made me believe in God again...  jk tho

I'm struggling with it. The primary villain is darkness - it's yet another game that thinks that 'not being able to see anything' is equivalent with 'moody and immersive'. There's other minor problems (eg: hitbox bigger than character) but mostly it's the darkness. The art is nice, so let me see it! Another problem is that so many of the upgrades are yawn-worthy 10% bonuses - coming from Binding of Isaac where every pickup alters your character in some way, a 10% bonus as a reward is pretty miserable.  It's not terrible. There's some interesting ideas, but also some things that don't quite gel. For example, the art is bleak gothic boarding school, but the main character has an anime face. Enemies attack you with things you'd find in such a boarding school, but the main character attacks with some sort of mystical zappiness that spins around her waist (admittedly, I haven't got past chap 1 yet, so this may get explained later)  I'd give it a thumbs up - it's a reasonable game - but that bloody darkness is just frustrating, and so far, hasn't added anything to gameplay nor immersion for me.

Oh Lords of the Fallen, let me count the ways you irk me... 1. Your 'Ghost' (Aka. Soul Stain) will rapidly begin to fade away if you die too many times. Forcing you to either let it vanish in a puff of smoke or do a suicide run in order to try and reach it in time. (And then presumably die afterwards.) 2. EVERYTHING HAS A SHEILD. Get used to waiting for the enemy to awlays make the first move. Your attacks bounce off and they turns too fast for you to circle around them. 3. Speakign of tunring around. The mobs have godly levels of tracking. Expect them to turn 180* at the end of a full out charge to hit you. And then recover in 1/10th of a second after they attack. 4. Of the fours bosses I know about, each and every one is 'Giant dude in full body armor'. This applies to a vast majority of the regular mobs as well so far. 5. The god awful 'combo' system - If you can magically figure out what the timing is for a weapon you will consume less stamina, and even do a combo if you're sual wielding smaller weapons. Unfortunately the timing so so random and so TIGHT that pulling it off intetionally is near impossible. And sucks to be you if you accidentally do a combo while trying to get in a single hit then get your face smashed in by a boss. 6. The frame rate. Ugh. The Chromatic Abberation. Ugh. Turn off the latter to fix the former, or you're going to be motion sick the entire time you play this as your FPS bounces between 25~50 frames every other second. Even if you have a card than can handle the game. 7. Forced vsync. Why? I had to go into the .txt file they gratefully provide us with and MANUALLY turn this off. 8. The Foundation Boost. Wow. Buy DLC to get a 'free' additional 2 stat points and a starting potion. Why are you doing this? 9. Bosses randomly gain 99% damage reduction, foring to to wait out their attacks or kill the minions they summon first.

This War of Mine provides an experience of war seen from an entirely new angle. For the very first time you do not play as an elite soldier, rather a group of civilians trying to survive in a besieged city. In the first few approaches the game seems to be difficult, we feel this hopeless war, but with every hour we learn more and more how it all works and what to focus on and then already it is hoped that it can survive. The game requires proper management of resources and good planning.  It is the player's responsibility to maintain their characters' health, hunger and mood levels until the declaration of a ceasefire, which occurs after a randomized duration.  During the daytime, hostile snipers will prevent the survivors from venturing outside, thereby offering the player time to craft tools from gathered materials, trade, upgrade the shelter, cook food and heal the survivors. At night, you are given the opportunity to venture out and scavenge nearby areas for valuable resources for their everyday survival. During the excursions, the survivors can encounter other NPC survivors of the war, who they can choose to help with gifts, or rob and kill.  The scavenging phase plays out as a tense, atmospheric 2D stealth game. While some abandoned locations are looters' playgrounds, the war-torn city features a lot of other unsavory types. Soldiers and militia might be willing to trade with you or simply ignore you, but they can turn on you in a heartbeat. Other survivors might shoot you on sight. Rummaging through a pile of rubble sends sound waves far into the distance, alerting people to your presence. Get spotted in a place where you shouldn't be, and you'll have a choice: run, hide, fight, or die.

app_name: Pier Solar and the Great Architects
review_text: Always wished there were more RPGs on the Genesis. I'm glad the guys at WaterMelon shared that opinion so strongly that they made their own. While the game suffered through development hell (the original Genesis version from my understanding... as well as this HD version which I feel like I waited a year after the scheduled release date to check out), it finally came. It managed to captivate me in the way RPGs did in my youth. I honestly didn't think I was going to enjoy it that much.  A few notes: - Battles are reminiscent of Lunar both in the way of style and challenge. Areas can seem gimmicky and lack variety in fights up until a boss or change of region, however. - Sound is wonderful and fitting of the game's environments and mood. Perfectly done in Genesis style. The HD music is even better. - The options to swap between original graphics and HD, as well as original and HD sound are nice.  - Without listing all the reasons, I feel the game has some replay value (I can see 1 or 2 more playthoughs in the future for mehself). Obviously I'm not looking at it with nostalgia goggles either. - The characters are likeable, which is good due to the fact you'll be spending quite a few hours with them.  Overall, I'd give it an 8 out of 10. There's not too many great RPGs on the Genesis, so this game is welcomed by me. I'd even go as far as to place it at my number three spot of favorite Genesis RPGs, falling short of only Phantasy Star IV and Shining Force II. If you're a fan of those titles and also Lunar, then definitely try to pick it up if ya see it on sale, and give it a shot.  Happy Gaming, friends!!

app_name: METAL GEAR SOLID V: THE PHANTOM PAIN
review_text: Note: I first played this on PS4 when it released, then purchased it again on Steam when it went on sale, so you can add ~60 hours to my playtime here.  It goes without saying that MGSV has its problems. It's unfinished to a point of embarassment. Its missions boil down to variations of 'destroy this', 'capture that', 'kill/extract this person'. Boss fights are nearly absent, with the ones that are there being completely uninteresting in both narrative and gameplay. Also, while I usually don't get too overbearing with my opinions on the portrayal of women in video games (though completely acknowledge that work is needed there), Quiet is honestly something the writers/developers should be ashamed about. The justifications for her being the way she is in this game are just silly. It would have been less weird if they had simply said 'we wanted to sell more copies'. Part of the reason it fails so hard is because this is following MGS3's (my favorite of the series) The Boss, which is quite possibly one of the best portrayals of a female character in a game. How do you manage to fall so hard after that?  Simply put, this just isn't a Metal Gear.  However...  That being said, in terms of pure mechanics, this game is on another level compared to other stealth games. There are so many systems in play, each of which have direct impact on everything you are doing at any time. From simply sitting in the ACC to infiltrating enemy territory, nearly every second of the game past the title screen has significant impact on the overal system. Given how much of a MG fanboy I am (and believe me, I am), the fact that I can still recommend this game despite all of the criticisms I gave before should speak to how strong and interesting the core gameplay is, even if it is just doing the same thing over and over. I just doesn't get old.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt is amazing. Worldbuilding at its finest, this installment throws you directly into the thick of it, battling monsters, man, and the forces of the world themselves in order to find and protect those you love. Working behind the scenes, Geralt of Rivia's hand touches almost every matter in the world. You will argue with kings, consult with emperors, battle gods, and slay hordes of relentless demons. Yours is the hand that controls Geralt, and thus the entirety of the world and the fate of mankind. Rivavling the works of Tolkien, Jordan, and Sanderson, the Witcher 3 provides dozens of hours of content in an immersive and massive world, filled with intrigue, lore, and battle. Npcs feel alive and bustling, music is tasteful and nice, combat is challenging and entertaining, boss battles are engaging and tough. I've put in over 40 hours into the mainstory alone and have just now completed it, and I've hardly touched the surface of the world. I wholeheartedly recommend this game to anyone who has enjoyed similar titles like the Elder Scrolls and Dragon Age, and are looking for a game that will blow you away. 10/10.

Get involved and never look back!

I love a good first-person puzzle solving game. I grew up with games like Myst (and its sequels), the Journeyman Project series, etc. I hadn't had a good first-person puzzle experience in a while and I stumbled upon Only If.   I was thrilled! It seemed similar in tone to the Stanley Parable, but involving more interaction and puzzle solving.   Sadly, my excitement was soon crushed beneath the heal of this awful awful AWFUL game.   First off, the game itself is incredibly buggy. In the Parlor where you pick a pawn (thus determining the course of the game you play), it initially didn't matter which one I chose as it always gave me the black pawn route. I thought this was a design choice until I later found out it wasn't and that the damned game was broken. Later, after using the code to get to the white pawn path, essential graphical clues don't actually appear where their supposed to. At least, they don't all the time. Oh, they sometimes will when they feel like it, but don't expect the damned things to do it every time. Lastly, in the black pawn route, I was able to walk right off the map and fall into the oblivion below. I couldn't even restart the game! I had to close the window manually to even try again!  So, this game is buggy and broken beyond actual enjoyment.   But that's not all!  When the game DID work, it didn't make it any more enjoyable. No, the 'puzzles' they want you to solve are sometimes impossible to figure out without a walkthrough. OR an objective has such abstract or imprecise conditions that you could be at it for an hour and STILL not get to the next step. 'Start here and run for 6 seconds. Turn Right'?? What determines what is six seconds?! Me?? You?? Do we go with the standard '1 Mississippi 2 Mississippi' or a quick '1 2 3 4 5 6'??  This game had a lot of clever ideas and interesting mechanics (the jumping bit with the pots was pretty fun), but overall it's a broken, nonsensical, pointless little game.

app_name: STAR WARS™ Jedi Knight: Dark Forces II
review_text: I think this may be one of the most poorly optimised retro releases to ever be pushed onto the steam storefront. Let me get this out of the way by saying this is solely a judgement of the Steam release, there is a good chance that the GOG release actually works.  When I initially bought this, the game had no soundtack, constant graphical glitches (Turning the entire screen into something the Lucky Charms Leprechaun would throw up) and extremely stiff and jerky controls. And by Stiff and Jerky I mean that actual aiming was nigh impossible and I had to resort to playing with the arrow keys to aim. It wasn't fun.  However today, I learned that the steam release recieved a patch mid-this year that fixed a lot of the issues that plagued the game upon release. But...  It's still broken. The controls are still floaty and the game constantly changes between screen resolutions when you press pause. Only this time there are no windowed cutscenes. The Menus are filled with options that are convoluted and even when attempting to modify in order to make the game run better, don't help.   The base controls themselves are bizarrely mapped, there is an underlying disconnect when having to cycle through two mimi-menus containing Force Powers and Items, each with different buttons, and weapons (which you cannot cycle through with the mouse wheel) are awkward to switch between, so I guess you can add a third in-game menu to that as well.  Even though using a lightsaber in combat is persuaded, It is not fun to use in a first person perspective and the aforementioned botched controls make it tiring to use and player death is constant when using it.  This all come to a conclusion when I entered a Dual Boss fight with two Dark Jedi; one a Damage Sponge Boss (a trope I despise in games) who has an unavoidable choke attack, the other a little gremlin guy who could turn jump around everywhere and turn invisible of all things. To quote a certain lovable rogue 'That's not how the force works!'  After emptying all my rockets and thermal detonators into them, they were somehow still standing and I was forced to jump and slash at them with a lightaber. 'This is not a good boss fight', I thought as I broke my legs from falling down a suspened platform, again.   I quicksaved a lot until finally winning the fight. Nothing happened so I assumed that a door had opened somewhere, the broken and floaty controls made me fall down the aforementioned suspened platform and die. After defeating the boss again. I quicksaved, only for the game to go into a cutscene an promptly crash. I will not be playing this game again.  What else is there to say? The Soundtack is typical Star Wars, orchestral and epic, the graphics are from 1997, no point in judging them. Everything else about this release is a disaster though. Please do not buy the Steam release of Dark Forces II. If you really want a good Star Wars game, try the original Dark Forces, at least that game works.

I have been a fan of the Total War games since Shogun was pixellated green samurai chasing each other around. I've probably played and loved these games more than almost any other computer game I've ever played. Up until Rome came out, my faith in this company was unshaken. However, the complete disappointment of Rome and the ongoing struggle of keeping Attila playable are definitely moving me away.  Guys, rule number one is don't break stuff when you release the new stuff. Isn't that software design 101? Yet time after time, we have to deal with problems caused by patches and new releases that aren't even addressed openly by the game publisher, let alone dealt with in a prompt manner for the customer. Creative, there is one thing you can't afford to have, and that's contempt for your customers.  The DLC money grab is just the name of the game now, but Total War's examples of this are pretty egregious. And although this is a personal preference rather than a criticism of the games themselves....Warhammer? Are you kidding me? From the company who's been bringing out the all-around best long-term historical simulations for so long? I can only imagine the DLC for that one...Elf tanks and Orc blimps!   Oh, how the mighty have fallen. Guys, you need quality control and you need it fast. You need to do a smidgen of market research that could suggest you may be alienating the historical simulation core customer with your decision to throw your effort (such as it is) into Dwarf cannons and such nonsense.  I'm going to think very carefully about purchasing any of these games in the future. It's like your favorite Chinese restaurant got put under new managment and things have begun to suck. Sad.

I have multiple problems with this game. The framerate is low (locked at 30fps, and I know I can get 60+ on ANY other game), the controls are very clunky on both keyboard and controller, and the game is unnecessarily difficult due to a combo of bad controls and steep difficulty curve. These all contribute to a poor experience.  Customization options were welcome, but a convoluted crafting system makes obtaining cosmetics a chore. Combining two items could give you one of the same items you used to craft, with no added perk. There were only 3-4 people online for both of my 20-30 minute sessions, so not many to show off the few cosmetics I had.  The English translation is choppy, and the story baffles me. It seems to be a lighthearted kids' game, but in the first level, Satan makes an appearance AND themes such as death, evil in its purest form, and the apocalypse are brought up. IN THE FIRST LEVEL! But I don't think this game is meant to be played for the story, anyway. It is definitely gameplay-centric. So no big deal. But overall, the game is simply not worth its current price.   EDIT: Whoa, the dev responded to my review minutes after me posting. And with that, I realized my original review was a bit too harsh, so I edited it accordingly. It was kinda unfair to judge a game immediately upon its release. I mean, even big companies like Bethesda launch patches directly following release. I figured this was a lazy port of a Japanese game, but now my gut tells me I'm wrong. The developer cares about their game, stating a desire to continually improve the game. And, maybe when the game is a bit more polished, I'll come back, as the concept is promising. Until then.

Despite having unlocked most of the major dialogue, the game wouldn't let me progress because I hadn't found a football.  There is also no save function or autosave, so be warned that quitting will reset the walking simulator (or 'game', if we're being generous) leaving you to plod to all prior locations.  Tl;dr: Painfully drawn out and seemingly devoid of content. Avoid.

Well, if you want to play the unique RPG maker games especially in survival horror theme, this game is for you.

I really want to like this game. It's such a fantastic idea and on the border of being great but it has just too many problems to recommend. If you're a low level killer, you'll be paired up with undoubtedly several high level survivors and chances are they will win with little to no effort. If you're a survivor, it'll take forever to find a match (if you even do) and then it's hit or miss on how well the game will perform which depends on the host's internet and the host seems to be the killer. Pretty massively-unbalanced gameplay, latency issues due to the lack of dedicated servers, clunky mechanics (which I can overlook on their own but in combination with latency problems they become pretty unbearable), poor optimization, and the few exploits in the game such as window spams make this game not worth my time. Maybe it'll be patched in the future and polished out to be fantastic but it just didn't there yet. Save your money for now and wait for improvement or another asymmetrical serial killer game.

5.4 hours playtime is reflective of the fact that Steam started tracking that stat after this game was released, not at time of release.  As I'm sure most folks know, this game needs a sequel, or at least a finale. Some semblance of care from the developers for the audience who has shown this series love for so long. For the uninitiated, this is what you're getting into:  HL2:EP2 was (and arguably still is) one of the most immersive FPS campaigns ever released. Excellent voice acting, excellent world design, gameplay mechanics and plot. However, if closure is something you desire, steer clear. In the ~10 years since release of this game (which I purchased in The Orange Box upon its release), Valve has gone from 'we're doing episodic releases!' to 'we're making hats and selling games!'. Speculation regarding an ending release has gone from hopeful to dismissive.   As of now, the fate of this world is left uncertain and unlikely ever to be revealed. Unless Valve was shooting for an extremely subtle metaphor for the real world, then this is a cliffhanger whose principals will be forever dangling.  10/10 For everything but the end.

Probably my favorite RPG game I have ever played. I have heard a lot of people bash it for basically being like an mmo but not because of game play and such. However, I am not a huge fan of MMO's so I could really care less. I have played through it about 3 or 4 times and it has not gotten old to me yet. Every time I play through the game I find a ton of extra options and choices that I can make that changes the outcome of the game significantly.  The combat options can seem overwhelming at first until you mess with them for a little bit. Once you get the hang of it you can pretty much control every move of your party by setting up parameters for all of their attacks and most every situation that you can get in to in the game.  I enjoyed all the dialog on my first play through, but to be honest on the next couple of times through the game, they did seem a bit cumbersome at times. Despite that fact, this is still definitely one of my favorite games.   The story line is awesome and the leveling system is not bad. It could be better, but once you figure out what type of companions you want to have playing with you in the game, it makes it far easier to develop your character.

The Bureau was an good but not great game. The gameplay was fun and not particularly repetitive, the story was fairly well written, and the graphics were good but not phenomenal. It never excelled, but it wasn't ever bad, either.  I think the best part about this game, for me, was the aesthetic and setting of the game. Few games explore the 1960's and the 'original' alien paranoia that came with them. However, the decision to put this in the X-COM family is a bit of a stretch: you DO command your squad, but you do so while in total control of Agent Carter. The gameplay is very similar to Mass Effect 2/3's, which isn't bad, but it doesn't lend itself to the expected X-COM gameplay.  Additionally, it is rather unclear how The Bureau fits into the X-COM universe as a whole. The game is set in '62, yet X-COM: Enemy Unkown claims that they were the first to encounter aliens on Earth. Additionally, while many of the species of aliens are the same between Enemy Unknown and The Bureau, the Etherials are quite different. I'm unsure if the games are set in two different universes or not, and the ending does little to answer that for me.  On the topic of the ending, without getting into too many spoilers, it doesn't make a whole lot of sense. The game throws out information that the player knows in favor of what the other characters can see, which leads to a lot of dissonance between what story the player interprets and what the game gives them.   All in all, I would recommend The Bureau, and I find it unlikely that most people wouldn't enjoy it. However, I do think the developer could've spent more time fleshing out the story, and that it would not have been unwise to leave the X-COM references out (or call it a Universe-B X-COM).

I recommend this game for many reasons, simplicity, ease of play, creativity, KRAKENS!!!!!!!!   But, seriously. This game is in its first release stages, no one should judge it against Civilization 5's recent updates and DLC's because it simply is not there yet, but in my mind, this ticks my box as being a sci-fi lover. Since playing Civilization 5, I had hoped future technology would have given me something like in this game, or XCOM units were like these, but in the end that would have been highly unrealistic even at the time period.  To sum up my likes and dislikes.  Likes:  (1) This game has multiple choices for civilizations, and allows you to choose what they were allowed to be, scientists, civilians, etcetera.  (2) The units are very versatile in this game, allowing for purity, harmony and the other one (Poor memory kicks in on the killer robot, golden metal looking dudes who like invading earth.) None the less, it does allow you to choose your own story when you are making a new civilization or race if you go harmony or cyborg killer robot yellow people.  (3) Satellites are the biggest improvement; sure, you have ICBMS in Civilization 4/5, but laser firing satellites? This is where I hope someone makes a mod for the Hyperion moon base.  Dislikes/Improvements: (1) I have had a few issues with bugs, crashes and such, nothing what can't be fixed with a patch, no biggy. (2) Techs take too little time to research, maybe instead of a branch like that; have the ordinary tree back, with a row allocated to each type of technology? (3) DLC needs to include more actual civilizations, or maybe sentient aliens, better character models, and more units, a lot more, maybe another victory type and unit branch like annihilation (4) I would enjoy seeing an improvement to the aliens, they are far too weak for my liking, and they should be something you do not want near you just in case, maybe also a random event such as say... When mining, because of the harmony victory needing a link to the planet (Avatar style) the aliens go into a frenzied state because of the damaged eco-system. (Unless this already happens, if it does ignore me, I just kill them without care. lol) (5) Extreme need for a customizable system to make your own race and such, perhaps a pre-loaded set of icons and a choice of character model, your own name, coloured units.  (6) Better or more profitable uses for energy, I didn't use energy because I was not tempted nor nearly wanting to use it, me being me, I am a bit of a tight ♥♥♥ when it comes to buying or paying out for stuff. I believe in reserve counter measures and the teachings of Sun Tzu. (Does not build builders, just steals them.)  To be honest, people who whine about the game being unbalanced just are not using the advantages of the current time to their advantage, the key to victory is to seek the enemies’ weakness, their disadvantages, be it unfair, but that is how war is won these days, be it political or physical. Civillization beyond Earth is a step into unknown territory in sense to the others.   Comparing Civ BY to Civ 5 is like comparing a brand new house to a house that has been lived in for ages; the new one is going to need improvements and such to make it look great. Whereas the old house is, going to be a house that usually already looks great and furnished because it has had time put into it over time. It is simply unfair.  I know, this sounds stupid and silly, but I have a point.   I will put it in simple terms.   Buy this game, respect that it will not be as good as all of the others.  If you don't want an unfinished, or better put, not quite fully outfitted, don't buy it, simple as, no need for people whining about the game being unbalanced, it's only been out for a couple of weeks, if you bought it thinking it would be balanced and perfect then that's your own fault. :P  So to simply put it, this game nearly ticks all my boxes. 7/10!

9.5/10 Bought this during the 80% off sale, I Have not regretted that purchase, and would pay more for it. The game offers wonderful graphics, but also will run on any reasonably modern system. Amazing action, a main mechanic is blowing things up. What nore does the action lover in one need?  The dual set of parachute and grappel wire works well, offering versatility and mobility, even without a jet, car, or boat. Its a key mechanic, and it works flawlessly. The parachute can take some getting used to, but once you have it, which you will get the hang of after just a few minutes, you'll be soaring across the island. With those two in hand, the island becomes your playground.  This game is an action movie you can play, bail out, and watch the explosion and carnage, Mow down enemies with dual sub-machine guns, take a quite approach with a sniper rifle, or just steal the enemie's helicopter with chain guns and use that. Getting chased? Use your grappel hook to swing the enemies into the road. Many options for constant action and sheer awesomness. Tired of that? The one shortcoming of this game; the story is more of the same with a few characters.  The map is massive, with Mountains, a city, a jungle, a desert, a lake/ocean, plenty of variety. And with each area, the main character's apperance changes, he gets wet after swimming, dusty in the desert, snow covered in the mountains, and looks normal while in the city. In each area, there are towns, military bases, wilderness, roads, and such to explore, even after you complete the storyline, you will have no shortage of areas to play in.  Anyone who likes blowing stuff up, or just anyone really, I strongly recommend this game to.

Saints Row IV. If you want a cheesey over-the-top game that involves super-powers and unrealistic guns and gameplay then this is for you. Below I'll give my input on whether this game is worth it, or not.  The Pros: You can literally become a superhero, you can jump up buildings, run faster than any car, set things on fire or turn them to ice, telekenetically throw tanks and helicopters around and simply cause absolute chaos in the city.  With a nice arsenal of guns you can open black holes, inflate people, disintegrate every car and person you see, lift people in the air with the abduction gun or simply run around with a rocket launcher blowing everything up for no reason.  The Cons: It takes time to level up your superpowers and with the amount of collectibles available it can get a little frustrating trying to find 'that last one' that keeps evading you. Along with that the marauding 'wardens' can frustrate you even more.  Although the arsenal is nice there could have been a bit more of imagination in the way of what the guns do, in my opinion they concentrated more on how the guns look (Through aesthetic customization) and not on how many guns there are or what they can cause.  Overall Rating: A good game to hop into for an hour or so just to blow things up, but not a long-term game.  I'd give it a 6/10, a decent purchase but don't expect much out of it and in my opinion not worth it without the expansive DLC.

review_text: Rome 2 is in my opinion a good game overall. I know there is a LOT of hate coming from the community about this game. There are still a few graphical bugs, however, my computer rarely crashes. The AI is not AS dumb as people say it is, as long as you're playing on a higher difficulty. However, no matter what the reviews say, this game is not NEARLY as bad as Empire total war on launch. Most of the problems, including the 5 minute turn time, have been fixed through the so far seven patches. The battles are extremely addictive and so actually require some tactics to win, even in the middle of the heat of the battle. Overall, the game NOW is quite fun and rather well made. I must disagree with most of the other reviewers. If I were to rate it on a scale, it would probably be a 7.5/8 out of 10.

I am... Genuinely impressed.  Normally point-and-click adventure games tend to either be bland, or only hold their draw for a short time and a single playthrough.  This one is incredibly deep.   What starts as an almost stereotypical childish adventure slowly gains layers of complexity, without losing any of the charm that a childish adventure can have.  The gameplay, however, possesses all the flaws of any Point-and-click game. Though this one does indeed try some interesting changes to the formula. Virtually every point-and-click adventure game that requires you to combine collected items to solve puzzles becomes an infuriating quagmire; unless you happen to find the exact combination, in the exact order, that the Developers designed, you cannot progress--regardless of whether or not your idea could work. Add to that the fact that you almost need to be able to read the Developers minds in order to solve many of them, and the game quickly becomes a matter of alternating between pulling teeth and finding a needle in a haystack.  Still, the plot alone can carry you through the whole game. There are a surprising amount of subtle and charming jokes, which are delivered quite well (If the main character cannot manage to find his success, he can always fall back on becoming a highly-skilled plumber with a charming Italian accent. And we all know how successful those can be).  You have to use a walkthrough to be able to really enjoy the game; which is the strongest mark against it. But this game is deffintely worth it's cost.  The ending in particular is incredibly satisfying, and leaves just enough open ends to tease any number of sequels. I greatly hope to see some, some day.

Purchased game at gamestop at the first available oppurtunity in April of 2014. Gamestop was the only place you could get it at the time besides directly from their website. I excited anticipated the game for over 6 months, finding out as it got closer to release that they were releasing cool weapon drop tickets for each week that you pre-ordered the game. Well as I said before I had pre-ordered the game and its season pass back in April of 2014, so I was like,'No way, I'm going to have a ton of awesome gear then since I've got roughly 8 months worth of these bonus items. Oh, and an exclusive game mode for multiplayer since I took the risk of investing so early by pre-ordering. Okay cool, thanks Techland, now I'm super stoaked. A game studio that appreciates the risk of investment that people take buying games so early.  The reality, for me, couldn't have been further from the truth. For starters, I learned while playing co-op with a friend that the steam version of the game (which wasn't available for purchase till ~September of 2014) had exclusive content that my gamestop version couldn't access, items as simple as a low-tier police baton with spikes. Furthermore I only had 5 of those special tickets for exclusive roll-of-the-stupid-dice weapons. I dug a little deeper and found that that very 'promo' was only from the beginning of the month of december, forward. At this point I already felt like I had treated a little ♥♥♥♥♥♥ but I kept my head up, cause hey I still got the exclusive game mode for pre-ordering right? WRONG:> within a month of release that status was revoked and all players could do it. Now before you write all of this off as whining or ♥♥♥♥♥ing, keep in mind the game mode 'Be the Zombie' was originally intended as a rare event where a player could enter another player's game at night. It was never originally intended to be a common sighting. On top of this connections at release were very shotty and broken as well.   So, onto the actual game itself and the dlcs. All in all, a waste of space. Dishonored + State of Decay = Dying Light. Bugs were numerous for months, zombies walking through walls, players getting stuck in the ceilings of interior rooms, and so on. On top of that anyone you co-op with isn't another individual in the game or another random survivor. No, they're you, literally you go around the map like freaking clones from Kamino. The only 'customizations' there are, is choosing what pair of pants your character wears/outfit and whatever weapons you find. The story started off quite well but then a major plot whole happened, and then it all just went downhill from there. I remember thinking,'okay this has to be almost over....right?' WRONG, '70% Completed' --it felt endless. And then you take a trip on acid basically, the major plot whole shows up again and then you get to the final part just to find out that 'Hey it's great in co-op! Co-op is where it's at!'---'THE FINAL SCENE IS SINGLEPLAYER ONLY!'---and then you go through all this cutscene push a key to take 1 step at a time. Literally like,'press q to jump to the left, press S to take a step right, and so on...'  Mechanics and core gameplay wise, its fine...standard these days. DLC...lacking--so boring I didnt even care to finish it. The game falls flat the longer you play it. Yes I know eventually everyone burns out, but its almost like an accelerated burn.  In the end, I wanted to like this game. I really did, but I wish I could get my $90.00 and 69 hours back. Its a money scam and anymore all they care about is making their next dollar. If you liked Kingdoms of Amalur and/or the Witcher, you will like this game. If you don't, it's probably because it IS challenging at first. Not Dark Souls level of challenging, but things are unforgiving. Those who complain about controls clearly just haven't figured out timing. Every mob gives you subtle clues that show you how to respond, and you can literally fight an entire boss fight not getting hit if you are smart, quick, and capable of reading the enemy's signals for one type of attack or another. Yes, I've died probably 80 times so far, but you can save whenever you want, and you can always do it better. Think of Kingdoms of Amalur, but tougher, or Witcher, but with FAR better controls, and you get this game (oh, and add the morality of Fable, to get that storyline feel).   The 'can't recommend' people honestly just couldn't hack the challenge, so keep this in mind when you pick it up.

As with many programs I've used, there are a few things which need to be fixed or that I think should be changed in Mosaico, but overall I find the software very useful and I've had a stable experience with it thus far. I saw people mention frequent crashes, but I guess either I'm just lucky here or it's been fixed by now in that regard, as I haven't had it crash yet. The rest of this review is mainly reports of minor bugs I've encountered and new feature suggestions for the developers.  I mainly am interested in the manual tiling of windows with Mosaico, and that works just fine for me. I added several different optional positions to the manual arrangement screen through the options to allow vertically dividing it into thirds. It's great that they can be added or removed for larger or smaller screens to minimize clutter.  I have a few suggestions about the manual tiling feature. I use a touch-screen monitor, and while it is possible to touch and drag windows easily enough to position them with Mosaico, I believe it could use some additional options for better taking advantage of multi-touch displays. I suggest the ability to tap outside of a window with a second finger while dragging the window to cancel Mosaico's tiling (which I prefer to leave enabled at all times). Reaching for the mouse and right-clicking seems pointless and isn't easy to do when touching the screen already, and hitting Q on the keyboard also requires another hand. I'd also like it if I could leave manual tiling disabled most of the time and drag with three fingers anywhere on a window to engage tiling (similar to Ubuntu's three-finger drag for window positioning). Besides these things, while this isn't important at all, I thought it would be good to allow the user to change the colors of manual tiling from the default RGB to custom ones, to make this feature seem more like an integrated/native part of the operating system. That's just a 'polishing' thing which could come later in Mosaico's development, though.  I briefly tried out the auto-arrange feature, and while it produced awkward results each time for me, I believe it could potentially be very useful if it sees some improvement. I also think there should be a keyboard shortcut for auto-arrange for when the user doesn't have Mosaico's main window open. When I used auto-arrange, it made one window I had on-screen not show up at all, another tall and narrow, and another small; this didn't cause the screen to be even close to completely filled by the windows. (The test windows were Opera 27.0, MediaMonkey 4.1.5, and Windows' File Explorer.)  Presently, it seems like Mosaico can't handle alternative taskbar positions very well when restoring saved layouts. I have mine on the left side of the screen, and Mosaico seems to ignore that and have my on-screen windows offset beneath the taskbar, leaving some empty space on the right side of the screen when I restore a layout.  Besides these things, Mosaico should also be able to handle arranging skinned windows, namely Steam's main one.  The main system on which I've used Mosaico uses a 27' touch monitor with a native resolution of 2560x1440 pixels and Windows 10 Tech Preview.

Gameplay in City of Steam is very reminiscent of most action RPGs on the market today. In fact, quite a favorable comparison could be made to Diablo 2 or Torchlight 2 from the standpoint that players work to level up skills to make them more powerful. On first glance, City of Steam's combat system is terrific and it certainly isn't bad by any stretch of the imagination. What is boils down to is that it is uninspiring in ways that the aforementioned games are not. As players level, they are granted skill power-ups in one of three disciplines. Points are allotted according to a player's wishes and combat skills are granted. In the end, it's not that the combat system is poor, it's just so much more button mashing of 1-2-3, rinse repeat.   Players are faced with pretty standard fare and work from a belt of abilities learned over time that increase in damage and effectiveness. I played as a Warder and my character had the typical arcing swings of both a great sword and two one-handed swords. It's pretty cool that players can choose a second weapon set for different skills and ways to fight monsters. Weapons can be changed out on the fly and it's worth having that ranged weapon handy to aggro only the monsters you want to rush out.  Questing is pretty standard fare as well. Head to a quest hub, grab 3-4 quests, head to the instances needed for completion, turn them in and done. Again, it's not unusual, just uninspiring and not as well done as most. The quest givers are not voiced which, depending on your particular style, is either a good or bad thing.   Players can utilize an isometric over the shoulder camera or toggle a free ranging camera on the fly. This is a marvelous touch on behalf of Mechanist, something that most players will like very much. In addition, questing can be directed if a player gets lost simply by clicking on the directional arrow that will automatically take characters to the desired location.  Still in the end, combat gets repetitive very fast. Between the same look and feel of all the instances and mashing the same combination of buttons over and over, it's a rather tiresome affair.

Metro 2033 [Redux] is a game that has a great premise, context and introduction, from the moment you start the game you get excited to start an interesting adventure... or so it seems.  The game lacks depth and story, so it can easily be sumarized into only two objectives, the first seems really simple, but something happens that deviates you from your straightforward path, so you spend ~8 hours doing things that do not matter at all, and then when you finally reach your destination, you get the only interesting objective in the game that lasts ~1-2 hours, leading to the ending that hardly explains all the questions you have, so you finish the game almost at the exact point you started in your 'adventure'.  Besides story, the game packs good graphics, a lot of action combined with stealth and mostly linear levels.  I recommend skipping this game and playing Metro Last Light [Redux], which is a much better game overall which I recommend.

review_text: I usually dont play through single player games. However, this is one of those games that you really get drawn into. Its immense and diverse world is great to play through. Its not a perfect game, but it is a masterpieceeee.

This game is for you if have played turn-based games before and you have liked them. If you have never played a turn-based game before this game certainly not for you.   I started playing this game when I gifted a copy to friend. Playing with another person against the A.I and each other is enjoyable it gets you away from the very predictable A.I. playing solo.  Big thumbs up! on the artwork in this game. Every race or faction has their own individual feel to them, the attention to detail and the little nuances that each unit has is rarely seen in games period and this is a good one. The digital artists spent some time and the matte painting is superb.  The UI or user interface is fast, fluid and makes the game a joy to play. The UI has a modern feel to it and it really helps the player navigate through all the micomanagement that is needed in this game. In late game when you have a large empire you can easily manage your entire empire from just the dropdown city screen, fantastic.   The animation in the combat is also very good. The characters, heroes, and the units each have their own way to surprize you. The sound fx are very good and well timed.   The weapon assets are fantastic. I enjoy the difference between the Iron, Titanium, Adamantium and Palladium metal weapons. Each tier of metals weapons really change the way the units and heroes look and their animation in the combat system.  The combat system is actually also very good. There is a learning curve but once you understand that you do have full control over all of your units, you will have fun with this game.  The FIDSI or food, industry, science, influence is the heart of this game. This game is about diplomacy and empire management. Your descisions are critical on the outcome of your empire. Store some food for winter or you will starve.   The quests are plentiful and easy enough to accomplish. The grammer in the writing for the quests and the details about them need a little work, but they are other wise fun and they keep you exploring.   What I like best is the low-poly artwork for the units and for the map itself. The low-poly keeps the game low file size and fast moving. You have an option in the video options to change the quality to  'fantastic' Whoa I just figured this out today after 200 hours in game, man now I have to play more map looks great!  Overall this game is well balanced with managment and combat and one of the better turn-based games out for PC. I will continue to play this game in the future.   P.S get this game and the DLC's on one of Steam's holiday sales, well worth the money.   Later

This game has promise, but there's a lot problems I have with it. The first problem I have with this game is the way it rewards you. The truth is that the game doesn't really reward players for going through the dungeons. Each dungeon you enter starts your character completely fresh.After you've defeated a creature in the dungeon you gain equiptment. After the dungeon is completed all that loot you've gained from that dungeon is taken away. Eventually you're back at your guild with nothing really to show for it. Sure you're able to upgrade your guild, but really all upgrading does is add more characters for you to play as. The difference between characters is fairly small. They all have cute different card titles. but a lot of the abilities on the card are the same as other classes. The classes in this game, don't really feel like they're getting stronger as you get further in the game. Improving your guild is pretty pointless and all the other characters don't seem to improve the game much. This game suffers from an overly simplistic card game mechanic and that's really where the game threw me off from truly enjoying my experience. Building your own dungeon was something that I thought was the most unique part of this game. The only issue with making your own dungeon, is that the movement in this game happens to be very on rails. You're not allowed to choose the part of the duneon you want go through. It tells you ahead of time where you'll be walking, but it's by force the way it puts you through a dunegon. I wish I had more control in where I moved in the dungeons and that my room placements weren't just to waste my turn. The game still has promise, but it needs more rewarding factors as well as more different card variety. It's still worth a look at, but at a much lower price.

A one-of-a-kind gaming experience in a magnificent 2D pixel art style that make me wonder why the hell they didn't do something like that when I was a kid. Anybody can play since there is literally need for only 4 buttons (go left, go right, go faster and action) to remember.  Don't expect to fight though: you are the king and you're on a horse. Your job is to hire people to be your soldiers, handymen, farmers, etc. Then, they will do the job for you. Soldiers will hunt for food around your kingdom (bunnies, deers, etc) and bring you money for their kills while protecting you at night, the handymen can build whatever you want, but they can also chop down trees and they will bring you money for each tree. Farmers will obviously do just that: farming, and every harvest will bring you money too.  At night, the monsters come out and every night will be harder to beat, so dont be cheap on the walls and remember that spreading your kingdom might be good for the money, but it's harder to defend.  It's a must play for anyone having a retro-sick day and needing some simple side scroller action. It's very strategic and it's a visual pleasure as every game will be slightly different since everything is procedural.

I really do feel like I'll come off as a broken record if I add my voice to the ten-thousand-something reviews here already, so I'll try to keep it short and sweet.  The storyline, if you ask me and plenty of other people, is well done. The characters are very memorable, have some interesting and  (At least somewhat)  unique personalities. Undertale was the first time in, well, ever that I found myself delighted when I saw Papyrus and Comic Sans in use. It was interesting to see how simple text, with barely any voices at all, can convey so much emotion. There were times that the game had me laughing quite a bit. And admitingly, on the other hand, Undertale was the first time that a videogame had me close to tears with emotion; but to be honest, I don't think that's a bad thing – it just shows how powerful of a game it can be.  The soundtrack worked perfectly with the game, as well. Helped to make the game much better, in my opinion. It's understandable if pixel art isn't your thing, but Undertale seems to pull it off well. It really does feel like some honest effort was put into making it look great.  Throughout playing the game, I have been desperately searching for flaws that the game may have. The puzzels are a bit of a challenge on the first try, but not too hard. They'll have you confused for maybe a minute, but they're by all means do-able. Whether this is a good thing or a bad thing is debatable. There's also the community. It shines in some spots, but in others, it's ... questionable. Personally though, I don't tend to keep a community in mind for rating a game; most of the time, how the community/fandom acts is not the direct result of creator intervention.  Games like Undertale are hard to come by.  REALLY  hard. It's rare that you find games such as this that shine, stand out from the rest, and are nearly perfect. The only flaws I find in Undertale are ones that, if you ask me, have little to no effect in the end.  Undertale is a full on 10/10. It's only like, ten bucks man. You're only doing yourself a favor by buying it.

Lost Horizon was a very enjoyable experience. This game has it all, nazi's, evil scientists, Tibetan monks, henchmen with weapons, and beautiful women. The main protagonist is Fenton Paddock, a down and out pilot who finds himself trapped in the wrong place at the wrong time. He is quick witted and often hilarious. One of the main drawbacks of my recent adventure game purchases has been the quality of the voice acting has been very low. This was not the case with Lost Horizon. The accents of the different characters are convincing and the delivery is very smooth. Nothing can kill my immersion faster than a character speaking who sounds like an actor reading a line. As far as the puzzles go, I did not come across any that required a guide, as they all seemed very rational. Overall, if you enjoy point & click adventures with puzzles that aren't illogical, voice acting that is top notch, and en exciting story, you need to pick this up!

TL;DR: If you like Space Rangers 2, or EV Nova (minus the story bits) Then you owe it to your inner space trader/pirate/bounty hunter to pick this gem up.  This game is quite fun. I don't have nearly enough play time, but I'm seriously liking what I have played. I'm an experienced spacer with these kinds of games. I started with Freelancer, and moved on to titles like Elite, FFE, Privateer, Oolite, Asci Sector, Escape Velocity Nova, Transcendence, Rebel Galaxy, Starpoint Gemini 2, Elite Dangerous, Hardwar, Star Sector, X3 Albion Prelude, etc. etc.  What I mean to say, is that sandbox space trading games are my kryptonite.  So what sets this game apart? Its lacking a in your face kind of story, it seems fairly simplistic, and the graphics look like a flash game. What sets it apart is playability, and how it organically opens up around you. When you first launch from the planet you might notice the UI, which is clean and easy to use. (Slightly less so if like me, you are colorblind...) Next you will notice the super easy controls. WASD, and your mouse do 99% of things, with J for jumping. Each sector is about the size of an EV Nova sector, but once you hit the borders, you hit a 'wall' useful for finding things. The map is excellent, and as you join the merchants guild, it will give you alot more information for trading.  Combat seems difficult due to the bounty system and the way combat itself works. To be a bounty hunter you need to 'kill-steal' or get the last hit on a ship for the bounty reward. Capturing lifepods isn't nearly as lucrative. This makes for a rather tense game of knowing when to shoot, or when to manually use weapons to get the bonus. Enemy AI is fairly good, and fleet battles are downright terrifying to fly through. Again, the flight controls are quite intuitive, and you feel like an ace pilot after a few minutes of flying. Lots of weapons and skills to upgrade to create your ideal ship. Ships come in an abundance of types, from corvettes to cruisers and so on. You can also recruit a squad of wingmen and order them around.  Trading depends on a dynamic economy with events. You need to move around a bit, taking advantage of the same events that draw pirates and competing traders alike. Once you join the merchants guilds, you can see real time prices on goods throughout the galaxy. It makes for a fun trading aspect, with you finding lucrative routes, and abandoning them for an event only to fight pirates the whole way. I never got bored, it evokes memories of Ev Nova and Space Rangers 2.  Missions are kind of rare, with them being offered by NPC's in the galaxy, No BBS board or anything like that. The end-game seems to consist of you helping one of the factions rule the galaxy, but I'm not anywhere near there yet.  Overall the game combines playability with hidden depth, and pulls it off wonderfully. If you can look past the graphics and need a nice, relaxing, after work game with some complexity and a living galaxy, I highly reccomend this title. Where Void Expanse fails, this game succeeds with flying colors, it helps that the Dev is already planning the next update, and adding several features as players suggest them.  Fantastic game. Like Endless Sky? Get this. Wish you had a real-time SR2 minus the story bits? Get this. Wanna fly a spaceship and do all the fun things gameplay wise that Elite Dangerous is ignoring? GET THIS.

WANNA LEARN HOW TO MAKE A PIE? NO WELL TOO BAD   Ingredients: 1/2 cup sugar 1/2 cup packed brown sugar 3 tablespoons all-purpose flour 1 teaspoon ground cinnamon 1/4 teaspoon ground ginger 1/4 teaspoon ground nutmeg 6 to 7 cups thinly sliced peeled tart apples 1 tablespoon lemon juice Pastry for double-crust pie (9 inches) 1 tablespoon butter 1 large egg white Additional sugar  Directions: In a small bowl, combine the sugars, flour and spices; set aside. In a large bowl, toss apples with lemon juice. Add sugar mixture; toss to coat.  Line a 9-in. pie plate with bottom crust; trim pastry even with edge. Fill with apple mixture; dot with butter. Roll out remaining pastry to fit top of pie. Place over filling. Trim, seal and flute edges. Cut slits in pastry.  Beat egg white until foamy; brush over pastry.

When I heard about this game from a friend, I was very relunctant to purchase this strategy game. At the time, I did not really care much for RTS games and this game looked like one in my opinion from the pictures but anyhow I purchased this game just to give it a try. When I first started the game, I had to turn down the main menu theme to save myself from being deaf in both of my ears. I went to play the game and noticed that the game had historical starting dates from Duke William to the end of the Mongal Horde which in my opinion was pretty cool. Not only that i could select a date, I could also play as any vassal and/or ruler that I pleased as long as they were a Christian of some sort, it was dissapointing that I couldn't play as the Pagan or even Muslim rulers but I decided to look past it... for now. Then alas, the U.I. The user interface was bare, there was no way of knowing what to do and the tutorials didn't not provide any help. In fact, I had to have my friend (Who didn't know anything either) teach me the basics of the game and its goals. Weeks went by and I quickly lost any interest in playing the game because of the difficulty and lack of knowing how to actually play. One fortunate day though I decided to give the game one last try and experiment on new ways to play and different ways to look at the game. I actually started to have fun playing the game, losing and retrying things like instead of being hostile towards a smaller power (that had powerful allies) i could be more diplomatic towards them and become allies. Crusader Kings II is a great game once you know how to play it and its unfortunately a game where you have to teach yourself to become better. That is why I'm giving this game 7/10, but players will get their money's worth especially with the replay value.  Message me if you would want a review on Crusader Kings II with the DLC expansions included.

I've been a part of Infestation: Survivor Stories almost near launch and have been through so much. It has gotten better and my review is going to be on what I feel about it now.  Basically, the game is alright. It's fun to play and it has a great concept but as usual, the dev's can't keep up with the hype. The game has been infested with Brazillians which I find extremely annoying considering there is no region-lock and the majority of players on U.S. SERVERS are Brazillians. Next, cheating was a major problem and seems like it still is. This game is bound for cheaters and with broken punkbuster + crappy FairFight, it seems like this problem will never end. These are 2 major reasons I am not into this game anymore.  As for the game itself, the graphics are alright but come with horrible optimzation. You need a high-end PC to run the game smooth with 50-60fps on high let alone ultra. Grass looks terrible as well as a lot of scenery which is 2D. Animations are pretty bad and are still buggy. Guns look great though and perform how they should although all items have a durability which is annoying considering how fast things break. I will never complain about hitreg or spread or bullet drop cause this game could be considered a simulator with that stuff. Dev's go back and forth changing loot systems but right now they are perfect. They also change how zombies attack/register hits and right now they are good as well.  Dev team is alright but some of them (the ones that stream) really need to GTFO cause they are stubborn and treat their own community like crap ignoring most of the problems people want solved. Otherwise really slow progress on the game including map development and new concepts. I am not surprised though. If only this game was not led by SERGEY TITOV (just look at Big Rigs) who almost ran this game to the ground for the first 6 months of it's existence. Overall dev team is unprofessional considering all the flaws you see in the game graphically and physically.  To conclude TL;DR - Game is around a 5-6/10 for the experience and feel you get when hunting/getting hunted AND ONLY that. Otherwise graphics are pretty bad, optimzation is bad, dev's are slow and lazy, animations and functions in game are bad, guns are good, shooting is good, CHEATERS ARE THE MAJORITY, too many annoying Brazilians, and that's it. Only reccommend this game if you are REALLY into zombie stuff and can live past getting wrecked by mostly cheaters while having BRs scream over chat/voice.

I bought Game Dev Tycoon (GDT) pretty early on; before it ever landed on steam. I thought their creative way of approaching DRM was amusing, and I ended up buying it on a whim.  Note that I did give the current version a try before I wrote this, seeing as they changed quite a bit of things. While some of the additions are pretty cool, I was really hoping that the game had addressed some of its deeper problems.  At first I really enjoyed GDT, and had a lot of fun for the first couple of hours.  Unfortunately, as other reviews have pointed out, the gameplay is rather shallow and became less and less interesting as the game went on. It quickly became a game of doing the same things over and over again. Attempts to step outside of the box were punished, and the game completely fails at rewarding any form of optimization whatsoever; it's just a continual throw money to make money cycle. This is only made worse by the updates, which allows you to keep records of what works and doesn't work for game types. You don't even have to remember the cycle anymore.  Alao keep in mind that this game has been accused of ripping off of a smartphone game called game dev story. (I can't say for sure one way or another since I've never played it, but there is enough accusations flinging out for me to be concerned).  It's not a bad game for 2-3 bucks, since it will likely satisfy you for a hour or two. But it is completely not worth the set price (or even the current summer sale price).

Great, lots of fun to be had here. I enjoyed the base game very much with many hours spent searching for materials and food and have replayed Breakdown a couple of times which is a good DLC. The story is interesting, the worldspace is nice to explore and the mechanics are well balanced.   Getting into a zombie fight is tense and un-nerving but tending to the needs of your home base makes these scenarios almost unavoidable at some point in your struggle to survive. But with distractions available, traps and good old cars to mow Z's down, whether you take a stealthy approach or go in hard, it all comes together to make for a great game.  I'm not a huge fan of action zombie survival games but took a chance on this one and it has proven to be one of my favorite games in recent years. Not least because it ran on my godawful PC. But it's also just a solid game, some action, some simulation, some stealth, some driving...and zombies...nice mix :)  + Engaging story, interesting State (of decay) + Mix of stealth, action, home base building & survival blend well together + Sandbox works pretty nicely  - Can get repetitive after a while - Home base modifications somewhat limited (would've been nice to be able to build more than 1 type of guard tower)  8/10

Banished is a fun and interesting city builder once you get the hang of it. Despite the riveting negative commentary that nearly persuaded me to give this game a pass I am certain I'll be playing this for quite some time. Comments about the game lacking content are completely unfounded in my opinion (besides, pong and asteroids didn't need content to be fun.)  The overall command system is fairly easy to master, and the 'micro-management' referred to by many is actually just assigning jobs to citizens (some of the comments made it sound like they had almost no autonomy) and ensuring you have everything they need to survive and thrive. Yes, your townsfolk will starve if you neglect to plan ahead, but I was able to keep my food stores full on my first try just by anticipating the worst. Think sustainability, and don't grow too quickly, and you'll be in for a great gaming experience.

Even though mid-game is little bit sluggish (again, nothing that Paradox wouldn't fix either in patches or expected DLCs), the game is fun as hell and you'll definitely lose your sense in time in real life. I've been playing since it was released, and though many reviews claim the game is 'unfinished', the entire simulation of a 1,000 year galaxy is orchestrated beautifully. From AI rebellions decimating the galaxy, betrayals from your own ambitious allies, even discovering an ancient Fallen Empire superior to all the games empires combined, Stellaris will never cease to amaze you with twists at every turn. The developers pay very close attention to the community, and continously expand the game on our behalf. From the games inception to production, Stellaris has captured the awe inspiring depths of space exploration, rising to the stars, and embracing the final frontier unlike many other games claiming to do so. 10/10

Completly linear game, dull and naive narration.  After 20 hours in the game i felt i was running a squad of pupils and late teenagers. Very slow pace, you need to go through much uninteresting cutscene before playing. It is more an 'interacting movie' than a game, and a bad one.  Unfriendly ergonomy, it is seen it is a console port, with redundant actions in menus and so on. No autosave.  Zero tactical challenge.  Clunky tank control (the tank hit box makes it bump most world object, and makes you loosing a lot of AP). AI is not challenging at all. Also sometime the game reset the position of the squad when a cutscene is fired, to place them in predefined locations for the next part of the scenario! Or 'immortal' units that can die only after another cutscene.  I think my problem with this game is cultural and generational... This game is reviewed as one of the best tactical games from console. I'm glad i dont have a console... I'd better go back to more interesting and deep tactical games that only PC can offer.  The only good thing in Valkyria is the graphics and art.  Not recommended.

Bought it in a sale, I've completed it on Centurion and played half way through the Legendary difficulty. The game has positive elements, the facial animation and use of facial animation during combat is especially impressive. The campaign is reasonably enjoyable albeit a hackneyed mash up of Rome / Gladiator and every other modern day TV representation of the Roman Era. The voice acting and real actor involvement in the story was well done.  What will spoil the experience for many is the actual game engine, specifically performance on some setups. It's all over the place. From 60fp, to 40fps, to 1fps in the space of a contextual execution. My library to date includes 2300+ games not including gaming platforms outside of Steam. This is as flaky as it gets, completely random and playing around with the settings makes nowhere near the impact which it should on performance as its not a hardware issue, it's quite clearly the game engine. Community hub is littered with similar complaints from people running the game on 970s & 980s and theres an absolute lack of support or acknowledgement of the issue.....  and for that very reason I wouldn't recommend the game to anyone.   Especially frustrating here as the PC in question runs Arkham Knight at steady 30-60 fps with all gameworks settings and everything else maxed bar a few AA / AF settings, and has no performance issues with Crysis 3.

Old and rather junky FPS with some hardcore mechanics, like rhytm-jumping and ricocheting. Narrative is sketchy - it's nice that you are playing flashbacks but out of body experience and changes of protagonists rarely fit the FPS genre; story itself is weak. Graphics are rather good, while animations and voice-acting are trashy. But music is god-tier.  The most mixed thing is level-design. First half of the game consists of same corridors, same underground stations (but unlike ones from Metro 2033 these are realistic and pretty ones, and not copypasted brown dumpsters full of hobos, so there is some local flavour there) and same train segments, which remind similar level from unreal tournament. While they are really fitting thematically they are very linear and boring. Later levels are more complex, but still, overall game is full of unclear objectives and boring fetches in semi-linear and samey labyrinths. Though sometimes developers really tried to do everything right. Sheer dullness of these levels made me nostalgic for unreal, and i don't even like that game.  Technically it's stable and works on modern systems, but resolution is capped, alt-tab freezes the game and it can't be played on surround.  It's ok. At least not another console soapy movie. Still it drags on a little too long.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Epic story and different endings. I like it a lot.

Edit: I've learned from watching Let's Plays that some of the following is false. It was a misunderstanding caused by the fact that the game never tells you what an exclamation mark by an airduct means. You'd assume it means an animatronic is coming out of that duct but it actually means the music box (in another room nowhere near any ducts) has to be wound but the game never tells you this.  Ironically, this kept causing me to miss my chance to wind the music box. I've left the rest of the review unedited so you can get an idea of how crippling it is that this game has such a severe lack of proper visual queues.  Because of this, I got killed twice on night 2 by Foxy & once by the puppet. The game even lies about how to deal with Foxy. It says to just shine your flashlight at him every now & then. That doesn't work. You actually have to flicker the light on & off rapidly & then turn the camera off.  Worst game I've ever played. Never has another game so thoroughly turned all of my enthusiasm into hate in less than 26 minutes. 3 months was tons more than enough time to make a game better than this & the game has been out for a long time anyway. Even if you get this game for free, you should never play it. It doesn't get much worse than this.

6/30/2015-  Fun arcade style fps, yes. Competitive shooter, no! (They want it to be viewed on competitve scale)  -Maps are too small in relation to respawn timer. You dont really feel the pressure of a bad choice.  -Theres no reason to help anyone up IF YOUR NOT A MEDIC, combat is always 1-5 seconds away from your spawn. If you're here because you liked Highschool Romance, put any comparisons out of your mind. The art style is similar, and the 'boy masquerades as girl' thing is there, but that's about the end of it. The tone of the story is much, much darker. And rather than three good endings and a 'you didn't do well enough' ending, there are 8 flat out bad ends, 1 outright good end, and depending on how you feel about the final end, an evil end.   Much like the Lea path in HSR 1, each of these ends is ridiculously complicated (in spite of the 1.3 update to make it 'easier', this is very much a game of either repeated trial-and-error and judicious use of the save system or using one of the rather helpful guides if you just want to get the end you're aiming for). Dark magic shenanigans seem to be par for the course for 'magic school' VNs, but slapping the Highschool Romance name on this is in art style and story hook only.
