Before I start, my hours may be a little hypocritical but here goes.  If you've played Portal, then you'll know that it was just about a masterpiece. How could Portal 2 hope to surpass it? Portal 2 basically does everything in its power to do just that, but does it? That depends on what you think made the first one so special. The first being a 3 hour game that had a lot of focus on being solely a puzzle game, with a small story as the backdrop. Portal 2 however, is around 8 hours long with the story being first and foremost and the puzzles now more complex with different concepts that try to spice things up.  The story is still Valves signature storytelling in which it doesn't really tell a story in the traditional sense. It kind of just hints at stuff and you have to piece it all together. With plenty of jokes throughout, it's definitely a humorous game that is way funnier than the first. The story stretches across 8 hours of gameplay, with twists and turns it never really feels like the story is stuck in one place for too long.  This is where the problems may lie. Can a puzzle game have a long story? Going from puzzle to set piece, it can feel like it's getting further and further away from a simple puzzle game. Portal didn't need a long story, that's what made it so perfect. It's core design was enough to keep you playing and by the end, it left you wanting more. Portal 2 tries to space out the puzzles so that they don't feel repetitive, and while it works for the most part, it's a simple flaw you can't avoid with a puzzle game. At some point, the puzzles stop feeling good to solve (which is even hinted at in the story) that maybe the game shouldn't have been so long.  Co-op is where you will have the most fun in terms of puzzle solving. That is, if you have someone you can play with who hasn't already played through it. I have played many player created co-op maps as well, which gives it far more replay value than the main game does. If you can bare playing a few flawed maps every now and again. There's also the level editor, while rather limited, is really user friendly. Just don't expect too much out of it.  In no way do these complaints make this a bad game. It IS a good game, and you SHOULD play it. It just has the misfortune of having to be compared to the first one. Which it has to be, cause of the giant '2' next to its name. If you loved the first one, it would be hard to say you'll have the same love for this one too. If you just thought the first one was okay, than perhaps you'll love this one. It's hard to replicate what made the first one so special, but Portal 2 doesn't really try to, It just settles for having its own specialness, which I can respect.  Portal is a slice of cake, while Portal 2 is having to eat the whole cake in one sitting. Wouldn't you just like one slice, as opposed to eating it all? Really? You would eat a whole cake? Well then, In that case, eat the whole cake.

For a completely free online multi-player shooter, this is fine. That is all you will get, and you should expect nothing more. The co-operative campaign can be completed in under two hours, and it is seven segments in length. There is a reasonable amount of weapons and equipment, but you will find that very few are worth using at all times. Enemy variety may be a problem for some as there are only ten different enemies in the game. After about six hours of this, you may grow tired, or you may be more interested in raising your level (which is not worth very much at all). In addition to these limitations, you will quickly realize that between zero and three servers with good latency are available at any time. There is fun to be had here, but it does not remain constant. There is simply not enough variation nor depth.  Alien Swarm is okay.

For me the best game of 2014.  Beyond Earth combines the elements of old school Alpha Centauri and Civilization V into one solid, beautifully made package.  The vanilla version (no doubt there will be loads of DLC and some expansions in the near future) is very streamlined and organized, while still managing to offer many different approaches towards achieving your ultimate goal during a scenario. For somebody, who haven't ever played any Civ game, it might seem confusing at first, but that will pass, trust me; for a veteran, you will hop in straight into a game, being instantly familiar with many features, yet still you do not feel it is just a Civ V rip-off. The wide spectrum of policies and quests allow you to tweak your civilization in more ways than ever before.  As great as the gameplay itself are the visuals. Civ V set the standards high, yet BE has them beaten. A variety of planetary landscape (earthlike or completely alien environment) is soon littered by your domes, biowells, generators etc., which all look futuristic and fit well with the visual theme. Your units and cities change looks based on your affinity, allowing you a little personal touch.  The soundtrack is breathtaking - the 'violin theme' gives me chills, reminding me a lot of the First Contact theme every time I hear it.  There are sone cons, however...  Diplomacy - is exactly the same nonsensical crap as in Civ V. Being on friendly terms with another civ does not mean squat, they attack you randomly, because reasons. Only way how to prepare for backstabbing a-hole AI is to keep a decent standing army of highest technological level possible. Luckily there is no United Nations in the New World and lets pray it will keep that way.  AI could have been smarter I guess. On the higher difficulties, the AI shamelessly cheats, but that is not the problem - the problem is that when attacking, the computer behaves like a moron, just bluntly rushing one of your cities with 4+ mobile units and when he fails, the remainder of his armies wanders aimlessly around where you kill the units one by one. When defending, the AI neglects defensive structures, which is fatal. Unlike in Civ V, the cities do not get upgraded in strength/hp when reaching a new technological era (since there no eras in BE, it would be affinity levels), so it is vital to build up both city strength and hitpoints to withstand a siege or a rush attack.  --------------------------------------------------------------------------  Summary: Beyond Earth delivers the highest quality among turn-based games. One has to wonder if the coming new content won't spoil such a masterpiece, like Brave New World did for Civ V - the strength of the game is in its simplicity (ok, simplicity is a poor word considering you have zillions of technologies to research, 80 policies to take and a customized choice of an upgrade for each building), while providing you with tons of fun, complex content.  Lets hope that further additions to the game will expand the already rich, beautiful new start for the Mankind.  10/10

Is Max Payne 3 a good game and a good Max Payne ? Answer : Yes it is a good game,but it is a very different Max Payne game Rating 8.5/10 Quality Price 8/10 The price in my opnion : 15 euros instead 20 euros Why?Let's see : To understand the story better you must play the previous games.Max Payne was a regular cop until one day his wife and his child were killed.Max Payne trys to find the ones who planned the killing of his loved ones.At the end of the previous games he finally avenges the killing of his family,but his life felt mizerabile and he wants to start all over again being hired as a bodyguard for a rich family in Brazil.  The storyline is amazing and very interseting but different from the previous Max Payne games(their storyline had a noir film atmosphere).The graphics are nice.The shooter mechanics are awsome having interesting things like Bullet Time or Shootdodge!The only bad thing about it is the story's lenght being relatively short being able to finish the game in about 10 hours. I didn't tried the multiplayer so I can't speak about it!  The story the graphics and the gameplay make the game a succes!Worth buying it!

Strike Suit Zero is one of the best games that I have had the honor of playing. The graphics are great, the story is interesting, the combat is absolutly fantasic and the music. OOOH the music. But without furthur or do here is the review.  Overall Rating: 8/10  Graphics: The graphics are absolutly fantastic. I don't have to specify why, because if you look at the screen shots, and the trailer you will see why. The effects, and the lights make it feel like your in a fire fight, and the sounds that go along with the graphics are just fantastic. Although since the lights and textures are bright and colorful and they are of abundance so players you are warned.  Combat: The combat is the highlight of this game. The combat goes as followed. You move with the W,A,S,D keys and can use the left shift to use thrusters to boost around the battle field. You have two guns, the Primary which is usually fast fireing and powerful that has unlimited ammunition that uses energy (which regenerates over time). Then there is a various amounts of missles in the game that you can use that have their own speical properties and effectivness in situations. Some times in combat you can have your weapons lock on to the enemy, and the bullets will follow them. Mot of the time I find myself using my primary guns, and to use them effectivly you have to arch your aim so that the enemy flys into it. Fortunatly it gives you a place to fire, so you know where you need to shoot (However you need to be close or the circle will not show up). The primary weapon has unlimited ammunition since it uses the energy of the ship, but the LMG's, and various missles have ammunition and can be depleted fast. There are many different ships you can use, and I personally perfer the Strike Suit. You can switch from Mech to ship with the space button and just like regular primary weapon fire, the Strike suit requires power to be used. How do you do that? By blowing things up! All in all the fighting in this game feels 'realistic' so to speak and is very fun and challenging. Although the large amount of enemys in the area of combat can be over whelming, it always pays off to be moving, and to not get to close to the main enemy ships until the fighters are taken care of. In that way its tactical and makes me want to stay with my team.   Music: The mustic brings a tear to my eyes, as its as epic as it is interesting. Many times I find music repeating but I don't mind that very much as it is quite epic and makes me fight harder and get into the heat of the moment.   Pros-  Music Graphics Combat light effects and sounds around the area story  Cons- No multiplayer Gets repetitive  Final Opinon: This game is really good. There are some things that didn't live up to my expectations, not much DLC, no multiplayer, not alot of missions, etc. But on the other hand the dlc isn't a need, the multiplayer would be fun but isn't a thing that needs to happen. The missions are long and increse in difficulty as you go on. The combat, music, and graphics are all in my own opinon great. I bought this game today, and played it for 2 hours. No regrets. If you like Mechs, and the Sci-Fi genres this game is worth the $19.99 and is worth picking up to play.

This is a great example of how outstanding music can elevate a game from good to great. I also do not want to disregard the writing in this game either. The creator managed to meaningful dialogue within the game without over doing it and without being corny.   The retro pixel art is outstanding. The pacing can suffer a little from time to time as you wander around looking for what to do next. But aside from that, I enjoyed gradually making my way through the 7 hour campaign. The enemies are not found in abundance or varied but all placed with thought behind the room design. That and other attention to detail show me the the developer who made this game truely cared about what he was making.  If you are in the mood for a retro survival horror game you probably won't do better than this.

app_name: The Lord of the Rings Online™
review_text: LOTRO is a pretty fun MMO I have enjoyed my time and don't regret any of it, especially the story mode which allows for immersive solo play. I know there have been some folks who played it when it had been sub based, and they make strong valid arguments for why it was better before. I wish I could have played it back then, but while this game now is pretty fun and unique I fear this game may be dying. Only for two reasons first each content update is closer and closer to mordor, maybe they have plans for after but this is a problem for an MMO that has an 'end game story'. Second the servers seem a little.....under populated some times, most players are now long time hardcore ones who play the high lvl maps so being a new player will be lonely for a bit. By all means play the game, the community is friendly and extremely helpful, the content is immerssive and well written, and the map is YUUUUUGE!!! Now once again I could very well be wrong about the end game, they may have other plans, either way if the Tolkien world and MMO's enthrall you then this is a MUST PLAY for you. Over all I have no gripes about this game, I give it 8.5/10 only because it badly needs a graphics revamp.

6.5/10 Many people praise this game, I will be one of those who do the opposite.  Good stuff: • It looks nice • The fauna is quite alive • You can stealth around, shoot at everything like crazy or mix that up as you wish  Bad stuff: • A pretty bad skill system • A rather dumb unlock system • Repetitive side missions • Nearly unresponsive flora • Unfitting music in some situations • Too many collectibles that are scattered around in a big world  Let's go into detail on the bad stuff (because the good stuff doesn't need any further explanation):  The skill system: Aside from the whole 'get enough XP and level up to get a skillpoint each time' thing (killing enemies in a certain way, finding collectibles, doing missions, clearing camps etc.), the skill system itself is quite unrewarding and pretty bad in general. Things you should know from the get-go (cooking grenades and sliding into crouch mode being the best examples) have to be learned. They may be part of the tutorial, but that's two skillpoints wasted on something I can do without military training.  And then there are skills that only get available when you've reached a certain point in the story, Ninja Step (the number one stealth skill) being the best example. I want to be a silent killer at the start, just like in Splinter Cell!  Okay, bad example, because you aren't a trained agent who has more experience in his pinky than anyone else would have in their whole life, but seriously, if this whole skill system has to be so unrealistic (I never touched an assault rifle, a skill magically makes me better at aiming and shooting with it), then let people unlock the skills they want, whenever they want. __________________________________________________  The unlock system and repetitive side missions: This relates to nearly everything useful in the game. It already starts with the map. Just like in the Assassin's Creed games, you have to climb and fix radio towers to unveil the part of the map they stand in. Additionally, every time you fix one, one of several weapons gets available for free in the store. Of course you can't choose which one, so if that revolver or sniper rifle you want so badly isn't on the list, you'll have to spend your hard-earned cash on it anyway.  Why 'anyway'? Because you can buy almost every weapon you want right at the start. If you have enough cash that is. Want a sniper rifle with a cool camouflage painting, a silencer and a better scope? You better have about 2600 dollars for that, otherwise you'll have to scavenge bodies and find loot chests. Or you do some of those other repetitive missions: Drive from A to B and deliver a package with unknown contents in time, kill a bunch of enemies with predefined weapons, assassinate group leaders (WHY IS IT ALWAYS MACHETE-ONLY!?) or help out a fellow islander. Over and over again.  Of course you can skip all that if you're lazy. The magical word is 'trainer'.  Oh and if you want to get special weapons, for example a silenced 1911 with a laser dot scope and extended magazine (no need to buy all those, it's a prefab), then you'll HAVE to do a certain number of side missions, so yeah, there's no way around that. The same goes for some skills. Wanna improve your bullet-proof vest? Buy 3 and invest a skillpoint. Make sure you purchased the skills that make that one available, too.  So, we have skills, weapons, the map, what else could annoy the crap out of you? Correct: HUNTING!  For example, you can carry only 1 (in a word: ONE) weapon at the start. Wanna carry more? Hunt a specific animal, skin it and THEN you can enhance your weapon belt. Of course this requires a different animal's skin every time. Not to froget that you need more than just one, because that would be too easy and destroy the purpose of hunting pretty fast (hunting isn't that rewarding money-wise). The final upgrade always requires a unique animal's skin which you get for doing a certain hunting mission. The bad thing here: There's a skill that doubles the outcome of skinning an animal, so instead of one tiger hide you'll get two. Why it's bad? Because you probably already got all upgrades before you can get that damn skill!  Let's end this point here before I get a heart attack. __________________________________________________  Unresponsive flora: If you played Far Cry 2, then you must've been amazed by the flora and its reaction to physical contact and especially fire. The grass and every bush moved away as you passed through it, tree branches broke off when bullets passed through them and when you set things on fire, you could witness 'the best fire physics ever' in a video game.  They left all that out in this one. Okay, some bushes move around when you drive through them, but that's it. Grass, bushes and trees catch fire, but the fire dies out after about 15 seconds and leaves black grass and still green bushes and trees. Either one of the game's designers was new, totally forgot about adding that or they just didn't want to bother with it and wanted to ship the game asap instead. No matter what the reason was, the atmosphere suffers from it. Especially when you're that Flamethrower-wielding and Molotov-throwing kind of person. __________________________________________________  Unfitting music in some situations: I'll make this one short. The music isn't that great overall, but I tend to lower the music's volume in most game settings anyway, because it makes it hard to listen to dialogue sometimes. Still, I'd rather vomit into my enemy's face instead of stabbing him when I suddenly hear some dubstep-ish music that is supposed to add some thrill or whatever. __________________________________________________  Too many collectibles scattered around in a big world: I'm a perfectionist. I tend to get 100% completion in the games I play. All achievements included (as long as they're doable). And I highly doubt I'm the only one who is like that. You can't compare Far Cry 3's world to Just Cause 2's, but the lack of fast travel points (camps and towns/villages only) make this world even worse in comparison to Just Cause 2's, because that has a village, a town, a city or whatever else around every corner and behind every third tree. Not to forget that Just Cause 2 has more and faster vehicles than all Far Cry games in general. Still, you can buy 'treasure maps' which show the locations of collectibles on your main map, but that still doesn't make things easier.  'See that little island there? There's a relic (a little stone statue that grants 250 XP, there's 120 of them, aside from 20 letters and memory cards which are also scattered around). You know you want it, so go there and get it, you freak!' 'But the nearest fast travel point is almost a kilometer away from it! Even if I drive there, I still have to find a way to get to it if it's in a hidden cave or on a hill!' 'Suck it up!'  I could ignore that stuff. I could just play the game like most people do and only collect what's on my way through the story, un-install the game as soon as the credits roll and play something else. But that doesn't change the fact that I know I haven't bothered getting 100% which wouldn't let me rest whenever I think of this game or whenever UPlay tells me that I haven't unlocked that one achievement thousands if not ten thousands of people already got. If they can do that, then why shouldn't I? __________________________________________________  So yeah, that's it. My (supposed-to-be) little review for this oh-so-epic game of the year. In case you're wondering, I haven't mentioned the story, because I haven't really touched that yet. It's neat so far, but maybe that'll change to the better or worse.  I'm not telling you to not buy this game, but I'm not telling you to buy it, either. You should get the idea.

Scourge: Outbreak is a must for a close group of friends (4 max) who want to enjoy some good 'ol fashion alien hunting. The game isn't that long but its worth the purchase.  First, it features a unique story that keeps you trying to figure it out since the beginning of the game. As you continue you will watch surprising cut scenes which give you a bigger picture to the story. The story line ride is que interesting to say the least.  In terms of game play, it's quite polished. I dont recall many bugs, the graphics and effects looked good, the maps were clean and straight-forward.  At different points the game slowed down a bit, like when we had to bomb the helicopter to pieces by tossing grenades at it. We thought it would never end and that the devs were playing a cruel joke on us, while watching a live feed of our game play, eating pizza and making fun of us. Long shot but hey, gotta use the imagination dont we? But, it picked up and got a bit more fast paced when encountering hordes of evil brain-washed vermin, or nasty bugs. It was a good balance.  A variety of weapons also kept the game interesting and thrilling, especially when the big boy guns were found. And of course nades-are-nades. Who doesn't like big booms that splatter green alien slime everywhere?  I had a blast, yet enjoyed it more with friends than in single mode - this seems to be shared by the majority of the gaming community. All in all, I think it's is a great game, that will stay fun for a long time.

So far this game is turning out very well! It is by the same makers of one of my favorite RPG's of all time, Divine Divinity!  Divinity: Original Sin has some great dialogue, tough choices and handles very well, even for an early release. Do yourself a favor and buy into this now if you love roleplaying games! The whole game will be playable in coop with a friend or two, these choices are hard as hell to pick even in solo play but I can imagine in coop these decisions in the game world will be realy ahrd to agree on!  Awesome dialogue, good controls, fun gameplay, interesting characters and a gameworld that is fantastic. And that is only the begining! This game is looking to be one of 2014's best rpgs, buy now!  If you enjoyed the old ultima rpgs, old Icewind Dale or the old divinity rpgs, this is JUST what you need! As with many of my favorite rpgs this does start a tad slow but things pick up and get better and better as the game goes along. Tons of ways you can go about combat and building you're character, it really is a masterpiece of a game that you can tell Larian put in alot of love into this one.  I have now played 185 hours now on the game and I just beat the last boss to beat the game. Overall this is a wonderful rpg and a breath of fresh air. There is a few small issues such as the ui could be a bit better and I had two small bugs but overall this is a very special rpg. Can't wait to start up a new game with some friends in coop. The combat is perfection, the game has funny humor, jam packed with content and it is fun as hell to play. CRPG's are back people! How great is this?  9.5/10

I have completed all the available episodes and had this to say:  Kentucky Route Zero is an incredibly aesthetically-pleasing game with a LOT of potential. Some parts of the game makes you begging to skip to the next chapter and, in very few occasions, actually has something worth hearing/seeing. But usually, those rare instances are brief and the game drops back into it's same, repetive, boring, routine again. The only thing thats preventing me from unrecommending this to you is because all the episodes haven't been released yet. Perhaps the remaing episodes could truly save the game, but for now, I can only recommend this game for folks who are seriously into those novelty or indie games.

The game itself is good. It is great fun when starting out. After playing for a whilt it may be a bit on the bland side because it is a bit easy and I find that harder difficulties often make the game easier as opposed to the easier difficulties because you don't have to deal with angry homeless immigrants.  That being said though, the music is so awesome that I don't really care. Even when I'm bored of the game I just randomly end up singing or humming along with the soundtrack. Kinda wish there were subtitles or subtitle translations for those songs instead of the radio announcer subtitles.

When I first saw this game on steam, it definitely caught my attention. Just from the art style alone. I love how smoothe the animations are. It's like watching a cartoon show except you're playing it. Aside from the awesome art and visuals in this game, the story is pretty interesting as well. It doesn't seem like your usual hack n'slash type of game either even though you could just get through the whole game just by hitting one button but I wouldn't recommend it. It has the basic RPG elements to it as well; earn exp to gain levels, which come by quickly I might add, which I think is nice, constantly getting new equipment to make yourself stronger... all that jazz. You even have a little companion with you, Fidget, that helps you throughout the game, and it isn't useless, only a little annoying. It can cast spells which you can mix with your attack to make the spell more powerful.  The only complaint I have with this game is your have to learn the most basic of skills which don't come until later on in the game like sliding and even grabbing onto vines to climb to higher points. The grabbing vines skill, I thought, was a little ridiculous to learn because it's nothing special, you just learn to grab a vine and can climb them. But that being said, I guess they don't want you doing everything right off the bat, there is backtracking to get treasures that you couldn't get before and that is something I like.  Overall, I think this game is amazing and I highly recommend it for anyone who enjoys action-RPGs.

This is such an underrated game. One of the best immersive sims I've played. The level design is incredible. I've never been a fan of high score games or speed runs, but the levels in this game are so complex and interesting that everytime I replay one I find something completely new. You feel like a Hitman having to case the area, find the opportunity you want to use and then execute it. Things never go as planned either which makes it really exciting and rewarding when you can flawlessly pull off a misson. The timed targets are an incredible addition too. Getting only one shot to kill a target creates really high stakes gameplay that's unlike anything I've played. Buy this game for the level design. The episodic format worked extremely well. Every level is memorable and endlessly replayable. It's one of those games that makes you feel awesome when you play. Each level is like a giant constantly moving puzzle. It's a hugely rewarding game. At least buy the first two missions. If you aren't compelled to buy the rest of the game after playing them, then it's probably not the game for you.

I've put a lot of hours into this, it's no casual game, but if you get into it it's really tons of fun.' ~~IcyDwarf   'If you like fast-paced action but are tired of first person shooters in which one shot is enough to kill you, you'll probably like this game. And if you like parkour and ball games, you'll like it even more. Even the tower defense fans will love this game!' ~~ProtoQuantum  Epigenesis presents a promising take on the next few hundred years: no grim future centered entirely on war, no post-apocalyptic earth where Darwinian human instinct has taken over; instead, humanity created the most popular sport in history, some strange hybrid of Basketball, Soccer, and Unreal Tournament. It's also an interesting case for why 'e-sports' doesn't necessarily have to conjure up images of multiplayer battle arenas and first-person shooters, but that more traditional (albeit fictional) sports can exist in that space alongside the likes of Dota 2 and Counter-Strike. I really like what Epigenesis represents: it's an innovative take on traditional sports that still provides the adrenaline rush of something like Quake or Unreal Tournament, in a well-produced package that you rarely see in games fresh out of Steam's Early Access.   'In Epigenesis, two teams face off in an attempt to plant a ball inside an enemy's goal more times in ten minutes than the opposition across a platformed ravine. The arenas aesthetically resemble something out of Tron but play like something out of Quake. Each player is given a non-lethal laser with two modes: rail (an instant, focused beam which pushes enemies back a small amount) and shot (a mode which fires a missile-like projectile which travels slowly but produces an explosion that pushes back enemies a lot). The gun is a key part of both offense and defense -- pushing an enemy back and causing them to fall off a ledge or preventing them from landing on one in the first place spells instant death, producing a 5-second respawn timer. In addition, hitting an enemy who's in the air with your melee attack sends them straight downward, and if they fail to land on a platform? Splat.  In order to score a goal, a player has to pick up the ball from the middle of the arena, take it to the enemy goal, and toss it in. It's simple, but when presented with power-ups like 'smokescreen' (which creates a cloud of smoke over a large area for a few seconds), along with arena-based hazards (like the desert maps' Twister, which pulls players it catches high up into the air), it gets complex and interesting. Upon scoring, a player is given a seed, which they can plant on one of the unoccupied platforms, which in turn spawns a plant, a kind of 'turret' which produces effects ranging from firing projectiles which slow enemies, to creating a shield in which players can hide. In addition, each plant provides the entire team with a buff to either jump height or speed. Each player also has a gauntlet called a 'meth-glove,' whose use is two-fold: the first, called 'methylation,' allows a player to power up a plant; the other, called 'demethylation,' allows them to cause an enemy's plant to wither and eventually become inactive. It's here where the strategy of Epigenesis starts to shine: its rules are deceptively easy to learn, but when other humans are thrown into the mix, their collective strategies coalesce into something truly spectacular.' ~~Tamriilin   I can not say anything that has not been already said except for this THE COMMUNITY IS NOT DEAD! Valdis Story is a well developed platforming action game that fits comfortably within the 'Metroidvania' genre. The story is interesting enough, creating a backdrop of a group of characters fighting for human souls in a war between 'angels' and 'demons', but the real draw is the gameplay. A selection of different characters, equipment, and skills provide a variety of options for the gamer to decide how to engage the enemies that one will encounter. Regardless of what path is traveled, however, many will find this game to be on the irritating side of difficult. Merciless enemies require patience and understanding of the mechanics and pitfalls of the game. Unblockables, lingering status effects, and a lack of the hit-invicibility that many old-school gamers can take for granted can lead to a devastating combination of unavoidable pain if you aren't cautious or have tremendous fast-twitch reactions.    The map is fantastically designed and, coupled with an astounding soundtrack, draws the player into the game quite well. The controls are responsive, but demand skill from the player to not make mistakes on their use. A gamepad is suggested for this game, and I am quite glad that key rebinding is a feature. Certain gameplay mechanics and level design decisions will undoubtedly enrage some players, such as rooms with gaps in the floor combined with enemies that stun and knockback, send the player back to an earlier part of the level, or the exacting timed platforming speedruns for certain pieces of gear.    Overall, this game is recommended IF the player likes the 'Metroidvania' style of games that require backtracking, AND enjoy the challenging but eventually rewarding type of learn and adapt gameplay in Dark Souls. Gamepad is definitely recommended.

I will admit, when I saw the trailer, and read the description, I was intrigued. A rogue-like that merged a First Person Perspective with a greco mythological setting. Gameplay includes a stealth mechanic, frantic melee combat complete with explorative puzzle solving and collection.   Now with those highlights set in stone, lets move onto what the game actually provides you, where it could improve, and why the game is most assuridly worth looking at should you find that you can look past its failings.  Graphics:  The game does not look stelar by any stretch of the imagination. While they are not bad, they are also not good. What they do succeed in is providing a very interesting mood and setting through out the several themes of dungeon you get to delve into. I unforunately found that I had to turn the settings to minimum in order to play the game at a stable frame-rate. I am not sure why, but even with the settings turned to the lowest, I still pulled 15 to 20 fPS every now and again, mostly when some of the environments looked 'impressive' or the action got intense. The game fortunately remained playable (mostly given the not as intellegent as they could be monsters). Some of the special effects seem to be more annoying than eye candy related, and the addition of a viewable hud (one that can be brought up like a menu / inventory) would have been appreciated. As it stands, the HUD is very minimal, and that is not a bad thing. Unfortunately, you are provided no information in regards to your characters capabilities. Does the Axe really do as much damage as the Spear? Does the Gladius really do more damage than the Dagger? Those questions are answered visually, and mostly through base interpretation, I.E. it takes 2 hits to kill this with the dagger and one with the sword, so sword does more damage.  Given that the game does not have any character development through out, this system does fulfill its purpose, but it still would be nice to see a representation of the items you can find and collect through out. As it stands, there is no point to invest in the weaker stuff. Period.  Which brings us to, Gameplay:  The game play suffers from being too simple, to an extreme, and having enemies that are either WAY to aggressively difficult to deal with, or enemies that are just rediculously easy to deal with. The notes in the game talk the first boss type character up as unbeatable, but you can get him to run into pit traps (removing him from the floor all together), or can simply trade blows with him easy enough. But the next boss monster, becomes so rediculously difficult to fight because he can hit you so much easier than his predicessor. Which brings us to the way damage is interpreted in the game. I am left feeling that it has the Call of Duty damage indicator via screen lighting. It seems that after taking one hit, it becomes much easier to see the world as the lighting starts to minimize itself. Then it progresses to greyscale and blurs progressively more as you take damage. Unfortunately, there is no indicator as to wether you are actually healing, or if you require some of the several healing items you can find through out the dungeon. With no health bar, or meter to find in a menu of sorts, you literaly have no idea if drinking a health potion will effect you or not unless your stcreen is blurry, same goes for picking up the food that is on the ground.  I mentioned before, that the enemies are very base in their AI patterns. Most seem to be very easily manipulated, or they are just easy to deal with period. You are supposed to be able to stealth around enemies, but I find that given the games tendency to force conflict on you, stealth really never works as an option. I found it easier to beat the enemy till they had to respawn, than it was to hide from them. Specially since the enemies REALLY don't need to be hid from. It would be nice to tweak the AI, or actually make it to where you can't actually kill the boss monsters without the special puzzle fight in the end of each of the dungeon sections. It would be much more interesting to make it to where you HAVE to stealth around the boss monsters till you actually get to a point where you can kill them. Just my suggestion. The first bosses chamber was a neat idea involving a creative kill. More thought and planning into the climatic showdown with your antagonistic boss monster would be greatly appreciated and would set the game up for some more polish and fun.  Regarding the character development as a 'rogue-like'. It really isn't there. You find gold through out and books, but you spend the gold on a finite amount of weapons, like... 8 or so (most of which are useless), and spend books on various spell podiums that are considered 'gifts from the gods'. Unfortunately, the gifts from gods are set to be one shot abilities that don't stay with you. Finding potions, you can only carry one, and you HAVE to drink them good and ill to find out what they do, no exceptions. That is really about it. It would have been nice to see character progression in a manner of speaking. You gain experience at the end of each dungeon through kills and explorative findings. The weapons should have better scaling in terms of worth so that it forces the players to 'work through' improving their characters, rather than saving and affording the best melee weapon in the game before the first boss is even killed. It would make for a more challenging and balanced game to either increase the cost, or make it to where you have to work through the game instaed of having everything available from the begining. What is currently in place, works. Don't get me wrong. It does work, but the flaws that are a part of the current system, I would concider a bit of a flaw. Most certainly not flawed enough to prevent me from enjoying what I am playing.  Combat feels a bit odd. While it is responsive, it doesn't really carry much weight with most of the weapons. It is functional though.  The randomly generated floors are functional, most definitely, but they do not push for more of what the basic game could provide. It would be really sweet to see more than a simple, 'find the key' and 'unlock the exit' scenario. If more objectives to solve each floor could be provided, it would add to the depth of the game, creating a MUCH more interesting play experience.  Sound: It is most certainly a bizarre choice to pair electronic riffs with the atmosphere of the game. I was initially turned off by it at first. I rolled my eyes, and chuckled, but eventually it started to feel more in place than I was led to expect. The ambient sounds actually work pretty well in regards to how smooth the transitions. You hear the echoes as the hooves of a satyr explore the halls looking for you. Initially it was pretty tense, at least till I realized I could totally punk the Satyre.  All and all, this game is flawed, Amaturish, and rough around the edges in terms of presentation. But what you get for the price is a game that stands as a fun game, that I found myself fully absorbed into. Despite its short comings, I find it to be really fun. I recommend the game. for a 6 dollar price tag, you can have a bunch of fun if you can look past the faults. Those of use who do enjoy the game, I am hoping share the voice that we would love to see this game refined. Thanks a bunch for the Devs. There are no regrets for my purchase.

One of the few games that actually does asymetrical multiplayer well and pretty much the only multiplayer only game I could recommend for the following reasons:  1: it is well paced. While other games with the same concept like 'Evolve' ended up as a gloryfied hide and seek mission, this one has really good pacing. The 4 Divers defend a robot digging for treasure, setting up defenses, while the 2 sharks try to simply murder them efficently. Several changes of location reset the status quo several times, and also give the shark-side moments to pick of single divers while they are trying to move to another location.  2: Both side feels unique, mostly the shark-side does, but since everything plays under the sea(insert little mermaid song here) you have to fight in a 3D environment, which in itself is not done all that often in Shooter games. Yes, most of the time you are in a literal 3D environment, but giving you free movement in every way constantly is something not done to often and opens up many new styles of gameplay. It also has a little bit of horror in there, since it can actually be quite terryfing as a diver. For the sharks? Not so much, it is rather tactical, since you are outnumberd 2:1, therefore, can't simply go in and eat every diver alive. No you have to coordinate your attacks with one another, need to think of ways to get out again before your targets can retaliate and even open up new paths by crashing walls open and attacking from angles your opponents didn't expect.  Sure, the game is far from perfect, but it is still one of the best asymetrical multiplayergames out there and probably everything 'Evolve' tried to be, just better, without beeing a fullprice release.

Influent is an edutainment title that tries to 'gamify' the language learning process by placing you in a virtual room filled with stuff that you click and learn their names and associated adjectives and verbs. The world space is very limited and only presents the inside of an apartment, and sadly there is no word association or sentence building exercises. Additionally, there are still some serious glitches and bugs littered across the game, including some serious memory leaks, slowdowns and poor design decisions.  Influent has a LOT of potential if the developer manages to include additional spaces (maybe girlfriend's apartment, a convenience store or work place?) with more involving word association games. As it stands now, there is very little meat on these bones, and I hesitate to recommend it if you are seriously pursuing a new language. 3/5 until further notice.

Tharsis is a game about strategy and luck, and making the best of a bad set of choices.  Tharsis is random like Poker is random; sometimes you get dealt a bad hand and lose, but skill is a much bigger factor overall than luck.  Tharsis is sort of like a single-player version of Battlestar Galactica: The Board Game. The goal isn't really to 'win', it's to barely limp to the finish line with a crew of nearly-insane astronauts and your ship exploding all around you.  Tharsis is occasionally frustrating, but an individual game is short enough that I rarely feel like losing is a major setback. I feel like I've accomplished something if I just get a little bit farther than last time.  Tharsis has a really nice UI. Picking up and dropping dice into the various slots feels really nice.  All of that aside, you probably don't want to play this game if you are easily frustrated by a game being unfair. Sometimes, you just lose, and have to choose between losing now, and losing on the next turn. Persistance is a big deal here.  If you're looking for a game where you have to weigh the benefits of cannabalism, or make a decision about whether you sacrifice a character now to keep the ship from falling apart, or just make the best out of an impossible situation, this game is for you.

Looks very simple, but it's quite addictive and challenging. Puzzles are quite clever and difficulties change a lot. Few puzzles can cause some hair pulling out of frustration though,    (Yes, I'm talking about the Cinderella one here.)    but satisfaction of finding one of the harder endings or figuring out a puzzle is rewarding and makes it all worth it. Different outcomes give it replay value, though once finding all possible endings it drops drastically. It is still worth of the price and definitely at its best when you use guides as little as possible. Definitely recommended for people who like serious puzzle games, thinking, something unique or clicking. Be prepared to click a lot.

This is not a game for those with ADHD. What I mean by that is, if you're the fast-travelling, cutscene-skipping, exploit-farming, team-abandoning, microphone-muting type, then you will very quickly get bored of this game. If you like to take your time and want to explore and understand the lore and background of a game, or if you're looking for a co-op game to enjoy with friends and meet new people, then you can absolutely have a great time in The Division. Don't rush through it, take your time and explore and you will absolutely get your money's worth and much more.

I love this game. It runs flawlessly for me (GTX 980), which I'd expect given its age. Not sure how well it works on other hardware, so make sure to check other people's performance if you're concerned about your own setup being able to handle it.  It's a lot of fun, and looks as good as it's ever going to thanks to the polish it's received. Storyline might be confusing for some, but I'll try explain this as easily as I can while remaining conscious on this much Morphine: Nephilim are cross-dressers who like to get jiggy with dead bodies... Wait, no, wrong creeps, I was thinking of Aunt Robert - they were combinations of Angels and Demons. Because why not, right? There were lots of them. They killed everything that moved.   The powers-that-be didn't like this and struck a deal with 4 such Neph: We'll give you supreme power if you f*** up anyone whose race starts with 'N' and ends with 'ephilim'. A few hours later, Death (who was known as Horace the Horrific at that point because of his Horrific taste in Halloween masks) cottoned on to what they meant, and he and his two Brothers, as well as his sister (called Fury, because... well, you know, 3000 years of PMS will do that to you, not to mention having 3 brothers who always leave the toilet seat up) decided to slaughter them all.  Stuff happened, more stuff happened after that, and then someone went and broke some Seals (the other Seals were so upset by this they all went Clubbing after the funeral) which indicated the Apocalypse was meant to begin.  Except it wasn't, but that didn't stop War (known in some circles as 'Stoney Tony') from f***ing up big time and starting the Apocalypse accidentally himself. Oops. I told him to lay off the Pixie Dust, but would he listen? Would he f***.   With Humanity wiped out, Horace was all, 'Goddammit Tony I can't take you anywhere AND PUT THAT BLUNT OUT FOR KANYE'S SAKE', so he then goes to try and reverse this catastrophic set of events, and prove Stoney's innocence.   Well, that and so he could get all stabby-slicey with the local demon populace.  Anyway, long story short, this game rocks, it looks good, it plays well now that it's fixed (devs fixed a catastrophic crash bug in 24 hours - hear that Rocksteady?) and the only complaints I know of right now are the fact that it doesn't run perfectly for everyone. Yeah, I know, it lags on Pocket Calculators, who woulda thought? If you don't already own it, then I've got some bad news for you - Horace will be COMING FOR YOU. Or was it ON you? Actually, yeah, that would be much worse.  My rating - 8/10

This game is something special. I'm that kind of person who doesn't play games generally and usually just watch gameplays on Youtube, planning to play the game if it seems interesting enough. In recent years there hasn't been many.   But then... oh boy, then this game happened. It's hard to describe but I instantly knew this game had potential. Gameplay is fun, the characters memorable, soundtrack ridiculously flawless, art and style modest but in a way charming and the story more than meets the eye. And you never know what comes next!  Some would say the game is not perfect, though: some characters are annoying, puzzles may be too simplistic and the game relies too much on people getting jokes. This still is more just nitpicking and doesn't affect the entirety of the game. I don't think I have had this much emotions in a video game in a long time.  Best spent 9,99€ ever.

Good :  * Very Pretty * Pacing and Weight of character movement feels good * Objective gametypes are cleverly done, and I think the modes are the best design feature in that sense. * Interesting character designs and abilities * Outstanding VO * Cool Story * Great cinematics   Neutral   * Having characters locked is an instant turn-off to me. I don't want to have to grind out a load of characters I don't like using to maybe unlock one that I also don't like and need to spend longer getting to one I do. They should all be available minute 1. * Gear seems pretty much pointless, as it's a waste of Shards in almost every game mode. * Matchmaking times leave something to be desired * Reports of AMD card issues (though that hasn't happened to me). * A lot of the abilities simply aren't worth using at times, so when you level up, it's annoying that you sometimes have to burn a point into choice a or b, when you would rather have a choice of just upgrading one ability instead. Might just be me on that, but something I've felt a few times. * UI is a tad clunky (though that's classic Gearbox in that respect). * Level Design is a tad dull with regards to map layout. The whole 'needing to use an ability to clear some walls' gets a bit tedious if you dare play as a tank or DPS character. * The scoring system doesn't help with the whole 'Don't encourage people to charge in and kill.' As it doesn't reward people that play the objective or the character role truely (ie; Healers are made to look useless on the scoreboard mid-game). * A LOT of quitters and AFK players. Which doesn't help at all. * Mildly infuriating when the team you play with is obsessed with getting kills, leaving you 1 vs 3 on an objective and you either have to die trying to hold it or surrender it. Perhaps this is just a flaw with MOBA FPS coming to the mainstream. Could bode poorly for Overwatch too, though time will tell there.   Bad   * The character balancing has fallen well and truly short. Some characters are just not worth using and as soon as you get through the roster unlocks, they'll never get used again. * You can already tell that in about 4 weeks time, this will not be a new player friendly game (I wasn't in the Beta, and I already get wooped a lot by people who clearly were, it's taken a lot of patience to keep at it). * Some healers have too much health. And the rate at which they can heal is insane. Miko is probably the best character in the game because as a Healer, it can still get kills with relative ease, whilst keeping everyone alive and can take a fair few hits before needing to withdraw * One Story mission is broken and too difficult for the masses (it took me a fair few attempts) * After a while, you can see everyone abandoning the firearm users for Melee, as its so much easier to control and land hits with them, and they seem to close with almost no real risk to themselves in mid-close range.   However, if you are going to play it, I'd say do it now, as I can't see this outliving the upcoming competition, so get in whilst there's an active crowd. It might get spikes with DLC packs and Steam Sales, but I wouldn't expect to see much of a growth beyond the next 4 weeks.

Proceed to wingsuit drop from the edge of the cottage, scraping our feet on the take-off, and whip out a challenge which my friend scored big on(I biffed a lot) -Spawned most of the up the big peak, and then went on a hike up the ridge to the most summity summit in the game, and used that as a drop point for all the sports. -Scoot down a hill taking turns setting the line, and stumble across the mother of all wingsuit drops, through a series of tunnels, mostly straight down  Before you buy: -DLC includes game content, including new sports. Multiple levels of difficulty make for high replayability, too. Be warned: the game comes to a very abrupt end -- by design -- in about 3000 BC (something that I find quite refreshing -- some games these days can drag on far too long!).  I've played it through twice now, and lost both times (the second time by just one turn, curse it!). Each playthrough took me about ten hours, and as I'm sure I'll try it again, I consider this game excellent value for money.

app_name: Doc Clock: The Toasted Sandwich of Time
review_text: Please, Please No. - Seriously, No.  This game is terrible (such so that I have it in a folder named 'Terrible' for which is was the first game to be placed into). Its an amazing game in concept that I would love to give the chance to be great...if it could manage to do that. Its a piece of garbage that is thrown together into a contraption that just barely manages to reach its goal (oh wow, I totally just explained the entirety of the gameplay)   Is it a cool idea, yes. Is it a fun game, ...hard to say because its basically unplayable. Several times has this game refused to work...or has done something so ...wrong I can't fix it even with the included time rewind. Imagine Prince of Persia: Sands of Time with its time rewind except instead of being a game mechanic that is cool and awesome its a tool used to make sure that if you break the game you can rewind to when it wasn't broken. Like...you can rewind time...which is ONLY used to fix mistakes either you or the game has made.   There are some levels I remember playing that I don't know what to do on, the goal is obviously there, but no meaningful solution is there to reach it. Most the time, I just tried to get as many flying items as I could to strap to myself to fly over everything so I wouldn't have to deal with the terrible game.   Finally, I know in full well that I did not beat this game and so I cannot honestly say my review has full credible weight...but I did give it a good try, I wanted this game to be fun and work.. knowing full well it could be bad. I was let down, I write this in hopes that you are not let down. I would say.. perhaps plead with you to not get this. Its not a bad game, its unplayable.

Plus the usual unlockables and goodies to find. The gameplay is solid which is to be expected from id, Bethesda ect.   Okay, what this game fails at. The story is bad... While the idea is great it isn't delivered in a way that you really get attached to characters or what is happening next. It's all quite predictable and before you know it you finish the game wanting to see a BIG climactic ending and all you get is some 30 second flash back, weak... The progression for this game is all over the place and so is the difficulty. Some parts you can simply blast threw without any thought of cover or worry for damage (I played this on normal) were others genuinely seem like the cranked up the difficulty with no warning just to extend gameplay. There is no multiplayer.   Should you buy this? If you like the classic shooter with the same old story and simple level design. Mixed with some nice graphics and some very far and few memorable moments. Then you may like this.

I immedietly noticed this game used the actual soundtrack from the series and has some actors from the series actually voice act their characters in the game. That's a big plus.   It's unfortunate that the rest of the game makes it seem as if the entire budged was thrown at exactly that, and everything else was rushed or just left alone.  There's constant flickering in some textures. When starting combat there's a very colorful, mosaic-like plane on the sword for a second. A lot of the animations are rather unnaturally looking. Example of this is heads turning far further back than humanly possible. Walking pases are off, the lip-sync looks like people didn't even try to sync it.. Being someone familiar with 3D modelling and animation I can tell you that this is rather poorly done. The textures are low resolution, too.   The dialogue is rather cringe-worthy at times and there are a lot of aspects that make this game not hold true to the actual environment of the universe. One immediate example is the Night's Watch. In this game they have good armour, a lot of food, halls are lit and seem warm. Whereas we know that the Night's Watch isn't being looked after in the books and the show. They need men, they need supplies; they don't have good armour.   The dialogue, as mentioned before, is pretty cringy. Something that seemed odd to me was (close to the beginning) that I, as Mors, got to carry out the death sentence of the first guy I track. That's a commander's duty, not mine. I'm some ranger, a good one yes, but I am still just a ranger. Some of the dialogue options you get are really bad and don't keep you immersed at all. Everything just seemed very forced.  The combat is rather lackluster. This kind of combat is good (like in Dragon Age) but it has to be done well.   All of this combined made it very hard to enjoy any of the story.  I know this isn't a great review at this point, I'll probably edit and fill it out later. But as of now I'd say don't get this game unless you get it in some bundle. I'm sure there are some people who could find enjoyment in this, but then again there are people who enjoy Goat Simulator P:  Jokes aside, I don't mean to bash this game; 3D animation and modelling is hard not to mention putting all of that together into a working game.  Basically; some may like this and some may not. It depends on you, but I wouldn't recommend it. If you're really that GoT-starved that you need anything you can get, go for it.  NOTE: my playtime is rather low, keep in mind I played this game offline, too.

Ignore my hours, if you want proof of experience just see my achievements.   TL;DR: The music is phenomenal, the gameplay is brutal, the graphics are simple, and the game is short and sweet.  Music  - The genre you're listening to in this is called 'Synthwave,' or 'New Retro Wave.'  - You will find artists like M|O|O|N, Scattle, Perturbator, and quite a few more.  - For the theme of the game, which takes on a retro feel, the sountrack placement and style is excellent.  Gameplay  - This is a top-down shooter game.  - You will most likely die in one hit (without other items in the game).  - Your objective is to kill everyone and everything on one floor, then move onto the next if required.  - If you get killed, you start your current floor over, so don't fret about restarting the entire level.  - Enemies are fast to react, albeit quite predictable in their responses.  Graphics  - The style of the game requires no special look bonuses.  - There is blood and gore EVERYWHERE.  - So much blood.  The Game  - The main game should take you roughly 5 hours of persistence and perseverence.  - Add another 5 or 6 hours and you can be completely done with the game.  - I'll say this right now, if you're like anyone, you're going to get mad. Just keep trying.  - As you progress and unlock new items to aid you, the game because significantly more interesting.  - Most enemies die in a single hit, make your shots count.

Pros:   - Vast weapon customization - Full controller support - Linear story.  Cons:   - Runs through U-Play - Finite campaign content.  Although U-Play has been nothing but a terrible client overlay for Far Cry 3, the game has lived up to the promises metacritics have croned about. Far Cry 3 is vibrant and beautiful. It is sneaky and explosive. It is the alpha and the omega of action-based first person open world shooters. That sounds pretty good, doesn't it? Well, you will have to trust my word and experience it yourself as there is nothing more satisfying than driving a speedboat, shooting incendiary arrows, and watching things go boom. Take out drug lords, explore an island of mystery and bring the myriad to justice. The campaign levels run through leveled zones with ammo and grenade depots that allow the player to keep pushing forward and never stop illiciting gunfire. One of my personal favorite experiences in game may be the silent covert assassination of an entire camp with nothing but a bowie knife. Absolutely wonderful game and excited for the next installment by Ubisoft. However, as the game does run through U-Play on PC, I urge consumers to be aware of the inconveniences associated. As for the client, I hope a solid rework the interface and the way the overlay functions while gaming arrives soon.   Bottom Line: Far Cry 3's weapon customization and actionbased gameplay lend it high replay value in the co-op market even though the solo content may be lack luster. Far Cry 3 = 82/100, U-Play Client = 55/100

This game was vastly different from any other game I've ever played and I've played my fair share. You are defenseless, but there's nothing really to kill you except for your own mind. The concept behind this game is amazing and the AI for the monster is one of the best I've ever seen. I've read it was supposed to be based on the dev's cat and I can totally see that. It behaves just like a real life cat would, and if you treat it thusly you can generally escape it.   This game was a very well rounded game in my opinion and once I started playing it I couldn't stop until I was finished. I completed the game in two days I think. Not only is it an incredibly unique and interesting idea, the execution was great as well. The visuals, and most importantly the flowers, were stunning and I could tell a lot of them were modelled after real flowers. The story line which you garner from notes left by people before was intriguing and chilling. The behavior of the monster just blew me away. The heartbeat noise alone was enough to get my own heart pounding. I could tell the dev put a TON of effort into thinking out the monster which I must applaud.  At first I disliked the triangulating your position aspect of the map, but after a while I came to like and respect it as an idea. Also I liked that it wasn't as hard as just wandering through the jungle, aimlessly and that you were actually pointed in the right direction gently. The size of the map and the details within the terrain were very well done.   I practically have nothing but praise for this game. I think one of my biggest... dislikes? Was that when you died you were merely sent back to your last save point. So there was really no threat.   Anywho, I would definitely recommend this game.  Probably one of the most memorable games I've ever played.

Got the game in early may. must say that the game has come a long way since then. content adding has been doen very well and the addition of special events on a monthly basis has made the game a lot less repetetive than what it used to be. the only thing i really dislike about this game is as much as all the essential components in warframe are able to be obtained for free using the crafting feature, there is no possible way to abtain free inventory slots for extra weapons other than the 10 you get from the start.  As for controls, they are pretty good, although the vertical wallrunning feature can bug at times. the recoil system is what to expect from a 3rd person shooter but they have made each weapon have it's own recoil trend. Fps gamers will have no trouble adjusting. controls can be changed in game as always but be wary of when you do it as changing in some cinematics will cause a CTD  UI has changed several times since the release and the latest one has been the best one yet. the missions are put into a web starting from mercury and spreading to the other planets. the other features in the main menu are placed accross the top of the screen. the UI has several minor and rare problems with locking.  Gameplay is really unique. not like any other 3rd person shooters ive every played. there is a good diversity in game types and the leveling system is fair. the game is very forgiving in early stages being a little too easy with an almost extreme bend in difficulty after the first few planets, where it comes to the point where missions may be unplayable solo and will require a team complete, epecially in higher T3 void missions and derelict. Every warframe has its own unique characteristics from health and shields to abilities and appearances, which can be enhanced and/ or altered using mods and apparel customisations found in missions or purchased in store using micro transactions in the game.  For a free to play, i must say it has some AAA title qualities in it and can be a fun game to play with friends. you will have to pay a little money down the track if you want to keep all your weapons in the future. Overall, nice experiment. it's coming along nicely, some good features.  Content 7/10 Updating 9/10 Gameplay 8/10 Controls 7/10 Graphics 9/10 Compatibility 8/10 Stability 7/10 OVERALL 7.8/10

If you however want to run around wielding a katana and sometimes shoot at stuff while being constantly badgered with achievements this might be something for you. I think this simulation game is a wonderful, living reminder of how great our railways once were. Some of the DLC we see is absolutely amazing, as you can see with this historical example in this video:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jFOIg4XN9Ks  So I'm saddened to see so many negative reviewers whose basis is the complaint that we have to pay for the DLC. Do they expect developers to make DLC for free in this day and age? Anyone who has made so much as a scenario for Train Simulator will know just how time consuming developing is. To make from scratch, a good route or a good model of a locomotive is mammoth task...and one that should be encouraged.  Simulation games like this are a threatened genre, a niche whose survival depends on revenue generated by DLC. If people keep complaining they might find that DTG turn it into an arcade game - that works on mobile devices. After all that's what sells to the broader market and will guarantee revenue.   So as I said, I think this simulation game is a wonderful, living reminder of how great our railways once were...but I would also add that it is also a reminder of how great computer games once were. It's orgins date back to the good old days of computer games, when developers didn't think too about on their intended market, but followed inspired idea, to the days of Microsoft Flight Simulator and Morrowind. It if development stops on it, like it for Microsoft Flight Simulator, all thse negative views will be partly responsible. And it's the kind of game we are unlikely to see ever again. Something to think about.

I've been waiting to get my hands on this game, and the reviews seemed fine enough to finally buy it. I've managed to get about one hour into the game, and my experience was this: - The game's graphics is inconsistent. Some parts - faces, petty details on leaves - are nice, but all in all this 2014 piece resembles something from the last decade. - The camera is horrible. The 3rd person view is inconvenient enough from the camera placing, but it glitches every time I've tried to move through a doorway, ending up in directions I did not want to look or be. - The monsters won't react to the attacks, they appear from nothing (I know it's also a feature, but I've died more times from monsters I couldn't even see, sense, or interact with in any way. THIS, however, is NOT part of the game.) - The dubbing and the voice acting is TERRIBLE. Not to mention the pacing of the dialogues, and the story itself. - Large/massive playspace is great. However, if I have to run for 30 minutes to get from A to B in a LINEAR game, just to spend 10 more minutes on badly-dubbed and scripted dialogues to get a loading screen, then it's just a waste of time. All in all, this game did not meet my long-built expectations. It could be a great Fatal Frame remake or copy, but it... Just isn't. I asked for a refund - for the very first time.

And the team behind the original game does it again.  Even intro made me smile, the way it makes fun of the intro of the Game of thrones TV series.  Like I said in my previous reviews of the series, it's well written, its funny and contains enough references to other fantasy worlds to make your inner geek wear a great smile on his face.  This time the main focus is on the game's version of a magical wizards university (and yes, there is a magical library involved at some point and there are plenty of nods to Harry Potter and Diskworld as well).  The dialogue and situations are as fun as ever, the puzzles are a less frustrating this time around and it is generally a good time if you like your traditional point an click adventure games.  A lot of the old NPC characters make a welcome return and the nice thing is is that you feel like they have been having some adventures of their own when you run into them again.  It does end on a (partial) cliffhanger though, so I really hope that we can get a BoUT 3.

This steam rerelease modernizes the graphics a bit along with making the game work at all with newer versions of Windows. For some reason the options are set to extremely low graphical fidelity and you can almost definitely enable maximum on every setting on whatever computer you have today. The UI looked dated when Immortal Defense was originally released, and it unsurprisingly still does, but that's a very minor quibble that you won't even notice after a few levels.

This a fun little filler of game that takes place in dungeon with connecting rooms. Your job is simply to kill all the mobs in the room and then grab the key to exit to another room. You keep doing this until you reach the boss, which, after beating, will grant you access to the next floor. There are four floors in this game, and runs can take you anywhere from a few minutes to a half hour, so it's great to pick up and play when you want something quick and light.   There are bonus levels and shops along the way to upgrade your stats such as speed, damage, rate of fire, health, and other goodies. If you save your money, you can buy permanent stats that will help you with future runs, which is nice since you lose everything when you die. The neat thing is you find yourself progressing fast enough, so you don't feel like it's a long grind.  Overall this is a simple game with decent replayability and just enough depth to make it enjoyable. It's perfect for me because I like to play a few quick rounds and then come back later. I certainly recommended this game.

Ziggurat is an absolutely fantastic first person dungeon-crawler shooter! I can honestly say that it doesn't do too many things wrong, but it definitely does SO many things right.   The game plays as a rogue-like, so the power ups, items, dungeon layouts, etc will all change for   So here's the Upside:  - The sense of speed is phenomenal and combines well with good maneuverability. - There is a decent amount of auto-aim so that the game doesn't become too frustrating with the difficulty.  - The weapons are variable and plentiful, with even more to be unlocked. - There are 4 categories of weapons: Wand (always have), Staves, Spell Books, and Alchemic Weapons. - It's fairly easy to keep your weapon meters full and ready to use.  - While there is a bit of a learning curve for sure, the challenge of becoming better is very rewarding and inviting.  Ex: Upon dying, you'll wrack up points that will unlock new abilities and upgrades at random. These upgrades can be found randomly within the dungeon or earned by leveling up.  - Speaking of upgrades, I enjoyed how you were given 3 at random upon leveling up, but certain upgrades allowed for more options or to pick more than one.  - I enjoyed how every floor of the dungeon had a randomized boss usually chosen from 3 different potential bosses with their own arenas and gimmicks.  - Another enjoyable feature were that not all rooms were used for spawning and fighting enemies.   Ex: There are enemy rooms, trap rooms, puzzle/treasure rooms, shrine rooms, and the occasional hidden room.  - The game has a good balance of platforming and fighting as a result of these various types of rooms.  - When it comes to unlockables, there are TONS of characters, each with very customized starting builds to keep the experience fresh as you go along, as well as unique challenges for unlocking them.  Ex: Some have more speed but less defense, some are more suited to staves or spell books or even the basic wand, some may gain experience faster. - Lastly, a very cool feature they included is a daily challenge that everyone can compete in. Everyone has the same wizard from the beginning and the goal is just to go as far as you can before dying. Then, you're posted on a leaderboard for that day.  Downside:  - The types of enemies are not the most plentiful, but they do a good job of using various enemies at once when fighting. - The learning curve can be a bit too much for many people.  - While 'easy' is too easy, 'normal' is considerably harder.  - There can be the issue of packing too many enemies into too small a room, thus making it more likely that you'll die.  - Due to the randomness of the upgrades and types of magic you find, you may end up having a poor run due to not getting enough of the proper upgrades you need or the right types of spell books, staves, alchemic weapons, etc.   Overall, the game is a wonderful experience that is worth the money. It has great replayability because there's always someone or something to unlock, but the process of learning and becoming better is also greatly rewarding. When you make it to the next floor you feel accomplished, but finally completing the game feels amazing. The controls are smooth, the graphics are enjoyably Alice-esque, and the upgrades and weapons are so diverse, creative, and random that you are guaranteed to have to play on your toes as each new experience with a new procedurally generated dungeon demands new strategies.   Final Notes: - Great challenge - Great reward

'The most innovate shooter I've played in years.'  Okay, that's out of the way. I remember being incredibly impressed by the original gamejam prototype quite a few years back and by the time it dropped off my radar, it was finally out. My opinion? Very good. They took the idea and ran with it and improved the formula to some great, new places and strategies. Is it content light? I would honestly say yes but the replay value is certainly there thanks to Killstagram showing off your more impressive kills, a challenge mode system with some interesting ways to restrict yourself and of course, endless mode. Is it worth 25 dollars? I'd frankly say 'that depends'. It's a great game, a very unique mechanic taken to its extreme but if all you want is the 'story', you'll probably be disappointed. I'm getting my money's worth out of this but it's hard to say that everyone will.

http://youtu.be/ynz6HU7ukbE   STiDM Gaming – Review This is a quick review based on my first 1.6 hours of playing the store mode. Up front, I enjoyed it, it’s a great little button masher and reminds me of playing double dragon on the NES, just with cute-as-heck characters both in appearance and personality. Here’s some of my favorite aspects.     Adorable characters:  The chibi art style makes every action on screen nothing short of cute, even some of the enemies, in my opinion follow this adorable aesthetic.    Adorable voice acting:  Majority of the characters in this game are female and my experience with Yuzuha (the Ninja Girl) had me giggling. From her shocked face during dialog to the animation of being defeated, I couldn’t help but find humor in both.    Character Progression:  I love the idea, in any game, of leveling and progressing a character. As you move along, you accumulate experience, which naturally levels you up. You can then allocate points into basic stats and unlock abilities.   Unlockables:  [/b] Though there may not be many, I love being able to unlock additional characters/costumes. Starting out, you have 4 characters, all of which have a few color variations.   Art and Animation:  From the backgrounds to the characters themselves, the art is fitting and the animation is smooth. The emotions that Yusuha would show had me rolling. Moving around was fluid and the particle graphics added to the excitement of downing each foe and executing special attacks.    Controls:  At first I used my Logitech gamepad but it was recognizing the controls in awkward locations. I could’ve remapped them but I grabbed an Xbox 360 controller and it recognized the controls perfectly without configuring. The responsiveness of the controller was spot on, I can’t really complain, I had no issues. There’s essentially two planes you fight on which I will call “front-stage” and “back-stage” for the sake of not knowing what to call them! [/list]  Ultimately, I loved it. I’m a nut for anime so I thoroughly enjoyed the Japanese voice-over and chibi art style. The gameplay is fun and can be challenging at first. I’ve yet to try to do any other mode other than story but I look forward to it.

A shockingly good metroidvania despite the visual presentation of the game. After playing the game for about 10 minutes I realized that under those old graphics is a monster of a game. Heavily influenced by Super Metroid in terms of world and level designs, as well as power-ups, this game really recaptures the magic. The game also has multiple endings, which may not be obvious when you play through it. There is a massive amount of content that is optional in order to find the alternative endings. You will need to solve many puzzle and riddles, and decipher another language akin to what was seen in FEZ if you want find out how deep the rabbit hole goes. Overall the game is well worth the asking price, and if you decide to chase after the bonus content, you will be driven to madness. The only real beef with the game is a lack of achievements.
