Downfall: ~~~~~~~~    Joe and Ivy Davis have been married for several years but their marriage has not been smooth sailing for a while. Ivy has not been herself lately as bad memories from the past keep haunting her. Joe organized a small vacation at the “Quiet Haven Hotel” in an attempt to work out their marriage. As they arrive at the “Quiet Haven Hotel”, they are welcomed by an incredible storm and heavy rain. Could this be an omen for what is to come?    Downfall is a point and click/rich story/adventure game where you play the character of Joe Davis.  First you will play a prologue (tutorial) where you will learn how to interact with other characters and as well as how use items from your inventory. In the prologue, you will be told how Joe and Ivy met for the first time when they were but spring chickens. It will also outlay Ivy’s prime concern at this particular time of her life, and what happened to Robbie, Joe’s little brother.  Then you will get a short, effective intro which will give you the feel of how the atmosphere will be portrayed in the game. As you enter the lobby of the hotel, Ivy will act in very strange ways, speaking in an incoherent manner and then back to normal several times. After speaking with manager, Joe will move up to their room and head off to bed. After a while dreaming, he wakes up in the room alone. From here, Joe will need to find out where his troubled wife is.   Throughout this dark adventure, Joe will meet interesting characters to talk to in hope of saving Ivy. There is Doctor Z, who conducts brain transplants; Agnes, who does not know Joe but will party with him; as well as other intriguing personalities. Most of the story will be in the hotel, but Joe will also visit places on the outside, keeping you alert and entertained.   Downfall look goods graphically with its peculiar atmosphere. All the mechanisms are easy to control with your keyboard. The highest resolution you can play this game is 800X600. The voiceover could have been better.   Positives: ~~~~~~~~~~  - Good & Interesting Story - Easy interface to use - Good graphics and atmosphere - Achievements & trading cards  Negatives: ~~~~~~~~~~   - Puzzles could have been harder - Highest resolution is 800X600 - Voiceover could have better  If you enjoy a rich story with a freaky atmosphere and strange characters, then downfall should be in your game library.  7/10  Key provided by developer/publisher for review purposes. After logging many hours on this game in the last two years, I finally decided to write a review. My taste in PC games mostly includes the strategy (especially turn based and pausable RTS) genre. I'm a fan of the X-Com Games, The Total War Series, Close Combat, etc. I played all of the Jagged Alliance Games and enjoyed them all. In my opinion, JABIA is a good game if you like this type of game; a game that requires a lot of management (inventory, strategy, assembling teams, et.). I found the planning and execution of my plans in this game a lot of fun. There are a few rough edges. They could have included a way to look at items ordered after you ordered them, as in the old JA Games. As it is now, you place the order and you have to remember what you ordered until it arrives and the only place it can arrive is at the Drassen Airport, requiring a lot of 'mule' trips by your mercs who will spend much of their time running back and forth for supplies and using newly hired mercs as mules when they come in. The end result of this is that I found myself spending much of my time organizing logistics for reenforcement and resupply. If, like me, you enjoy this in a game, then you won't mind it and may even have fun with it. If this kind of micro-management isn't to your taste, skip this game.  The other part of the game is the tactical combat. This is done using a real time pausable system that requires many pauses and movement order updates during combat to be succesful (survive). With a small group of mercs (one or two teams of 6 each) it can be very exciting and great fun, but as the game goes on, and especially when you are getting ready for the final assaults on the enemy strongholds, you will probably have several dozen mercs going against hundreds of bad guys in just a few sectors. At this point the tactical combat becomes overwhelming as it is virtually impossible to be everywhere at once and are therefore forced to send a small group of mercs at a time against many enemies to whittle them down slowly, and it becomes a drawn out meat grinder of attrition. Not much sneaking around, tactical planning here. Just a whole bunch of shooting against very long odds. I think JA2 did much better by forcing the player, VIA limited finances and merc contracts, to run fewer teams and keep the game managable, while not requiring quite so many mercs to succesfully complete the game. When you hire mercs in BIA, you pay the fee up front and they are yours for the whole campaign regardless of the length of time they work for you, and the money you find and earn in this game will easily run into the hundreds of thousands, so there is a balance issue here. Also, militia are pretty innafective, and when they die the weapons you equip them with dissapear. I found it much more effective, once you've been in the game for awhile to just leave teams of mercs within striking distance of primary towns and mines that you have captured and send them in when enemies try to attack. Mercs can move amazingly fast along roads and, as mentioned before, you'll have the money and therefore can afford the extra mercs to do the job.   Some things I really like about this game:  -Friendly fire is a threat. This adds some realism that I welcome, especially with grenades and explosives. You don't have the automatic grenade placement of the new Xcom. 'Watch out for that tree branch...Hit the dirt!'  -The strategic logistical and organizational planning and the tactical planning for each sector. A lot of fun here if you like that sort of thing.  -The great fun of fast moving firefights once you get used to the interface, especially when the skirmishes are small.  There are some annoying bugs and design flaws, a few of which are:  -In close combat you can't shoot at an enemy when he gets close to you. You'll hear comments like 'Can't make the shot' and your merc will just stand there as the enemy soldier cuts you to pieces, even if you have a pistol or SMG.  -Unlike the other good games in this series, you can't repair any equipment but weapons. Body armor just takes damage until it's useless, and it is very expensive.  -Sometimes when told to fire repeatedly at one target, your mercs will cycle through the count-down time for aiming, then cycle through again without firing. They seem to be able to do this indefinately so you have to watch them carefully.  -When in a firefight, mercs are often forced into a kneeling position (frequently exposing themselves to enemy fire) when doing certain tasks, like first aid, clearing a weapon or disarming a mine. This also happens when you try to arm a LAW (Light Anti-tank Weapon). This unfortunate glitch will get you hit fast!  -Game stops working occasionally for no apperent reason ('JABIA has stopped working'). This doesn't happen often, but just when you are about to win an intense twenty minute firefight, it's a bit annoying.  NOTE: There is a very good mod that helps this game quite a bit called the 'Realistic Rebalancing Mod for Jagged Alliance - back in Action' by KILLATOMATE. I highly recommend using this mod to help some balancing issues and decrease the frequency of some glitches within the game.  As it is, I find this to be a very good game that has a lot of replayability. It's interesting to start off with various combinations of mercs to build different teams, then progress theough the game by taking areas of the map over in different order, etc. If they had taken a little more time and care in the development of this game, it could have been great.

review_text: Great game, perfect sequel to the first one. Great improvements across the board, literally the only two things I prefer about the first game, is firstly, the length, this game is far shorter, and the ending kinda gets boring so you start rushing through the final level. And secondely the nostalgia, though far more fun to play with, and have on a team, the characters from this game just arent as memorable as Bastilla and Carth. Strike Suit Zero is one of the best space simulators to come along for a long time, and is comparable to Freelancer, Homeworld and O.R.B. in terms of enjoyability. While it has striking similarities with Project Sylpheed, it concentrates more on action, fighter-to-fighter dogfights, and smaller realistically-powerful weapon loadouts. The story is somewhat watery, but is a tried and true plot; the interactions with Control (and the A.I. 2.0) are amusing and probably simulate true intelligent and powerful A.I. There is a wide variety of settings for playing the game, and even the mouse and keyboard (for me) worked wonderfully. I would give this a 9, 9.5 maybe out of 10, and would recommend buying this, especially if you can get it on sale, to any lover of space games that aren`t strategy.

This game does something very subtle that makes it unplayable for me. Whenever you side step the camera tilts. People don't walk this way, we almost never bank our heads when walking sideways. In fact we make great effort to keep our heads stable and upright. The effect in the game gives me motion sickness.  When I stop walking I don't need to see the camera settle, sure people do settle their weight but this also adds nausea to the feel of the game.   This may be a great game if you're not affected by these things, but I'm seeing it more and more in first person games and it ruins the experience for me.

I don't consider this videogame as RUBBISH. Many people think that but I disagree. Actually, I enjoyed the storyline. After all, you are an orc and not the usual human greenskins killer! The game has different defects. The animations are not really synchronized and the AI looks pretty stupid many times. Those must be improved in a sequel honestly. Graphically I expected something really better, so I was a bit disappointed when I start playing it. I don't wanna talk about the gameplay.. it's really boring and it must be redone completely. I think a 'more action' style would be more funny than simply clicking on skills and look at your characters doing the job for ya. There's also some micro-management in that, but nothing serious. As I said before, the storyline is nice and I played it just for it. Actually, there are different side quests, but, actually, I don't know if they affect the story at all. Oh,yes, one more thing: In my opinion, the 'missions' are too short. Most of the time you'll be watching videos. They are not that bad but I'd like to play something more too. The right balance should be found. I didn't pay for a movie, but a videogame ;). This may be exaggerated but, still, the good balance must be found. I'd recommend this game if you wanna play a nice storyline and love orcs.

Anna: Extended Edition represents the natural maturation of Dreampainters debut title. Italian developers corrected the most bitter points of their previous work in order to create a deep and complete game, an homage at the same time to the never-dying adventure game genre and to the most deep and sick horror.Anna Extended Edition has brand new accessible puzzles, a larger area to explore and stronger narrative material. Its so much richer compared to the standard edition that even those who have already finished the original game will find many good reasons for a second play through.In this Extended Edition Dreampainters has given a more professional look to Anna. Now the Italian adventure is also less cryptic and more understandable, although many plot points are still unclear and some puzzles must be solved again with a lot of trial & error.Anna Extended Edition improves the basic title from every point of view. The game is one of the best Horror Adventure available on the market.

This game, Planetside 2 is hands down the best free to play game I have ever played. It is an amazingly huge game with astonishingly large landmasses and varied lands and environments to wage all out intense, thrilling, and immersing war in. You can play as one of three factions all with their own quirks. The gameplay has a lot of depth, and it may take a while to get used to. You can take control of many different kinds of vehicles. From light and agile aircrafts to strong and bulky tanks. There are three continents you will wage war over, with a total of two thousand soldiers on a server. There are no matches or loading screens, how power fluctatues is dependant on how well your team fights. Oh, did I mention the graphics for this game are simply amazing? If you're sceptical, you should just download and try this gem. It is free to play so you have nothing to lose. I don't have enough room to describe this game! Just try it!

I do not know where to start, to be honest. This game was simply, one of the best games I have ever played, for years. When I was little my dad bought me FarCry, and I played it, but it didn't interest me as much, because I was more of an arena gamer. But this game here, man, simply blew my mind. The campaign got me stuck to the characters, and the missions actually gave a challenge. I never bought any thing or used skill points because I never really payed attention to those, but when I started using them, it made the game alot more fun. The campaign has tons of plot-twists and made the game more exciting, and for the choices you choose, it creates a more diverse story.  As an improvement for the game, it would be better if the 'Boss battles' or combat battles would be more realistic, because I feel like I am in a dream whilst dueling Vaas/Hoyt.   One Hole in the story though, its a slight hole though. IN the beginning, you see a guy in a beanie wearing glasses: THat ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ is Vincent Salas. Who is he? Is he the friend Vaas mentions in the beginning, 'DO YOU WANT ME TO CUT YOU OPEN LIKE I DID YOUR FRIEND(Topkek he forgot to say no homo)' Anyways, this character was supposed to be in the game where you accidentally shoot him in the game when you are with Vaas. His name isnt even in the intro, yet you still see him and you are with him. And he was never in any part of the game besides the intro, and none of the characters even mentioned him. But if none of the characters mentioned him, that would mean he never existed; then who was Vaas mentioning in the beginning? Anyways, Vincent was just cut from the game for some reason, but he is presumed dead.  Overall this game was pretty damn amazing. 10/10 With badass approval.

I just had two AI trade Connecticut Ave (giving the other the monopoly) for States Ave (no opportunity to build) one-for-one. Nothing to sweeten the deal, just an even trade.  Had the same thing happen in a previous game when AI traded Boardwalk for Marvin Gardens + $2. Gave both the chance to build. WTF?  With the cards, AI-drawn cards generally earn them money or properties, while player-drawn cards seem to cost money every time.   Best advice is to get a minimum of one of each color early. When someone owns ONE of a color you don't, trade for money and keep it. if they get two, they'll never give you one for anything other than a rediculous amount of cash, or something on which they can build. Otherwise the AI will trade-f#ck you.  The only thing religious on Christmas in my family is our love of Monopoly with NO house rules, and the crap I'm seeing with the AI here is so absurd, even the worst players in real life wouldn't do. I'm no sore loser, but it takes the fun out of it completely.

Receiver features the most fully simulated gun mechanics in any game I've played yet. The concept is very solid, challenging, and satisfying: operating each part of a gun individually. There is a mod that adds a Remington 870, a Thompson submachine gun, and another handgun. Each weapon type behaves and is operated differently. There is even more that can be done in simulating the weapons, however, and the nuances of the weapons couldn't be captured in just a week's time for development. I hope to see these complex gun mechanics in other games and I hope to see the concept developed further, perhaps in VR, where key memorization is not as needed.

What an excellent third-person zombie survival experience. This game is a must play, and here's why... (TL;DR - Buy it if you're a zombie survival enthusiast, you won't be dissappointed.)  The game starts you off literally in the midst of an attack, akin to an opening scene of any classic zombie film. From the get go you're immersed in this world where the narrative outcome is a result of the player's choices. It's a wide open sandbox and has a linear story, but you're never forced to complete this before that. Run, kill, explore, scavenge - enjoy it like any other sandbox title.   The enviornment is well done, ranging from a woodsy rural small town, country fields and farm housing, to a budding community including town hall and all. Zombie types range from the classic walker/shambler to mutant types keeping the action fresh. The sounds are well done, but limited. NPC and player avatar voiceovers are from a small group of people, though the chatter borders a fine line of eliminating the stress and depressing tones of the game, to outright annoyingly persistant check-ins.   Nearly never a dull moment while exploring as mechanics of fatigue provides a sense of urgency while scavenging, while keeping alert and noise to a minimum. Combat is solid as each melee hit feels like the weapon you're using has weight to it. Developing skill perks as your proficiency in the weapons used increase with use is great, having different survivors excel in a variety of weapons. Each NPC is slightly different than the other, but over time using them will increase the skill base.   Having a home location, you'll need to gather supplies for periodically, or send out runners to collect goods you've previously discovered. There are several locations each with unique built-in add-ons, though each play through the housing locations are always the same. A random location generator would have been a welcomed feature.  All in all, I've thoroughly enjoyed this game so much that I purchased it for the Xbox 360 on it's release, then bought it on Steam when I won a copy of Breakdown (a fantastic DLC mode worth checking out as well.) and highly recommend it to any zombie survival enthusiast. Personally, it's the best model out to date and can not wait for UndeadLabs to develope their MMO of this or any sequels in store.

Blast Em!,  My review of this game is that i feel it's a nice simple game, which is also refleshing and pretty enjoyable.  I've also noticed that since playing it my ability to priortize concentration and reflex's and reaction timing as improved, (you subconscious feel it improving as the number of enemies appear the longer you stay in the game).  Key Points :- Improvment of Concentration. (Able to ignore movement in background image).  Reaction Timing. (improves split second thinking on which you can touch and cant touch).   Reflexes / Improved Motor Control. (lowers the chance of you moving the mouse to much), (i.e. moving your mouse just a little tomuch upwards and hitting an enemy).  I've also noticed that since playing this my reaction timing as improved in games like CoD or Payday. (i'm better able to see slight movement on screen, and improved chances of getting headshots).  As also improved reflexes for games like Dont starve. where you need to hit fast moving monsters before they hit you. (as they are a pain in the backside to hit before they've managed to hit you at least twice).  So totally worth the money paid to get this game. I hope to see more work from the devs, maybe one day they will make a game thats as good as the R-type games.

This game will not appeal to everyone...it is a very dark, gloomy environment, with very simple graphics. But it's a very different game...much different than what most are use to seeing/playing.   The point of the game is to have your group survive in a bombed out city...you may have to steal from others, possibly hurt or kill others, and all your choices will have consequences. Your choices will affect the mental and emotional states of the individuals in your group...and because you can make different choices on other play-throughs, this game can be played multiple times, with varying changes based on your decisions.  Key word to remember is that your group is trying to 'survive' -- does not mean there will be rainbows and sparkles. It is gloomy, and sad, and thought provoking...and many will try it and decide they do not like it right away.   But I think it is worth giving it a try -- if you can get it on sale (I just bought the game for $3.99 USD), what do you really have to lose other than the time you put into playing the game?  Update: Day 44 of my first play-through, and lost a member of the group (shot/killed by bandits)...once they are dead you can't get a 'do over', (unless you quit the game and start a new game). Just adding this to show that as mentioned above there are consequences to your decisions in-game (I chose to scavenge/explore a dangerous area and got someone killed)...no resurrection in this game...that is a good thing...you need to think before you act.  This game may seem overly 'simple & boring' to some initially...give it a chance and you will see there is more depth to it than you may think at first.

As a gamedesigner, i think this game succeeds immensely on art and small features, but fails on the core aspect of the game.  It is confusing and difficult due to a design mishap imo. Hear me out cause its tough as ♥♥♥♥ to describe.   You start. Gear up and move in a tactical point and click way. So the map is square divided, like xcom, fireemblem etc right? but.. its not turn based, so basicly its real time point and click with the fluidity of a tactical/board type of game lol. Confusing? well, to make things worst, when an enemy spots you, he moves square by square as if in a strategy game lol, but to attack him you have to spam the mouse, because the more you click the faster you atk?!? Plus the tile real time point and click movement, is frustrating as times, since you end up walking around what you want to interact...   a confusion of genres that makes no sense...  Not only that, but the art is just perfect for an rpg, a tactical rpg, which in a way i was led to believe. But no, this game can be described as half strategy, half rpg, half roguelike and bigger half confusion and lack of direction....   as a 'upgrade on death' type of game, this game fails for me. Because simply put, you gear up for a playthrough but when you die, you lose your gear. Ok. But you playthrough to make new gear... Wut? where is the upgrade? i see 1 cycle of never ending difficulty, confusion and ♥♥♥♥. There is a stat upgrader, which just adds to the mess and pile of 'dont understand doesnt feel right'...  Sry but it feels that they were heading at a great classical rpg/strat game (fireemblem style) with modern art touch and made a turn into action 'bastion' style game.... The product is a waste with great graphics.

The Descendant tries really hard. Somebody obviously put some thought into how the game would look, a style reminiscent of Telltale adventures. Decent taste in influences buoyed my hope... the vaults of Fallout decorated with the computers and cryogenic pods of the Alien franchise. Tragically, that kindred spirit wears thin within the first ten minutes. All components for a successful game are present, but they're sewn together with dental floss. The click-to-walk controls are endlessly frustrating; coupled with ♥♥♥♥-poor fixed camera angles, the clunky maneuvers make you wonder if your character has been skipping meds. Occasionally you'll get to button mash while cranking valves or opening doors or something equally boring. These trifles are trumped by poor dialogue, amateurish voice acting, and tediously linear gameplay. I'd like to believe the story of The Descendant is worth telling, but it certainly isn't worth playing.

This game is amazing. There is no arguement, no doubt, and absolutely no reason why you shouldn't play. Sure the visuals are a bit dated, but no game has immersed me just as perfectly as this one did. The music, or lack thereof, is a prime example of this. There is only music in scenes that truly need it, and when it is there you don't notice because it is just a part of the environment, but sometimes it leaves you with just the sounds of the environment, the enemies, your footsteps, and your weapons of total annihilation. The game becomes one with you. The story is another prime aspect of this, with nothing given to you in the form of a cutscene, no narration, and no straight out explanation. Everything you know is through experience, or what you overhear from others around you. You are part of the game, and it is a fantastic experience.  I nominated this game for the 'Test of Time' award because of what I said above. The game is wonderful, and it is a great game to come back to any time I am looking for something to play.

Feels dumbed down compared to Bioshock 1 and 2. Girl throws you ammo, health, salts, and brings you back to life. Really annoying getting stuff handed to me like that all the time. Plasmids and other perks feel dumbed down compared to the other games in the series. Only caring two weapons is just annoying, doesn't make the game any harder. It just makes me less creative in a fire fight. I felt no need to combo my attacks with my plasmids and just stuck with spamming them. In the old game you would put sticky mines on the cieling and then a trap that launches them into it. Or the one two punch combo. I found the Handy Men to be really op. They have speed, power, and armor to boot. In the old games the big daddies had at least one flaw in their attack pattern or the way they fight. Not being able to switch to armor piercing rounds on my pistol to help me take something like that down is a real pain if I decide to only take a pistol. So I also miss having the alternate ammo types for the weapons. The alternate ammo taps increased the verity of how you could approach fights by laying down more traps or using specialized damage ammo that is good against people, armor, or just elemental ammo that shock/burn. Over shield is pretty stupid and this just feels like they rubbed COD all over the gameplay or any other generic 1st person shooter.   Story is alright. I found it annoying after winning the game. DLC also suck because they added back the ability to hold lots of weapons, but made it into more of a stealth game. In the old ones who could juggle stealthy and action appraoch as you saw fit. DLC story manages to almost ruin Bioshock story for me. I hate that girl so much.

I couldn't stop playing this all day, for ten days straight, and i am still not quite tired of it.  This Game is a little Gem, nicely polished over the years. A lot of fun for little money. A definate recommendation, if you think a crossover of Diablo and Minecraft could be your thing.  The gameplay is straight forward. There is a steady path of progression which you can follow. Or you can just have a break from that and enjoy the sandbox.  The game is extremely gear dependant and not so much about actual 'mad player skills'. If you find yourself dying a lot, the next tier of equipment will most likely get you back on your feet again. The challenge lies in obtaining that.  There is a very well written community wiki. It covers pretty much every aspect of the game, and you will need to consult it a lot to eliminate confusion about items and to get ideas about what to do next.   One big meh exists: The inventory System could use alot of love, with the vast amount of different Items it is quite easy to lose track of what you have .....and where. Especially with these tiny icons and non existant auto-sorting functions.  Tip for beginners, Plan a good Storage area and start sorting things right from the beginning. It will spare you sooo much annoying searching later on.

5 November 2014  I want to recommend this game so much, trust me I do. This is the breath of fresh air we all so desperately need after the unsatisfying Call of Duty: Ghosts. Playing through Advanced Warfare's campaign was a blast! The singe player is absolutely worth while. The multiplayer, however, is where things go a bit downhill.   For starters, (as I'm sure many, if not all have heard) there are currently no dedicated servers. I feel like whenever I join a game, it's a gamble on whether or not I'll be playing a nice game of CoD or just sit in frustration as players run in place and teleport around.   Second, the good weapons are actually given to you in the early ranks. Weapons like the AK12 prove to be great for new players who aren't used to Call of Duty games and are still in the early ranks, because the weapons are overpowered. That deminishes some of the satisfaction and incentive for actually reaching higher ranks.  Third, the Zombies feature is going to be a priced DLC. Personally, I think it should have been a part of the base game like the black ops games or even world at war.  I do plan on playing this game more, because I can look through the problems that it has. However, I do hope that some (if not all) issues will be resolved in future patches. Hopefully I'll be able to recommend this game 100%.

Amazing game , with highly realistic HUD (very few details), great choice of weapons and assymetrical loadouts (choose your own scope, ammo type, foregrip or bipod, silencer, etc), fun game modes, in both cooperative and multiplayer, and maps are fun, if a tad repetitive. Night maps and NVG are cool, and are so much harder than day. Forces teamwork and communication, but mostly making sure you're covering your team's back and not shooting at each other in a crossfire. High tension pulse beating moments, adrenaline rushes, moments of victory, despair, and frustration. Class based combat system with different weapons available to each, and a realistic load limit. Many other features throughout, and an overall 10/10.  For only $10 USD.

I have been playing this game on and off for the last ten years. I can honestly say it has to be my all-time favourite, and the very first game I ever bought which was a fantasy RPG.  You are plunged into an alien world, playing as a foreigner to this land (and you certainly feel it too) with beautiful landscapes and so much open plot you can play a different game every time. The vanilla version of the game is wonderful, but add the thousands of mods you can install onto it and it becomes almost perfect.   Granted, this game is not for the faint-hearted: It's hard. Very hard. From the moment you get off that prison ship in Seyda Neen. However, put enough time into it and all the hard work pays off, rewarding you with incredible weapons, spells and armour, or even the ability to create even better equipment.  Don't let the out-dated graphics put you off either; there are tons of mods that provide graphics overhauls, not to mention the Morroblivion mod and the forthcoming Skywind mod.  I can honestly say this is so much better than Skyrim in terms of the diversity of all the creatures and denizens of Morrowind, the massive story and side-quests, the lore and just that feeling you get when you find yourself lost in the Bitter Coast, taking in the scenery, exploring forgotten tombs, foraging strange fauna and hoping to Azura that you don't encounter another damn Cliff Racer.

One of my new favorite point & click adventure games ever made, alongside  Chains of Satinav  and its sequel  Memoria .  The first act is a little slow and the third act a little straightforward, but the writing, dialogue, puzzles, theme and pretty much everything else is incredible! Well deserving of its glowing reviews on Steam and across the internet. I also really liked the various outcomes of your choices during the game's finale, in which even some decisions early on in the game had an impact.  One of Wadjet Eye's best games, if not THE best.

Some pros   + The game is very addicting and can make me sit up all evening + The community has made a load of great mods + The game is very modable + Truck textures on the inside are great + The map is relatively big. You can purchase map DLCs and install map mods to get even more roads to explore (I <3 ProMods) + SCS actualy listens to the community compared to Valve... (CS:GO) + There is many beautiful loads to choose from and you can always get more or swap out some with community mods! + The truck simulation is very realistic and you can toggle on/off some of the settings + TrackIR, VR and multimonitor support is good :)    Some cons   - The game should have snow seasons and that is not just a texture but it is actually animated in a way. I would love to drive in the winter and see my tracks behind me. Also a snow plow should be perfect to this :) - The beacons you can choose are rotors and not blinking lights which modern day trucks have to this date - High beams are not powerful in lenght no matter how many lights you put on your truck - Textures are outdated. The textures used in ETS 2 is pretty much a copy from the original ETS - I would like to see cabin lights - (*) The AI traffic should really be fixed  (*) When you are inside a roundabout, the car that should be stopping for you sometimes just drives out in front of you and you end up crashing in them, giving you a big fine for it. When you indicate out from an intersection, the AI traffic mostly stops for you but when they stop, they either stop right infront of you making it impossible for your trailer to get around, or they will stop and then drive away again.  I have probably played the game for like 1200 hours outside steam. I honestly did not know about steam before my cousin introduced me to CSGO in mid 2015. Right now, I have 225 hours in the game on steam and the game is more of fun now compared to when it first released. There has been so much stuff added to the game since it released, and there are many more mods now then before.  I pre-ordered this game as soon as it was pre-orderable, but the company i pre-ordered from took over a month to deliver so I could sady not get the full feeling of the game from the beginning. Also, I was playing on a laptop back then which I could barely play on low settings without the truck stopping every few settings because of lag. This did for sure not stop me from playing the game. It was so much fun and i spent most of my day playing this game, even though I had like 5 fps. (got a VR ready computer now if anyone cares).
If you've ever dreamt of being a space ninja, now's your chance to live out that dream, but not in real life, in a game. This game, which is awesome.

Warframe is Third - Person Shooter. It is free to play, but it is still in beta. Even though Steam is saying that Warframe is released, it is actuallly still in Beta. It is a multiplayer co-op game, but it almost has like a story mode. You fight against bots and there are no PvP modes and such. The stages are planets, with places inside to play in. There are the 9 planets, even though Pluto isn't one, so I guess 8 planets plus Moon stages. Warframe was just released on March 26, 2013 so not a lot of information yet. It also has parkouring in it, which makes the game even more badass. Score: 8/10 stars. The gameplay is kind of unique with all the classes (which are not free) and swords and stuff that makes you want to play the game a lot more.
Love this game, Klem
